“You have asked wisely what my art can do;
I grudge you nothing that I know to teach,
not even now—for in both word and will
I would be mild with you; in your wise soul
you can attain all things, and your place
is highest in Heaven among the sons of Jove,
who loves you with the fullness of his love.”
source_text:
To whom thus Mercury with prudent speech:—
‘Wisely hast thou inquired of my skill:
I envy thee no thing I know to teach
Even this day:—for both in word and will
I would be gentle with thee; thou canst reach
All things in thy wise spirit, and thy sill
Is highest in Heaven among the sons of Jove,
Who loves thee in the fulness of his love.

text:
Fasten the threads to my life, my Lord,
Then I am ready to leave!
Only a glance at the horses—
Swift! That will suffice!
source_text:
Tie the strings to my life, my Lord,
Then I am ready to go!
Just a look at the horses --
Rapid! That will do!

text:
The eagle, on the rocky height,  
Keeps you in view as prey in sight;  
Within his breast no pity lies,  
Only stern necessity applies.  
But man, to whom alone is given  
A direct beam from pitying heaven,  
Boasts a heart made kindly, humane—  
And slays the creatures for his pleasure slain!
source_text:
The eagle, from the cliffy brow,
Marking you his prey below,
In his breast no pity dwells,
Strong necessity compels:
But Man, to whom alone is giv’n
A ray direct from pitying Heav’n,
Glories in his heart humane—
And creatures for his pleasure slain!

text:
I move from bed to bed, I lie near the others who sleep  
one by one,  
I dream within my dream all the dreams of the other dreamers,  
And I become the other dreamers.
source_text:
I go from bedside to bedside, I sleep close with the other sleepers
each in turn,
I dream in my dream all the dreams of the other dreamers,
And I become the other dreamers.

text:
But we shall quit this cave of shadow-thronged
And dwell among the Gods, and through each day
Hold lofty converse, sharing all they own
Of lavish wealth and spoil that never fails;
And from the portion that my father gave
To Phoebus, I shall wrest my own away,
Which if my father will not—none the less I,
The robber-king, may still but make the trial.
source_text:
‘But we will leave this shadow-peopled cave
And live among the Gods, and pass each day
In high communion, sharing what they have
Of profuse wealth and unexhausted prey;
And from the portion which my father gave
To Phoebus, I will snatch my share away,
Which if my father will not—natheless I,
Who am the king of robbers, can but try.

text:
For as long as life’s dear blood runs warm,
No thought of her shall ever leave my mind;
Though fairest be the grace of her form,
She holds the truest, kindest heart.
O, know ye wha’s, &c.
source_text:
For while life’s dearest blood is warm,
Ae thought frae her shall ne’er depart,
And she, as fairest is her form,
She has the truest, kindest heart.
O wat ye wha’s, &c.

text:
O book, O chants—must all of this come to only this?
Must we barely make it to this first beginning of ourselves?—and yet it is
enough, O soul;
O soul, we have indeed appeared—that is enough.
source_text:
O book, O chants! must all then amount to but this?
Must we barely arrive at this beginning of us? --and yet it is
enough, O soul;
O soul, we have positively appear’d--that is enough.

text:
I wondered how it would shine in gold  
When Richard rode to mill;  
And then I longed to slip away,  
But some force gripped my will.
source_text:
I thought how yellow it would look
When Richard went to mill;
And then I wanted to get out,
But something held my will.

text:
What marvels have we witnessed  
and heard at the Mermaid—words so quick, so finely lit,  
as though whoever spoke them had poured his whole bright wit  
into a jest, and, having done so,  
resolved to spend the rest  
of his slow, unlovely life  
in folly.
source_text:
What things have we seen
Done at the Mermaid! heard words that have been
So nimble and so full of subtle flame,
As if that any one from whence they came
Had meant to put his whole wit in a jest,
And has resolved to live a fool the rest
Of his dull life.

text:
While briars and honeysuckle bud bright green,  
And partridges cry loudly at evening,  
And morning pussy-willows can be seen,  
my muse is stirred,  
This liberty from an unknown friend,  
I pray you excuse.
source_text:
While briers an’ woodbines budding green,
An’ paitricks scraichin loud at e’en,
An’ morning poussie whiddin seen,
Inspire my muse,
This freedom, in an unknown frien’,
I pray excuse.

text:
Once Paumanok,  
When lilac fragrance filled the air, and Fifth-month grass was rising,  
Along this seashore, amid some briers,  
Two feathered visitors from Alabama, the pair together,  
And their nest, and four pale-green eggs freckled brown,  
And every day the male bird near at hand went to and fro,  
And every day the female bird bent on her nest, silent, bright-eyed,  
And every day I, a curious boy, never too near, never troubling them,  
Carefully peering, taking in, interpreting.
source_text:
Once Paumanok,
When the lilac-scent was in the air and Fifth-month grass was growing,
Up this seashore in some briers,
Two feather’d guests from Alabama, two together,
And their nest, and four light-green eggs spotted with brown,
And every day the he-bird to and fro near at hand,
And every day the she-bird crouch’d on her nest, silent, with bright eyes,
And every day I, a curious boy, never too close, never disturbing
them,
Cautiously peering, absorbing, translating.

text:
He hears the lambs’ unguarded cry,  
And the ewes’ soft answer in return;  
He keeps watch while they rest in peace,  
For they know their shepherd has come near.
source_text:
For he hears the lambs’ innocent call,
And he hears the ewes’ tender reply;
He is watchful while they are in peace,
For they know when their shepherd is nigh.

text:
Gone is the merry morris beat;  
Gone the song of Gamelyn’s heat;  
Gone the hard-girt outlaw lying  
Idle in the “green shaw” sighing;  
All have passed and gone away!  
And if Robin were cast today  
Suddenly from his grassy grave,  
And if Marian were to have  
Once more her days among the trees,  
She would weep, and he would craze:  
He would swear, for all his oaks,  
Felled by dockyard hammer-strokes,  
Had rotted out on salt-sea seas;  
She would weep that all her bees  
Sang no more to her—strange thought!  
Honey cannot be had for naught!
source_text:
Gone, the merry morris din;
Gone, the song of Gamelyn;
Gone, the tough-belted outlaw
Idling in the "grenè shawe;"
All are gone away and past!
And if Robin should be cast
Sudden from his turfed grave,
And if Marian should have
Once again her forest days,
She would weep, and he would craze:
He would swear, for all his oaks,
Fall'n beneath the dockyard strokes,
Have rotted on the briny seas;
She would weep that her wild bees
Sang not to her--strange! that honey
Can't be got without hard money!

text:
The monks, in wonder, stand and shiver still,  
They break through the chancel’s shadowed gloom;  
From St. Edmond’s shrine, behold—a skeleton hand  
Points toward the black marble tomb.
source_text:
The Monks in amazement shuddering stand,
They burst through the chancel’s gloom,
From St. Edmond’s shrine, lo! a skeleton’s hand,
Points to the black marble tomb.

text:
I would not dare to leave my friend,
For if he should die while I was gone,
And I, too late, should come at last
To the heart that had been waiting for me;
source_text:
I should not dare to leave my friend,
Because -- because if he should die
While I was gone, and I -- too late --
Should reach the heart that wanted me;

text:
Among those wild mountains my path shall still be,
Each stream foaming downward through its own green, narrow lea;
For there, with my lassie, I wander the long day,
While above us, unheeded, the swift hours of love pass away.
source_text:
Amang thae wild mountains shall still be my path,
Ilk stream foaming down its ain green, narrow strath;
For there, wi’ my lassie, the day lang I rove,
While o’er us unheeded flie the swift hours o’love.

text:
Words—book-words—what are you?
No words at all, for listen and look:
My song is out there in the open air, and I must sing,
With banner and pennant fluttering.
source_text:
Words! book-words! what are you?
Words no more, for hearken and see,
My song is there in the open air, and I must sing,
With the banner and pennant a-flapping.

text:
Another radiance kindled on his mouth,
That mouth from which there once was drawn the breath
That gave it force to pierce the guarded mind
And move into the heart that panted underneath
With lightning and with music: damp death
Had quenched its tenderness upon his icy lips;
And, like a falling star that brands a band
Of moonlit vapor, as the cold night grips,
It kindled through his pallid limbs, then passed into eclipse.
source_text:
Another Splendour on his mouth alit,
That mouth, whence it was wont to draw the breath
Which gave it strength to pierce the guarded wit,
And pass into the panting heart beneath
With lightning and with music: the damp death
Quenched its caress upon his icy lips;
And, as a dying meteor stains a wreath
Of moonlight vapour, which the cold night clips,
It flushed through his pale limbs, and passed to its eclipse.

text:
I see where Ernani walks the wedding garden,  
Through the perfume of night roses, shining, his bride clasped in his hand,  
And hears the hellish summons, the horn’s vow of death.
source_text:
I see where Ernani walking the bridal garden,
Amid the scent of night-roses, radiant, holding his bride by the hand,
Hears the infernal call, the death-pledge of the horn.

text:
So said he one bright morning; and through the day  
His heart beat heavily against his side;  
And to his heart he silently did pray  
For strength to speak; yet still the crimson tide  
Choked back his voice, and beat his purpose astray—  
It fevered all his proud thought of such a bride,  
Yet drew him down to meekness like a child:  
Alas! when passion is at once meek and wild!
source_text:
So said he one fair morning, and all day
His heart beat awfully against his side;
And to his heart he inwardly did pray
For power to speak; but still the ruddy tide
Stifled his voice, and puls'd resolve away--
Fever'd his high conceit of such a bride,
Yet brought him to the meekness of a child:
Alas! when passion is both meek and wild!

text:
FIRST VOICE (FROM THE MOUNTAINS):
For three times three hundred thousand years
we have stood above the earthquake’s couch;
and often, as men shook beneath their fears,
our countless multitude has shivered too.
source_text:
FIRST VOICE (FROM THE MOUNTAINS):
Thrice three hundred thousand years
O’er the Earthquake’s couch we stood:
Oft, as men convulsed with fears,
We trembled in our multitude.

text:
To tell if he was patient, partly content,  
Was dying as he thought, or something else meant;  
Was it a kindly day for him to die,  
And did the sunshine turn his way?
source_text:
To know if he was patient, part content,
Was dying as he thought, or different;
Was it a pleasant day to die,
And did the sunshine face his way?

text:
My lord, I know your gracious ear  
Finds no complaint in vain;  
That hope emboldens me to tell  
My lowly grief again,  
How saucy Phoebus’ scorching beams,  
In summer’s blazing pride,  
Wither and waste my foaming streams,  
And drain my crystal tide.
source_text:
My lord, I know your noble ear
Woe ne’er assails in vain;
Embolden’d thus, I beg you’ll hear
Your humble slave complain,
How saucy Phoebus’ scorching beams,
In flaming summer-pride,
Dry-withering, waste my foamy streams,
And drink my crystal tide.

text:
Old inheritances—these arts, libraries, legends, collections, and  
the craft handed down through the shops—shall we value them so high?  
Shall we value our money and business so high? I have no objection,  
I value them as high as the highest—yet a child born of woman and  
man I value beyond all measure.
source_text:
Old institutions, these arts, libraries, legends, collections, and
the practice handed along in manufactures, will we rate them so high?
Will we rate our cash and business high? I have no objection,
I rate them as high as the highest--then a child born of a woman and
man I rate beyond all rate.

text: In Book I we saw Hyperion, still a god, troubled by omens, and now in Book III we behold the rising into godhead of his heir, the young Apollo. The poem ends abruptly at the very instant of Apollo’s transformation, and how Keats meant to bring it to its close we can never know.
source_text:
In Book I we saw Hyperion, though still a god, distressed by portents,
and now in Book III we see the rise to divinity of his successor, the
young Apollo. The poem breaks off short at the moment of Apollo's
metamorphosis, and how Keats intended to complete it we can never know.

text:
An ancient tower rose up in thought,
And showed how Dettingen’s bold hero fought;
Yet, far from ever sinking into nought,
It still supports
A lord who in the western climes has fought
With his trusty sword.
source_text:
An ancient tower to memory brought
How Dettingen’s bold hero fought;
Still, far from sinking into nought,
It owns a lord
Who far in western climates fought,
With trusty sword.

text:
As I drifted out with life’s great tide,  
As I followed the coast I know so well,  
As I walked where the waves keep washing Paumanok,  
Where they hiss up rough and sibilant,  
Where the fierce old mother never stops calling for her lost ones,  
I, deep in thought on a late autumn afternoon, looking south,  
Held fast by this charged self, from whose pride I cast my poems,  
Was taken by the spirit that runs in the lines beneath my feet,  
The edge, the silt that speaks for all the water and all the land of the earth.
source_text:
As I ebb’d with the ocean of life,
As I wended the shores I know,
As I walk’d where the ripples continually wash you Paumanok,
Where they rustle up hoarse and sibilant,
Where the fierce old mother endlessly cries for her castaways,
I musing late in the autumn day, gazing off southward,
Held by this electric self out of the pride of which I utter poems,
Was seiz’d by the spirit that trails in the lines underfoot,
The rim, the sediment that stands for all the water and all the land
of the globe.

text:
Sharp as the arrows  
Of that silver orb,  
Whose fierce beam thins  
In the clear white dawn  
Until we can scarcely see it—we only feel it there.
source_text:
Keen as are the arrows
Of that silver sphere,
Whose intense lamp narrows
In the white dawn clear
Until we hardly see—we feel that it is there.

text:
Foreboding is the lengthening shadow on the lawn,
A sign that sinking suns have gone;
The message sent to startled grass
That darkness soon will sweep across.
source_text:
Presentiment is that long shadow on the lawn
Indicative that suns go down;
The notice to the startled grass
That darkness is about to pass.

text:
Farewell, my rhyme-making brother!
Too long have we been strangers to each other:
Now let us rest our minds together,
In brotherly devotion;
Let envy thrash about in tether,
That black infernal notion!
source_text:
Fareweel, “my rhyme-composing” brither!
We’ve been owre lang unkenn’d to ither:
Now let us lay our heads thegither,
In love fraternal:
May envy wallop in a tether,
Black fiend, infernal!

text:
“How could it be,” then Phoebus said,
“that you, a tiny child, born but yesterday,
a being nourished on a mother’s milk and kisses,
could skin two monstrous heifers with your hands?
Even I myself may one day have cause to fear
your power, child of Cyllenian May,
when you have grown robust and tall.” He spoke, and bound
the infant’s wrists with rigid willow bands.
source_text:
‘How was it possible,’ then Phoebus said,
‘That you, a little child, born yesterday,
A thing on mother’s milk and kisses fed,
Could two prodigious heifers ever flay?
Even I myself may well hereafter dread
Your prowess, offspring of Cyllenian May,
When you grow strong and tall.’—He spoke, and bound
Stiff withy bands the infant’s wrists around.

text:
Poet Willie, poet Willie, fire a volley at the Doctor,  
with your “Liberty’s Chain” and all your wit;  
you never swung a leg across Pegasus’ flank,  
you only caught the stench where he had sh—t.  
Poet Willie, you only caught the stench where he had sh—t.
source_text:
Poet Willie! poet Willie, gie the Doctor a volley,
Wi’ your “Liberty’s Chain” and your wit;
O’er Pegasus’ side ye ne’er laid a stride,
Ye but smelt, man, the place where he sh—t.
Poet Willie! Ye but smelt man, the place where he sh—t.

text:
I am the hunted slave, I flinch from the dogs’ savage bite,  
Hell and despair press hard on me, again and again the marksmen crack,  
I grip the fence rails, my blood drips thin with the seep of my skin,  
I sink among the weeds and stones,  
The riders drive their reluctant horses near, crowd close,  
Mock my spinning ears and strike me hard across the head with whip handles.
source_text:
I am the hounded slave, I wince at the bite of the dogs,
Hell and despair are upon me, crack and again crack the marksmen,
I clutch the rails of the fence, my gore dribs, thinn’d with the
ooze of my skin,
I fall on the weeds and stones,
The riders spur their unwilling horses, haul close,
Taunt my dizzy ears and beat me violently over the head with whip-stocks.

text:
Space is open and austere,  
And in its center, clouded, dim,  
A shrine of shadow stands,  
While, amid thrones of radiance,  
Darkness gathers in the blue of crystal air.  
Golden worlds turn and gleam.  

In winged flight,  
Out from each point of the Infinite,  
Like a thousand dawns within one night,  
The glories rise and spread;  
And through dread thunder and through darkness  
Light and music are shed,  
And in their canopied chariots borne,  
By living wings aloft above,  
The giant Powers advance,  
Gloomy or bright as the thrones they occupy.
source_text:
Space is roofless and bare,
And in the midst a cloudy shrine,
Dark amid thrones of light.
In the blue glow of hyaline
Golden worlds revolve and shine.
In ... flight
From every point of the Infinite,
Like a thousand dawns on a single night
The splendours rise and spread;
And through thunder and darkness dread
Light and music are radiated,
And in their pavilioned chariots led
By living wings high overhead
The giant Powers move,
Gloomy or bright as the thrones they fill.

text:
What joy can gold put in my hand?
I do not care a single strand;
The boy I love is all I need,
And he’s my own dear Willy.
source_text:
She. What’s a’ the joys that gowd can gie?
I dinna care a single flie;
The lad I love’s the lad for me,
And that’s my ain dear Willy.

text:
This dust was once the man,  
Kind, plain, just, and firm, beneath whose careful hand  
Against the foulest crime ever known in any land or age,  
The Union of these States was preserved.
source_text:
This dust was once the man,
Gentle, plain, just and resolute, under whose cautious hand,
Against the foulest crime in history known in any land or age,
Was saved the Union of these States.

text:
Her eyes were open, yet she still surveyed,  
Fully awake, the shapes her slumber made:  
A sorrowful change had almost driven out  
The dear delights her pure and deep dream knew throughout.  
Then fair Madeline began to weep,  
And, sighing many times, to murmur words asleep;  
Still fixed upon Porphyro was her sight;  
Who knelt there, hands together, with an eye of plight,  
Dreading to stir or speak, she seemed so strangely bright.
source_text:
Her eyes were open, but she still beheld,
Now wide awake, the vision of her sleep:
There was a painful change, that nigh expell'd
The blisses of her dream so pure and deep
At which fair Madeline began to weep,
And moan forth witless words with many a sigh;
While still her gaze on Porphyro would keep;
Who knelt, with joined hands and piteous eye,
Fearing to move or speak, she look'd so dreamingly.

text:
MEPHISTOPHELES [WHO AT ONCE SEEMS TO HAVE AGED GREATLY]:
I find the people ripe for judgment’s hour,
Since last I climbed the wizard mountain’s height;
And as my little barrel has turned cloudy,
So too the world is tapped down to the lees.
source_text:
MEPHISTOPHELES [WHO AT ONCE APPEARS TO HAVE GROWN VERY OLD]:
find the people ripe for the last day,
Since I last came up to the wizard mountain;
And as my little cask runs turbid now,
So is the world drained to the dregs.

text:
From the handwriting, it is never wholly clear which marks are dashes,  
which are commas, and which are full stops, nor is it fully  
clear which first letters begin in capitals.
source_text:
From the handwriting, it is not always clear which are dashes,
which are commas and which are periods, nor it is entirely
clear which initial letters are capitalized.

text:
May sorrow seize the man sae mean,  
Though bright his purse wi’ coin be clean,  
Wha follows ony saucy lass  
That walks sae proud and high.
source_text:
But sorrow tak’ him that’s sae mean,
Altho’ his pouch o’ coin were clean,
Wha follows ony saucy quean,
That looks sae proud and high.
O Tibbie, I hae seen the day, &c.

text:
And I have dreamed that the aim and heart of the life we know,  
the passing,  
is to shape and determine the self of the life unseen, the lasting.
source_text:
And I have dream’d that the purpose and essence of the known life,
the transient,
Is to form and decide identity for the unknown life, the permanent.

text:
Its eyes, though wild, were still all dewy-bright
with love, and kept all ghostly dread at bay
from the poor girl, by the spell of that light,
while it unwove the horrid web of the late
darkened days—the murderous spite of pride
and avarice—the black pine roof
in the forest, and the sodden, turf-clad dell,
where, wordless, from stabs he fell.
source_text:
Its eyes, though wild, were still all dewy bright
With love, and kept all phantom fear aloof
From the poor girl by magic of their light,
The while it did unthread the horrid woof
Of the late darken'd time,--the murderous spite
Of pride and avarice,--the dark pine roof
In the forest,--and the sodden turfed dell,
Where, without any word, from stabs he fell.

text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
So be it then.
I have small misgiving for my wager,
And if I lose, then ’tis your turn to crow;
Then savor your triumph with a full breast.
Ay, dust he shall devour, and gladly so,
Like my old paramour, the renowned Snake.
source_text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
Well and good.
I am not in much doubt about my bet,
And if I lose, then ’tis Your turn to crow;
Enjoy Your triumph then with a full breast.
Ay; dust shall he devour, and that with pleasure,
Like my old paramour, the famous Snake.

text:
Maybe you’re headed there too.
Who can say?
If you arrive before I do,
Keep a small place for me
Near the two I’ve lost!
source_text:
Perhaps you 're going too!
Who knows?
If you should get there first,
Save just a little place for me
Close to the two I lost!

text:
Each care and fear, when you are near,
I always brave them, O!
Young kings upon their throne at first
Are not so blessed as I am, O!
And I will kiss you yet, yet, &c.
source_text:
Ilk care and fear, when thou art near
I evermair defy them, O!
Young kings upon their hansel throne
Are no sae blest as I am, O!
And I’ll kiss thee yet, yet, &c.

text:
The man’s body is holy, and the woman’s body is holy,  
No matter who it is, it is holy—is it the lowest one in the  
crew of laborers?  
Is it one of the blank-faced immigrants newly come to the wharf?  
Each belongs here, or anywhere, as fully as the rich, as fully as  
you,  
Each has his or her place in the long procession.
source_text:
The man’s body is sacred and the woman’s body is sacred,
No matter who it is, it is sacred--is it the meanest one in the
laborers’ gang?
Is it one of the dull-faced immigrants just landed on the wharf?
Each belongs here or anywhere just as much as the well-off, just as
much as you,
Each has his or her place in the procession.

text: What deserves special notice is the deft use Keats makes here of contrast—between the merciless cold outside and the warmth of love within; between the trembling age of the Bedesman and Angela, and the ardent youth of Porphyro and Madeline; between the clamor and revelry and the stillness of Madeline’s chamber; and, as Mr. Colvin has observed, in the moonlight that, chill and sepulchral when it falls elsewhere, becomes for Madeline a halo of radiance, an angelic light.
source_text:
To be specially noted is the skilful use which Keats here makes of
contrast--between the cruel cold without and the warm love within; the
palsied age of the Bedesman and Angela, and the eager youth of Porphyro
and Madeline; the noise and revel and the hush of Madeline's bedroom,
and, as Mr. Colvin has pointed out, in the moonlight which, chill and
sepulchral when it strikes elsewhere, to Madeline is as a halo of glory,
an angelic light.

text:
Child of Light! your limbs are blazing, etc. (2 5 54.)
The first printed edition (1820) gives lips instead of limbs, but the word membre in Shelley’s Italian prose rendering of these lines confirms limbs, the reading of B. (Locock).
source_text:
Child of Light! thy limbs are burning, etc. (2 5 54.)
The editio princeps (1820) has lips for limbs, but the word membre in
Shelley’s Italian prose version of these lines establishes limbs, the
reading of B. (Locock).

text:
Think on it, lover! you and I  
Allowed to stand face to face;  
After a life, a death, we'll say—  
For that was death, and this is thee.
source_text:
Think of it, lover! I and thee
Permitted face to face to be;
After a life, a death we'll say, --
For death was that, and this is thee.

text:
For I, too, have forgotten,
wrapped in these small powers of progress, politics, culture,
wealth, invention, civilization,
I have lost my sense of your quiet, ever-swaying force, you
mighty, elemental surges,
in which, and upon which, we drift, and each of us is borne aloft.
source_text:
For I too have forgotten,
(Wrapt in these little potencies of progress, politics, culture,
wealth, inventions, civilization,)
Have lost my recognition of your silent ever-swaying power, ye
mighty, elemental throes,
In which and upon which we float, and every one of us is buoy’d.

text:
He saw his mystic friend’s whole being quiver,
Even in those innermost, most shadowed deeps—
And with a calm, deliberate voice he spake,
source_text:
Beheld his mystic friend’s whole being shake,
Even where its inmost depths were gloomiest—
And with a calm and measured voice he spake,

text:
Hail, Poetry, shy nymph apart!  
In chasing you, how many have gone astray  
from common sense, or slowly worn themselves thin  
amid the heaps of idle talk;  
and oh, how often your own joys have starved  
even as you kept on giving all your favors!
source_text:
Hail, Poesie! thou Nymph reserv’d!
In chase o’ thee, what crowds hae swerv’d
Frae common sense, or sunk enerv’d
’Mang heaps o’ clavers:
And och! o’er aft thy joes hae starv’d,
’Mid a’ thy favours!

text:
By that last book of yours, we think,
you’ve damned yourself twice over to contempt;
we warned you while you still stood on the brink,
and from your blackened name will shrink
the child not yet born.
source_text:
‘By that last book of yours WE think
You’ve double damned yourself to scorn;
We warned you whilst yet on the brink
You stood. From your black name will shrink
The babe that is unborn.’

text:
Stroke the cool brow, so oft made burning,
Lift, if you can, the languid hair;
Feel the hard and stubborn fingers—
No thimble more shall ever wear.
source_text:
Stroke the cool forehead, hot so often,
Lift, if you can, the listless hair;
Handle the adamantine fingers
Never a thimble more shall wear.

text:
For my friends and for my brethren’s sake,  
And for my dear-loved Land o’ Cakes,  
I lift this prayer in flame:  
Lord, send a rough-hoofed troop from Hell  
Across all those who’d Scotland buy or sell,  
To trample them in the mire!
source_text:
Now, for my friends’ and brethren’s sakes,
And for my dear-lov’d Land o’ Cakes,
I pray with holy fire:
Lord, send a rough-shod troop o’ Hell
O’er a’ wad Scotland buy or sell,
To grind them in the mire!

They glide, like phantoms, into the wide hall;
Like phantoms, to the iron porch, they glide;
Where lay the Porter, in uneasy sprawl,
With a huge empty flaggon by his side:
The wakeful bloodhound rose, and shook his hide,
But his sagacious eye an inmate owns:
By one, and one, the bolts full easy slide:--
The chains lie silent on the footworn stones;--
The key turns, and the door upon its hinges groans.

text:
The leaves of spent autumnal woods shall wander round thy head;  
The blossoms of the dewy spring shall glisten at thy feet:  
Yet thou or this old world must sink beneath the frost that binds the dead,  
Ere midnight wears its frown away, ere morning smiles, ere thou and peace may meet.
source_text:
The leaves of wasted autumn woods shall float around thine head:
The blooms of dewy spring shall gleam beneath thy feet:
But thy soul or this world must fade in the frost that binds the dead,
Ere midnight’s frown and morning’s smile, ere thou and peace may meet.

text: Carton brought the conversation to a close at this point by rising to assist him with his outer coat. “But you,” said Mr. Lorry, returning to the subject, “you are young.”
source_text: Carton terminated the conversation here, by rising to help him on with his outer coat; “But you,” said Mr. Lorry, reverting to the theme, “you are young.”

text: There was no reply. I had made my first point! For the first time, the smith was not up and ready. The company noticed it. A good effect.
source_text: There was no answer. I had scored my first point! For the first time, the smith wasn't up and ready. The company noticed it. Good effect.

text: “There is one curious thing,” said one of the sons, a small old man very like his father; “that is, none of his children have ever wandered away; we have all stayed”—he might almost have said stayed seated—“all our lives, close about him.”
source_text: “There's one interesting thing,” said one of the sons, a little old man much like his father; “that is, that none of his children have ever gone meandering off; we've all remained”--he might almost have said remained seated--“all our lives, right about him.”

text: “Is it not impressive, Mr. Darnay?” Lucie asked. “There have been evenings when I have sat here till I almost imagined—but even the shadow of such a foolish thought gives me a shiver to-night, when everything is so dark and solemn—”
source_text: “Is it not impressive, Mr. Darnay?” asked Lucie. “Sometimes, I have sat here of an evening, until I have fancied--but even the shade of a foolish fancy makes me shudder to-night, when all is so black and solemn--”

text: “And would you believe it,” she went on, turning back to Mrs. Britling, “that man Hickson stood there behind his counter—where I have dealt with him for years—and positively refused to let me have more than a dozen tins of sardines. Refused! Outright!”
source_text: "And would you believe me," she went on, going back to Mrs. Britling, "that man Hickson stood behind his counter--where I've dealt with him for _years_, and refused absolutely to let me have more than a dozen tins of sardines. _Refused!_ Point blank!

text: He waited and waited, and the clock struck twelve; yet Doctor Manette did not return. Mr. Lorry came back, and found no news of him, and brought none. Where could he be?
source_text: He waited and waited, and the clock struck twelve; but Doctor Manette did not come back. Mr. Lorry returned, and found no tidings of him, and brought none. Where could he be?

text: “No. And she had been getting on so nicely too. She’s scarcely any better now than she was at Christmas again. She keeps on asking whether you have come, and whether there’s any news, till one is quite wearied out with answering her. Shall I go and tell her you’ve come?”
source_text: “No. And getting on so nicely as she was too. She’s but very little better now again than she was at Christmas. She keeps on asking if you be come, and if there’s news, till one’s wearied out wi’ answering her. Shall I go and say you’ve come?”

text: “I have delivered that letter,” Charles Darnay said to Mr. Lorry. “I could not have agreed to your being entrusted with any written reply, but perhaps you will accept one given by word of mouth?”
source_text: “I have delivered that letter,” said Charles Darnay to Mr. Lorry. “I would not consent to your being charged with any written answer, but perhaps you will take a verbal one?”

text: “Ah! so you’ve changed your mind, have you?” growled Sikes, noting the tear that quivered in her eye. “So much the better for you, then; so much the better.”
source_text: “Oh! you’ve thought better of it, have you?” growled Sikes, marking the tear which trembled in her eye. “All the better for you, you have.”

text: “What I have arranged is that you are to ride into town late to-night; leave here about nine, and stay there awhile, so as to get back about twelve. If nothing has come by eleven to-night, they say there is no chance whatever.”
source_text: “What I’ve arranged is, that you shall ride to town the last thing to-night; leave here about nine, and wait a while there, getting home about twelve. If nothing has been received by eleven to-night, they say there’s no chance at all.”

text: The door opened, and Jim came in and shut it behind him. He looked gaunt and very grave. Poor boy, he was only twenty-two—and already weighted with a family! He needed a new overcoat, and he had no gloves.
source_text: The door opened and Jim stepped in and closed it. He looked thin and very serious. Poor fellow, he was only twenty-two—and to be burdened with a family! He needed a new overcoat and he was without gloves.

text: “Ah!” said Boldwood, with a voice now somewhat more buoyant. “Oh, Troy, if she is the one you prefer most, why did you come between us and wound my happiness?”
source_text: “Ah!” said Boldwood, in a more elastic voice. “Oh, Troy, if you like her best, why then did you step in here and injure my happiness?”

text: He had scored a goal. He had done it neatly. The perambulator, it seemed, did not matter. Though the blow had apparently roused the baby. At the edge of his consciousness there hovered the figure of Mr. Britling saying: "Aunty. You really mustn't wheel the perambulator—_just_ there."
source_text: He'd shot a goal. He'd done it well. The perambulator it seemed didn't matter. Though apparently the impact had awakened the baby. In the margin of his consciousness was the figure of Mr. Britling remarking: "Aunty. You really mustn't wheel the perambulator--_just_ there."

text: “I didn’t take it,” stammered Noah, no longer with his legs stretched out like an independent gentleman, but drawing them up as well as he could beneath his chair; “it was all her doing; you’ve got it now, Charlotte, you know you have.”
source_text: “I didn’t take it,” stammered Noah, no longer stretching out his legs like an independent gentleman, but coiling them up as well as he could under his chair; “it was all her doing; yer’ve got it now, Charlotte, yer know yer have.”

text: “Gad, that’s true!” said the doctor. “Where is he? Show me the way. I’ll look in again as I come down, Mrs. Maylie. That’s the little window he got in by, eh? Well, I could never have believed it!”
source_text: “Gad, that’s true!” said the doctor. “Where is he? Show me the way. I’ll look in again, as I come down, Mrs. Maylie. That’s the little window that he got in at, eh? Well, I couldn’t have believed it!”

text: "Wa-a-a-l," he said with a tremor of laughter, "now who'd have thought it?" and gave himself a deliberately American smile before going down.
source_text: "Wa-a-a-l," he said with a quaver of laughter, "now who'd have thought it?" and smiled a consciously American smile at himself before going down.

text: “We can say the few words we have to say in here, my dear,” said the Jew, flinging open a door on the first floor; “and as there are chinks in the shutters, and we never let our neighbours see lights, we’ll place the candle on the stairs. There!”
source_text: “We can say the few words we’ve got to say in here, my dear,” said the Jew, throwing open a door on the first floor; “and as there are holes in the shutters, and we never show lights to our neighbours, we’ll set the candle on the stairs. There!”

text: “Well—if we manage to arrange it so. I wonder whether Thomasin has been at Blooms-End of late. I hope she has. But probably not, since she is, I believe, expecting to be confined in a month or so. I wish I had thought of that sooner. Poor Mother must indeed be very lonely.”
source_text: “Well—if we make it so. I wonder if Thomasin has been to Blooms-End lately. I hope so. But probably not, as she is, I believe, expecting to be confined in a month or so. I wish I had thought of that before. Poor Mother must indeed be very lonely.”

text: “Well, he knew it early this morning, for I learned it at ten o’clock, when Charley returned. Indeed, he is what I should call a lucky man. What a fool you were, Eustacia!”
source_text: “Well, it was known to him this morning early, for I knew it at ten o’clock, when Charley came back. Now, he is what I call a lucky man. What a fool you were, Eustacia!”

text: “And you must!” she said, with gratitude. “A thousand thanks for your devotion, Gabriel! Good night—I know you are doing all you can for me.”
source_text: “And you better!” she said, gratefully. “Thank you for your devotion, a thousand times, Gabriel! Goodnight—I know you are doing your very best for me.”

text: “I don’t think I do, if I may say what is in my mind. I am not fit to be matched against you to-night. Is the tea-board still there, Lucie? I cannot see.”
source_text: “I don’t think I do like, if I may speak my mind. I am not fit to be pitted against you to-night. Is the teaboard still there, Lucie? I can’t see.”

text: “Take it easy, Sandy. All I need to know is how _much_ of an ogre is invisible; then I’ll know where to find his vitals. Don’t be frightened—I’ll make short work of these bunco-steerers. Stay right where you are.”
source_text: “You be easy, Sandy. All I need to know is, how _much_ of an ogre is invisible; then I know how to locate his vitals. Don't you be afraid, I will make short work of these bunco-steerers. Stay where you are.”

text: “All right,” she replied. “I think hard. I like the house, this house—mine alone, no pay the rent, seven dollars a month.”
source_text: “Alla right,” she answered. “I thinka da hard. I lika da house, dis house—all mine, no paya da rent, seven dollar da month.”

text: Great God! what a scene has but now unfolded before me! I am still bewildered by the memory of it. I scarcely know whether I shall have strength to recount it; yet the narrative I have set down would be incomplete without this last and terrible catastrophe.
source_text: Great God! what a scene has just taken place! I am yet dizzy with the remembrance of it. I hardly know whether I shall have the power to detail it; yet the tale which I have recorded would be incomplete without this final and wonderful catastrophe.

text: “And what have I heard! What have I heard!” cried Rose. “That the consciousness of his deep disgrace so wrought upon my own father that he avoided all—there, we have said enough, Harry, we have said enough.”
source_text: “And what have I heard! What have I heard!” cried Rose. “That a sense of his deep disgrace so worked upon my own father that he shunned all—there, we have said enough, Harry, we have said enough.”

text: “But I thought—Gabriel, I have been troubled by the fear that I have offended you, and that you are leaving because of it. It has grieved me deeply, and I could not keep myself from coming.”
source_text: “But I thought—Gabriel, I have been uneasy in the belief that I have offended you, and that you are going away on that account. It grieved me very much and I couldn’t help coming.”

text: “If he was coming here, he would have brought the dog,” said Kags, stooping to inspect the animal, which lay panting upon the floor. “Here! Give him some water; he has run himself faint.”
source_text: “If he was coming here, he’d have come with the dog,” said Kags, stooping down to examine the animal, who lay panting on the floor. “Here! Give us some water for him; he has run himself faint.”

text: “And there are two religions going on in the nation now—High Church and High Chapel. And, thinks I, I’ll keep things fair; so I went to High Church in the morning, and High Chapel in the afternoon.”
source_text: “And there’s two religions going on in the nation now—High Church and High Chapel. And, thinks I, I’ll play fair; so I went to High Church in the morning, and High Chapel in the afternoon.”

text: “Did you notice Mr. Boldwood’s doings in church this morning, miss?” Liddy went on, intimating by the remark the direction her thoughts had taken.
source_text: “Did you notice Mr. Boldwood’s doings in church this morning, miss?” Liddy continued, adumbrating by the remark the track her thoughts had taken.

text: “Yes, Miss Eustacia—I will, if I may kiss it as well. A quarter of an hour. And I’ll swear I’ll do my very utmost to let you take my place without anybody’s finding it out. Don’t you think someone might know your tongue, miss?”
source_text: “Yes, Miss Eustacia—I will, if I may kiss it too. A quarter of an hour. And I’ll swear to do the best I can to let you take my place without anybody knowing. Don’t you think somebody might know your tongue, miss?”

text: “It is no weather fit for wasting idle courtesies upon dead folk. They are beyond giving you thanks. Come—it is unprofitable to linger here.”
source_text: “It is not weather meet for doing useless courtesies unto dead folk. They are past thanking you. Come--it is unprofitable to tarry here.”

text: “Well, then, you can unthink it. They bore every strain without difficulty—until the Interdict. Since then, they have only kept up a brave show; in their hearts they are trembling. Set your mind on it—when the armies come, the mask will drop.”
source_text: “Well, then, you may unthink it. They stood every strain easily --until the Interdict. Since then, they merely put on a bold outside--at heart they are quaking. Make up your mind to it --when the armies come, the mask will fall.”

text: “That’s right,” he said with gratitude, “where I am. But where am I at—I mean, where am I? Oh, yes, in the chart-room. Well, some people—”
source_text: “That’s right,” he said gratefully, “where I am. But where am I at—I mean, where am I? Oh, yes, in the chart-room. Well, some people—”

text: “I hope you don’t mean to say, sir,” said Mr. Giles, trembling all the while, “that he is going to die. If I believed that, I should never know happiness again. I would not cut a boy off—no, not even Brittles here—not for all the plate in the county, sir.”
source_text: “I hope you don’t mean to say, sir,” said Mr. Giles, trembling, “that he’s going to die. If I thought it, I should never be happy again. I wouldn’t cut a boy off: no, not even Brittles here; not for all the plate in the county, sir.”

text: “I tell you what it is,” said Sikes; “if you haven’t caught the fever, and got it coming on now, there’s something more than common in the wind, and something dangerous too. You’re not a-going to— No, damme! you wouldn’t do that!”
source_text: “I tell you wot it is,” said Sikes; “if you haven’t caught the fever, and got it comin’ on, now, there’s something more than usual in the wind, and something dangerous too. You’re not a-going to—. No, damme! you wouldn’t do that!”

text: “She’s a honour to her sex,” said Mr. Sikes, as he filled his glass and brought his huge fist down upon the table. “Here’s her health, and may they all be like her!”
source_text: “She’s a honour to her sex,” said Mr. Sikes, filling his glass, and smiting the table with his enormous fist. “Here’s her health, and wishing they was all like her!”

text: “Go on!” cried the Dodger. “What, where’s your spirit? Don’t you feel any pride in yourself? Would you go and make yourself dependent on your friends?”
source_text: “Go!” exclaimed the Dodger. “Why, where’s your spirit? Don’t you take any pride out of yourself? Would you go and be dependent on your friends?”

text: “I tossed them aside—it was only a passing annoyance. What a fool I am! Here—come and help me look for them—we must find them again.”
source_text: “I threw them away—it was a momentary irritation. What a fool I am! Here—come and help me to look for them—we must find them again.”

text: “Have no fear! I shall soon be beyond the reach of harming you, and the others will soon be far from here, God willing! Now, fetch help and take me to the coach.”
source_text: “Have no fear! I shall soon be out of the way of harming you, and the rest will soon be far from here, please God! Now, get assistance and take me to the coach.”

text: “I hadn’t a soul to look after the shop,” replied the man. “Every one who might have given me help had gone off in the pursuit. I couldn’t get hold of anybody until five minutes ago, and I’ve been running here all the way.”
source_text: “I hadn’t a soul to mind the shop,” replied the man. “Everybody who could have helped me, had joined in the pursuit. I could get nobody till five minutes ago; and I’ve run here all the way.”

text: “Bad fortune!” cries The Vengeance, stamping her foot upon the chair, “and there are the tumbrils! And Evrémonde will be dispatched in a wink, and she not here! See her knitting in my hand, and her empty chair waiting for her. I cry out with vexation and disappointment!”
source_text: “Bad Fortune!” cries The Vengeance, stamping her foot in the chair, “and here are the tumbrils! And Evrémonde will be despatched in a wink, and she not here! See her knitting in my hand, and her empty chair ready for her. I cry with vexation and disappointment!”

text: “What the devil induced you to keep lingering there in the rain?” said Monks, turning round and addressing Bumble, after he had bolted the door behind them.
source_text: “What the devil made you stand lingering there, in the wet?” said Monks, turning round, and addressing Bumble, after he had bolted the door behind them.

text: “I have had the happiness, Doctor Manette, of being so much at home here,” he at last began, “for some year and a half, that I trust the subject upon which I am about to venture may not--”
source_text: “I have had the happiness, Doctor Manette, of being so intimate here,” so he at length began, “for some year and a half, that I hope the topic on which I am about to touch may not--”

text: “Perhaps you dislike the rudeness of my rebuking you, for I know it is rude; but I thought it might do some good.”
source_text: “Perhaps you don’t like the rudeness of my reprimanding you, for I know it is rudeness; but I thought it would do good.”

text: “And that tale of the black man who murdered his wife Desdemona? It is a pleasingly dismal one, and would suit you most excellently just now.”
source_text: “And that story of the black man, who murdered his wife Desdemona? It is a nice dismal one that would suit you excellent just now.”

text: “Colours for the counties,” said Wilkins, “and if there’s no coloured cloth to be had, there’s always red flannel. Anything is preferable to leaving the crowd of people to go roaming about....”
source_text: "Colours for the counties," said Wilkins, "and if there isn't coloured cloth to be got there's--red flannel. Anything is better than leaving the mass of people to mob about...."

text: She hid her face in her hands. “Oh, it is a mistake!” she groaned. “And some day he will regret it, and remember me!”
source_text: She covered her face with her hands. “O, it is a mistake!” she groaned. “And he will rue it some day, and think of me!”

text: --and set the green fire blazing! After waiting only forty seconds this time, I flung my arms wide and hurled forth the crushing syllables of that word among words:
source_text: --and lit up the green fire! After waiting only forty seconds this time, I spread my arms abroad and thundered out the devastating syllables of this word of words:

text: “‘Heaven forfend! Even if you were indeed guilty, for that can only reduce you to despair, and not impel you toward virtue. I too am wretched; I and my family have been condemned, though innocent; judge, then, whether I can be insensible to your afflictions.’”
source_text: “‘Heaven forbid! Even if you were really criminal, for that can only drive you to desperation, and not instigate you to virtue. I also am unfortunate; I and my family have been condemned, although innocent; judge, therefore, if I do not feel for your misfortunes.’

text: “Stranger things than that will happen when it does arrive,” replied madame. “I have them both here, beyond question; and they are both here for their own deserts; that is enough.”
source_text: “Stranger things than that will happen when it does come,” answered madame. “I have them both here, of a certainty; and they are both here for their merits; that is enough.”

text: “Stay quietly here until such a document is prepared, and then come with me to whatever place I may judge most suitable for the purpose of signing it?”
source_text: “Remain quietly here, until such a document is drawn up, and proceed with me to such a place as I may deem most advisable, for the purpose of attesting it?”

text: There was something in the fact that he had been taken prisoner. “That other officer”—Mr. Direck’s officer, that was—“had been lying there for days.” Teddy had been shot through the upper arm and stunned by a falling beam. When he came to himself, he was disarmed, with a German standing over him....
source_text: There was something about his being taken prisoner. "That other officer"--that was Mr. Direck's officer--"had been lying there for days." Teddy had been shot through the upper arm, and stunned by a falling beam. When he came to he was disarmed, with a German standing over him....

text: Venn made as though he would withdraw to the kitchen, when Thomasin said, with a pleasant sharpness, and continuing at her sewing, “Of course you must sit down here. And where is your fifty-cow dairy situated, Mr. Venn?”
source_text: Venn moved as if he would retire to the kitchen, when Thomasin said with pleasant pertness as she went on with some sewing, “Of course you must sit down here. And where does your fifty-cow dairy lie, Mr. Venn?”

text: “Indeed! You are a fine fellow to be objecting and advising!” cried Mr. Lorry. “You wish you were going yourself? And you, a Frenchman by birth? Truly, you are a sagacious counsellor.”
source_text: “Indeed! You are a pretty fellow to object and advise!” exclaimed Mr. Lorry. “You wish you were going yourself? And you a Frenchman born? You are a wise counsellor.”

text: “The unfortunate circumstances—if you had happened upon us on any other day,” Mr. Ford began with smooth politeness, only to be cut off by Mr. Ends, whose peevish eyes bore out the sharpness of his temper.
source_text: “The unfortunate circumstances—if you had chanced here any other day,” Mr. Ford began suavely, only to be interrupted by Mr. Ends, whose cranky eyes justified themselves in his shortness of temper.

text: “She promises that you shall—quite natural,” said the scheming lover, every nerve in him stirring at the presumption Liddy’s words seemed to authorize—that his darling had even considered marrying again.
source_text: “She promises that you shall—quite natural,” said the strategic lover, throbbing throughout him at the presumption which Liddy’s words appeared to warrant—that his darling had thought of re-marriage.

text: “You remember that five-dollar bill you gave me when I didn’t have the fare for the car? Well, there it is, with ninety-nine brothers of different ages, though all of the same size.”
source_text: “You remember that fiver you gave me the time I didn’t have carfare? Well, there it is, with ninety-nine brothers of different ages but all of the same size.”

text: “You could not discover the place to which these people had gone,” said Monks, “but where friendship breaks down, hatred will often make a passage. My mother found it, after a year of crafty searching—ay, and found the child.”
source_text: “You couldn’t find the spot to which these people had repaired,” said Monks, “but where friendship fails, hatred will often force a way. My mother found it, after a year of cunning search—ay, and found the child.”

text: “You are a sensible little woman,” said Mrs. Yeobright, smiling. “I wish you and he—no, I don’t wish for anything. There, it is nine o’clock,” she broke off, as a whizzing and a dinging sounded below.
source_text: “You are a practical little woman,” said Mrs. Yeobright, smiling. “I wish you and he—no, I don’t wish anything. There, it is nine o’clock,” she interrupted, hearing a whizz and a dinging downstairs.

text: “Why have you been staying here all these days without telling me? You have left your home. I am afraid that I may be to blame for this?”
source_text: “Why have you been living here all these days without telling me? You have left your home. I fear I am something to blame in this?”

text: “Ah, perchance, my head being so distraught by the manifold matters unto which pertain the confusions of these but lately adventured haps and fortunes, whereby not I alone nor you alone, but every one of us, it seemeth to me--”
source_text: “Ah, peradventure my head being distraught by the manifold matters whereunto the confusions of these but late adventured haps and fortunings whereby not I alone nor you alone, but every each of us, meseemeth--”

text: Probably some friend of Lady Frensham’s, he supposed. Yet—the notion of Zeppelins over London did strike Mr. Britling as distinctly absurd. He would not have cared to be caught saying so himself.... There had never been Zeppelins over London. They were merely gas-bags....
source_text: Probably a friend of Lady Frensham's, he thought. Still--the idea of Zeppelins over London did seem rather ridiculous to Mr. Britling. He would not have liked to have been caught talking of it himself.... There never had been Zeppelins over London. They were gas bags....

text: “True truth, Mr. Darnay, believe me! I have wandered from my point; I was speaking of our being friends. Now you know me; you know I am incapable of all those loftier and nobler flights of men. If you doubt it, ask Stryver, and he will tell you as much.”
source_text: “Genuine truth, Mr. Darnay, trust me! I have gone aside from my purpose; I was speaking about our being friends. Now, you know me; you know I am incapable of all the higher and better flights of men. If you doubt it, ask Stryver, and he’ll tell you so.”

text: Spring moved forward with rapid step; the weather grew fair, and the heavens were without a cloud. It astonished me that what had once been a desert, wild and melancholy, should now be adorned with the fairest flowers and freshest verdure. My senses were pleased and revived by a thousand delightful fragrances and a thousand lovely sights.
source_text: “Spring advanced rapidly; the weather became fine and the skies cloudless. It surprised me that what before was desert and gloomy should now bloom with the most beautiful flowers and verdure. My senses were gratified and refreshed by a thousand scents of delight and a thousand sights of beauty.

text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin said, shaking his hand and motioning him toward the lone chair, while he himself settled on the bed. “But how did you know where I lived?”
source_text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin answered, shaking hands and waving him to the solitary chair, himself taking to the bed. “But how did you know where I lived?”

text: Venn was greatly astonished by this confession, though he did not make it very plain; such displays may attend remarks that are but a step beyond what is expected, yet they are commonly reserved in more intricate instances of two steps and more. “Indeed, miss,” he replied.
source_text: Venn felt much astonishment at this avowal, though he did not show it clearly; that exhibition may greet remarks which are one remove from expectation, but it is usually withheld in complicated cases of two removes and upwards. “Indeed, miss,” he replied.

text: “I’ll tell you what,” whispered the gentleman to Mr. Brownlow; “he will not come to you to-morrow morning. I saw him falter. He is deceiving you, my good friend.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you what,” whispered that gentleman to Mr. Brownlow; “he won’t come up to you tomorrow morning. I saw him hesitate. He is deceiving you, my good friend.”

text: “And she made up her mind that the wedding must be over before you got back,” said Mrs. Yeobright, “so that there should be no chance of her meeting you, and going through a very painful time. That is why she has gone to him; they have settled to be married this morning.”
source_text: “And she determined that the wedding should be over before you came back,” said Mrs. Yeobright, “that there might be no chance of her meeting you, and having a very painful time of it. That’s why she has gone to him; they have arranged to be married this morning.”

text: “My wife appears to believe I’m a burglar,” he said, with reckless composure. “I never heard anyone call her mad; so it must be I. And if I am mad, then they can’t do anything to me for killing you two fools in my madness.”
source_text: “My wife seems to think I am a burglar,” he said, recklessly. “I never heard of her being crazy; therefore I must be. And if I’m crazy, they can’t do anything to me for killing you two fools in my madness.”

text: “She, above all,” said Ernest, “needs consolation; she blamed herself for having brought about my brother’s death, and that left her in great misery. But now that the murderer has been found—”
source_text: “She most of all,” said Ernest, “requires consolation; she accused herself of having caused the death of my brother, and that made her very wretched. But since the murderer has been discovered—”

text: “What do you think now?” said the Jew, once they had made their way back into the passage. “Apart from ourselves, there isn’t a soul in the house except Toby and the boys; and they’re safe enough. Look here!”
source_text: “What do you think now?” said the Jew, when they had regained the passage. “Besides ourselves, there’s not a creature in the house except Toby and the boys; and they’re safe enough. See here!”

text: “I have considered it my duty to come today. A new proposal has been made to me, and it has somewhat astonished me. It will have a great bearing on Thomasin; and I have determined that it should at least be spoken of to you.”
source_text: “I have thought it my duty to call today. A new proposal has been made to me, which has rather astonished me. It will affect Thomasin greatly; and I have decided that it should at least be mentioned to you.”

text: “What is this?” cried Mr. Lorry, out of breath and bewildered. “What is the matter? Lucie! Manette! What has happened? What has brought you here? What is it?”
source_text: “What is this?” cried Mr. Lorry, breathless and confused. “What is the matter? Lucie! Manette! What has happened? What has brought you here? What is it?”

text: “You have seen both dolls and birds to-day,” said Madame Defarge, with a motion of her hand toward the spot where they had last been visible; “now, go home!”
source_text: “You have seen both dolls and birds to-day,” said Madame Defarge, with a wave of her hand towards the place where they had last been apparent; “now, go home!”

text: “And so my entire plan is upset! It is very inconvenient that you refuse to assist my family even to the slight degree of saying plainly that you will have nothing to do with us.”
source_text: “And so my whole plan is interfered with! It is very inconvenient that you refuse to help my family even to the small extent of saying distinctly you will have nothing to do with us.”

text: “You do not go on with your story of your matrimonial intentions,” returned Carton, with an indifferent air; “I wish you would stick to that. As for me—will you never understand that I am incorrigible?”
source_text: “You don’t get on with your account of your matrimonial intentions,” answered Carton, with a careless air; “I wish you would keep to that. As to me--will you never understand that I am incorrigible?”

text: Thomasin paused for a moment, and then placed the baby in Venn’s hands. “Don’t squeeze her, Diggory,” she said, “or hurt her little arm; and keep the cloak close over her like this, so that the rain may not drop upon her face.”
source_text: Thomasin hesitated a moment, and then delivered the baby into Venn’s hands. “Don’t squeeze her, Diggory,” she said, “or hurt her little arm; and keep the cloak close over her like this, so that the rain may not drop in her face.”

text: “But she has a will—not to mention a temper—and I shall be nothing better than a slave to her. I could do anything at all with poor Fanny Robin.”
source_text: “But she has a will—not to say a temper, and I shall be a mere slave to her. I could do anything with poor Fanny Robin.”

text: “No, miss.... No, I say; though, to be sure, it might be Sergeant Troy come home on furlough, though I have not seen him. He was here once in that fashion when the regiment was at Casterbridge.”
source_text: “No, miss.... No, I say; but really it might be Sergeant Troy home on furlough, though I have not seen him. He was here once in that way when the regiment was at Casterbridge.”

text: “You handed ’em over to him, you red-headed sneak, slippin’ in behind the push there,” Martin went on, flingin’ the knuckles into the water. “I seen you, an’ I was figurin’ what you was up to. If you ever try a thing like that again, I’ll beat cheh to death. Do you understand?”
source_text: “You passed ’em to him, you red-head sneakin’ in behind the push there,” Martin went on, as he tossed the knuckles into the water. “I seen you, an’ I was wonderin’ what you was up to. If you try anything like that again, I’ll beat cheh to death. Understand?”

text: “It would bring our adventure to an end before it had scarcely begun; and we, being unarmed, could make no stand against that armed band. If we are to bring our enterprise to success, we must not merely seem peasants, but conduct ourselves as peasants.”
source_text: “It would end our adventures at the very start; and we, being without weapons, could do nothing with that armed gang. If we are going to succeed in our emprise, we must not only look the peasant but act the peasant.”

text: “I thought we might learn something today,” Coggan went on. “I heard wheels go by my door soon after dark—you were away somewhere.” He cast a look at Gabriel. “Good heavens above us, Oak, how white you are! You look as if you were a corpse!”
source_text: “I fancied we should know something to-day,” continued Coggan. “I heard wheels pass my door just after dark—you were out somewhere.” He glanced round upon Gabriel. “Good heavens above us, Oak, how white your face is; you look like a corpse!”

text: “Well—perhaps it is better to keep silence; but I cannot go any farther than that. I cannot flatter, and if my standing here is to be maintained only by soothing him, then my standing must be forfeited.”
source_text: “Well—perhaps it is best to be silent; but I can’t go further than that. I can’t flatter, and if my place here is only to be kept by smoothing him down, my place must be lost.”

text: Elizabeth watched my agitation for some time in a timid and anxious silence; yet there was something in my look that conveyed terror to her, and, trembling, she asked, “What is it that disturbs you, my dear Victor? What is it you fear?”
source_text: Elizabeth observed my agitation for some time in timid and fearful silence, but there was something in my glance which communicated terror to her, and trembling, she asked, “What is it that agitates you, my dear Victor? What is it you fear?”

text: “Meanwhile,” said the Marquis, “I shall preserve the family’s honour and tranquillity, if you will not. But you must be wearied. Shall we conclude our conference for the night?”
source_text: “Meanwhile,” said the Marquis, “I will preserve the honour and repose of the family, if you will not. But you must be fatigued. Shall we terminate our conference for the night?”

text: “If they have—I do not see how they can; but if they have—any allusion to me,” said Rose, “pray let me hear it at another time. I have neither strength nor spirits for it now.”
source_text: “If they have—I do not know how they can, but if they have—any reference to me,” said Rose, “pray let me hear them at some other time. I have not strength or spirits now.”

text: “Oho! wouldn’t it?” roared the champion of the root of evil. “Tell me where your exclusive circles would be if the first Astor hadn’t had the money to pay for his steerage passage over?”
source_text: “Oho! won’t it?” thundered the champion of the root of evil. “You tell me where your exclusive circles would be if the first Astor hadn’t had the money to pay for his steerage passage over?”

text: “Why didn’t you choose a pleasant subject?” she was saying. “We know there are ugly things in the world, but that is no reason—”
source_text: “Why didn’t you select a nice subject?” she was saying. “We know there are nasty things in the world, but that is no reason—”

text: “My poor wife!” he said. “My dear wife, if it were not for you—I think I could kill myself tonight. Don’t cry, my dear. Don’t, don’t cry. You do not know how much comfort you give me. You do not know how much you help me.”
source_text: "My poor wife!" he said, "my dear wife. If it were not for you--I think I could kill myself to-night. Don't cry, my dear. Don't, don't cry. You do not know how you comfort me. You do not know how you help me."

text: “Oh, don’t tell me what you were going to say,” Mrs. Sowerberry broke in. “I am nobody; do not consult me, pray. _I_ have no wish to force myself into your secrets.” As Mrs. Sowerberry spoke, she gave a hysterical laugh, which seemed likely to have violent consequences.
source_text: “Oh, don’t tell me what you were going to say,” interposed Mrs. Sowerberry. “I am nobody; don’t consult me, pray. _I_ don’t want to intrude upon your secrets.” As Mrs. Sowerberry said this, she gave an hysterical laugh, which threatened violent consequences.

text: He settled his cheeks in his hands and stayed that way for a long while. His eyes looked fixedly before him, though at nothing. His thoughts drifted, widened, and grew dim. At last he brought his mind back to that final notion. “Just send the fiddle—without a word.”
source_text: He rested his cheeks between his hands, and remained so for a long time. His eyes stared unseeingly. His thoughts wandered and spread and faded. At length he recalled his mind to that last idea. "Just send the fiddle--without a word."

text: “Yes, but this is different,” Martin protested. “She—I—well, you know how it is: perhaps she won’t even be there. She goes to the university.”
source_text: “Yes, but this is different,” Martin objected. “She—I—well, you see, it’s this way: maybe she won’t be there. She goes to the university.”

text: “Is that all, my dear Henry? How could you imagine that my first thought would not turn at once to those dear, dear friends whom I love, and who are so worthy of my affection?”
source_text: “Is that all, my dear Henry? How could you suppose that my first thought would not fly towards those dear, dear friends whom I love and who are so deserving of my love?”

text: “There is still hope. I have forty years of labor yet in me, and why should you lose heart? I am only at a difficult turning. I wish people would not be so quick to imagine that there can be no progress without sameness.”
source_text: “There is hope yet. There are forty years of work in me yet, and why should you despair? I am only at an awkward turning. I wish people wouldn’t be so ready to think that there is no progress without uniformity.”

text: “I thought it was excellent,” he said hesitatingly, “from what little I read. I had no idea he was such a—such a rascal. I suppose that comes out in his other books.”
source_text: “I thought it was great,” he said hesitatingly, “the little I read. I had no idea he was such a—a scoundrel. I guess that crops out in his other books.”

text: “Be so good as to tell your governor that Blathers and Duff are here, will you?” said the stouter of the two, smoothing his hair down and setting a pair of handcuffs upon the table. “Oh! Good evening, master. Might I have a word or two with you in private, if you please?”
source_text: “Tell your governor that Blathers and Duff is here, will you?” said the stouter man, smoothing down his hair, and laying a pair of handcuffs on the table. “Oh! Good-evening, master. Can I have a word or two with you in private, if you please?”

text: “This way of life you speak of,” she said, “sounds so futile, so without purpose. Have you no work in the world that might engage you more?”
source_text: “This way of living that you speak of,” she said, “sounds so futile and purposeless. Haven’t you any work to do in the world that might interest you more?”

text: The other traveller turned his eyes, with interest, toward the van window, and, without taking them away, said, “I presume I might look in upon her?”
source_text: The other traveller turned his eyes with interest towards the van window, and, without withdrawing them, said, “I presume I might look in upon her?”

text: “You’ll find it all in the grammar,” she went on. “There’s another thing I noticed in the way you speak. You use ‘don’t’ where you ought not to. ‘Don’t’ is a contraction, and it stands for two words. Do you know what they are?”
source_text: “You’ll find it all in the grammar,” she went on. “There’s something else I noticed in your speech. You say ‘don’t’ when you shouldn’t. ‘Don’t’ is a contraction and stands for two words. Do you know them?”

text: “Well,” he said; “of course, I can't sit here and frame a funereal discourse, right off, without any writing-desk; but there's stock enough to make a sermon out of, any time.”
source_text: “Well,” he said; “of course, I can't set here and compose a funereal discourse, off-hand, without no writing-desk; but there's stock enough to make a sermon of, any time.”

text: “Everyone says it is only one among several, and that there will be others—if there are not already—driving out all emigrants, and sentencing to death all who come back. That was what he meant when he said your life did not belong to you.”
source_text: “Everybody says it is but one of several, and that there will be others--if there are not already--banishing all emigrants, and condemning all to death who return. That is what he meant when he said your life was not your own.”

text: Before many more conjectures could be entertained, Yeobright had drawn near; and, seeing the little hair-cutting company, he turned aside to join them. source_text: Before many more surmises could be indulged in Yeobright had come near; and seeing the hair-cutting group he turned aside to join them. Marching up, and looking critically at their faces for a moment, he said, without introduction, “Now, folks, let me guess what you have been talking about.”

text: “It was all done for the—for the best, sir,” replied Giles. “I was certain it was the boy, or I should never have interfered with him. I am not by nature an inhuman man, sir.”
source_text: “It was all done for the—for the best, sir,” answered Giles. “I am sure I thought it was the boy, or I wouldn’t have meddled with him. I am not of an inhuman disposition, sir.”

text: “Oh, no, no,” Oliver replied hastily; “but the eyes look so mournful; and from where I sit, they appear to be fixed on me. It makes my heart throb,” Oliver added in a low tone, “as though it were alive, and wished to speak to me, but could not.”
source_text: “Oh no, no,” returned Oliver quickly; “but the eyes look so sorrowful; and where I sit, they seem fixed upon me. It makes my heart beat,” added Oliver in a low voice, “as if it was alive, and wanted to speak to me, but couldn’t.”

text: “When you were speaking to Lucie,” Mr. Lorry repeated. “Yes. I wonder that you are not ashamed to utter Lucie’s name! And to be wishing yourself on your way to France at this hour of the day!”
source_text: “When you were talking to Lucie,” Mr. Lorry repeated. “Yes. I wonder you are not ashamed to mention the name of Lucie! Wishing you were going to France at this time of day!”

source_text: “Perhaps they will take something to drink first, Mrs. Maylie?” said the doctor: his face brightening, as if some new thought had occurred to him.

text: “Come down any time; I’ll be at home all afternoon,” Ruth answered over the telephone to his faltering question about when he might bring back the borrowed books.
source_text: “Come down any time; I’ll be at home all afternoon,” was Ruth’s reply over the telephone to his stammered request as to when he could return the borrowed books.

text: “And you will find yourself late, as usual,” said Wildeve. Then, dropping his voice, he added, “You had best turn back now. I have claimed her, and won her. Good night, reddleman!” With that, Wildeve went away.
source_text: “And you slow, as you will find,” said Wildeve. “And,” lowering his voice, “you may as well go back again now. I’ve claimed her, and got her. Good night, reddleman!” Thereupon Wildeve walked away.

text: “If those eyes of yours were bed-winches,” Miss Pross replied, “and I were an English four-poster, you should not draw a splinter from me. No, you wicked foreign woman; I am your match.”
source_text: “If those eyes of yours were bed-winches,” returned Miss Pross, “and I was an English four-poster, they shouldn’t loose a splinter of me. No, you wicked foreign woman; I am your match.”

text: “Thirteen hundred—there you are, Kelly,” said Anthony, tearing a check from the pad. “Your thousand, and the three hundred dollars you were short. You don’t despise money, do you, Kelly?”
source_text: “Thirteen hundred—there you are, Kelly,” said Anthony, tearing off a check. “Your thousand, and the $300 you were out. You don’t despise money, do you, Kelly?”

text: “I choose them as men in earnest, of my own name—Jacques is my name—to whom the sight is likely to do good. Enough; you are English; that is another matter. Stay there, if you please, for a little moment.”
source_text: “I choose them as real men, of my name--Jacques is my name--to whom the sight is likely to do good. Enough; you are English; that is another thing. Stay there, if you please, a little moment.”

text: “Come in, come in,” said Mrs. Yeobright; and Clym stepped forward to greet them. “How is it that you are so late? Grandfer Cantle has been here a very long while, and we thought you would have come along with him, since you live so near to one another.”
source_text: “Come in, come in,” said Mrs. Yeobright; and Clym went forward to welcome them. “How is it you are so late? Grandfer Cantle has been here ever so long, and we thought you’d have come with him, as you live so near one another.”

text: Charley went away, and Eustacia felt life awaken in her more and more. Here, at last, was something to do; here was someone to see, and a delightfully adventurous means of seeing him. “Ah,” she said to herself, “it is only that I have lacked an object to live for—that is all the matter with me!”
source_text: Charley departed, and Eustacia felt more and more interest in life. Here was something to do: here was some one to see, and a charmingly adventurous way to see him. “Ah,” she said to herself, “want of an object to live for—that’s all is the matter with me!”

text: “The room was growing dim to his eyes; the world was closing in upon him. I glanced around me and saw that the hay and straw had been trampled over the floor, as though there had been a struggle.
source_text: “The room was darkening to his sight; the world was narrowing around him. I glanced about me, and saw that the hay and straw were trampled over the floor, as if there had been a struggle.

text: “Don’t mock me. But enough of this. I will not be downcast any longer. I am going from home this afternoon, unless you object very strongly. There is to be a village picnic—a gipsying, they call it—at East Egdon, and I shall go.”
source_text: “Don’t taunt me. But enough of this. I will not be depressed any more. I am going from home this afternoon, unless you greatly object. There is to be a village picnic—a gipsying, they call it—at East Egdon, and I shall go.”

text: “I’ll be carried out if I go!” the girl cried out fiercely; “and I can make that a task two of you won’t care to undertake. Isn’t there anyone here,” she asked, glancing around, “who’ll see a plain message delivered for a poor creature like me?”
source_text: “I shall be carried out if I go!” said the girl violently; “and I can make that a job that two of you won’t like to do. Isn’t there anybody here,” she said, looking round, “that will see a simple message carried for a poor wretch like me?”

text: “Ay—so ’a do seem, Billy Smallbury—so ’a do seem.” The words were in themselves greatly unsteady, and by reason of the circumstance they were rendered yet more so, for the jolting of the waggon was not without effect upon the speaker’s larynx. They came from the man who held the reins.
source_text: “Ay—so ’a do seem, Billy Smallbury—so ’a do seem.” This utterance was very shaky by nature, and more so by circumstance, the jolting of the waggon not being without its effect upon the speaker’s larynx. It came from the man who held the reins.

text: “Andrew Randle, here is yours—come back in a day or two and finish thanking me. Temperance Miller—oh, and here is another, Soberness—both women, I suppose?”
source_text: “Andrew Randle, here’s yours—finish thanking me in a day or two. Temperance Miller—oh, here’s another, Soberness—both women I suppose?”

text: “That sounds rather interesting,” said the policeman. “Though it does seem to me there’s been rather a long time between meetings. Haven’t you heard anything from your friend since you left?”
source_text: “It sounds pretty interesting,” said the policeman. “Rather a long time between meets, though, it seems to me. Haven’t you heard from your friend since you left?”
