text: “Keep your ground. If any man stirs—yes, even the king—before I grant him leave, I will strike him with thunder, I will burn him with lightning!”
source_text: “Stay where you are. If any man moves--even the king--before I give him leave, I will blast him with thunder, I will consume him with lightnings!”

text: “And tell her how anxious I have been, and how much I have suffered, and how dearly I long to see her. You will not refuse me this, mother?”
source_text: “And say how anxious I have been, and how much I have suffered, and how I long to see her. You will not refuse to do this, mother?”

text: “Master Yeobright, you must look me over as well. I’ve changed for the better, haven’t I, eh?” said Grandfer Cantle, rising and setting himself at a distance of something more than half a foot from Clym’s eye, so as to invite the closest scrutiny.
source_text: “Master Yeobright, look me over too. I have altered for the better, haven’t I, hey?” said Grandfer Cantle, rising and placing himself something above half a foot from Clym’s eye, to induce the most searching criticism.

text: You should have seen her face then! Gratitude? Lord, what use have you for words to name such a thing? Words are only painted fire; a look is the fire itself. She gave that look, and bore it away to the treasury of heaven, where all things divine have their place.
source_text: You should have seen her face then! Gratitude? Lord, what do you want with words to express that? Words are only painted fire; a look is the fire itself. She gave that look, and carried it away to the treasury of heaven, where all things that are divine belong.

text: “Why, how is this?” muttered the Jew, his face altering; “only two of them? Where is the third? They can’t have fallen into trouble. Hark!”
source_text: “Why, how’s this?” muttered the Jew: changing countenance; “only two of ’em? Where’s the third? They can’t have got into trouble. Hark!”

text: “And to me it is as though it had been years ago—long years, with death between. And now I am going home, Mr. Oak.”
source_text: “And to me as if it were years ago—long years, and I had been dead between. And now I am going home, Mr. Oak.”

text: “Begin here—I have already told you what comes before.” By then he was sunk deep in drowsiness. As I passed out through his door, I heard him murmur faintly in his sleep, “Good den to you, fair sir.”
source_text: “Begin here--I've already told you what goes before.” He was steeped in drowsiness by this time. As I went out at his door I heard him murmur sleepily: “Give you good den, fair sir.”

text: “I fancied they were talking rather too much to be set about their work properly, my dear,” returned Mr. Bumble, with a wandering glance at a pair of old women by the wash-tub, who were exchanging their notes of admiration upon the workhouse-master’s humility.
source_text: “I thought they were talking rather too much to be doing their work properly, my dear,” replied Mr. Bumble: glancing distractedly at a couple of old women at the wash-tub, who were comparing notes of admiration at the workhouse-master’s humility.

text: “Make it up again—if so mortal a quarrel can ever truly be made up. I almost wish you had not told me. Yet do try to be reconciled. There are ways, after all, if you are both willing.”
source_text: “Make it up again—if a quarrel so deadly can ever be made up. I almost wish you had not told me. But do try to be reconciled. There are ways, after all, if you both wish to.”

text: “No, he wanted his heavy coat sent on by express. But my wife did not recover from the shock for some time. Wonderful thing, that telegraph—there’s a man standing a hundred miles off, likely enough, and harpooning an idea clean into your mind.”
source_text: “No, he wanted his thick coat expressed up. But my wife didn't get over the shock for some time. Wonderful thing, that telegraph--here's a man standing a hundred miles off, like enough, and harpooning an idea chock right into your mind.”

text: “Don’t you suppose I may have had something to do with it?” said Soapy, with a touch of sarcasm, though still in a friendly tone, as one welcomes good fortune.
source_text: “Don’t you figure out that I might have had something to do with it?” said Soapy, not without sarcasm, but friendly, as one greets good fortune.

text: “There are too many better—that is the torment of it. It was she, Thomasin, and she alone, who led your husband to do as he did—I would swear it!”

text: “It was very brave of you to help Arthur as you did—and being a stranger,” she said tactfully, sensing his embarrassment though not knowing what caused it.

text: He spoke for a few minutes longer before bidding good night. He did not at once turn homeward; and beneath the tree where he had kept watch, he lifted his eyes to a window and said softly, “That appointment was with you, Ruth. I held it for you.”
source_text: He talked on a few minutes before saying good night. He did not go home immediately; and under the tree where he kept his vigils he looked up at a window and murmured: “That date was with you, Ruth. I kept it for you.”

text: “It was condescension, after all,” said Eustacia with warmth. “And had I then known what I know now—that a month after my marriage I should be living on this wild heath—I—I should have thought twice before consenting.”
source_text: “It was condescension, nevertheless,” said Eustacia vehemently. “And if I had known then what I know now, that I should be living in this wild heath a month after my marriage, I—I should have thought twice before agreeing.”

text: “I am greatly obliged to you for your interest in my niece; yet I fear there would be objections. More than that, she is devoted to this man.”
source_text: “I am much obliged to you for your interest in my niece; but I fear there would be objections. More than that, she is devoted to this man.”

text: “Well, isn’t that so? There’s all this Ulster gun-running—you heard the way she spoke of it? Isn’t it enough to drive the south into open revolt?…”

text: “Eustacia has told me. He did show her a little attention a year ago; but there was no harm in that. I like her all the better.”

text: “You can scarcely imagine how heavily such a fear bears upon the mind of the afflicted, and how hard—how nearly impossible—it is for him to compel himself to speak a single word on the subject that lies so oppressively upon him.”
source_text: “You have no idea how such an apprehension weighs on the sufferer’s mind, and how difficult--how almost impossible--it is, for him to force himself to utter a word upon the topic that oppresses him.”

text: “Oh, yes—not the slightest difference, Mr. Boldwood—not the least,” said Gabriel at once. He had not a correspondent in the world, nor was there any letter likely to reach him whose contents the whole parish would not have been glad to read.
source_text: “Oh yes—not a bit of difference, Mr. Boldwood—not a bit,” said Gabriel, readily. He had not a correspondent on earth, nor was there a possible letter coming to him whose contents the whole parish would not have been welcome to peruse.

text: “If that is your present mood, my friend, you may be pleased to find a letter that has been lying here these past few days for you; I believe it is from your cousin.”

text: “Then go at once, in Heaven’s name!” she said, her eyes flashing at his, though never quite meeting them. “Do not let me see your face any more.”

text: “Sir _Kay_, forsooth! Ah, call me endearing names, my dearest, call me a sailor! In twice a thousand years shall the unholy contrivance of man strive in vain to breed the like of this noble falsehood!”

text: “He was making signs to her when I saw her,” contended Madame Defarge; “I cannot mention the one without the other; and I must not hold my peace, and leave the matter wholly to him, this little citizen here. For I am no poor witness.”

text: “No,” replied Mr. Limbkins; “though, as it is an ugly business, we think you ought to be satisfied with something less than the premium we offered.”
source_text: “No,” replied Mr. Limbkins; “at least, as it’s a nasty business, we think you ought to take something less than the premium we offered.”

text: “Foolish enough, this,” muttered the doctor to himself; “the boy must have gone wrong somewhere. Here! Put that in your pocket, and shut yourself up again.” With these words he tossed the hunchback a coin, and went back to the carriage.
source_text: “Stupid enough, this,” muttered the doctor to himself; “the boy must have made a mistake. Here! Put that in your pocket, and shut yourself up again.” With these words he flung the hunchback a piece of money, and returned to the carriage.

text: Clym set his breakfast aside. “Then there is some kind of scandal, and that is what is troubling Thomasin. Was this what made her ill?”
source_text: Clym pushed away his breakfast. “Then there is a scandal of some sort, and that’s what’s the matter with Thomasin. Was it this that made her ill?”

text: “That is a fine young lady indeed, to be pitied and wept over! How does it feel? Is it worth being put on trial for one’s life, to be the object of such sympathy and compassion, Mr. Darnay?”
source_text: “That’s a fair young lady to be pitied by and wept for by! How does it feel? Is it worth being tried for one’s life, to be the object of such sympathy and compassion, Mr. Darnay?”

text: “But there is still time,” said Brissenden. “Who knows but that you may yet prove the humble means of saving him. Why didn’t you let me swat him just once? I should have liked to have had a hand in it.”
source_text: “But there is yet time,” quoth Brissenden. “Who knows but what you may prove the humble instrument to save him. Why didn’t you let me swat him just once? I’d like to have had a hand in it.”

text: “Yes; but what is the use of speaking of magnificent rooms? And, by the way, the Little Trianon would suit us charmingly to live in, and you might stroll in the gardens by moonlight and imagine yourself in some English shrubbery; it is laid out after the English fashion.”
source_text: “Yes. But what’s the use of talking of gorgeous rooms? By the way, the Little Trianon would suit us beautifully to live in, and you might walk in the gardens in the moonlight and think you were in some English shrubbery; it is laid out in English fashion.”

text: “Yes; that’s all you women ever think about,” Sikes answered. “Fine young chaps! Well, they’re as good as dead, so it doesn’t make much difference.”
source_text: “Yes; that’s all you women think of,” answered Sikes. “Fine young chaps! Well, they’re as good as dead, so it don’t much matter.”

text: “In his, Hume’s, mind,” came the reply. “And Hume’s mind was the same as yours, with this one difference: he was wise enough to admit that there was no reply to Berkeley.”
source_text: “In his, Hume’s, mind,” was the reply. “And Hume’s mind was the same as yours, with this difference: he was wise enough to admit there was no answering Berkeley.”

text: “I tell you,” said the man, clenching his hands and stamping violently upon the floor, “I tell you I will not have her laid in the earth. She could not rest there. The worms would worry her—not eat her—she is so wasted away.”
source_text: “I tell you,” said the man: clenching his hands, and stamping furiously on the floor,—“I tell you I won’t have her put into the ground. She couldn’t rest there. The worms would worry her—not eat her—she is so worn away.”

text: “Ah, yes.” She went on without so much as turning her head, and for many paces nothing further was heard from her side save the rustle of her gown against the heavy ears of corn. Then she took up the matter again, rather tartly—
source_text: “Ah, yes.” She walked on without turning her head, and for many steps nothing further was heard from her quarter than the rustle of her dress against the heavy corn-ears. Then she resumed rather tartly—

text: “I am glad, for your sake, to hear it,” observed Monks. “Light your lantern! And be gone from here as swiftly as you can.”
source_text: “I am glad, for your sake, to hear it,” remarked Monks. “Light your lantern! And get away from here as fast as you can.”

text: “Only you here?” she cried, with an air of disappointment, the emptiness of which was betrayed by the colour mounting in her face and by her half-ashamed little laugh. “Where is Mrs. Yeobright?”
source_text: “Only you here?” she exclaimed, with a disappointed air, whose hollowness was proved by her rising redness and her half-guilty low laugh. “Where is Mrs. Yeobright?”

text: “He has been here these several days—three or four, I cannot say how many—I cannot gather my thoughts. Some errand of generosity brought him hither, unknown to us; he was halted at the barrier and dispatched to prison.”
source_text: “Has been here some days--three or four--I don’t know how many--I can’t collect my thoughts. An errand of generosity brought him here unknown to us; he was stopped at the barrier, and sent to prison.”

text: Can you marvel that such thoughts carried me away in a fury? I can only marvel that, at that instant, instead of giving utterance to my sensations in cries and torment, I did not break forth among mankind and perish in the endeavor to destroy them.
source_text: “Can you wonder that such thoughts transported me with rage? I only wonder that at that moment, instead of venting my sensations in exclamations and agony, I did not rush among mankind and perish in the attempt to destroy them.

text: “Exactly—seem foolish-like; and that’s very bad for the poor things that be so, though I only guess as much, to be sure,” said Grandfer Cantle, still with strong endeavour to maintain a seemly bearing and countenance.
source_text: “Exactly—seem foolish-like; and that’s very bad for the poor things that be so, though I only guess as much, to be sure,” said Grandfer Cantle, still strenuously preserving a sensible bearing and mien.

text: Frank continued, as though he had not heard her. “There are matters to be settled even before my own concern for you; amends to be made—attachments of which you know nothing. If you repent of having married, so do I.”
source_text: Frank went on as if he had not heard her. “There are considerations even before my consideration for you; reparations to be made—ties you know nothing of. If you repent of marrying, so do I.”

text: “True, true; it cannot be gainsaid!” cried a brisk young gentleman—one Mark Clark by name, a cordial and agreeable soul, whom, if you chanced to meet him anywhere in your travels, you were sure to know; to know him was to drink with him; and to drink with him was, unhappily, to pay the reckoning.
source_text: “True, true; it can’t be gainsaid!” observed a brisk young man—Mark Clark by name, a genial and pleasant gentleman, whom to meet anywhere in your travels was to know, to know was to drink with, and to drink with was, unfortunately, to pay for.

text: “No,” said Harry; “repeat it to me, if you will—repeat it at last! I will lay at your feet whatever station or fortune I may possess; and if you still abide by your present resolve, I will not, by word or deed, seek to alter it.”
source_text: “No,” said Harry; “to hear you repeat it, if you will—finally repeat it! I will lay at your feet, whatever of station of fortune I may possess; and if you still adhere to your present resolution, will not seek, by word or act, to change it.”

text: “Yes. The wires have no ground without the cave. They lead forth from the positive brush of the dynamo; through the negative brush there is a ground; the other ends of the wires come back again to the cave, and each is grounded apart by itself.”
source_text: “Yes. The wires have no ground-connection outside of the cave. They go out from the positive brush of the dynamo; there is a ground-connection through the negative brush; the other ends of the wire return to the cave, and each is grounded independently.”

text: “Drink, Henry Fray—drink,” said Jan Coggan with much magnanimity, he being a man who entertained Saint-Simonian notions of share and share alike where liquor was concerned, as the vessel, in its slow revolution among them, showed signs of drawing near to him.
source_text: “Drink, Henry Fray—drink,” magnanimously said Jan Coggan, a person who held Saint-Simonian notions of share and share alike where liquor was concerned, as the vessel showed signs of approaching him in its gradual revolution among them.

text: Oliver, more dead than alive, gasped out, “Yes.” Sikes, pointing with the pistol-barrel toward the street-door, briefly cautioned him to observe that he was within shot the whole way; and that if he drew back, he should fall dead on the instant.
source_text: Oliver, more dead than alive, gasped out, “Yes.” Sikes, pointing to the street-door with the pistol-barrel, briefly advised him to take notice that he was within shot all the way; and that if he faltered, he would fall dead that instant.

text: “Not that you are to do aught, but only to tell me whither she goes, whom she sees, and, if it be possible, what she says; to note the street, should it prove to be a street, or the house, should it prove to be a house; and to bring back to me every particular you can obtain.”
source_text: “Not to do anything, but to tell me where she goes, who she sees, and, if possible, what she says; to remember the street, if it is a street, or the house, if it is a house; and to bring me back all the information you can.”

text: “My dear young lady,” returned the surgeon, with a mournful shake of the head; “crime, like death, is by no means the portion of the old and withered only. The youngest and fairest are but too often its chosen victims.”
source_text: “My dear young lady,” rejoined the surgeon, mournfully shaking his head; “crime, like death, is not confined to the old and withered alone. The youngest and fairest are too often its chosen victims.”

text: “Make your obeisance to the board,” said Bumble. Oliver brushed away two or three tears that yet lingered in his eyes; and, seeing no board save the table, happily made his bow to that.
source_text: “Bow to the board,” said Bumble. Oliver brushed away two or three tears that were lingering in his eyes; and seeing no board but the table, fortunately bowed to that.

text: “I would just as soon not relate it—rather not. There is, besides, no cause why I should, seeing that you will probably never have much to do with me.”
source_text: “I would just as soon not tell it—rather not. There is no reason either why I should, as you probably will never have much to do with me.”

text: “That’s right—that’s the way to take it,” the cub observed with an airy assurance, though even then his eyes had begun to wander anxiously toward the door.
source_text: “That’s right—that’s the way to take it,” the cub announced airily, though he had already begun to glance anxiously at the door.

text: “I have heard a score of such stories to-day,” said he; “there would appear to be enough of them; yet I can find nothing fit for a sermon, and still they seem to look for a full and particular biography.”
source_text: “I have heard a score of such stories to-day,” he said; “there seem to be enough of them; but I can't find anything adapted to a sermon, and yet they seem to expect a detailed biography.”

text: “And so I am proprietor of certain knights,” said I, as we rode away. “Who would ever have supposed that I should live to take an account of assets such as these. I shall not know what to do with them; unless I raffle them off. How many of them are there, Sandy?”
source_text: “And so I'm proprietor of some knights,” said I, as we rode off. “Who would ever have supposed that I should live to list up assets of that sort. I shan't know what to do with them; unless I raffle them off. How many of them are there, Sandy?”

text: He heard an old lady, leaning forth from an offending motor-car in Piccadilly Circus, engaged in a heated altercation with a police officer. “Zeppelins indeed!” she cried. “What utter nonsense! As if they would _dare_ to come here! And who would _let_ them, I should like to know?”
source_text: He heard an old lady who was projecting from an offending automobile at Piccadilly Circus in hot dispute with a police officer. "Zeppelins indeed!" she said. "What nonsense! As if they would _dare_ to come here! Who would _let_ them, I should like to know?"

text: “The child,” said the girl, glancing up all at once, “is better where he lies than among us; and if no mischief come to Bill of it, I hope he lies dead in the ditch, and that his young bones may rot there.”
source_text: “The child,” said the girl, suddenly looking up, “is better where he is, than among us; and if no harm comes to Bill from it, I hope he lies dead in the ditch and that his young bones may rot there.”

text: “Oh! so that is the boy, is it?” said the undertaker, holding the candle aloft above his head, that he might obtain a better view of Oliver. “Mrs. Sowerberry, will you have the goodness to step this way for a moment, my dear?”
source_text: “Oh! that’s the boy, is it?” said the undertaker: raising the candle above his head, to get a better view of Oliver. “Mrs. Sowerberry, will you have the goodness to come here a moment, my dear?”

text: “There’ll be murder in the matter, too,” replied the hump-backed man, coolly, “if you do not take your hands off. Do you hear me?”
source_text: “There’ll be Murder the matter, too,” replied the hump-backed man, coolly, “if you don’t take your hands off. Do you hear me?”

text: “You perceive,” continued Mr. Brownlow; “setting this poor girl wholly aside, and granting it were possible to bring these scoundrels to justice without endangering her safety, what advantage should we accomplish?”
source_text: “You see,” pursued Mr. Brownlow; “placing this poor girl entirely out of the question, and supposing it were possible to bring these scoundrels to justice without compromising her safety, what good should we bring about?”

text: “You cannot, and you do not,” she said with modest composure. “There is no such abrupt feeling in persons. I will hear no more of you. Pray attend to me: I wish I knew what o’clock it may be—I am going—I have already squandered too much time here!”
source_text: “You cannot and you don’t,” she said demurely. “There is no such sudden feeling in people. I won’t listen to you any longer. Hear me, I wish I knew what o’clock it is—I am going—I have wasted too much time here already!”

text: His unreasoning anger frightened her, and she seemed to glide away from him, without any visible movement, as she said, “I am but a girl—do not speak to me so!”
source_text: His unreasonable anger terrified her, and she glided from him, without obviously moving, as she said, “I am only a girl—do not speak to me so!”

text: Those people up yonder have no notion of the Will that surges up in England. They are timid, little, manoeuvring folk, afraid of property, afraid of newspapers, afraid of trade-unions. text: “Yes, sir,” answered the servant. “He asked whether there were any muffins in the house; and, when I told him there were, he said he had come to tea.”
source_text: “Yes, sir,” replied the servant. “He asked if there were any muffins in the house; and, when I told him yes, he said he had come to tea.”

text: “I knew he was shamming,” said Fang, as though that were proof beyond all question. “Let him lie there; he’ll be tired of it presently.”
source_text: “I knew he was shamming,” said Fang, as if this were incontestable proof of the fact. “Let him lie there; he’ll soon be tired of that.”

text: “Here is the hang-out.” Brissenden set down his demijohn at the upper entrance, in readiness for the ascent. source_text: “Here’s the hang-out.” Brissenden rested his demijohn at the upstairs entrance, preliminary to the climb. text: “Oh, yes,” said Maria, “though I did feel somewhat anxious for the young man. Yet, when one considers of what materials he had to fashion a sermon,--why, how could he do otherwise? And still, I make no doubt he gives himself all the credit.”
source_text: “Oh, yes,” said Maria, “though I did feel nervous for the young man. Still, when you come to think what materials he had to make a sermon out of,--why, how could he help it! And yet, I doubt not he takes all the credit to himself.”

text: “There, now, you do not like him, and I wish I had never spoken of him!” she exclaimed, almost peevishly. “And I ought not to have done it, either, only he keeps worrying me so that I scarcely know what to do!”
source_text: “There, now, you don’t like him, and I wish I hadn’t mentioned him!” she exclaimed almost petulantly. “And I shouldn’t have done it, either, only he keeps on bothering me so till I don’t know what to do!”

text: “That a common man should be shamed before the world is nothing; but it would be dishonour to the _king_ if any who had beheld his minister naked should not also behold him delivered from his shame. If I might crave that my garments be brought me again--”
source_text: “That a common man should be shamed before the world, is nothing; but it were dishonor to the _king_ if any that saw his minister naked should not also see him delivered from his shame. If I might ask that my clothes be brought again--”

text: “Will you undertake to explain how it came to be supposed that it had been addressed hither, in the hope that we should know whither to forward it, and that it has been lying here some time?”
source_text: “Will you undertake to explain, that we suppose it to have been addressed here, on the chance of our knowing where to forward it, and that it has been here some time?”

text: “Well, had there been no Queen Guenever, it would not have come so soon; yet it would have come, in any case. It would have come for your own sake in due season; by good fortune, it happened to come for the queen’s.”
source_text: “Well, if there hadn't been any Queen Guenever, it wouldn't have come so early; but it would have come, anyway. It would have come on your own account by and by; by luck, it happened to come on the queen's.”

text: “Both she and my son disobeyed me in marrying; therefore I have no concern with their households. Their troubles are of their own contriving.” Mrs. Yeobright sought to speak with severity; yet the tale of her son’s condition had touched her more deeply than she was willing to let appear.
source_text: “Both she and my son disobeyed me in marrying; therefore I have no interest in their households. Their troubles are of their own making.” Mrs. Yeobright tried to speak severely; but the account of her son’s state had moved her more than she cared to show.

text: “It wants but a quarter to six at present,” said Bathsheba, glancing at her watch. “I daresay he’ll be in directly. Well, now then”—she bent her eyes upon the book—“Joseph Poorgrass, are you there?”
source_text: “It wants a quarter to six at present,” said Bathsheba, looking at her watch. “I daresay he’ll be in directly. Well, now then”—she looked into the book—“Joseph Poorgrass, are you there?”

text: “Yes. And if there were laid before you a flock of birds, unable to take wing, and you were bidden to fall upon them and pluck them of their feathers for your own profit, would you not fall first upon those birds whose plumes were the fairest?”
source_text: “Yes. And if you were shown a flock of birds, unable to fly, and were set upon them to strip them of their feathers for your own advantage, you would set upon the birds of the finest feathers; would you not?”

text: “You will keep yourself to the questions, and offer no remarks. You are not here to display your blood or your graces, and nothing of the kind shall be allowed. Can you write?”

text: “Seeking them from me, my nephew,” said the Marquis, and with his forefinger he touched him upon the breast; they were by now standing beside the hearth. “You will seek them in vain for ever, be well assured of that.”
source_text: “Seeking them from me, my nephew,” said the Marquis, touching him on the breast with his forefinger--they were now standing by the hearth--“you will for ever seek them in vain, be assured.”

text: “Plots. Though just Heaven knows I am innocent of any. Is it likely? Who would ever think of plotting with a poor, slight, feeble creature such as I?”
source_text: “Plots. Though the just Heaven knows that I am innocent of any. Is it likely? Who would think of plotting with a poor little weak creature like me?”

text: “But think of him now!” she cried with enthusiasm. “Think what his income allows him. His early privations are repaid a thousand times over.”
source_text: “But think of him now!” she cried enthusiastically. “Think of what his income affords him. His early denials are paid for a thousand-fold.”

text: “Oh no, indeed. I had but come down, Clym, to ask you—” At this he went with her through the doorway into the inner room beyond, and the door closing behind them, Eustacia and the mummer who sat beside her, the sole other beholder of the entertainment, saw and heard no more.
source_text: “O no, indeed. I merely stepped down, Clym, to ask you—” Here he followed her through the doorway into the private room beyond, and, the door closing, Eustacia and the mummer who sat next to her, the only other witness of the performance, saw and heard no more.

text: “No,” replied the old lady, handing it to him in a mechanical fashion. Oliver cast his eyes upon it, and perceived that it was addressed to Harry Maylie, Esquire, at the country-seat of some great lord; at what place, he could not determine.
source_text: “No,” replied the old lady, giving it to him mechanically. Oliver glanced at it, and saw that it was directed to Harry Maylie, Esquire, at some great lord’s house in the country; where, he could not make out.

text: “‘Despair not. To be without friends is indeed a hard lot; yet the hearts of men, when not warped by some manifest regard to their own advantage, are filled with brotherly kindness and charity. Rest, therefore, upon your hopes; and if these friends be good and amiable, despair not.’
source_text: “‘Do not despair. To be friendless is indeed to be unfortunate, but the hearts of men, when unprejudiced by any obvious self-interest, are full of brotherly love and charity. Rely, therefore, on your hopes; and if these friends are good and amiable, do not despair.’

text: Surprise and amazement leapt from face to face throughout the house; the queen’s pleased smile died away at the name of Sir Kay, and she looked cast down; and the page whispered in my ear with an accent and a manner full of extravagant derision--

text: “Maggie Toole’s got herself a fellow at last,” ran the gossip among the paper-box girls. “Look at Mag’s floor-walker”—thus the Give and Takes put their easy contempt into words.
source_text: “Maggie Toole’s got a fellow at last,” was the word that went round among the paper-box girls. “Pipe Mag’s floor-walker”—thus the Give and Takes expressed their indifferent contempt.

text: “I am persuaded I shall greatly surprise you,” said Rose, with a natural air of confusion; “but you once displayed much benevolence and goodness toward a very dear young friend of mine, and I feel certain you will be interested to hear of him once more.”
source_text: “I shall surprise you very much, I have no doubt,” said Rose, naturally embarrassed; “but you once showed great benevolence and goodness to a very dear young friend of mine, and I am sure you will take an interest in hearing of him again.”

text: “Yes; and that, too, was in the dead middle of the night. The gate would not open, do what he might, and, knowing the Devil’s hand was in it, he knelt him down.”
source_text: “Yes; that were the middle of the night, too. The gate would not open, try how he would, and knowing there was the Devil’s hand in it, he kneeled down.”

text: “My grandfather. He went out for a long walk today, and as he was making his way home he came upon some person who spoke to him of a wedding that had been broken off—he fancied it might be yours, and I knew that it was.”
source_text: “My grandfather. He took a long walk today, and as he was coming home he overtook some person who told him of a broken-off wedding—he thought it might be yours, and I knew it was.”

text: Eustacia blushed for the first time that day. “Well, I am a questionable gift,” she said softly. “I had thought you meant the gift of contentment—which he has, and I have not.”
source_text: Eustacia blushed for the first time that day. “Well, I am a questionable gift,” she said quietly. “I thought you meant the gift of content—which he has, and I have not.”

text: “I know this messenger, guard,” said Mr. Lorry, stepping down into the road, and being helped from behind with somewhat more haste than ceremony by the two other passengers, who forthwith clambered into the coach, closed the door, and drew up the window. “He may approach; there is nothing amiss.”
source_text: “I know this messenger, guard,” said Mr. Lorry, getting down into the road--assisted from behind more swiftly than politely by the other two passengers, who immediately scrambled into the coach, shut the door, and pulled up the window. “He may come close; there’s nothing wrong.”

text: “So the Unknown was near the dead stag likewise? Is it not just possible that he himself did the killing? His loyal zeal—under a mask—has a look that is but a shade too suspect. But what is your highness’s notion for putting the prisoner to the rack? Where lies the profit?”
source_text: “So the Unknown was near the dead stag, too? Isn't it just possible that he did the killing himself? His loyal zeal--in a mask--looks just a shade suspicious. But what is your highness's idea for racking the prisoner? Where is the profit?”

text: “For the love of God,” said Mr. Brownlow, with solemn earnestness, “do not speak so now, upon the very brink of death; but tell me where they are. You know that Sikes is dead; that Monks has made his confession; that there is no hope of any further profit. Where are those papers?”
source_text: “For the love of God,” said Mr. Brownlow solemnly, “do not say that now, upon the very verge of death; but tell me where they are. You know that Sikes is dead; that Monks has confessed; that there is no hope of any further gain. Where are those papers?”

text: “I reckon you will grant that, for some years past, there has been in all these kingdoms but one pair of eyes that has not kept a steady sidewise watch upon the queen and Sir Launcelot--”
source_text: “I reckon you will grant that during some years there has been only one pair of eyes in these kingdoms that has not been looking steadily askance at the queen and Sir Launcelot--”

text:
The hoary Sire, the fatal stroke  
Long, long be pleased to spare;  
To bless this little filial flock,  
And show what good men are.
source_text:
The hoary Sire—the mortal stroke,
Long, long be pleas’d to spare;
To bless this little filial flock,
And show what good men are.

text:
SEMICHORUS 1 [SONG WITHIN]:
Hark! hark! he comes apace,
A most fell discord in his pace.
Reeling, muse-less, clumsy, crying,
Far adown his stony dwelling;
Let us, by some comic charm,
School the still unschoolable from harm.
By all means must he be blinded,
If my counsel be but minded.
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 1 [SONG WITHIN]:
Listen! listen! he is coming,
A most hideous discord humming.
Drunken, museless, awkward, yelling,
Far along his rocky dwelling;
Let us with some comic spell
Teach the yet unteachable.
By all means he must be blinded,
If my counsel be but minded.

text:
My footfall frightens the wood-drake and wood-duck on my far and  
day-long ramble,  
They lift together, they slowly wheel about.
source_text:
My tread scares the wood-drake and wood-duck on my distant and
day-long ramble,
They rise together, they slowly circle around.

text:
Nor whither went, nor wherefore came  
The second time to me,  
Nor surely what it verily was,  
Have I the skill to say.
source_text:
Nor where it went, nor why it came
The second time to me,
Nor definitely what it was,
Have I the art to say.

text:
Word above all, as lovely as the heavens,
Lovely that war and all its works of slaughter must in time be
utterly forgotten,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night, without cease, with gentle
soothing, wash again, and yet again, this steadfast world;
For my foe is dead, a man as godlike as myself is dead,
I gaze where he lies pallid and still in the coffin—I draw nigh,
I bend and lightly touch with my lips the white face in the coffin.
source_text:
Word over all, beautiful as the sky,
Beautiful that war and all its deeds of carnage must in time be
utterly lost,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night incessantly softly
wash again, and ever again, this solid world;
For my enemy is dead, a man divine as myself is dead,
I look where he lies white-faced and still in the coffin--I draw near,
Bend down and touch lightly with my lips the white face in the coffin.

text:
And who now feels discord or grief?
Love is the universe to-day—
These are the thralls of shadowy morrow,
That dim Life’s labyrinthine way.
source_text:
And who feels discord now or sorrow?
Love is the universe to-day—
These are the slaves of dim to-morrow,
Darkening Life’s labyrinthine way.

text:
While we rehearse our boundless wealth, 'tis for thee, dear Mother,  
We hold it all, each part, to-day, inseparable in thee;  
Think not our song, our pageant, merely for base wares or gold—  
It is for thee, the soul within thee, electric, spiritual!  
Our farms, our arts, our harvests, we possess in thee! cities and States in thee!  
Our freedom all in thee! our very lives in thee!
source_text:
While we rehearse our measureless wealth, it is for thee, dear Mother,
We own it all and several to-day indissoluble in thee;
Think not our chant, our show, merely for products gross or lucre--
it is for thee, the soul in thee, electric, spiritual!
Our farms, inventions, crops, we own in thee! cities and States in thee!
Our freedom all in thee! our very lives in thee!

text:
l. 265. Pleiad.
The Pleiades are seven stars
that form a constellation.
Cf. Walt Whitman, 'On the beach at night.'
source_text:
l. 265. Pleiad. The Pleiades are seven stars making a constellation.
Cf. Walt Whitman, 'On the beach at night.'

text:
And as we sailed, a strange and awful tale  
That Woman told, like some mysterious dream  
That makes the slumberer’s cheek with wonder pale!  
’Twas midnight, and around, a shoreless stream,  
The wide sea rolled, when that majestic theme  
Enshrined within her heart found utterance, and she bent  
Her looks on mine; those eyes, a kindling beam  
Of love divine, into my spirit sent,  
And ere her lips could move, made the air eloquent.
source_text:
And as we sailed, a strange and awful tale
That Woman told, like such mysterious dream
As makes the slumberer’s cheek with wonder pale!
’Twas midnight, and around, a shoreless stream,
Wide ocean rolled, when that majestic theme
Shrined in her heart found utterance, and she bent
Her looks on mine; those eyes a kindling beam
Of love divine into my spirit sent,
And ere her lips could move, made the air eloquent.

text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone  
Can surely search and prove us;  
He knows each chord, its varied tone,  
Each spring, its several bias:  
Then at the balance let us cease,  
Nor seek to set it right;  
What’s done we may in part surmise,  
But know not what withstood it.
source_text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone
Decidedly can try us;
He knows each chord, its various tone,
Each spring, its various bias:
Then at the balance let’s be mute,
We never can adjust it;
What’s done we partly may compute,
But know not what’s resisted.

text:
Each richer . . . murderer. Such is the spoil they have won through  
their deed—the guilt of murder—nothing more.
source_text:
l. 224. Each richer . . . murderer. This is what they have gained by
their deed--the guilt of murder--that is all.

text:
And she beheld princes couched beneath the sheen
Of sunlike jewels; and round each temple-court
In dormitories set, row after row,
She saw the priests asleep—all of one sort—
For all had been instructed thus, to be so.—
The peasants in their huts, and in the port
The sailors she beheld, rocked upon the waves,
And the dead hushed within their dreamless graves.
source_text:
And she saw princes couched under the glow
Of sunlike gems; and round each temple-court
In dormitories ranged, row after row,
She saw the priests asleep—all of one sort—
For all were educated to be so.—
The peasants in their huts, and in the port
The sailors she saw cradled on the waves,
And the dead lulled within their dreamless graves.

text:
“I haven’t the slightest notion; besides, ’tis no matter, since he paid thee less heed  
than any of the rest. Now, had he taken more, it would have signified a great deal.”
source_text: “I haven’t a notion; besides, ’tis no difference, since he took less notice of you than any of the rest. Now, if he’d taken more, it would have mattered a great deal.”

text:
One noble house shall be the house of song,
Others for other arts—learning, the sciences, shall all abide here,
None shall be slighted, none but shall here be honored, aided, and made an example.
source_text:
One stately house shall be the music house,
Others for other arts--learning, the sciences, shall all be here,
None shall be slighted, none but shall here be honor’d, help’d, exampled.

text:
And still she slept an azure-lidded sleep,  
In blanched linen, smooth, and lavender'd,  
While he from out the closet bore a heap  
Of candied apple, quince, and plum, and gourd  
With jellies sweeter than the creamy curd,  
And lucent syrups, tinct with cinnamon;  
Manna and dates, in argosy convey'd  
From Fez; and every spiced dainty, one by one,  
From silken Samarcand to cedar'd Lebanon.
source_text:
And still she slept an azure-lidded sleep,
In blanched linen, smooth, and lavender'd,
While he from forth the closet brought a heap
Of candied apple, quince, and plum, and gourd
With jellies soother than the creamy curd,
And lucent syrops, tinct with cinnamon;
Manna and dates, in argosy transferr'd
From Fez; and spiced dainties, every one,
From silken Samarcand to cedar'd Lebanon.

text:
IONE:  
Ah, me! dear sister,  
The tide of sound hath ebbed away from us,  
And thou dost feign to rise from out its wave,  
Because thy words fall like the lucid, tender dew  
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.
source_text:
IONE:
Ah me! sweet sister,
The stream of sound has ebbed away from us,
And you pretend to rise out of its wave,
Because your words fall like the clear, soft dew
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.

text: “Well, there be calamities where least we look for them, whether or no. Neighbours, if Mrs. Yeobright should die, think ye we should be taken up and tried for the manslaughter of a woman?”
source_text: “Well, there’s calamities where we least expect it, whether or no. Neighbours, if Mrs. Yeobright were to die, d’ye think we should be took up and tried for the manslaughter of a woman?”

text:
The water-blooms beneath the little stream  
Dropped from the stalks where erst they used to gleam;  
And the whirling eddies bore them here and there,  
As winds had done with those in upper air.
source_text:
The water-blooms under the rivulet
Fell from the stalks on which they were set;
And the eddies drove them here and there,
As the winds did those of the upper air.

text:
Is this then a touch? quivering me into a newer self,
Flame and ether hurrying up through my veins,
False and traitorous end of me reaching and thronging to aid them,
My flesh and blood casting out lightning to smite what is scarcely
other than myself,
On every side lewd tempters hardening my limbs,
Milking the udder of my heart for its withheld drop,
Using me wantonly, granting no refusal,
Robbing me of my choicest as though for some design,
Unbuttoning my garments, gripping me by the naked waist,
Beguiling my bewilderment with the quiet of sunlight and fieldward pastures,
Shamelessly slipping the fellow-senses aside,
They were bribed to exchange themselves for touch and to crop at my borders,
No pity, no heed for my ebbing strength or my wrath,
Calling the rest of the herd to come and feast on them awhile,
Then all together to stand on a headland and harry me.
source_text:
Is this then a touch? quivering me to a new identity,
Flames and ether making a rush for my veins,
Treacherous tip of me reaching and crowding to help them,
My flesh and blood playing out lightning to strike what is hardly
different from myself,
On all sides prurient provokers stiffening my limbs,
Straining the udder of my heart for its withheld drip,
Behaving licentious toward me, taking no denial,
Depriving me of my best as for a purpose,
Unbuttoning my clothes, holding me by the bare waist,
Deluding my confusion with the calm of the sunlight and pasture-fields,
Immodestly sliding the fellow-senses away,
They bribed to swap off with touch and go and graze at the edges of me,
No consideration, no regard for my draining strength or my anger,
Fetching the rest of the herd around to enjoy them a while,
Then all uniting to stand on a headland and worry me.

text:
Laws for creations,  
For puissant artists and leaders, for new broods of teachers and  
perfect literati for America,  
For noble savants and musicians yet to come.  
All must bear relation to the ensemble of the world, and the  
compact truth of the world,  
There shall be no theme too marked--all works shall show  
the divine law of indirectness.
source_text:
Laws for creations,
For strong artists and leaders, for fresh broods of teachers and
perfect literats for America,
For noble savans and coming musicians.
All must have reference to the ensemble of the world, and the
compact truth of the world,
There shall be no subject too pronounced--all works shall illustrate
the divine law of indirections.

text:
I tasted wheat, and loathed the chaff,
And thanked the generous friend;
Wisdom looks comelier when beheld
From far, than near at hand.
source_text:
I tasted wheat, -- and hated chaff,
And thanked the ample friend;
Wisdom is more becoming viewed
At distance than at hand.

text:
In answer then, the sea,  
Lingering not, hastening not,  
Whisper’d unto me through the night, and very clearly ere the dawn,  
Lisp’d to me the low and delectable word death,  
And once again death, death, death, death  
Hissing in melody, neither as the bird nor as my awaken’d child’s heart,  
But drawing close as secretly for me, whispering at my feet,  
Creeping thence unerringly up to mine ears and bathing me softly over all,  
Death, death, death, death, death.
source_text:
Whereto answering, the sea,
Delaying not, hurrying not,
Whisper’d me through the night, and very plainly before daybreak,
Lisp’d to me the low and delicious word death,
And again death, death, death, death
Hissing melodious, neither like the bird nor like my arous’d child’s heart,
But edging near as privately for me rustling at my feet,
Creeping thence steadily up to my ears and laving me softly all over,
Death, death, death, death, death.

text:
There is a triumph in the finer mind  
When truth, long put to shame,  
Moves forward calm toward her supreme,  
Her God her only name.
source_text:
There 's triumph of the finer mind
When truth, affronted long,
Advances calm to her supreme,
Her God her only throng.

text:
Reckoning before thee, O soul, when thou, the season won,  
The seas all pass’d, the headlands weather’d, the voyage done,  
Encompass’d, thou dost face and meet God, yielding, the end attain’d,  
As, full of friendship, love made perfect, the Elder Brother found,  
The Younger melts in tenderness within his arms.
source_text:
Reckoning ahead O soul, when thou, the time achiev’d,
The seas all cross’d, weather’d the capes, the voyage done,
Surrounded, copest, frontest God, yieldest, the aim attain’d,
As fill’d with friendship, love complete, the Elder Brother found,
The Younger melts in fondness in his arms.

text:
No bone had he to hold him,
His utterance was the gush
Of countless humming-birds at once
From an upper bush.
source_text:
No bone had he to bind him,
His speech was like the push
Of numerous humming-birds at once
From a superior bush.

text:
As one by one the stately players vanish,
From that vast drama on history’s endless stage,
That glaring, partisan scene of war and peace—of old and new in conflict,
Wrought through rage, through fear, through midnight dread, and many a weary pause;
All gone—and since, in numberless graves receding, softening,
Conqueror’s and vanquish’d—Lincoln’s and Lee’s—now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days—and match’d to the days!
Thou from the prairies!—tangled and many-vein’d and hard hath been thy lot,
To wonder has it been performed!
source_text:
As one by one withdraw the lofty actors,
From that great play on history’s stage eterne,
That lurid, partial act of war and peace--of old and new contending,
Fought out through wrath, fears, dark dismays, and many a long suspense;
All past--and since, in countless graves receding, mellowing,
Victor’s and vanquish’d--Lincoln’s and Lee’s--now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days--and equal to the days!
Thou from the prairies!--tangled and many-vein’d and hard has been thy part,
To admiration has it been enacted!

text: My days were passed in close observance, that I might more swiftly master the tongue; and I may boast I advanced more rapidly than the Arabian, who understood but little and spake in broken accents, whilst I comprehended and could counterfeit almost every word that was uttered.
source_text: “My days were spent in close attention, that I might more speedily master the language; and I may boast that I improved more rapidly than the Arabian, who understood very little and conversed in broken accents, whilst I comprehended and could imitate almost every word that was spoken.

text:
What though, like commoners of air,  
We stray away, we know not where,  
To house or hall alight;  
Yet nature’s graces, hills and woods,  
The sweeping vales, the foaming floods,  
Are free to all by right.  
In days when daisies deck the ground,  
And blackbirds whistle clear,  
With honest joy our hearts shall bound  
To greet the coming year:  
On braes whene’er we please, then,  
We’ll sit and soothe a tune;  
Syne rhyme till’t, we’ll time till’t,  
And sing it when we’re done.
source_text:
What tho’, like commoners of air,
We wander out, we know not where,
But either house or hal’,
Yet nature’s charms, the hills and woods,
The sweeping vales, and foaming floods,
Are free alike to all.
In days when daisies deck the ground,
And blackbirds whistle clear,
With honest joy our hearts will bound,
To see the coming year:
On braes when we please, then,
We’ll sit an’ sowth a tune;
Syne rhyme till’t we’ll time till’t,
An’ sing’t when we hae done.

text:
Nothing is sinful to us beyond ourselves,  
Whatsoe'er appears, whatsoe'er appears not, we are fair or  
sinful within ourselves alone.
source_text:
Nothing is sinful to us outside of ourselves,
Whatever appears, whatever does not appear, we are beautiful or
sinful in ourselves only.

text:
I know a place where summer strives  
With frost so well rehearsed,  
She each year leads her daisies back,  
And briefly writes, “Lost.”
source_text:
I know a place where summer strives
With such a practised frost,
She each year leads her daisies back,
Recording briefly, "Lost."

text:
PROMETHEUS:
There are twin ills:
To speak, and to behold; grant me one.
There were the names, great Nature’s hallowed watchwords, they
Were lifted high in shining blazonry;
The nations thronged around, and cried aloud,
With one accord, Truth, liberty, and love!
Then, sudden, from the heavens fell fierce confusion
Among them: strife, deceit, and fear arose:
And tyrants rushed in, and divided the spoil.
Such was the shadow of the truth I saw.
source_text:
PROMETHEUS:
There are two woes:
To speak, and to behold; thou spare me one.
Names are there, Nature’s sacred watchwords, they
Were borne aloft in bright emblazonry;
The nations thronged around, and cried aloud,
As with one voice, Truth, liberty, and love!
Suddenly fierce confusion fell from heaven
Among them: there was strife, deceit, and fear:
Tyrants rushed in, and did divide the spoil.
This was the shadow of the truth I saw.

text:
Youth, ample, ardent, amorous—youth brimful of grace, of vigor, of enchantment,  
Know you that Age may follow hard upon you with equal grace,  
force, enchantment?
source_text:
Youth, large, lusty, loving--youth full of grace, force, fascination,
Do you know that Old Age may come after you with equal grace,
force, fascination?

text:
I got thy letter, and the birds’;
The maples never knew
That thou wert coming,—I declare,
How red their faces grew!
But, March, forgive me—
And all those hills
Thou leftest me to hue;
There was no purple fitting there,
Thou took’st it all with you.
source_text:
I got your letter, and the birds';
The maples never knew
That you were coming, -- I declare,
How red their faces grew!
But, March, forgive me --
And all those hills
You left for me to hue;
There was no purple suitable,
You took it all with you.

text:
Each and all, like ministrant angels, were  
To the Sensitive Plant a sweet joy to bear,  
Whilst the lagging hours of day went by  
Like windless clouds across a tender sky.
source_text:
Each and all like ministering angels were
For the Sensitive Plant sweet joy to bear,
Whilst the lagging hours of the day went by
Like windless clouds o’er a tender sky.

text:
When first I gaed to woo my Jenny,
Ye then was trotting wi’ your minnie:
Tho’ ye was wylie, slee, an’ funny,
Ye ne’er was donsie;
But hamely, tawie, quiet, an’ cannie,
An’ unco sonsie.
source_text:
When first I gaed to woo my Jenny,
Ye then was trotting wi’ your minnie:
Tho’ ye was trickie, slee, an’ funnie,
Ye ne’er was donsie;
But hamely, tawie, quiet, an’ cannie,
An’ unco sonsie.

text:
O how shall I, unskilfu’, dare  
The poet’s craft and calling?  
The tunefu’ powers, in happy hours,  
That murmur inspiration;  
Even they maun take a nobler flight  
Than aught they e’er have shown us,  
Ere they recite, in equal strain,  
The charms o’ lovely Davies.
source_text:
O how shall I, unskilfu’, try
The poet’s occupation?
The tunefu’ powers, in happy hours,
That whisper inspiration;
Even they maun dare an effort mair
Than aught they ever gave us,
Ere they rehearse, in equal verse,
The charms o’ lovely Davies.

text:
Even as a bark, which from a rift of mountains,  
Dark, huge, and beetling, on a river  
That there draws in the might of all its fountains,  
Comes forth, while with the speed its timbers quiver,  
Sails, oars, and stream, all bent on one endeavour;  
So, from that rift of light a winged Form  
On all the winds of heaven still drawing ever  
Floated, expanding as it drew: the storm  
Pursued it with fierce blasts, and lightnings swift and warm.
source_text:
Even like a bark, which from a chasm of mountains,
Dark, vast and overhanging, on a river
Which there collects the strength of all its fountains,
Comes forth, whilst with the speed its frame doth quiver,
Sails, oars and stream, tending to one endeavour;
So, from that chasm of light a winged Form
On all the winds of heaven approaching ever
Floated, dilating as it came; the storm
Pursued it with fierce blasts, and lightnings swift and warm.

text:
How the ancient mountains drip with sunset,  
And the brake of dun!  
How the hemlocks are touched with tinsel  
By the wizard sun!
source_text:
How the old mountains drip with sunset,
And the brake of dun!
How the hemlocks are tipped in tinsel
By the wizard sun!

text:
ASIA:
Speak but his name: a world that pines in pain
Craves only this: his name; curses shall drag him down.
source_text:
ASIA:
Utter his name: a world pining in pain
Asks but his name: curses shall drag him down.

text:
’Twas like an eye that seemed to smile on me.  
I gazed, until, by sunmade pallor waned, it sank  
Beneath the billows of the surging sea;  
Yet from its rays my spirit deep love drank,  
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank  
To one thought—one sole image—ay, for ever!  
Even as the dawn, on vapours dimly dank,  
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver  
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.
source_text:
‘’Twas like an eye which seemed to smile on me.
I watched, till by the sun made pale, it sank
Under the billows of the heaving sea;
But from its beams deep love my spirit drank,
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank
Into one thought—one image—yes, for ever!
Even like the dayspring, poured on vapours dank,
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.

text:
I'll tell you how the sun arose, --
In ribbons, one by one.
The steeples floated in amethyst,
The tidings like squirrels ran.
source_text:
I'll tell you how the sun rose, --
A ribbon at a time.
The steeples swam in amethyst,
The news like squirrels ran.

text:
These brethren, by a thousand signs made plain  
What love Lorenzo bare unto their sister,  
And how she loved him too, each did unchain  
His bitter thoughts to other, well-nigh wilder,  
That he, the servant of their trade’s design,  
Should in their sister’s love be blithe and glad,  
When ’twas their plot, by slow degrees and guile,  
To draw her to some high-born lord and his olive-trees.
source_text:
These brethren having found by many signs
What love Lorenzo for their sister had,
And how she lov'd him too, each unconfines
His bitter thoughts to other, well nigh mad
That he, the servant of their trade designs,
Should in their sister's love be blithe and glad,
When 'twas their plan to coax her by degrees
To some high noble and his olive-trees.

text:
More sights and more adventures, stranger, than thou’dst deem for;
Times once again, now mostly just after sunrise or before sunset,
Sometimes in springtime, oftener in autumn, under weather perfectly clear, in
open view,
Encampments far and nigh, the thronging streets of cities and the shop-windows,
(Explain it as thou wilt—believe it or not—it is all true,
And my comrade there could bear thee witness—we have oft confabbed
thereof,)
Men and scenes, beasts, trees, hues and lines, as plain as plain could be,
Homesteads and home-dooryards, paths bordured with box, lilacs in the corners,
Weddings in churches, thanksgiving feasts, the homecoming of sons long absent,
Gloomy burials, the mother beneath crape-veil and the daughters,
Suits in courts, jury and judge, the accused within the box,
Contenders, battles, multitudes, bridges, wharves,
Now and then faces marked with grief or with gladness,
(I could name them in this very hour, did I behold them once again,)
Reveal’d to me—right there at the sky’s edge,
Or plainly yonder to the left upon the hill-tops.
source_text:
More experiences and sights, stranger, than you’d think for;
Times again, now mostly just after sunrise or before sunset,
Sometimes in spring, oftener in autumn, perfectly clear weather, in
plain sight,
Camps far or near, the crowded streets of cities and the shopfronts,
(Account for it or not--credit or not--it is all true,
And my mate there could tell you the like--we have often confab’d
about it,)
People and scenes, animals, trees, colors and lines, plain as could be,
Farms and dooryards of home, paths border’d with box, lilacs in corners,
Weddings in churches, thanksgiving dinners, returns of long-absent sons,
Glum funerals, the crape-veil’d mother and the daughters,
Trials in courts, jury and judge, the accused in the box,
Contestants, battles, crowds, bridges, wharves,
Now and then mark’d faces of sorrow or joy,
(I could pick them out this moment if I saw them again,)
Show’d to me--just to the right in the sky-edge,
Or plainly there to the left on the hill-tops.

text:
It’s no the keen winter wind,
It’s no the whirling drift and snaw;
But aye the tear wells in my e’e,
To think on him that’s far awa.
source_text:
It’s no the frosty winter wind,
It’s no the driving drift and snaw;
But aye the tear comes in my e’e,
To think on him that’s far awa.

text:
And thenceforth shall there be so fast a friendship  
’Twixt thee and me, that neither Fortune,  
The monstrous phantasm that doth dog success,  
That thrifty miser, that unfettered spendthrift,  
Who still, with varying hand, doth interchange  
Ill and good, disgrace and glory; nor shall Time,  
That guiding star of ages, unto whose light  
The winged years hasten through the spaces  
Of their unequal rounds; nor  
Heaven itself, whose fair and lustrous stars  
Do rule the world and lend it ornament, can ever make  
The smallest breach betwixt thee and me,  
Since now I find a haven in thy grace.
source_text:
And thenceforth shall so firm an amity
’Twixt thee and me be, that neither Fortune,
The monstrous phantom which pursues success,
That careful miser, that free prodigal,
Who ever alternates, with changeful hand,
Evil and good, reproach and fame; nor Time,
That lodestar of the ages, to whose beam
The winged years speed o’er the intervals
Of their unequal revolutions; nor
Heaven itself, whose beautiful bright stars
Rule and adorn the world, can ever make
The least division between thee and me,
Since now I find a refuge in thy favour.

text:
It is a far, far treasure to divine,  
And estimate the pearl  
That slipped between my simple fingers then  
When I was but a girl at school!
source_text:
It 's far, far treasure to surmise,
And estimate the pearl
That slipped my simple fingers through
While just a girl at school!

text:
With azure, wavering, stumbling buzz,
Between the light and me;
And then the windows failed, and then
I could not see to see.
source_text:
With blue, uncertain, stumbling buzz,
Between the light and me;
And then the windows failed, and then
I could not see to see.

text:
Auld Neibour,
I’m thrice times doubly o’er your debtor,
For your auld-farrant, kindly letter;
Tho’ I maun say I doubt ye flatter,
Ye speak sae fair;
For my puir, silly, rhyming clatter
Some less maun fare.
source_text:
Auld Neibour,
I’m three times doubly o’er your debtor,
For your auld-farrant, frien’ly letter;
Tho’ I maun say’t I doubt ye flatter,
Ye speak sae fair;
For my puir, silly, rhymin clatter
Some less maun sair.

text:
If I have stray’d in those dark paths  
Of life I should have shunn’d,  
As something, loudly, in my breast,  
Remonstrates I have done;
source_text:
If I have wander’d in those paths
Of life I ought to shun,
As something, loudly, in my breast,
Remonstrates I have done;

text:
CHORUS:
O Slavery! thou frost of the world’s first time,
That slayest its blossoms, and leav’st its thorns all bare!
Thy grasp hath marked these limbs with crime,
These brows thy branding garland wear,
Yet the free heart, the steadfast soul
Scorn thy control!
source_text:
CHORUS:
O Slavery! thou frost of the world’s prime,
Killing its flowers and leaving its thorns bare!
Thy touch has stamped these limbs with crime,
These brows thy branding garland bear,
But the free heart, the impassive soul
Scorn thy control!

text:
I must, dearest Agnes, the night is far spent—  
I must roam this evening to Strasburg, intent  
I must seek the sad tomb where my forefathers lie,  
And must delve up their relics from under the stone cold and dry.
source_text:
I must dearest Agnes, the night is far gone—
I must wander this evening to Strasburg alone,
I must seek the drear tomb of my ancestors’ bones,
And must dig their remains from beneath the cold stones.

text:
Coffin that fares through alleys and streets,  
Through day and through night with the great cloud darkening the land,  
With the pomp of the inloop’d flags, with the cities draped in black,  
With the show of the States themselves as of crape-veil’d women standing,  
With processions long and winding and the flambeaus of the night,  
With the countless torches kindled, with the silent sea of faces and the  
uncover’d heads,  
With the waiting depot, the arriving coffin, and the sombre faces,  
With dirges through the night, with the thousand voices mounting strong  
and solemn,  
With all the mournful voices of the dirges pour’d about the coffin,  
The dim-lit churches and the shuddering organs—where amid these  
thou journeyest,  
With the tolling, tolling bells’ perpetual clang,  
Here, coffin that slowest passest,  
I give thee my sprig of lilac.
source_text:
Coffin that passes through lanes and streets,
Through day and night with the great cloud darkening the land,
With the pomp of the inloop’d flags with the cities draped in black,
With the show of the States themselves as of crape-veil’d women standing,
With processions long and winding and the flambeaus of the night,
With the countless torches lit, with the silent sea of faces and the
unbared heads,
With the waiting depot, the arriving coffin, and the sombre faces,
With dirges through the night, with the thousand voices rising strong
and solemn,
With all the mournful voices of the dirges pour’d around the coffin,
The dim-lit churches and the shuddering organs--where amid these
you journey,
With the tolling tolling bells’ perpetual clang,
Here, coffin that slowly passes,
I give you my sprig of lilac.

text:
The homeward-bound and the outward-bound,  
The lovely lost swimmer, the ennuye, the onanist, the maiden that  
loves unrequited, the money-maker,  
The actor and actress, those done with their parts and those  
waiting to begin,  
The loving boy, the husband and wife, the voter, the nominee  
that is chosen and the nominee that hath fail’d,  
The great already known and the great any time after to-day,  
The stammerer, the sick, the perfect-form’d, the homely,  
The criminal that stood in the dock, the judge that sat and sentenced  
him, the ready lawyers, the jury, the audience,  
The laugher and weeper, the dancer, the midnight widow, the red squaw,  
The consumptive, the erysipelatous, the idiot, he that is wrong’d,  
The antipodes, and every one between this and them in the dark,  
I swear they are even’d now—one is no better than the other,  
The night and sleep have made them like and restored them.
source_text:
The homeward bound and the outward bound,
The beautiful lost swimmer, the ennuye, the onanist, the female that
loves unrequited, the money-maker,
The actor and actress, those through with their parts and those
waiting to commence,
The affectionate boy, the husband and wife, the voter, the nominee
that is chosen and the nominee that has fail’d,
The great already known and the great any time after to-day,
The stammerer, the sick, the perfect-form’d, the homely,
The criminal that stood in the box, the judge that sat and sentenced
him, the fluent lawyers, the jury, the audience,
The laugher and weeper, the dancer, the midnight widow, the red squaw,
The consumptive, the erysipalite, the idiot, he that is wrong’d,
The antipodes, and every one between this and them in the dark,
I swear they are averaged now--one is no better than the other,
The night and sleep have liken’d them and restored them.

text:

But then a voice replied—‘Life!’—I turned, and knew
(O Heaven, show mercy unto such wretchedness!)
source_text:

text:
As Highland cliffs by thunder riven,
When lightnings kindle the stormy heaven,
Are dashed with crashing rattle;
As flames amid a hundred woods,
As headlong foam from hundred floods,
Such is the rage of Battle.
source_text:
As Highland craigs by thunder cleft,
When lightnings fire the stormy lift,
Hurl down with crashing rattle;
As flames among a hundred woods,
As headlong foam from a hundred floods,
Such is the rage of Battle.

text:

With no abroad-made sound,
Each clasped the other's Crucifix -
No other tie were found -

text:

And dawning is but dreary,  
When birks stand bare at Yule.

Cauld is the e’enin blast,
O’ Boreas o’er the pool,
An’ dawin’ it is dreary,

source_text: The trimmings of her dress, as they quivered against the light, showed how agitated she was, and at last she burst out crying. “And you’ll not—press me—about anything more—if I say in five or six years?” she sobbed, when she had power to frame the words.
