source_text: “There was two of ’em in it,” continued Blathers; “and they had a boy with ’em; that’s plain from the size of the window. That’s all to be said at present. We’ll see this lad that you’ve got upstairs at once, if you please.”

text: “I had not thought that one I called my own would come to treat me so. He addressed me as if I meant to thwart him and do him harm, as though I could ever desire evil for him!”
source_text: “I did not think one whom I called mine would grow up to treat me like this. He spoke to me as if I opposed him to injure him. As though I could wish him ill!”

text: “The parish pays for the grave, half a crown, but not the bell shilling, because the bell is a luxury: yet ’a can hardly do without the grave, poor body. However, I expect our mistress will pay for all.”
source_text: “The parish pays the grave half-crown, but not the bell shilling, because the bell’s a luxery: but ’a can hardly do without the grave, poor body. However, I expect our mistress will pay all.”

text: “Well, Miss—begging your pardon—I thought you might like them. But I know I can’t rival you in laying my thoughts out on my tongue. I was never much of a hand at what goes on inside me. Still, I thank you. Come, give me your hand.”
source_text: “Well, Miss—excuse the words—I thought you would like them. But I can’t match you, I know, in mapping out my mind upon my tongue. I never was very clever in my inside. But I thank you. Come, give me your hand.”

text: “Ah, when my wife and I were married at Norcombe Church,” said the old maltster, who did not like to find himself shut out of the conversation, “we were reckoned the handsomest pair in the neighbourhood—everybody said it.”
source_text: “Ah, when I and my wife were married at Norcombe Church,” said the old maltster, not pleased at finding himself left out of the subject, “we were called the handsomest couple in the neighbourhood—everybody said so.”

text: “And now consider what has come to pass,” said he, with emphasis. “Twice in every month there is fresh meat upon my table.” He paused there, to let the fact take full effect, then went on—“and eight times salt meat.”
source_text: “And now consider what is come to pass,” said he, impressively. “Two times in every month there is fresh meat upon my table.” He made a pause here, to let that fact sink home, then added --“and eight times salt meat.”

text: “It’s the worst part of having dealings with women,” said the Jew, setting down his club again; “but they’re shrewd, and we can’t manage, in our business, without them. Charley, show Oliver to bed.”
source_text: “It’s the worst of having to do with women,” said the Jew, replacing his club; “but they’re clever, and we can’t get on, in our line, without ’em. Charley, show Oliver to bed.”

text: “You may come in; you will not disturb him,” she said, coming after him. “The door is fastened only so that no one who happens to come by may intrude upon him while he is lying here, should I be in the garden or upstairs.”
source_text: “You may go in; you will not disturb him,” she said, following behind. “My reason for fastening the door is that he may not be intruded upon by any chance comer while lying here, if I should be in the garden or upstairs.”

text: “Never mind what it is. Believe this: I cannot bring myself to lose you. I must have you always beside me. Even this very evening I dislike the thought of letting you go. There is but one cure for this uneasiness, dearest—you must be my wife.”
source_text: “Never mind what it is. Believe this, I cannot let myself lose you. I must have you always with me. This very evening I do not like to let you go. There is only one cure for this anxiety, dearest—you must be my wife.”

text: “I am not surprised at it; it is not so long since you were pretty far advanced upon the road to another. You speak so faintly.”
source_text: “I don’t wonder at it; it’s not so long since you were pretty far advanced on your way to another. You speak faintly.”

text: “Very well, I shall leave you.” He caught her hand unexpectedly and kissed it—for the first time since her marriage. “What light is that on the hill?” he added, as though to conceal the tenderness.
source_text: “Very well, I will leave you.” He took her hand unexpectedly, and kissed it—for the first time since her marriage. “What light is that on the hill?” he added, as it were to hide the caress.

text: “He promised the stacks would be attended to, and now they are all left neglected! Is there nothing I can do to help? Liddy is afraid to come out. Fancy finding you here at such an hour! Surely I can do something?”
source_text: “He promised that the stacks should be seen to, and now they are all neglected! Can I do anything to help? Liddy is afraid to come out. Fancy finding you here at such an hour! Surely I can do something?”

text: “Hold her at anchor for a minute,” the officer ordered. “There’s a man aboard we’re after—a Philadelphia burglar named ‘Pinky’ McGuire. He’s right there on the back seat. Mind the side, Donovan.”
source_text: “Keep her at anchor for a minute,” ordered the officer. “There’s a man on board we want—a Philadelphia burglar called ‘Pinky’ McGuire. There he is on the back seat. Look out for the side, Donovan.”

text: “Yes, Paris must be a taking place,” said Humphrey. “Fine shop-windows, trumpets, and drums; and here are we abroad in all winds and weathers—”
source_text: “Yes, Paris must be a taking place,” said Humphrey. “Grand shop-winders, trumpets, and drums; and here be we out of doors in all winds and weathers—”

text: “Well, he has come into a fortune of eleven thousand pounds—his uncle died in Canada, just after learning that all the family he was sending home had gone down in the Cassiopeia; so Wildeve has come into everything, and had not in the least been expecting it.”
source_text: “Well, he has come into a fortune of eleven thousand pounds—uncle died in Canada, just after hearing that all his family, whom he was sending home, had gone to the bottom in the Cassiopeia; so Wildeve has come into everything, without in the least expecting it.”

text: “Three Cripples,” repeated Noah, “and a very good sign too. Now, then! Keep close at my heels, and come along.” Having thus enjoined him, he pressed the rattling door open with his shoulder, and entered the house, his companion following close behind.
source_text: “Three Cripples,” repeated Noah, “and a very good sign too. Now, then! Keep close at my heels, and come along.” With these injunctions, he pushed the rattling door with his shoulder, and entered the house, followed by his companion.

text: But for that latter inconvenience, the carriage would probably not have stopped; carriages were often known to drive on, and leave their wounded behind them, and why should they not? But the frightened valet had scrambled down in great haste, and there were twenty hands upon the horses’ bridles.
source_text: But for the latter inconvenience, the carriage probably would not have stopped; carriages were often known to drive on, and leave their wounded behind, and why not? But the frightened valet had got down in a hurry, and there were twenty hands at the horses’ bridles.

text: “Yes, I do mean to tell you so,” replied Sikes. “The old lady has had ’em these twenty years; and if you were to offer her five hundred pound, they’d not come into the matter.”
source_text: “Yes, I do mean to tell you so,” replied Sikes. “The old lady has had ’em these twenty years; and if you were to give ’em five hundred pound, they wouldn’t be in it.”

text: “Mr. Lorry, pray look once more upon the prisoner. If he were muffled and concealed, as those two travellers were, is there aught in his figure and height to make it improbable that he should have been one of them?”
source_text: “Mr. Lorry, look again upon the prisoner. Supposing him wrapped up as those two passengers were, is there anything in his bulk and stature to render it unlikely that he was one of them?”

text: “I cannot conceive whither she could have been bound,” said Clym to some one. “She had plainly come a long distance; yet, even when she was able but now to speak, she would not tell me where. What, in truth, do you think of her?”
source_text: “I cannot think where she could have been going,” said Clym to someone. “She had evidently walked a long way, but even when she was able to speak just now she would not tell me where. What do you really think of her?”

text: “Faber says we must lay in what we can. This war—it will stop everything. One cannot say what may come of it. I have the children to consider, and so here I am. I was at Hickson’s before nine....”
source_text: "Faber says we have to lay in what we can. This war--it's going to stop everything. We can't tell what will happen. I've got the children to consider, so here I am. I was at Hickson's before nine...."

text: By this time she had risen; and they, without any design, sauntered on together, speaking not a word, for two or three minutes; when Eustacia, breaking the silence, said, “I presume I must congratulate you.”
source_text: By this time she had risen; and they involuntarily sauntered on together, without speaking, for two or three minutes; when Eustacia broke silence by saying, “I assume I must congratulate you.”

text: The elder brother turned his regard upon the younger, who, with a haughty air, said, “There is a case of medicines here;” and fetched it from a closet, placing it upon the table.
source_text: “The elder brother looked to the younger, who said haughtily, ‘There is a case of medicines here;’ and brought it from a closet, and put it on the table.

text: “Heavy! What be ye talking of? What be ye made for?” returned the male traveller, shifting his own little bundle, as he spoke, to the other shoulder. “Oh, there ye are, resting again! Well, if ye are not enough to wear out any man’s patience, I know not what is!”
source_text: “Heavy! What are yer talking about? What are yer made for?” rejoined the male traveller, changing his own little bundle as he spoke, to the other shoulder. “Oh, there yer are, resting again! Well, if yer ain’t enough to tire anybody’s patience out, I don’t know what is!”

text: “What a noble thing is capital punishment! Dead men do not repent; dead men do not bring awkward tales to the light. Ah, it is a noble thing for the trade! Five of ’em hanging in a line, and not one left to turn informer, nor to go white-livered!”
source_text: “What a fine thing capital punishment is! Dead men never repent; dead men never bring awkward stories to light. Ah, it’s a fine thing for the trade! Five of ’em strung up in a row, and none left to play booty, or turn white-livered!”

text: Here was trouble again—a conflict of authority. No one knew which of us to believe; I yet had some remnant of reputation left. The magician’s scorn was quickened, and he said:
source_text: Here was trouble again--a conflict of authority. Nobody knew which of us to believe; I still had some reputation left. The magician's scorn was stirred, and he said:

text: “Frankly, though—pray do not take offense at it—I tell you this only that you may know exactly how you stand in his regard—he has no liking for your radical opinions, and he thinks you indolent. I know very well that you are not so. I know you labor hard.”
source_text: “Frankly, though, and don’t let it hurt you—I tell you, to show you precisely how you stand with him—he doesn’t like your radical views, and he thinks you are lazy. Of course I know you are not. I know you work hard.”

text: “A bad one! I’ll eat my head if he be not a bad one,” growled Mr. Grimwig, who contrived, by some ventriloquial faculty, to utter the words without so much as stirring a muscle of his countenance.
source_text: “A bad one! I’ll eat my head if he is not a bad one,” growled Mr. Grimwig, speaking by some ventriloquial power, without moving a muscle of his face.

text: “Hold, thou white-livered hound!” cried the robber, shouting after Toby Crackit, who, besting the matter with his long legs, was already in the lead. “Hold!”
source_text: “Stop, you white-livered hound!” cried the robber, shouting after Toby Crackit, who, making the best use of his long legs, was already ahead. “Stop!”

text: “It is in the Middle Ages,” said Mr. Britling. “What an odd, pertinacious business it seems to have been. First one bomb, then another; and at last the man with the pistol. While we were walking in the rose garden. It is like something out of ‘The Prisoner of Zenda.’”
source_text: "It's in the middle ages," said Mr. Britling. "What an odd, pertinaceous business it seems to have been. First one bomb, then another; then finally the man with the pistol. While we were strolling about the rose garden. It's like something out of 'The Prisoner of Zenda.'"

text: “You will never hold to your plan of education, I am quite certain; and then all will be well with me; and so I promise to be yours for ever and ever.”
source_text: “You will never adhere to your education plan, I am quite sure; and then it will be all right for me; and so I promise to be yours for ever and ever.”

text: “He knows you,” replied the girl; “and knew you were here, for it was by hearing him name the place that I discovered you.”
source_text: “He knows you,” replied the girl; “and knew you were here, for it was by hearing him tell the place that I found you out.”

text: “If, without troubling him,” she said, lifting her hand to Mr. Lorry as he bent above them, after many a sounding of his nose, “all might be contrived for our departure from Paris forthwith, so that, from the very door, he could be borne away--”
source_text: “If, without disturbing him,” she said, raising her hand to Mr. Lorry as he stooped over them, after repeated blowings of his nose, “all could be arranged for our leaving Paris at once, so that, from the very door, he could be taken away--”

text: “It is a poor lad from the free-school, sir,” returned Mr. Bumble, “who has been very near murdered—murdered all but, sir—by young Twist.”
source_text: “It’s a poor boy from the free-school, sir,” replied Mr. Bumble, “who has been nearly murdered—all but murdered, sir,—by young Twist.”

text: “And the babes in their cradles—every mother’s son of them! And at home by the hearth, whenever you lift your eyes, there shall I be—and whenever I lift mine, there shall you be.”
source_text: “And the babies in the births—every man jack of ’em! And at home by the fire, whenever you look up, there I shall be—and whenever I look up there will be you.”

text: “She is innocent, my Elizabeth,” said I, “and that shall be made clear; fear nothing, but suffer your spirits to be cheered by the certainty of her acquittal.”
source_text: “She is innocent, my Elizabeth,” said I, “and that shall be proved; fear nothing, but let your spirits be cheered by the assurance of her acquittal.”

text: “Well, then, do you know that, without any further proof whatsoever, you may marry again in about six years from now—provided no one objects to it or lays any blame upon you?”
source_text: “Well, then, do you know that without further proof of any kind you may marry again in about six years from the present—subject to nobody’s objection or blame?”

text: “Yes, truly. When Doctor Manette was set at liberty, you, his ancient servant, had the care of him, I am aware. He was committed to your keeping. You perceive that I am acquainted with the circumstances?”
source_text: “Yes, indeed. When Doctor Manette was released, you, his old domestic, had the charge of him, I know. He was delivered to you. You see I am informed of the circumstances?”

text: “Then let us go on—if you are ready. Our nearest path is toward that holly-bush with the dark shadow which you see there below.”
source_text: “Then let us walk on—if you are ready. Our nearest way is towards that holly bush with the dark shadow that you see down there.”

text: “I see you were not,” said the stranger, while a quiet sarcasm played about his mouth; “else you would have known my name. You do not know it. I should advise you not to ask for it.”
source_text: “I see you were not,” said the stranger; an expression of quiet sarcasm playing about his mouth; “or you have known my name. You don’t know it. I would recommend you not to ask for it.”

text: “I wish Tamsie were not such a confoundedly good little woman,” said Wildeve, “so that I might be true to you without doing hurt to a deserving person. It is I who am the sinner after all; I am not worth the little finger of either of you.”
source_text: “I wish Tamsie were not such a confoundedly good little woman,” said Wildeve, “so that I could be faithful to you without injuring a worthy person. It is I who am the sinner after all; I am not worth the little finger of either of you.”

text: “You know you cannot do otherwise, for all your moods and changes!” she replied defiantly. “Say what you please; strive as you may; keep from me as far as you can—you will never forget me. You will love me all your life long. You would leap at the chance to marry me!”
source_text: “You know you can’t do otherwise, for all your moods and changes!” she answered defiantly. “Say what you will; try as you may; keep away from me all that you can—you will never forget me. You will love me all your life long. You would jump to marry me!”

text: “Then,” said the doctor, with some impetuosity, “I put it to you once more whether you can deem it reasonable that this promise to the girl should be held binding; a promise made with the best and kindest intentions, but, in truth—”
source_text: “Then,” said the doctor impetuously, “I put it to you again, whether you think it reasonable that this promise to the girl should be considered binding; a promise made with the best and kindest intentions, but really—”

text: As the keeper of the wine-shop came in at the door, the spy saluted him by lifting his hat and saying, with an ingratiating smile, “Good day, Jacques!” Defarge drew up suddenly, and stared at him.
source_text: As the keeper of the wine-shop entered at the door, the spy saluted him by touching his hat, and saying, with an engaging smile, “Good day, Jacques!” Defarge stopped short, and stared at him.

text: “If you please, mistress,” said a shrivelled old pauper woman, hideously ugly, as she thrust her head within the door, “Old Sally is a-going fast.”
source_text: “If you please, mistress,” said a withered old female pauper, hideously ugly: putting her head in at the door, “Old Sally is a-going fast.”

text: “There the truth is spoken at last!” said the soldier in answer. “Never tell me that a young lady may live in a hum of admiration and not be aware of somewhat of it. Ah, well, Miss Everdene, you are—pardon my blunt fashion—you are rather a wrong to our sex than anything else.”
source_text: “There the truth comes out!” said the soldier, in reply. “Never tell me that a young lady can live in a buzz of admiration without knowing something about it. Ah, well, Miss Everdene, you are—pardon my blunt way—you are rather an injury to our race than otherwise.”

text: “Are you going up to the Cripples, Fagin?” cried the little man, calling after him. “Hold there! I’ve no objection to taking a drop there along with you!”
source_text: “Are you going up to the Cripples, Fagin?” cried the little man, calling after him. “Stop! I don’t mind if I have a drop there with you!”

text: “But not for me,” said the Jew, submissively. “He might have been of use to me. When there are two parties to a bargain, it is but reasonable that the interests of both should be consulted; is it not, my good friend?”
source_text: “But not mine,” said the Jew, submissively. “He might have become of use to me. When there are two parties to a bargain, it is only reasonable that the interests of both should be consulted; is it, my good friend?”

text: “Yes. They came to bid us submit. You see, they did not truly come to test the torpedoes; that was but an incident.”
source_text: “Yes. They came to command us to make submission. You see they didn't really come to test the torpedoes; that was merely an incident.”

text: “You are ambitious, Eustacia—no, not quite ambitious, but luxurious. I ought, I suppose, to be of the same temper, if I am to make you happy. And yet, so far from it, I could live and die in a hermitage here, provided only that I had honest work to occupy me.”
source_text: “You are ambitious, Eustacia—no, not exactly ambitious, luxurious. I ought to be of the same vein, to make you happy, I suppose. And yet, far from that, I could live and die in a hermitage here, with proper work to do.”

text: “Is there very much, do you think, in the notion that some part of this Ulster trouble is a German intrigue? You and Mr. Philbert were saying such things—”
source_text: "Is there very much, do you think, in the suggestion that some of this Ulster trouble is a German intrigue? You and Mr. Philbert were saying things--"

text: “She is indeed a most interesting and charming young lady,” said Mr. Direck. “There is in her a kind of Western college freedom of spirit—and yet something about her that is not American at all.”
source_text: "She's a very interesting and charming young lady indeed," said Mr. Direck. "With a sort of Western college freedom of mind--and something about her that isn't American at all."

text: “No, no,” interposed Monks. “I—I knew nothing of that; I was about to learn the truth of the tale when you came up with me. I did not know the cause. I supposed it to be but a common quarrel.”
source_text: “No, no,” interposed Monks. “I—I knew nothing of that; I was going to inquire the truth of the story when you overtook me. I didn’t know the cause. I thought it was a common quarrel.”

text: The reddleman bore the wound and continued: “He is a man who marks the looks of women, and you could turn him to your own will like withywind, if only you had the mind.”
source_text: The reddleman suffered the wound and went on: “He is a man who notices the looks of women, and you could twist him to your will like withywind, if you only had the mind.”

text: “I have not forgotten. But why have you come so unexpectedly to repeat the question, when you had promised only to come next Saturday? I thought I was to have plenty of time in which to consider.”
source_text: “I have not forgotten. But why have you come so unexpectedly to repeat the question, when you only promised to come next Saturday? I thought I was to have plenty of time to consider.”

text: “Come!” replied Stryver, with a boastful laugh, “though I lay no claim to being the very soul of Romance (for I trust I know myself better), yet I am a more tender-hearted sort of fellow than _you_.”
source_text: “Come!” rejoined Stryver, laughing boastfully, “though I don’t prefer any claim to being the soul of Romance (for I hope I know better), still I am a tenderer sort of fellow than _you_.”

text: “Here is another letter,” said Mrs. Maylie, pausing a moment in thought; “but whether to send it now, or to wait till I can see how Rose proceeds, I hardly know. I would not send it on, unless I apprehended the worst.”
source_text: “Here is another letter,” said Mrs. Maylie, pausing to reflect; “but whether to send it now, or wait until I see how Rose goes on, I scarcely know. I would not forward it, unless I feared the worst.”

text: “Then they are in the ditch!” cried Harry. “Follow after, and keep as close upon me as you may.” Having said this, he leaped the hedge and bounded away with such swiftness that it became a matter of no small difficulty for the others to hold near him.
source_text: “Then, they are in the ditch!” said Harry. “Follow! And keep as near me, as you can.” So saying, he sprang over the hedge, and darted off with a speed which rendered it matter of exceeding difficulty for the others to keep near him.

text: “I had hoped and arranged matters differently. I had thought, and I still think, that the best thing for you would be to study shorthand—you already know type-writing—and go into father’s office. You have a good mind, and I am confident you would succeed as a lawyer.”
source_text: “I had hoped and planned otherwise. I had thought, and I still think, that the best thing for you would be to study shorthand—you already know type-writing—and go into father’s office. You have a good mind, and I am confident you would succeed as a lawyer.”

text: “But why could not the poor woman—” she broke in, brokenly. Then she left the rebellion of her thought unspoken, to cry aloud: “Oh! It is degrading! It is not nice! It is nasty!”
source_text: “But why couldn’t the poor woman—” she broke in disconnectedly. Then she left the revolt of her thought unexpressed to cry out: “Oh! It is degrading! It is not nice! It is nasty!”

text: “What is the matter?” said the man who had made Miss Pross scream; and he spoke in English, in a vexed and abrupt voice, though in a low tone.
source_text: “What is the matter?” said the man who had caused Miss Pross to scream; speaking in a vexed, abrupt voice (though in a low tone), and in English.

text: “Ah! mercy, indeed, ma’am,” was the answer. “I do but hope this will teach master never to keep any more of these dreadful creatures, who are born murderers and robbers from their very cradles. Poor Noah! he was all but killed, ma’am, when I came in.”
source_text: “Ah! mercy indeed, ma’am,” was the reply. “I only hope this’ll teach master not to have any more of these dreadful creatures, that are born to be murderers and robbers from their very cradle. Poor Noah! He was all but killed, ma’am, when I came in.”

text: “Well, I was whisperin’ that it’d be a good notion if you could manage to unearth a gentleman friend—for her” (he indicated her companion), “and then we might go off and have an ice-cream soda somewhere, or coffee, or anything of that sort.”
source_text: “Well, I was whisperin’ it’d be a good idea if you could dig up a gentleman friend—for her” (indicating her companion), “and then, we could go off an’ have ice-cream soda somewhere, or coffee, or anything.”

text: Mr. Britling presently resumed the conversation. “For me it came all in a breath, with neither doubt nor hope. I kept hoping to the very end that nothing would come near Hugh. And then it was as though a black shutter had dropped—in a moment....”
source_text: Mr. Britling presently went on with the talk. "For me it came all at once, without a doubt or a hope. I hoped until the last that nothing would touch Hugh. And then it was like a black shutter falling--in an instant...."

text: The walls of the yard here are likely of an earlier date—perhaps monastic. Consider, for instance, that grey patch in the corner. The barn itself is Georgian.
source_text: "The walls of the yard here are probably earlier: probably monastic. That grey patch in the corner, for example. The barn itself is Georgian."

text: “There is no harm in it,” said the young man with frankness. “I will make myself known—if you please—Mr. Towers Chandler. After our dinner, which I shall endeavour to render as agreeable as may be, I shall wish you good-evening, or see you safely to your door, as you may choose.”
source_text: “There is nothing wrong about it,” said the young man, candidly. “I’ll introduce myself—permit me—Mr. Towers Chandler. After our dinner, which I will try to make as pleasant as possible, I will bid you good-evening, or attend you safely to your door, whichever you prefer.”

text: “Mr. Yeobright is better; but Mrs. Yeobright and Mr. Wildeve are dead and cold. The doctor says they were quite gone before they were taken from the water.”
source_text: “Mr. Yeobright is better; but Mrs. Yeobright and Mr. Wildeve are dead and cold. The doctor says they were quite gone before they were out of the water.”

text: “Age, some forty years; stature, about five feet nine; hair black; complexion dark; on the whole, a rather handsome countenance; eyes dark, the face thin, long, and sallow; the nose aquiline, though not straight, but with a singular bias toward the left cheek; expression, therefore, sinister.”
source_text: “Age, about forty years; height, about five feet nine; black hair; complexion dark; generally, rather handsome visage; eyes dark, face thin, long, and sallow; nose aquiline, but not straight, having a peculiar inclination towards the left cheek; expression, therefore, sinister.”

text: “So she will, upon my life; I never once thought of that,” said Oak, and he paused. “Yet I must tell him to-night, I suppose, for he is at work so far off, and goes early.”
source_text: “So she will, upon my life; I never thought of that,” said Oak, pausing. “Yet I must tell him to-night, I suppose, for he’s working so far off, and leaves early.”

text: “Nothing, sir—but we may know it to-night. William Smallbury is gone to Casterbridge, where, as is supposed, her young man lives, and the other men be making inquiries everywhere.”
source_text: “Nothing, sir—but we may know to-night. William Smallbury is gone to Casterbridge, where her young man lives, as is supposed, and the other men be inquiring about everywhere.”

text: “What, a map? What do you mean by that? Do you not know what a map is? There, there, never mind, say no more, I dislike explanations; they only cloud a matter till one can make nothing of it. Off with you, dear; good-day; show her the way, Clarence.”
source_text: “What, a map? What are you talking about? Don't you know what a map is? There, there, never mind, don't explain, I hate explanations; they fog a thing up so that you can't tell anything about it. Run along, dear; good-day; show her the way, Clarence.”

text: “None upon earth. I love Elizabeth, and await our union with delight. Let the day, then, be appointed; and on that day I will devote myself, in life or in death, to the happiness of my cousin.”
source_text: “None on earth. I love Elizabeth and look forward to our union with delight. Let the day therefore be fixed; and on it I will consecrate myself, in life or death, to the happiness of my cousin.”

text: “Yes, yes; and I know well enough what that means. It grieves me, Clym. You are spending your life here to no purpose; and all for her sake alone. Had that woman not existed, you would never have thought of this scheme of teaching at all.”
source_text: “Yes, yes; and I know what that amounts to. It troubles me, Clym. You are wasting your life here; and it is solely on account of her. If it had not been for that woman you would never have entertained this teaching scheme at all.”

text: “Well, I heard that you had once been very fond of Eustacia—no more than that, though it was let fall by degrees. You ought not to be angry!”
source_text: “Well, I heard that you used to be very fond of Eustacia—nothing more than that, though dropped in a bit-by-bit way. You ought not to be angry!”

text:
Year that quiver’d and reel’d beneath me!
Your summer wind was warm enow, yet the air I drew did freeze me,
A heavy gloom fell through the sunshine and made darkness of me,
Must I change my conquering songs? said I within myself,
Must I indeed learn to intone the chill dirges of the baffled?
And moody hymns of overthrow?
source_text:
Year that trembled and reel’d beneath me!
Your summer wind was warm enough, yet the air I breathed froze me,
A thick gloom fell through the sunshine and darken’d me,
Must I change my triumphant songs? said I to myself,
Must I indeed learn to chant the cold dirges of the baffled?
And sullen hymns of defeat?

text:
Snuffing near his prey;
Yet he soothes their fears away
When he licks their hands,
And mute beside them stands.
source_text:
Smelling to his prey;
But their fears allay
When he licks their hands,
And silent by them stands.

text: “Mark me well, boy!” cried Sikes, setting his other hand upon Oliver’s throat; “if ever he but breathe a soft word, seize him fast! D’ye hear!”
source_text: “See here, boy!” said Sikes, putting his other hand to Oliver’s throat; “if he speaks ever so soft a word, hold him! D’ye mind!”

text:
While I was fearing it, it came,
Yet came with less of fear,
Because that fearing it so long
Had almost made it dear.
There is a fitting to dismay,
A fitting to despair.
'Tis harder knowing it is due,
Than knowing it is there.
The trying on of uttermost,
The morning it is new,
Is more terrible than wearing it
A whole existence through.
source_text:
While I was fearing it, it came,
But came with less of fear,
Because that fearing it so long
Had almost made it dear.
There is a fitting a dismay,
A fitting a despair.
'Tis harder knowing it is due,
Than knowing it is here.
The trying on the utmost,
The morning it is new,
Is terribler than wearing it
A whole existence through.

text:
Some one casts a mattress out, --
The children hasten by;
They wonder if It perished there, --
I used to, when a boy.
source_text:
Somebody flings a mattress out, --
The children hurry by;
They wonder if It died on that, --
I used to when a boy.

text:
They banish’d him across the sea.  
Yet ere the bud had deck’d the tree,  
Down either cheek the pearls did run,  
While I embrac’d my John Highlandman.  
Sing hey, &c.
source_text:
They banish’d him beyond the sea.
But ere the bud was on the tree,
Adown my cheeks the pearls ran,
Embracing my John Highlandman.
Sing hey, &c.

text:
I shall fashion the true poem of riches,  
To win for the body and the soul whate’er abides and passes onward  
and is not dropt by death;  
I shall pour forth egotism and reveal it under all, and I shall be the  
bard of personality,  
And I shall show of male and female that either is but the equal of  
the other,  
And sexual organs and acts! do ye centre in me, for I am resolv’d  
to tell you with brave, lucid voice, to prove you illustrious,  
And I shall show that there is no flaw in the present, and  
can be none in the future,  
And I shall show that whatsoever befalls any one may be turn’d to  
beauteous ends,  
And I shall show that naught can happen more beauteous than death,  
And I shall weave a thread through my poems that time and events are  
compact,  
And that all the things of the universe are perfect miracles, each  
as deep as any.
source_text:
I will make the true poem of riches,
To earn for the body and the mind whatever adheres and goes forward
and is not dropt by death;
I will effuse egotism and show it underlying all, and I will be the
bard of personality,
And I will show of male and female that either is but the equal of
the other,
And sexual organs and acts! do you concentrate in me, for I am determin’d
to tell you with courageous clear voice to prove you illustrious,
And I will show that there is no imperfection in the present, and
can be none in the future,
And I will show that whatever happens to anybody it may be turn’d to
beautiful results,
And I will show that nothing can happen more beautiful than death,
And I will thread a thread through my poems that time and events are
compact,
And that all the things of the universe are perfect miracles, each
as profound as any.

text:
O fare thee weel, my bonnie lass,
O fare thee well, my deary!
Thou art a merry, bonnie lass,
But thou hast a wakeful minnie.
source_text:
O fare thee weel, my bonie lass,
O fare thee well, my hinnie!
Thou art a gay an’ a bonnie lass,
But thou has a waukrife minnie.

text:
She sweeps with brooms of many colors,
And leaves the ravelings behind;
O housewife of the evening west,
Come back, and dust the pond!
source_text:
She sweeps with many-colored brooms,
And leaves the shreds behind;
Oh, housewife in the evening west,
Come back, and dust the pond!

text:
I mused upon the griefs of ruined man,
I mused upon the unrelenting wrath of Kings;
My enrapt soul abode on those that bind
The tangled volume of intermingling things,
When fell and wild disorder unto man grim sorrow brings.
source_text:
I pondered on the woes of lost mankind,
I pondered on the ceaseless rage of Kings;
My rapt soul dwelt upon the ties that bind
The mazy volume of commingling things,
When fell and wild misrule to man stern sorrow brings.

text:
That which thus sings, thus speaks,
When no one is here, --
Will the gown wherein I wept
Answer me to wear?
source_text:
That which sings so, speaks so,
When there's no one here, --
Will the frock I wept in
Answer me to wear?

text:
Now safe the stately sawmont sail,
And trouts beprick’d wi’ crimson hail,
And eels, weel-ken’d for supple tail,
And geds for greed,
Since, dark in Death’s fish-creel, we bewail
Tam Samson’s dead!
source_text:
Now safe the stately sawmont sail,
And trouts bedropp’d wi’ crimson hail,
And eels, weel-ken’d for souple tail,
And geds for greed,
Since, dark in Death’s fish-creel, we wail
Tam Samson’s dead!

text:
He goeth forth to chant the burial lay,
And bind the wandering sprite,
Whose shadowy, restless semblance haunts
The Abbey’s drear aisle this night.
source_text:
He goeth to sing the burial chaunt,
And to lay the wandering sprite,
Whose shadowy, restless form doth haunt,
The Abbey’s drear aisle this night.

text:
“’Twould do a martel man no harm to ken what’s hid beneath her bonnet,” said Billy Smallbury,  
just entered, with his one tooth borne before him.  
“She can spaik true language, and must ha’ sense somewhere. D’ye foller me?”
source_text: “’Twould do a martel man no harm to have what’s under her bonnet,” said Billy Smallbury, who had just entered, bearing his one tooth before him. “She can spaik real language, and must have some sense somewhere. Do ye foller me?”

text:
The bee is not afeared of me,  
I know the butterfly;  
The comely people of the wood  
Receive me cordially.
source_text:
The bee is not afraid of me,
I know the butterfly;
The pretty people in the woods
Receive me cordially.

text:
Of bronze and flame
The north, this night!
How meet its shapes,
How concerted with itself,
How far from any alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
Toward universe, or me,
It stains my simple spirit
With hues of majesty,
Till I assume more ample postures,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance in them.
source_text:
Of bronze and blaze
The north, to-night!
So adequate its forms,
So preconcerted with itself,
So distant to alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
To universe, or me,
It paints my simple spirit
With tints of majesty,
Till I take vaster attitudes,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance of them.

text:
You left for me the bounds of pain,
Vast as the heaving sea,
Set betwixt eternity and time,
Your mind, and mine, and me.
source_text:
You left me boundaries of pain
Capacious as the sea,
Between eternity and time,
Your consciousness and me.

text:
The lover may shimmer and shine,  
Drawing near to his bonnie, bright plaything:  
Yet wedlock will quickly make plain  
He has won but a drest-up naething.
source_text:
The lover may sparkle and glow,
Approaching his bonie bit gay thing:
But marriage will soon let him know
He’s gotten—a buskit up naething.

text:
Poor Man, the fly, doth oft hum past thy side,  
And oft, by chance, as he draweth thee nigh,  
Thy cursed old elbow itcheth up for joy  
And hellish pleasure!  
Already in thy fancy’s eye,  
Thy sicker treasure.
source_text:
Poor Man, the flie, aft bizzes by,
And aft, as chance he comes thee nigh,
Thy damn’d auld elbow yeuks wi’joy
And hellish pleasure!
Already in thy fancy’s eye,
Thy sicker treasure.

text:
His bill an auger seems,  
His head, a cap and frill.  
He laboreth at every tree, --  
A worm his dearest goal.
source_text:
His bill an auger is,
His head, a cap and frill.
He laboreth at every tree, --
A worm his utmost goal.

text:
Art thou he who wouldst take a station to teach, or to be a poet here in
the States?
The place is august, the terms obdurate.
source_text:
Are you he who would assume a place to teach or be a poet here in
the States?
The place is august, the terms obdurate.

text:
Is death’s bright eclat.  
O thou uncharted fame  
That not a beggar would accept,  
Could he but have the power to spurn!
source_text:
Is the eclat of death.
Oh, thou unknown renown
That not a beggar would accept,
Had he the power to spurn!

text:
“Yes, of a tolerable favour,” Sam replied. “Lord! all the country grants that it is one of the strangest things in the world,
that such a woman should have come to dwell up there.”
source_text: “Yes, tolerably well-favoured,” Sam replied. “Lord! all the country owns that ’tis one of the strangest things in the world that such a woman should have come to live up there.”

text:
For thou art mine, and I am thine,
Till that feared day of doom shall climb,
I am thine, and thou art mine—
Night is fled—I must depart.
source_text:
‘For thou art mine, and I am thine,
‘Till the dreaded judgement day,
I am thine, and thou art mine—
Night is past—I must away.’

text:
All draped and dim with subtle moss,  
All fretted through with weed,  
The little cage of "Currer Bell,"  
In quiet Haworth laid.
source_text:
All overgrown by cunning moss,
All interspersed with weed,
The little cage of 'Currer Bell,'
In quiet Haworth laid.

text:
The watchmen abandon every other region of me,  
They have forsaken me, defenceless before a crimson raider,  
All gather on the headland to behold and aid the assault on me.
source_text:
The sentries desert every other part of me,
They have left me helpless to a red marauder,
They all come to the headland to witness and assist against me.

text:
NOTES:
_104 Aeaea, Circe’s isle.—[SHELLEY’S NOTE.]
_112 The viper was the heraldic emblem of the Visconti,
despots of Milan.—[SHELLEY’S NOTE.]
source_text:
NOTES:
_104 Aeaea, the island of Circe.—[SHELLEY’S NOTE.]
_112 The viper was the armorial device of the Visconti,
tyrants of Milan.—[SHELLEY’S NOTE.]

text:
Throb! throb, ye drums!--blare! bugles, blare!
Above the traffic of cities--above the clamor of wheels in the streets;
Are couches prepared for sleepers by night in the houses? no sleepers
may lie in those couches,
No trader’s bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--should
they endure?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer essay to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to plead his cause before the judge?
Then clatter swifter, weightier drums--ye bugles, wilder, blow.
source_text:
Beat! beat! drums!--blow! bugles! blow!
Over the traffic of cities--over the rumble of wheels in the streets;
Are beds prepared for sleepers at night in the houses? no sleepers
must sleep in those beds,
No bargainers’ bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--would
they continue?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer attempt to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to state his case before the judge?
Then rattle quicker, heavier drums--you bugles wilder blow.

text:
Great Father, thou dost clearly know before  
That all I utter unto thee is sooth;  
I am a man most veracious evermore,  
And wholly unacquainted with untruth.  
At sunrise Phoebus came, but with no band  
Of Gods to bear him witness, in great wrath,  
Unto my dwelling, seeking there his heifers,  
And saying that I needs must show him where they are,
source_text:
‘Great Father, you know clearly beforehand
That all which I shall say to you is sooth;
I am a most veracious person, and
Totally unacquainted with untruth.
At sunrise Phoebus came, but with no band
Of Gods to bear him witness, in great wrath,
To my abode, seeking his heifers there,
And saying that I must show him where they are,

text:
It is not to dissolve thee that thou wert born of thy mother and
father, it is to know thee,
It is not that thou shouldst be wavering, but that thou shouldst be resolute,
Something long in making and shapeless is come and made in thee,
Thou art from hence secure, whatever cometh or departeth.
source_text:
It is not to diffuse you that you were born of your mother and
father, it is to identify you,
It is not that you should be undecided, but that you should be decided,
Something long preparing and formless is arrived and form’d in you,
You are henceforth secure, whatever comes or goes.

text:
A carol ending sixty-nine—a close—a reiteration,  
My lines in joy and hope still running on the selfsame line,  
Of thee, O God, Life, Nature, Freedom, Poetry;  
Of thee, my Land—thy rivers, prairies, States—thou dappled Flag I love,  
Thy total kept entire—of north, south, east, and west, thy  
all the items;  
Of me myself—the jocund heart yet beating in my breast,  
The body wreck’d, old, poor, and paralyzed—the strange inertia  
falling like a pall around me,  
The burning fires beneath my sluggish blood not yet extinct,  
The undiminish’d faith—the clustered groups of loving friends.
source_text:
A carol closing sixty-nine--a resume--a repetition,
My lines in joy and hope continuing on the same,
Of ye, O God, Life, Nature, Freedom, Poetry;
Of you, my Land--your rivers, prairies, States--you, mottled Flag I love,
Your aggregate retain’d entire--Of north, south, east and west, your
items all;
Of me myself--the jocund heart yet beating in my breast,
The body wreck’d, old, poor and paralyzed--the strange inertia
falling pall-like round me,
The burning fires down in my sluggish blood not yet extinct,
The undiminish’d faith--the groups of loving friends.

text:
He loves a marshy acre,
A ground too chill for corn.
Yet when a child, and barefoot,
I more than once, at morn,
source_text:
He likes a boggy acre,
A floor too cool for corn.
Yet when a child, and barefoot,
I more than once, at morn,

text:
Attend close, my dear scholars,
Doctrines, politics, and civilization spring from you,
Sculpture and monuments and any thing inscribed anywhere are reckoned in you,
The essence of histories and statistics, as far back as the records
reach, is in you this hour, and myths and tales the same,
If you were not breathing and walking here, where would they all be?
The most renown’d poems would be ashes, orations and plays would
be vacuities.
source_text:
List close my scholars dear,
Doctrines, politics and civilization exurge from you,
Sculpture and monuments and any thing inscribed anywhere are tallied in you,
The gist of histories and statistics as far back as the records
reach is in you this hour, and myths and tales the same,
If you were not breathing and walking here, where would they all be?
The most renown’d poems would be ashes, orations and plays would
be vacuums.

text: Parley with the specious chaos, and strike an understanding with the fair-seeming confusion of joy and pain.
source_text:
l. 195. Intrigue with the specious chaos, enter on an understanding
with the fair-looking confusion of joy and pain.

text:
By heedless chance I turn’d mine eyes,  
And, by the moonbeam, quiver’d to see  
A stern and stalwart ghaist arise,  
Attir’d as minstrels are wont to be.
source_text:
By heedless chance I turn’d mine eyes,
And, by the moonbeam, shook to see
A stern and stalwart ghaist arise,
Attir’d as Minstrels wont to be.

text: “Ah, fair my lord, pray thou him! Thou canst, if thou wilt. Ah, he suffereth so; and it is for me—for _me_! And how can I endure it? Would I might behold him die—a sweet, swift death; oh, my Hugo, I cannot bear this one!”
source_text: “Ah, fair my lord, do thou persuade him! Thou canst an thou wilt. Ah, he suffereth so; and it is for me--for _me_! And how can I bear it? I would I might see him die--a sweet, swift death; oh, my Hugo, I cannot bear this one!”

text:
“True, true,” said Joseph Poorgrass. “The Balls were ever a most excitable kind.  
I knew the boy’s grandsire—a man most nervously modest, aye, even to genteel refin’dness.  
It was blush, blush with him, almost as much as ’tis with me—  
not but what that, in me, ’tis a fault!”
source_text: “True, true,” said Joseph Poorgrass. “The Balls were always a very excitable family. I knowed the boy’s grandfather—a truly nervous and modest man, even to genteel refinery. ’Twas blush, blush with him, almost as much as ’tis with me—not but that ’tis a fault in me!”

text:
In his arms he upbore  
Her, armed with sorrow sore;  
Till before their path  
A couching lion lay.
source_text:
In his arms he bore
Her, armed with sorrow sore;
Till before their way
A couching lion lay.

text:
Open throat of my soul, pouring forth gladness,  
Eyes of my soul beholding perfection,  
Natural life within me, loyal in praise of things,  
Evermore attesting the triumph of things.
source_text:
Open mouth of my soul uttering gladness,
Eyes of my soul seeing perfection,
Natural life of me faithfully praising things,
Corroborating forever the triumph of things.

text:
They say that Time assuageth, --
Time never did assuage;
A very sorrow waxes strong,
As sinews do, with age.
source_text:
They say that 'time assuages,' --
Time never did assuage;
An actual suffering strengthens,
As sinews do, with age.

text:
But wi’ a Lord!—my shin doth bear,
A Lord—a Peer—an Earl’s own heir!
Yet higher still, my bonnet!
And sic a Lord!—lang Scots ells twa,
Our Peerage he o’erlooks them a’,
As I o’erlook my sonnet.
source_text:
But wi’ a Lord!—stand out my shin,
A Lord—a Peer—an Earl’s son!
Up higher yet, my bonnet
An’ sic a Lord!—lang Scoth ells twa,
Our Peerage he o’erlooks them a’,
As I look o’er my sonnet.

text:
ULYSSES:
Attend, then, what chastisement I devise
For this fell monster, and what sure escape
From your hard servitude.
source_text:
ULYSSES:
Listen then what a punishment I have
For this fell monster, how secure a flight
From your hard servitude.

text:
CHORUS OF SPIRITS AND HOURS:  
Then twist the web of the mystic measure;  
From the deeps of the sky and the borders of earth,  
Come, fleet Spirits of power and of pleasure,  
Swell the dance and the music of mirth,  
As the waves of a thousand streams sweep by  
To an ocean of splendour and harmony!
source_text:
CHORUS OF SPIRITS AND HOURS:
Then weave the web of the mystic measure;
From the depths of the sky and the ends of the earth,
Come, swift Spirits of might and of pleasure,
Fill the dance and the music of mirth,
As the waves of a thousand streams rush by
To an ocean of splendour and harmony!

text:
More popular poets robe the ideal in
familiar and sensible
imagery. Shelley loved to idealize the real—to endow the mechanism of
the material universe with a soul and a voice, and to bestow these also
upon the most delicate and abstract emotions and thoughts of the mind.
Sophocles was his great master in this kind of imagery.
source_text:
More popular poets clothe the ideal with familiar and sensible
imagery. Shelley loved to idealize the real—to gift the mechanism of
the material universe with a soul and a voice, and to bestow such also
on the most delicate and abstract emotions and thoughts of the mind.
Sophocles was his great master in this species of imagery.

text:
Dear friend, whoever thou art, receive this kiss,  
I bestow it especially on thee, forget me not,  
I feel as one who has finished the day’s labor and may retire awhile,  
I take once more my many translations, from my avataras  
ascending, while others doubtless still await me,  
An unknown sphere, more real than I had dream’d, more near, darts  
awakening rays about me, So long!  
Bear in mind my words, I may return again,  
I love thee, I depart from matter,  
I am as one unbodied, triumphant, dead.
source_text:
Dear friend whoever you are take this kiss,
I give it especially to you, do not forget me,
I feel like one who has done work for the day to retire awhile,
I receive now again of my many translations, from my avataras
ascending, while others doubtless await me,
An unknown sphere more real than I dream’d, more direct, darts
awakening rays about me, So long!
Remember my words, I may again return,
I love you, I depart from materials,
I am as one disembodied, triumphant, dead.

text:
LUCRETIA [IN A HURRIED AND CONFUSED MANNER]:
I think he sleeps;
Yet wake him not, I pray thee, grant me time awhile,
He is a wicked and a wrathful man;
Should he be roused from out his sleep to-night,
Which is, I know, a hell of angry dreams,
It were not good; indeed it were not good.
Wait till day break...
[ASIDE.]
Oh, I am deadly sick!
source_text:
LUCRETIA [IN A HURRIED AND CONFUSED MANNER]:
I think he sleeps;
Yet, wake him not, I pray, spare me awhile,
He is a wicked and a wrathful man;
Should he be roused out of his sleep to-night,
Which is, I know, a hell of angry dreams,
It were not well; indeed it were not well.
Wait till day break...
[ASIDE.]
Oh, I am deadly sick!

text:
As in a vision waking,
E’en while I chant, I watch it rise, and mark and foretell, without and within,
Its manifold array.
source_text:
As in a waking vision,
E’en while I chant I see it rise, I scan and prophesy outside and in,
Its manifold ensemble.

text:
An’ noo we’re pent in dens an’ hollows,
And chased, as was brave William Wallace,
Wi’ constables—thae blackguard fallows,
An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!
source_text:
An’ now we’re dern’d in dens and hollows,
And hunted, as was William Wallace,
Wi’ constables-thae blackguard fallows,
An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!

text:
For a’ his meal and a’ his maut,
For a’ his fresh beef and his saut,
For a’ his gowd and white sillerie,
And auld men shall never daunt me.
To daunt me, &c.
source_text:
For a’ his meal and a’ his maut,
For a’ his fresh beef and his saut,
For a’ his gold and white monie,
And auld men shall never daunton me.
To daunton me, &c.

text:
Eagle! why dost thou mount above that tomb?
To what high and star-pavèd heaven-home
Dost thou sail?—
I am the likeness of fleet Plato’s soul,
Ascending unto heaven; Athens holds
His body here below.
source_text:
Eagle! why soarest thou above that tomb?
To what sublime and star-ypaven home
Floatest thou?—
I am the image of swift Plato’s spirit,
Ascending heaven; Athens doth inherit
His corpse below.

text:
“I may not stay a minute, thank ye,”  
he answered to her greeting.  
“I am driving to East Egdon;  
but I came round here but to tell you the news.  
Perhaps you have heard—  
of Mr. Wildeve’s fortune?”
source_text: “I can’t stay a minute, thank ye,” he answered to her greeting. “I am driving to East Egdon; but I came round here just to tell you the news. Perhaps you have heard—about Mr. Wildeve’s fortune?”

text:
A day that ne’er shall fade away,
So far I scaled the upland way,
I din’d with one of noble rank.
source_text:
A ne’er-to-be-forgotten day,
Sae far I sprackl’d up the brae,
I dinner’d wi’ a Lord.

text:
Her small form at the gate  
The angels sure descried,  
For I could never seek her  
On the frail, mortal side.
source_text:
Her little figure at the gate
The angels must have spied,
Since I could never find her
Upon the mortal side.

text:
Spirit of Nature! here  
In this unending wilderness  
Of worlds, beneath whose folded vastness  
Even high-wing'd Fancy reels,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.  
Yet not the lightest leaf  
That trembles at the passing gale  
Is less alive with thee,—  
Yet not the humblest worm  
That hides in graves and feeds upon the dead  
Less partakes thy eternal breath.  
Spirit of Nature! thou  
Imperishable as this glorious scene,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.
source_text:
Spirit of Nature! here
In this interminable wilderness
Of worlds, at whose involved immensity
Even soaring fancy staggers,
Here is thy fitting temple.
Yet not the lightest leaf
That quivers to the passing breeze
Is less instinct with thee,—
Yet not the meanest worm.
That lurks in graves and fattens on the dead,
Less shares thy eternal breath.
Spirit of Nature! thou
Imperishable as this glorious scene,
Here is thy fitting temple.

text: “A man who’s doing well somewhere else wouldn’t stay here two or three weeks for nothing,” said Fairway. “He’s got some scheme in his head—depend upon that.”
source_text: “A man who is doing well elsewhere wouldn’t bide here two or three weeks for nothing,” said Fairway. “He’s got some project in ’s head—depend upon that.”

text: “Don’t be so cast down, shepherd!” said Mark Clark, glancing reproachfully at the candle, which seemed to hold some peculiar attraction for his gaze.
source_text: “Don’t take on so, shepherd!” said Mark Clark, looking reproachfully at the candle, which appeared to possess special features of interest for his eyes.

text: “Thank God!” said the old gentleman. “This is a great happiness to me, a very great happiness. But you have not told me where he is now, Miss Maylie. You must excuse my blaming you, but why did you not bring him?”
source_text: “Thank God!” said the old gentleman. “This is great happiness to me, great happiness. But you have not told me where he is now, Miss Maylie. You must pardon my finding fault with you,—but why not have brought him?”

text: “Extermination is sound doctrine, my wife,” said Defarge, with some uneasiness; “in the main, I have no objection to it. But this Doctor has suffered greatly; you saw him to-day; you noticed his face when the paper was read.”
source_text: “Extermination is good doctrine, my wife,” said Defarge, rather troubled; “in general, I say nothing against it. But this Doctor has suffered much; you have seen him to-day; you have observed his face when the paper was read.”

text: “Help!” the man cried again. “Ay, I’ll help you, you young rascal! What books are these? So you’ve been stealing them, have you? Hand them over.” As he spoke, the man snatched the volumes from his grasp and dealt him a blow upon the head.
source_text: “Help!” repeated the man. “Yes; I’ll help you, you young rascal! What books are these? You’ve been a stealing ’em, have you? Give ’em here.” With these words, the man tore the volumes from his grasp, and struck him on the head.

text:
He came in
While I was gone,
To borrow some tin
Was what brought him on,
And then he went out;
So I was inside
And he was without.
source_text: “He came in When I was out, To borrow some tin Was why he came in, And he went without; So I was in And he was out.”

text: “Yes, yes; that is all very well,” said Wildeve abruptly. “But we must take things as they are. Whatever blame may rest with me for having brought it about, Thomasin’s position is at present much worse than yours. I merely tell you that I am in a strait.”
source_text: “Yes, yes; that’s very well,” said Wildeve peremptorily. “But we must look at things as they are. Whatever blame may attach to me for having brought it about, Thomasin’s position is at present much worse than yours. I simply tell you that I am in a strait.”

text: “Pray do not speak of it, sir,” said Oak, in haste. “We cannot tell what may come to pass. So many changes may yet overtake ’ee. There’s many a slip, as they say—and I would advise you—I know you will pardon me this once—not to be _too sure_.”
source_text: “Pray don’t speak of it, sir,” said Oak, hastily. “We don’t know what may happen. So many upsets may befall ’ee. There’s many a slip, as they say—and I would advise you—I know you’ll pardon me this once—not to be _too sure_.”

text: “What then!” Sikes returned, with a frightful oath. “If he was kept alive until I got there, I’d crush his skull beneath the iron heel of my boot into as many grains as there are hairs upon his head.”
source_text: “What then!” replied Sikes; with a tremendous oath. “If he was left alive till I came, I’d grind his skull under the iron heel of my boot into as many grains as there are hairs upon his head.”

text: “I’ll lick you to-morrow,” he heard Cheese-Face promise; and he heard his own voice, thin and quavering with unshed tears, consenting to be there on the morrow.
source_text: “I’ll lick you to-morrow,” he heard Cheese-Face promise; and he heard his own voice, piping and trembling with unshed tears, agreeing to be there on the morrow.

text: “And he shall have it, too!” cried the man, giving him another blow and catching Oliver by the collar. “Come along, you young villain! Here, Bull’s-eye, look after him, boy! Look after him!”
source_text: “And he shall have it, too!” rejoined the man, administering another blow, and seizing Oliver by the collar. “Come on, you young villain! Here, Bull’s-eye, mind him, boy! Mind him!”

text: “What are you talking about, saying he has neither honour nor glory for!” cried Fagin, shooting an angry glance at his pupil. “Wasn’t he always the top-sawyer among you all? Is there one of you that could touch him, or come near him, on any scent! Eh?”
source_text: “What do you talk about his having neither honour nor glory for!” exclaimed Fagin, darting an angry look at his pupil. “Wasn’t he always the top-sawyer among you all! Is there one of you that could touch him or come near him on any scent! Eh?”

text: He had been there that evening. “I suppose you’ve heard the Egdon news, Eustacia?” he said, without lifting his eyes from the bottles. “The men have been talking of it at the Woman as though it were a matter of national moment.”
source_text: He had been there this evening. “I suppose you have heard the Egdon news, Eustacia?” he said, without looking up from the bottles. “The men have been talking about it at the Woman as if it were of national importance.”

text: “The other day they said you were trifling with him, and you came very near to proving that you were not; of late they have said that you are not, and you forthwith begin to show—”
source_text: “The other day they said you were trifling with him, and you almost proved that you were not; lately they have said that you be not, and you straightway begin to show—”

text: “Maybe he’s made of different stuff from what it takes to wear them,” said Gabriel grimly. “Well, that’s enough of this. Go on, Cainy—quick.”
source_text: “Perhaps he’s made of different stuff than to wear ’em,” said Gabriel, grimly. “Well, that’s enough of this. Go on, Cainy—quick.”

text: “Exactly. They hold every approach, and when the Church’s knights come up, there’s bound to be music. The lip of the precipice above the cave—”
source_text: “That's it. They command every approach, and when the Church's knights arrive, there's going to be music. The brow of the precipice over the cave--”

text: “O, Diggory! don’t you know me?” she said. “Why, of course you cannot, packed up like that. What do you mean? I have not been crying here, and I have not been here before.”
source_text: “O, Diggory! don’t you know me?” said she. “But of course you don’t, wrapped up like this. What do you mean? I have not been crying here, and I have not been here before.”

text: “Step in, lady,” said Jerry, gathering up his reins. The young woman climbed into the cab; the doors shut with a bang; Jerry’s whip snapped in the air; the crowd in the gutter drew aside, and the elegant hansom darted away ’crosstown.
source_text: “Step in, lady,” said Jerry, gathering his lines. The young woman stepped into the cab; the doors shut with a bang; Jerry’s whip cracked in the air; the crowd in the gutter scattered, and the fine hansom dashed away ’crosstown.

text: “It was quite a mistake,” Bathsheba went on, “for aunt to tell you I had a young man already. I have no sweetheart at all—and never have had one; and I thought that, as things go with women, it was such a pity to let you go away believing I had several.”
source_text: “—It was quite a mistake—aunt’s telling you I had a young man already,” Bathsheba went on. “I haven’t a sweetheart at all—and I never had one, and I thought that, as times go with women, it was _such_ a pity to send you away thinking that I had several.”

text: “Oh, is that so?” Soapy sneered, piling insult upon petty theft. “Well, why don’t you fetch a policeman? I took it—your umbrella! Why don’t you call a cop? There’s one standing on the corner.”
source_text: “Oh, is it?” sneered Soapy, adding insult to petit larceny. “Well, why don’t you call a policeman? I took it. Your umbrella! Why don’t you call a cop? There stands one on the corner.”

text: “I’m glad to hear it,” said Sikes, with a grim look at Oliver; “for the sake of his young carcass, which might else have had to pay for it. Come here, youngster, and let me give you a lecture, which is best gone through with at once.”
source_text: “I’m glad to hear it,” said Sikes, looking grimly at Oliver; “for the sake of his young carcase: as would otherways have suffered for it. Come here, young ’un; and let me read you a lectur’, which is as well got over at once.”

text: “I feel—almost too much—to think,” he said, with a grave simplicity. “I have come to speak to you without any preface. My life is not my own now that I have seen you plainly, Miss Everdene—I have come to ask you to become my wife.”
source_text: “I feel—almost too much—to think,” he said, with a solemn simplicity. “I have come to speak to you without preface. My life is not my own since I have beheld you clearly, Miss Everdene—I come to make you an offer of marriage.”

text: I thought," he said, "that if I was going to go to war, I had better do the thing properly. It seemed—rather half and half—not to be fit for the trenches.... I ought to have told you....
source_text: "I thought," he said, "that if I was to go to war I'd better do the thing properly. It seemed--sort of half and half--not to be eligible for the trenches.... I ought to have told you...."

text: “Somehow—I was rather overwhelmed by the thought of the men in that place. I was trying to picture how they must have felt it. From the moment when they understood they were going.
source_text: "Somehow--I was sort of overcome by the thought of the men in that dive. I was trying to imagine how they felt it. From the moment when they realised they were going.

text: Defarge climbed up on the box, and gave the order, “To the Barrier!” The postilion cracked his whip, and they rattled away beneath the faint, swaying lamps.

text: The girl had, indeed, an extraordinarily pretty smile, and there was something in her soft, bright brown eye—something like the quick flutter of a little bird. And she was like someone he knew! Yes, she was. She was quite ready to be addressed.
source_text: The girl really had an extraordinarily pretty smile, and there was something in her soft bright brown eye--like the movement of some quick little bird. And--she was like somebody he knew! Indeed she was. She was quite ready to be spoken to.

source_text: “A boy!” exclaimed Mr. Giles, valiantly, pushing the tinker into the background. “What’s the matter with the—eh?—Why—Brittles—look here—don’t you know?”

text: “Ay. It seems her old uncle’s things were not good enough for her. She has bought almost everything new. There are heavy chairs for the stout, weak and wiry ones for the slender; and great watches, growing well-nigh to the size of clocks, to stand upon the chimney-piece.”
source_text: “Ay. Seems her old uncle’s things were not good enough for her. She’ve bought all but everything new. There’s heavy chairs for the stout, weak and wiry ones for the slender; great watches, getting on to the size of clocks, to stand upon the chimbley-piece.”

text: “There—that will do,” she said, though she did not draw it back. “Still, I suppose you are thinking you would like to kiss it? You may, if you care to.”
source_text: “There—that’s long enough,” said she, though without pulling it away. “But I suppose you are thinking you would like to kiss it? You may if you want to.”

text: “In contrast with slaves, and with the Israelites in Egypt, and people of that sort!” A bitter tear slid down Eustacia’s cheek, though he did not see it. There had been a note of indifference in his voice, and it told her that he felt no unmixed sorrow over an end that to her was a sheer horror.
source_text: “In comparison with slaves, and the Israelites in Egypt, and such people!” A bitter tear rolled down Eustacia’s face, which he did not see. There had been nonchalance in his tone, showing her that he felt no absolute grief at a consummation which to her was a positive horror.

source_text: “But it’s a forty-dollar wheel, in good condition,” Martin had argued. “And you’ve only let me have seven dollars on it. No, not even seven. Six and a quarter; you took the interest in advance.”

text: “Yes, that will do. It quite befits a round-faced child like him,” said Bathsheba. She added the words in a small but readable hand; folded the sheet into an envelope, and dipped her pen to write the direction.
source_text: “Yes, that shall be it. It just suits itself to a chubby-faced child like him,” said Bathsheba. She inserted the words in a small though legible handwriting; enclosed the sheet in an envelope, and dipped her pen for the direction.

text: “There now, don’t be a little fool. Wait until you are told. Why, Bathsheba, you have lost all the spirit and sauciness you used to have, and upon my life, if I had known what a chicken-hearted creature you were beneath all your boldness, I’d never have—I know what.”
source_text: “There now, don’t you be a little fool. Wait till you are told. Why, Bathsheba, you have lost all the pluck and sauciness you formerly had, and upon my life if I had known what a chicken-hearted creature you were under all your boldness, I’d never have—I know what.”

source_text: “So far, miss (as you have remarked), this is the story of your regretted father. Now comes the difference. If your father had not died when he did--Don’t be frightened! How you start!”

text: “I consented to it,” Thomasin replied firmly. “I am a practical woman now. I believe in hearts not at all. I would marry him under any circumstances since—since Clym’s letter.”
source_text: “I agreed to it,” Thomasin answered firmly. “I am a practical woman now. I don’t believe in hearts at all. I would marry him under any circumstances since—since Clym’s letter.”

text: “If any one of these men, or all of them together, were inclined to spare him—which is a bold assumption, for what is his life, or any man’s, to them!—I question whether they would dare to spare him after the showing in the court.”

text: “It is life,” he said, “and life is not always beautiful. And yet, perhaps because I am oddly made, I find something beautiful in it. It seems to me that the beauty is made ten times greater because it is there—”

text: “It does seem long when one looks ahead. Do not place too much reliance on such promises, sir. Remember that you have already once been deceived. Her intention may be kindly; but then—she is still young.”
source_text: “It seems long in a forward view. Don’t build too much upon such promises, sir. Remember, you have once be’n deceived. Her meaning may be good; but there—she’s young yet.”

text: “I—I didn’t—I know I ought never to have imagined sending that valentine—forgive me, sir—it was a wanton act, such as no woman with any self-respect ought to have committed. If you will only forgive my thoughtlessness, I promise never to—”

source_text: Fairway had meanwhile concluded a critical gaze at Yeobright. “Now, you may not believe it,” he said to the rest of the room, “but I should never have knowed this gentleman if I had met him anywhere off his own he’th—he’s altered so much.”

text: He lighted his cigarette, said good-night, and went on. “Now wouldn’t that shake you?” he muttered under his breath. “That copper thought I was drunk.” He smiled to himself and pondered. “I guess I was,” he added; “but I didn’t suppose a woman’s face would do it.”
source_text: He lighted his cigarette, said good night, and went on. “Now wouldn’t that rattle you?” he ejaculated under his breath. “That copper thought I was drunk.” He smiled to himself and meditated. “I guess I was,” he added; “but I didn’t think a woman’s face’d do it.”

text: “Fine; it has given me everything I asked of it. You’ve changed a great deal, Jimmy. I never imagined you were two or three inches taller.”
source_text: “Bully; it has given me everything I asked it for. You’ve changed lots, Jimmy. I never thought you were so tall by two or three inches.”

text: “You haven’t a penny, Mart, and that’s why you’re walking. Exercise!” She tried to sniff with contempt, but managed only a little sniffle. “Here, let me see.”
source_text: “You haven’t a penny, Mart, and that’s why you’re walkin’. Exercise!” She tried to sniff contemptuously, but succeeded in producing only a sniffle. “Here, lemme see.”

text: “I beg you to note that I have come here of my own accord, in answer to that written appeal from a fellow countryman which lies before you. I ask for nothing more than the chance to do so without delay. Is not that my right?”
source_text: “I entreat you to observe that I have come here voluntarily, in response to that written appeal of a fellow-countryman which lies before you. I demand no more than the opportunity to do so without delay. Is not that my right?”

text: “Yes, I suppose so; but I feel like new wine in an old bottle here. It seems to me that sash windows ought to be fitted everywhere, and that these old wainscoted walls should be lightened a little; or else the oak should be taken away altogether, and the walls covered with paper.”
source_text: “Yes—I suppose so; but I feel like new wine in an old bottle here. My notion is that sash-windows should be put throughout, and these old wainscoted walls brightened up a bit; or the oak cleared quite away, and the walls papered.”

text: “Indeed, there is no such matter between us,” she said, turning aside. “I do not permit strangers to be forward and impertinent—even when they speak in my praise.”
source_text: “Indeed there’s no such case between us,” she said, turning away. “I don’t allow strangers to be bold and impudent—even in praise of me.”

text: “Now do not be offended by my asking all these questions; for I am only a dull man of business, and you are a woman of business.”

text: “Here—by the way of the plantation, and all the way down the hill,” said Farmer Oak, wearing an air of extreme knowledge concerning some matter in his thoughts, as he looked toward a far-off point in the direction he had named, and then turned back to meet the eyes of his companion.
source_text: “Here—going through the plantation, and all down the hill,” said Farmer Oak, with an aspect excessively knowing with regard to some matter in his mind, as he gazed at a remote point in the direction named, and then turned back to meet his colloquist’s eyes.
