source_text: “Oh no—dexterity. I merely gave point to your bosom where the caterpillar was, and instead of running you through checked the extension a thousandth of an inch short of your surface.”

text: Clym gazed across the heath. “I like Venn well enough,” he replied after a little pause. “He is a very honest man, and, withal, an astute one. He is clever too, as is shown by his having won your favour. But truly, Thomasin, he is not quite—”
source_text: Clym looked at the heath. “I like Venn well enough,” he answered at last. “He is a very honest and at the same time astute man. He is clever too, as is proved by his having got you to favour him. But really, Thomasin, he is not quite—”

text: “I cannot make it out,” he murmured. “Or perhaps it is the editors who cannot make it out. There is nothing amiss in that. They print worse every month. Everything they print is worse—almost everything, in any case.”
source_text: “I can’t understand,” he murmured. “Or maybe it’s the editors who can’t understand. There’s nothing wrong with that. They publish worse every month. Everything they publish is worse—nearly everything, anyway.”

text: “I know not how it may be with you ministers,” said he. “I was present at the funeral; I listened to the biographical discourse; I am aware that it afforded much satisfaction; I have pondered it through the night; and now what I would fain know is, what on earth there was in it about Uncle Capen.”
source_text: “I don't know about you ministers,” he said. “I attended the funeral; I heard the biographical discourse; I understand it gave great satisfaction; I have reflected on it over night; and now, what I want to know is, what on earth 'there was in it about Uncle Capen.”

text: “You will,” said Rose, after a pause, “take some money from me, which may perhaps enable you to live without dishonesty—at any rate until we meet again?”
source_text: “You will,” said Rose, after a pause, “take some money from me, which may enable you to live without dishonesty—at all events until we meet again?”

text: “You might have wished it, for it saddens me as it does you,” he said, with feeling and deference. “As for revealing—the word itself is impossible between us two.”
source_text: “You might have wished it, because it makes me as sad as you,” he said with emotion and deference. “As for revealing—the word is impossible between us two.”

text: “And you mean, ma’am, if I may make bold with the words, that a young woman would scarcely go to visit her young man without putting herself in trim,” said Jacob, casting his mind back upon former experience. “Ay, that is true—she would not, ma’am.”
source_text: “And you mean, ma’am, excusing my words, that a young woman would hardly go to see her young man without dressing up,” said Jacob, turning his mental vision upon past experiences. “That’s true—she would not, ma’am.”

text: “Glad to meet you, Prince,” said he, in good humour. “Ay, I should say you were incog. enough. I am obliged by your offer of assistance—but I do not quite see in what way your butting-in could serve the matter any. It is, as you may suppose, a rather private affair—but I thank you all the same.”
source_text: “Glad to meet you, Prince,” he said, good humouredly. “Yes, I’d say you were incog. all right. Thanks for your offer of assistance—but I don’t see where your butting-in would help things any. It’s a kind of private affair, you know—but thanks all the same.”

text: “Oh, oh, so that is his reply! Whence does he take his grand manner? And who, then, am I, that I should be so used? Must I go and entreat a man who has himself entreatied me?”
source_text: “Oh, oh, that’s his answer! Where does he get his airs? Who am I, then, to be treated like that? Shall I beg to a man who has begged to me?”

text: “I desire neither thanks, nor do I deserve any,” was the careless reply. “In the first place it was nothing to accomplish; and in the second, I know not why I did it. Mr. Darnay, suffer me to put a question to you.”
source_text: “I neither want any thanks, nor merit any,” was the careless rejoinder. “It was nothing to do, in the first place; and I don’t know why I did it, in the second. Mr. Darnay, let me ask you a question.”

text: “Yes; yet the grievous part of it is that, though Paul was admirable as a man in the Bible, he would scarcely have answered in real life.”
source_text: “Yes; but the worst of it is that though Paul was excellent as a man in the Bible he would hardly have done in real life.”

text: “Now, Liddy, you have been looking into my books without so much as telling me; and I said you were not to! How do you know that it would suit me? It would not suit me at all.”
source_text: “Now, Liddy, you’ve been looking into my books without telling me; and I said you were not to! How do you know it would suit me? It wouldn’t suit me at all.”

text: “What do I know!” said the postmaster, with a shrug of his shoulders; “there may be, or there may not be; there will be, perhaps. It is all one. What would you have?”
source_text: “What do I know!” said the postmaster, shrugging his shoulders; “there may be, or there will be. It is all the same. What would you have?”

text: “No. By some accident I am possessed of a power over one of the keepers here, and by virtue of that I stand before you. I come from her—your wife, dear Darnay.”
source_text: “No. I am accidentally possessed of a power over one of the keepers here, and in virtue of it I stand before you. I come from her--your wife, dear Darnay.”

text: Martin reflected for a moment, then said, “Maybe that will serve, though with me I suppose it is otherwise. I have never cared—much. If you can set it on, it is all right, most likely.”
source_text: Martin considered for a moment, then answered, “Perhaps that will do, but with me I guess it’s different. I never have cared—much. If you can put it on, it’s all right, most likely.”

text: “‘Because we love life overmuch, and hope and fear are fled, We offer brief thanksgiving To whatsoever gods there may be That no life endures forever; That dead men rise no more; That even the weariest river Must wind at last to some safe sea.’”
source_text: “‘From too much love of living, From hope and fear set free, We thank with brief thanksgiving Whatever gods may be That no life lives forever; That dead men rise up never; That even the weariest river Winds somewhere safe to sea.’”

text: “Yes. I am the cause of the party—I myself. Had it not been for me, there would never have been any. I cannot make it clearer—there is nothing further to be made clear. I wish I had never set eyes upon Weatherbury.”
source_text: “Yes. I am the reason of the party—I. If it had not been for me, there would never have been one. I can’t explain any more—there’s no more to be explained. I wish I had never seen Weatherbury.”

text: “No, the trenches have disappointed me. They present nothing but an irksome sort of domestic life. They are not to be compared with our quarters in the rear. There is no traffic to speak of. I have not discovered so much as one excuse for discharging my rifle. I do not think I shall ever discharge my rifle at an enemy—ever....
source_text: "No, the trenches have disappointed me. They are a scene of tiresome domesticity. They aren't a patch on our quarters in the rear. There isn't the traffic. I've not found a single excuse for firing my rifle. I don't believe I shall ever fire my rifle at an enemy--ever....

text: “Yes, yes,” said Grandfer Cantle, with mournful assurance. “Well, this is a poor night indeed for them that have prospered in their day; and if ever I were such a dab at the hautboy or tenor viol, I shouldn’t have the heart to be twanging tunes upon ’em now.”
source_text: “Yes, yes,” said Grandfer Cantle, with melancholy conviction. “Well, this is a bad night altogether for them that have done well in their time; and if I were ever such a dab at the hautboy or tenor viol, I shouldn’t have the heart to play tunes upon ’em now.”

text: “Some of our old nurses used to speak to me of a broken heart,” replied Oliver, rather as though he addressed himself than made answer to Noah. “I think I can well conceive what it must be to die of such a thing!”
source_text: “Of a broken heart, some of our old nurses told me,” replied Oliver: more as if he were talking to himself, than answering Noah. “I think I know what it must be to die of that!”

text: “Certainly, my dear, certainly,” replied the old gentleman. “Stay. There is a pitcher of water in the corner by the door. Bring it hither, and I will give you a basin in which to wash, my dear.”
source_text: “Certainly, my dear, certainly,” replied the old gentleman. “Stay. There’s a pitcher of water in the corner by the door. Bring it here; and I’ll give you a basin to wash in, my dear.”

text: “There—there is enough of that,” interposed Sikes, impatiently; and bending over his recumbent friend, he whispered a few words in his ear: at which Mr. Crackit laughed exceedingly, and honoured Oliver with a long stare of astonishment.
source_text: “There—there’s enough of that,” interposed Sikes, impatiently; and stooping over his recumbant friend, he whispered a few words in his ear: at which Mr. Crackit laughed immensely, and honoured Oliver with a long stare of astonishment.

text: “Very well; and if you have a mind to hear of them, I will acquaint you with my designs for the future. I shall place nine thousand pounds on permanent investment, retain one thousand as money in hand, and with the remaining thousand travel for a year or so.”
source_text: “Very well; and I will tell you of my plans for the future, if you care to hear them. I shall permanently invest nine thousand pounds, keep one thousand as ready money, and with the remaining thousand travel for a year or so.”

text: Thomasin gathered herself after her first astonishment and took the packet. “That is all, ma’am—I wish you good night,” he said, and was gone from her sight.
source_text: Thomasin recovered from her surprise, and took the packet. “That’s all, ma’am—I wish you good night,” he said, and vanished from her view.

text: “You’ll drive me to something desperate,” muttered the girl, laying both hands upon her breast, as though by sheer force to keep down some violent outbreak. “Let me go, will you—this minute—this instant.”
source_text: “You’ll drive me on the something desperate,” muttered the girl placing both hands upon her breast, as though to keep down by force some violent outbreak. “Let me go, will you,—this minute—this instant.”

text: “You are not likely to find them there,” said Venn, coming after him. “What did you go and do so wild a thing as that for? Here is the box. The dice cannot be far off.”
source_text: “You are not likely to find them there,” said Venn, following. “What did you do such a crazy thing as that for? Here’s the box. The dice can’t be far off.”

text: “She is a born nurse,” said Mr. Direck, and then, somewhat in the manner of a man speaking to a public meeting: “But this sort of thing calls forth all the good that there is in a woman.”
source_text: "She's a natural born nurse," said Mr. Direck, and then rather in the tone of one who addressed a public meeting: "But this sort of thing brings out all the good there is in a woman."

text: “No; not even by flash Toby Crackit,” replied Sikes. “He says he has had on sham whiskers, and a canary waistcoat, all the blessed time he has been hanging about down there, and it’s no manner of use.”
source_text: “No; not even by flash Toby Crackit,” replied Sikes. “He says he’s worn sham whiskers, and a canary waistcoat, the whole blessed time he’s been loitering down there, and it’s all of no use.”

text: “You see he knows me!” cried Nancy, appealing to those about her. “He cannot help himself. Do make him come home, good people, or he’ll kill his dear mother and father, and break my heart!”
source_text: “You see he knows me!” cried Nancy, appealing to the bystanders. “He can’t help himself. Make him come home, there’s good people, or he’ll kill his dear mother and father, and break my heart!”

text: “The child also,” observed Jacques Three, with a meditative relish in his utterance, “has golden hair and blue eyes. And we seldom have a child there. It is a pretty sight!”
source_text: “The child also,” observed Jacques Three, with a meditative enjoyment of his words, “has golden hair and blue eyes. And we seldom have a child there. It is a pretty sight!”

text:
Mourn thou, O Sun, vast fountain of the light!  
Mourn, Empress of the hushed and shadowy night!  
And ye, bright starnies, glittering in your flight,  
For Matthew mourn!  
Through all your orbs he’s borne his soul from sight,  
Ne’er to return.
source_text:
Mourn him, thou Sun, great source of light!
Mourn, Empress of the silent night!
And you, ye twinkling starnies bright,
My Matthew mourn!
For through your orbs he’s ta’en his flight,
Ne’er to return.

text:
Fairest blossom, mark the lily  
Flourishing beneath the sunny ray:  
Let the blast sweep o’er the valley,  
See it prostrate in the clay.
source_text:
Fairest flower, behold the lily
Blooming in the sunny ray:
Let the blast sweep o’er the valley,
See it prostrate in the clay.

text:
BEATRICE [AWAKING]:
I was but dreaming
That we were all in Paradise. Thou knowest
This cell doth seem a sort of Paradise
Since our father’s presence.
source_text:
BEATRICE [AWAKING]:
I was just dreaming
That we were all in Paradise. Thou knowest
This cell seems like a kind of Paradise
After our father’s presence.

text:
They were two cousins, near as twins to view,  
Save that from out the calendar of sins  
Nature had blotted their affection—torn  
Asunder only by their births forlorn.  
And so together they upgrew, like flowers  
That on one stem drink one sun, one rain of showers,  
That in their purple season either lull  
Or wake, the same hand gathering when full;  
The same fair clime shall wither them in death.  
This lovely day smiles upon all that breathe  
In love—and who loved ever as didst thou,  
Fiordispina? Hardly Cosimo, now  
Whose heart and brain are kindled through and through  
With such a vision as eclipses view  
Of that fair idol from whose form it came.  
He faints, dissolved in a great sea of flame;  
But thou art like a planet, throned above;  
But thou art very Love, that rules the move  
Of his enthralled soul: such passion must  
Find sin and sorrow, had not gentle August May  
Brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.
source_text:
They were two cousins, almost like to twins,
Except that from the catalogue of sins
Nature had rased their love—which could not be
But by dissevering their nativity.
And so they grew together like two flowers
Upon one stem, which the same beams and showers
Lull or awaken in their purple prime,
Which the same hand will gather—the same clime
Shake with decay. This fair day smiles to see
All those who love—and who e’er loved like thee,
Fiordispina? Scarcely Cosimo,
Within whose bosom and whose brain now glow
The ardours of a vision which obscure
The very idol of its portraiture.
He faints, dissolved into a sea of love;
But thou art as a planet sphered above;
But thou art Love itself—ruling the motion
Of his subjected spirit: such emotion
Must end in sin and sorrow, if sweet May
Had not brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.

text:
“Well, be damned if this be not the queerest beginning ever I knew,” said Mr. Fairway.  
“I meant not thee at all. There is another in the country, then!  
Why didst thou reveal thy misfortune, Christian?”
source_text: “Well, be damned if this isn’t the queerest start ever I know’d,” said Mr. Fairway. “I didn’t mean you at all. There’s another in the country, then! Why did ye reveal yer misfortune, Christian?”

text:
“O nay, father!” said Jacob. “Your turnip-hoeing was in the summer, and your malting in the winter of those selfsame years,  
and ye ought not to reckon both halves, father.”
source_text: “O no, father!” said Jacob. “Your turnip-hoeing were in the summer and your malting in the winter of the same years, and ye don’t ought to count-both halves, father.”

text:
“To-morrow morn I would that two or three of you should make
Inquiries in the villages around, if they have seen
Such a person as Fanny Robin. Do it softly;
There is no cause for alarm as yet. She must have gone
Whilst we were all at the fire.”
source_text: “To-morrow morning I wish two or three of you to make inquiries in the villages round if they have seen such a person as Fanny Robin. Do it quietly; there is no reason for alarm as yet. She must have left whilst we were all at the fire.”

text:
MAMMON:
Disinherited
My first-born son Chrysaor, for he
Went to the public gatherings, and would ever
Stand there babbling of trade, of public trust,
Of thrift, and pure unmixed coin,
And other matters, ultra-radical;
And would have settled my estate, named the Fool’s Paradise,
And sums in fairy-money, bonds, and bills,
Upon my trained daughter Banknotina,
And wed her to the gallows.
source_text:
MAMMON:
Disinherited
My eldest son Chrysaor, because he
Attended public meetings, and would always
Stand prating there of commerce, public faith,
Economy, and unadulterate coin,
And other topics, ultra-radical;
And have entailed my estate, called the Fool’s Paradise,
And funds in fairy-money, bonds, and bills,
Upon my accomplished daughter Banknotina,
And married her to the gallows.

text:
[Terminus]  
Enough, the Centenarian’s tale is spent,  
The two, the past and present, have exchanged,  
I myself, as linker, as chansonnier of a great future, now do speak.
source_text:
[Terminus]
Enough, the Centenarian’s story ends,
The two, the past and present, have interchanged,
I myself as connecter, as chansonnier of a great future, am now speaking.

text:
Come, winter, with thy wrathful howl,
And, raging, bow the naked tree;
Thy gloom shall calm my cheerless soul,
When nature, too, is sad like me!
And maun I still, &c.
source_text:
Come winter, with thine angry howl,
And raging, bend the naked tree;
Thy gloom will soothe my cheerless soul,
When nature all is sad like me!
And maun I still, &c.

text:

us round. It were hard to find a passage more perfumed.
source_text:
PAGE 110. ll. 41 seq. The dark, warm, sweet atmosphere seems to enfold

text:
Then grace them wi’ your kind regard,
And your poor supplicant shall still—
I had near said, for ever kneel,
But that’s a word I needna deal;
For prayin, I hae sma’ command o’t,
I’m baith dead-sweer, an’ sairly skant o’t;
But I’se repeat each poor man’s prayer,
That kens or hears about you, sir—
source_text:
Then patronize them wi’ your favor,
And your petitioner shall ever—
I had amaist said, ever pray,
But that’s a word I need na say;
For prayin, I hae little skill o’t,
I’m baith dead-sweer, an’ wretched ill o’t;
But I’se repeat each poor man’s pray’r,
That kens or hears about you, sir—

text:
Though feeble as the lama that springs on the mountains,
And gifted not with the swift-winged footfalls of air,
Yet, yet shall I drink from the purest of fountains,
Though a fiercer than tiger is lurking there.
Though, more dreadful than death, it doth scatter despair,
Though its shadow doth darken the day,
And the gloom of the deepest dismay
Diffuses the power of soul-chilling terror around,
And broods o'er the corpses that moulder on the ground.
source_text:
Though weak as the lama that bounds on the mountains,
And endued not with fast-fleeting footsteps of air,
Yet, yet will I draw from the purest of fountains,
Though a fiercer than tiger is there.
Though, more dreadful than death, it scatters despair,
Though its shadow eclipses the day,
And the darkness of deepest dismay
Spreads the influence of soul-chilling terror around,
And lowers on the corpses, that rot on the ground.

text:
Tread softly on this slender place!
The widest earth that springs
Is not so vast as that full breast
These emerald borders ring.
source_text:
Step lightly on this narrow spot!
The broadest land that grows
Is not so ample as the breast
These emerald seams enclose.

text: “Well, since that is not my fortune, it bears no meaning for me; yet I should fain behold the sport, if there be naught of black art in it, and if a man may look on without cost or falling into any perilous wrangle?”
source_text: “Well, as that’s not my fortune there’s no meaning in it to me. But I should like to see the fun, if there’s nothing of the black art in it, and if a man may look on without cost or getting into any dangerous wrangle?”

text:
For me, I swear by sun an’ moon,
An’ every star that burns aboon,
Ye’ve cost me twenty pair o’ shoon,
Just gaun to seek ye;
An’ every ither pair worn down,
Mair ta’en I’m wi’ ye.
source_text:
For me, I swear by sun an’ moon,
An’ ev’ry star that blinks aboon,
Ye’ve cost me twenty pair o’ shoon,
Just gaun to see you;
An’ ev’ry ither pair that’s done,
Mair taen I’m wi’ you.

text:
From Freedom’s shape divine,
From Nature’s inmost shrine,
Strip every impious idol, rend
Delusion veil by veil;
O’er Ruin, desolate,
O’er Falsehood’s fallen state,
Sit thou sublime, unawed; let the Destroyer pale!
And equal laws be thine,
And wingèd words let sail,
Freighted with truth e’en from the throne of God:
That wealth, surviving fate,
Be thine.—All hail!
source_text:
From Freedom’s form divine,
From Nature’s inmost shrine,
Strip every impious gawd, rend
Error veil by veil;
O’er Ruin desolate,
O’er Falsehood’s fallen state,
Sit thou sublime, unawed; be the Destroyer pale!
And equal laws be thine,
And winged words let sail,
Freighted with truth even from the throne of God:
That wealth, surviving fate,
Be thine.—All hail!

text: “Peace! Ye know not what ye ask. Shall I starve the one I love, to gain a tender death? I deem thou knewest me more nearly.”
source_text: “Peace! Ye wit not what ye ask. Shall I starve whom I love, to win a gentle death? I wend thou knewest me better.”
“I’ll go—I’ll go,” said Eustacia in an unusually quick tone for her; and she glanced eagerly towards the window whence the moth had flown; but nothing appeared there.

text:
O, bitter mockery of the gaudy bier,
While downward is the hapless Vital driven!
The cave-enshrined Beggar, with a conscience clear,
In rags expires, unnoted, and to Heaven.
source_text:
O, bitter mockery of the pompous bier,
While down the wretched Vital Part is driven!
The cave-lodged Beggar, with a conscience clear,
Expires in rags, unknown, and goes to Heaven.

text:
And so his Soul would not be glad,  
But sighed within him; like a doe  
That sighs within a cavern sad,  
It lay maimed and waning, day by day,  
Till all its vital life was gone.
source_text:
And so his Soul would not be gay,
But moaned within him; like a fawn
Moaning within a cave, it lay
Wounded and wasting, day by day,
Till all its life of life was gone.

text:
I call no Goddess forth to fire my strains,  
A feigned Muse may suit the bard who feigns:  
Friend of my life! my fervent spirit burns,  
And all the tribute of my heart returns,  
For boons bestowed, for goodness ever new,  
The gifts yet dearer, as they came from you.  
Thou orb of day! thou other, paler light!  
And all ye myriad sparkling stars of night!  
If aught that giver from my mind efface,  
If I that giver’s bounty e’er disgrace,  
Then roll to me along your wand’ring spheres,  
Only to number out a villain’s years!  
I lay my hand upon my swelling breast,  
And grateful would, but cannot speak the rest.
source_text:
I call no Goddess to inspire my strains,
A fabled Muse may suit a bard that feigns:
Friend of my life! my ardent spirit burns,
And all the tribute of my heart returns,
For boons accorded, goodness ever new,
The gifts still dearer, as the giver you.
Thou orb of day! thou other paler light!
And all ye many sparkling stars of night!
If aught that giver from my mind efface,
If I that giver’s bounty e’er disgrace,
Then roll to me along your wand’rig spheres,
Only to number out a villain’s years!
I lay my hand upon my swelling breast,
And grateful would, but cannot speak the rest.

text:
Come, be glad!—and lay thee down  
On the fresh grass newly mown,  
Where the Grasshopper doth sing  
Merrily—one joyous thing  
In a world of sorrowing!
source_text:
Come, be happy!—lie thee down
On the fresh grass newly mown,
Where the Grasshopper doth sing
Merrily—one joyous thing
In a world of sorrowing!

text:
If I should not be living
When the robins come,
Leave the one with scarlet cravat
A memorial crumb.
source_text:
If I shouldn't be alive
When the robins come,
Give the one in red cravat
A memorial crumb.

text:
Did the harebell unbind her girdle  
For the lover bee,  
Would the bee then hallow harebell  
As he did before?
source_text:
Did the harebell loose her girdle
To the lover bee,
Would the bee the harebell hallow
Much as formerly?

text:
And wherever her airy footfall fell,
Her flowing hair upon the grassy dell
Wiped out its faint trace, with a shadowy sweep,
Like a sunlit storm on the dark-green deep.
source_text:
And wherever her aery footstep trod,
Her trailing hair from the grassy sod
Erased its light vestige, with shadowy sweep,
Like a sunny storm o’er the dark green deep.

text:
To-day, with bowed head and downcast eyes, thou, too, Columbia,
Less for the mighty crown in grief laid low—less for the Emperor,
Thy true condolence breathest, sending forth o’er many a salt-sea mile,
Mourning a good old man—a faithful shepherd, patriot.
source_text:
To-day, with bending head and eyes, thou, too, Columbia,
Less for the mighty crown laid low in sorrow--less for the Emperor,
Thy true condolence breathest, sendest out o’er many a salt sea mile,
Mourning a good old man--a faithful shepherd, patriot.

text:
There lay Lorenzo, slain and laid within,
There in that forest ended his great love;
Ah! when a soul doth thus its freedom win,
It aches in loneness, ill at peace thereof
As the break-covert blood-hounds of such sin:
They dipped their swords in water, and did move
Their horses homeward, with convulsed spur,
Each wealthier by his being a murderer.
source_text:
There was Lorenzo slain and buried in,
There in that forest did his great love cease;
Ah! when a soul doth thus its freedom win,
It aches in loneliness--is ill at peace
As the break-covert blood-hounds of such sin:
They dipp'd their swords in the water, and did tease
Their horses homeward, with convulsed spur,
Each richer by his being a murderer.

text:
SEMICHORUS 2:
There the wanton nightingales
Are waking through the broadest noon.
When one in bliss or sorrow fails,
And through the windless ivy-boughs,
Sick with sweet love, droops faint and dies
Upon its mate’s music-panting breast;
Another from the swaying nest,
Watching the languid ending close
Of the last strain, then lifts on high
The wings of the feeble melody,
Till some new thrill of feeling bear
The song, and all the woods grow mute;
Then is heard through the shadowy air
The beat of wings, and mounting there
Like many a flute by waters set,
Sounds overflow the listener’s brain
So sweet, that joy is nearly pain.
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 2:
There the voluptuous nightingales,
Are awake through all the broad noonday.
When one with bliss or sadness fails,
And through the windless ivy-boughs,
Sick with sweet love, droops dying away
On its mate’s music-panting bosom;
Another from the swinging blossom,
Watching to catch the languid close
Of the last strain, then lifts on high
The wings of the weak melody,
Till some new strain of feeling bear
The song, and all the woods are mute;
When there is heard through the dim air
The rush of wings, and rising there
Like many a lake-surrounded flute,
Sounds overflow the listener’s brain
So sweet, that joy is almost pain.

text:
I honor Assyria, China, Teutonia, and the Hebrews,  
I take to me each doctrine, myth, god, and demigod,  
I discern that the ancient chronicles, bibles, genealogies, are true, without  
exception,  
I avow that all bygone days were what they needs must have been,  
And that they in no wise could have been better than they were,  
And that to-day is what it must be, and that America is,  
And that to-day and America in no wise could be better than they are.
source_text:
I respect Assyria, China, Teutonia, and the Hebrews,
I adopt each theory, myth, god, and demigod,
I see that the old accounts, bibles, genealogies, are true, without
exception,
I assert that all past days were what they must have been,
And that they could no-how have been better than they were,
And that to-day is what it must be, and that America is,
And that to-day and America could no-how be better than they are.

text:
On the fairest time of June
Thou mayst go, with sun or moon,
Or the seven stars to light thee,
Or the polar ray to right thee;
Yet thou never shalt behold
Little John, nor Robin bold;
Never one, of all the clan,
Thrumming on an empty can
Some old hunting ditty, while
He doth his green way beguile
To fair hostess Merriment,
Down beside the pasture Trent;
For he left the merry tale
Messenger for spicy ale.
source_text:
On the fairest time of June
You may go, with sun or moon,
Or the seven stars to light you,
Or the polar ray to right you;
But you never may behold
Little John, or Robin bold;
Never one, of all the clan,
Thrumming on an empty can
Some old hunting ditty, while
He doth his green way beguile
To fair hostess Merriment,
Down beside the pasture Trent;
For he left the merry tale
Messenger for spicy ale.

text:
dismal cirque
Of Druid stones, upon a lonely moor,
When the cold rain commences at the close of eve,
In sombre November, and their chancel vault,
The heaven itself is darkened all through night.
source_text:
dismal cirque
Of Druid stones, upon a forlorn moor,
When the chill rain begins at shut of eve,
In dull November, and their chancel vault,
The heaven itself, is blinded throughout night.

text:
Hark to me, ye reverend core,
As counsel for poor mortals
Who oft do pass through Wisdom’s door
For Folly’s foolish portals:
I, for their heedless, careless sake,
Would here propose defences—
Their droll misdeeds, their dark mistakes,
Their failings and mischance.
source_text:
Hear me, ye venerable core,
As counsel for poor mortals
That frequent pass douce Wisdom’s door
For glaikit Folly’s portals:
I, for their thoughtless, careless sakes,
Would here propone defences—
Their donsie tricks, their black mistakes,
Their failings and mischances.

text:
l. 259. Striving . . . itself.
Her doubt of her brothers is made known
through her endeavor not to give
her fears away to them.
source_text:
l. 259. Striving . . . itself. Her distrust of her brothers is shown
in her effort not to betray her fears to them.

text:
Do the feasting gluttons feast?
Do the full-fed sleepers sleep? have they barred and bolted doors?
Still be ours the meagre fare, and the blanket on the ground,
Pioneers! O pioneers!
source_text:
Do the feasters gluttonous feast?
Do the corpulent sleepers sleep? have they lock’d and bolted doors?
Still be ours the diet hard, and the blanket on the ground,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

text:
murmurs . . . sands. In this portrayal of the god’s  
speech is the very soul of the element  
he embodies.

ll. 171-2. murmurs . . . sands. In this description of the god's

text:
And aged Silenus, wavering a green staff  
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a band  
Came, merry, as in the olive thickets thick  
The cicadae come, with noonday dew made drunk:  
And Dryope and Faunus came apace,  
Baiting the God to sing them somewhat new;  
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,  
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.
source_text:
And old Silenus, shaking a green stick
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a crew
Came, blithe, as in the olive copses thick
Cicadae are, drunk with the noonday dew:
And Dryope and Faunus followed quick,
Teasing the God to sing them something new;
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.

text:
‘Let the loaded ordnance roll
Till the lifeless air takes soul
With the clatter of the wheels,
And the thunder of the steeds’ heels.
source_text:
‘Let the charged artillery drive
Till the dead air seems alive
With the clash of clanging wheels,
And the tramp of horses’ heels.

text:
Yet, though the moments mournful flee,
O let me deem we yet shall be;
That single beam of solace free
May still on thy Chloris fall, Love!
O wert thou, &c.
source_text:
But, dreary tho’ the moments fleet,
O let me think we yet shall meet;
That only ray of solace sweet,
Can on thy Chloris shine, Love!
O wert thou, &c.

text:
The quivering vapours of the hazy noontide,  
Which like a sea o’er the warm earth do glide,  
Wherein every sound, and odour, and beam,  
Move, as reeds in one single stream;
source_text:
The quivering vapours of dim noontide,
Which like a sea o’er the warm earth glide,
In which every sound, and odour, and beam,
Move, as reeds in a single stream;

text:
I shall make a song for these States, that no single State may, under any  
conditions whatsoever, be brought beneath another State,  
And I shall make a song that there be comity by day and by  
night among all the States, and betwixt any two of them,  
And I shall make a song for the President’s hearing, full of  
weapons with threatening points,  
And behind the weapons innumerable discontented faces;  
And a song make I of the One wrought out of all,  
The fang’d and glittering One whose head is over all,  
Resolute warlike One encompassing and above all,  
(Howe’er high the head of any other, that head is over all.)
source_text:
I will make a song for these States that no one State may under any
circumstances be subjected to another State,
And I will make a song that there shall be comity by day and by
night between all the States, and between any two of them,
And I will make a song for the ears of the President, full of
weapons with menacing points,
And behind the weapons countless dissatisfied faces;
And a song make I of the One form’d out of all,
The fang’d and glittering One whose head is over all,
Resolute warlike One including and over all,
(However high the head of any else that head is over all.)

text:
“An ye prattle here the livelong day, justice shall go astray.  
Think ye the culprits will bide within their father’s house?  
They are in flight, they wait not.  
See that a band of horse be sent upon their trail.”
source_text: “An ye prattle here all the day, justice will miscarry. Think ye the criminals will abide in their father's house? They are fleeing, they are not waiting. You should look to it that a party of horse be set upon their track.”

text:
The men, flung out in party-factions,
Then settle a’ in dark debauches.
Ae night they’re daft wi’ drink and whoring,
The neist day life is past enduring.
source_text:
The men cast out in party-matches,
Then sowther a’ in deep debauches.
Ae night they’re mad wi’ drink an’ whoring,
Niest day their life is past enduring.

text:
“The king call’d down his nobles all,
By one, by two, by three;
Earl Marshal, I’ll go shrive the queen,
And thou shalt wend with me.
source_text: “The king′ call’d down′ his no-bles all′, By one′, by two′, by three′; Earl Mar′-shal, I’ll′ go shrive′-the queen′, And thou′ shalt wend′ with me′.

text:
Many things I teach, to aid thee in becoming eleve of mine;  
Yet if blood like mine course not in thy veins,  
If thou be not in silence chosen by lovers, nor in silence choose lovers,  
Of what avail is it that thou seekest to become eleve of mine?
source_text:
Many things to absorb I teach to help you become eleve of mine;
Yet if blood like mine circle not in your veins,
If you be not silently selected by lovers and do not silently select lovers,
Of what use is it that you seek to become eleve of mine?

text:
While guileless neighbors
Converse of the "early dead,"
We, prone to circumlocution,
Observe that birds are fled!
source_text:
While simple-hearted neighbors
Chat of the 'early dead,'
We, prone to periphrasis,
Remark that birds have fled!

text:
“As near as I know, there is not anybody that would be asking very particular after you, if you _was_ disposed of; so I need not take this devil-and-all of trouble to explain matters to you, if it warn’t for your own good.
D’ye hear me?”
source_text: “As near as I know, there isn’t anybody as would be asking very partickler arter you, if you _was_ disposed of; so I needn’t take this devil-and-all of trouble to explain matters to you, if it warn’t for your own good. D’ye hear me?”

text:
‘Time passed—I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor turn of seasons made
Its mark, but thoughts and tears that nothing cheers:
And I grew, in the end, as one half-shade,
A smoke, a cloud the winds have worn and frayed,
Till it was thin as air; until, one eve,
A Nautilus upon the fountain strayed,
Unfurling his blue sail where Heaven’s breath did not leave,
Among the waves and whirling eddies driven.
source_text:
‘Time passed, I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor change of seasons made
Its note, but thoughts and unavailing tears:
And I became at last even as a shade,
A smoke, a cloud on which the winds have preyed,
Till it be thin as air; until, one even,
A Nautilus upon the fountain played,
Spreading his azure sail where breath of Heaven
Descended not, among the waves and whirlpools driven.

text:
l. 22. already . . . rung.  
He was dead to the world.  
Yet this slight sign  
should likewise prepare us  
for the poem's close.
source_text:
l. 22. already . . . rung. He was dead to the world. But this hint

text:
What dost thou in that stately hall?  
Away, Galloway, and seek  
Some close, foul, dungeon'd cave,  
The very likeness of thy cheek.
source_text:
What dost thou in that mansion fair?
Flit, Galloway, and find
Some narrow, dirty, dungeon cave,
The picture of thy mind.

text:
Thus—though uncreative—
A clear, intense apprehension
Of his mind’s labor had made alive
The things on which it wrought; I deem
It waked a kind of thought in sense.
source_text:
Thus—though unimaginative—
An apprehension clear, intense,
Of his mind’s work, had made alive
The things it wrought on; I believe
Wakening a sort of thought in sense.

text:
Just now I’ve ta’en the fit o’ rhyme,
My daftish noddle’s in full prime.
My fancy’s sprung up high and fine,
At sudden call;
Hae ye a little leisure-time
To hear the whole?
source_text:
Just now I’ve ta’en the fit o’ rhyme,
My barmie noddle’s working prime.
My fancy yerkit up sublime,
Wi’ hasty summon;
Hae ye a leisure-moment’s time
To hear what’s comin?

text: “I’ve dropped a ring,” he said apologetically as he climbed out. “It was my mother’s, and I should hate to lose it. I won’t keep you a minute—I saw where it went.”
source_text: “I’ve dropped a ring,” he apologised, as he climbed out. “It was my mother’s, and I’d hate to lose it. I won’t detain you a minute—I saw where it fell.”

text: “No; I told you all there was to tell about father yesterday,” she said. “I’m sure you can’t be at a loss for something to put in; why, father lived a hundred years—and more, too, for he was a hundred years and six days, you remember.”
source_text: “No; I told you everything there was about father yesterday,” she said. “I 'm sure you can't lack of things to put in; why, father lived a hundred years--and longer, too, for he was a hundred years and six days, you remember.”

text: “Perhaps she does see me,” Oliver whispered, clasping his hands together; “perhaps she has been sitting beside me. I can almost feel as though she had.”
source_text: “Perhaps she does see me,” whispered Oliver, folding his hands together; “perhaps she has sat by me. I almost feel as if she had.”

text: “I do—whenever I get the chance, which lately has not been often (my mother was a Parisienne)—and they have a proverb there, _Qui aime bien, châtie bien_—‘He chastens who loves well.’ Do you understand me?”
source_text: “I do—when I have an opportunity, which latterly has not been often (my mother was a Parisienne)—and there’s a proverb they have, _Qui aime bien, châtie bien_—‘He chastens who loves well.’ Do you understand me?”

text: “O, what is it? Mother, are you so ill—are you not dying?” he cried, bending to kiss her face. “It is I, your Clym. How have you come here? What can it all mean?”
source_text: “O, what is it! Mother, are you very ill—you are not dying?” he cried, pressing his lips to her face. “I am your Clym. How did you come here? What does it all mean?”

text: Quite unlike the aching satisfaction of the bruised and shattered Mr. Direck was the frame of mind of his uninjured host. He, too, could not sleep, but his wakefulness had in it no kind of exaltation. The day had been altogether too much for him; his head, to use an excellent American phrase, was “busy.”
source_text: Very different from the painful contentment of the bruised and broken Mr. Direck was the state of mind of his unwounded host. He too was sleepless, but sleepless without exaltation. The day had been too much for him altogether; his head, to borrow an admirable American expression, was "busy."

text: “No,” said Mr. Lorry, in answer to the House; “I think I have put the question to everyone here now, and no one can tell me where this gentleman is to be found.”
source_text: “No,” said Mr. Lorry, in reply to the House; “I have referred it, I think, to everybody now here, and no one can tell me where this gentleman is to be found.”

text: “Now what do you think, Mr. Cruncher,” said Miss Pross, whose agitation was so extreme that she could scarcely speak, or stand, or move, or even live: “what do you think of our not setting out from this courtyard? Since another carriage has already left from here to-day, it might excite suspicion.”
source_text: “Now what do you think, Mr. Cruncher,” said Miss Pross, whose agitation was so great that she could hardly speak, or stand, or move, or live: “what do you think of our not starting from this courtyard? Another carriage having already gone from here to-day, it might awaken suspicion.”

text: “Then I will answer.” She braced herself to say it. “I don’t think you were meant to write. Forgive me, dear. You force me to say it; and you know I know more about literature than you do.”
source_text: “Then I will answer.” She steeled herself to do it. “I don’t think you were made to write. Forgive me, dear. You compel me to say it; and you know I know more about literature than you do.”

text: “He had only just looked in, merely in passing—quite in his old manner, which I had thought he had lately given up. But the tower spouts used to splash upon the stones, and we are at a loss, for this was like the boiling of a pot.”
source_text: “Only just looked in in passing—quite in his old way, which I thought he had left off lately. But the tower spouts used to spatter on the stones, and we are puzzled, for this was like the boiling of a pot.”

text: “Why don’t you go away, Martin?” Gertrude had pleaded. “Go off and find work somewhere, and settle yourself down. Then, when all this has blown over, you can come back.”
source_text: “Why don’t you go away, Martin?” Gertrude had begged. “Go away and get a job somewhere and steady down. Afterwards, when this all blows over, you can come back.”

text: “Excuse me,” I said, breaking in, “but can you show me one of this kind? It is something new to me. I must examine it. I shall scour the town until I come upon one. Its habitat must be here on Broadway.”
source_text: “Excuse me,” I interrupted, “but can you produce one of this type? It is a new thing to me. I must study it. I will search the town over until I find one. Its habitat must be here on Broadway.”

text: “Indeed, sir? That was before my time here, sir. Before our people’s time here, sir. The George was in other hands then, sir.”
source_text: “Indeed, sir? That was before my time here, sir. Before our people’s time here, sir. The George was in other hands at that time, sir.”

text: “Well, well!” said the old lady, in a good-humoured way; “you get well as quickly as ever you can, dear, and it shall be hung up again. There! I promise you that! Now, let us talk of something else.”
source_text: “Well, well!” said the old lady, good-humouredly; “you get well as fast as ever you can, dear, and it shall be hung up again. There! I promise you that! Now, let us talk about something else.”

text: “Lucie and I have been there; though only in passing. We have seen enough of it to know that it abounds in interest; little more.”
source_text: “Lucie and I have been there; but only casually. We have seen enough of it, to know that it teems with interest; little more.”

text: “That is quite all right,” came the reassuring reply. “You needn’t be frightened of us. We’re only plain, homely people—Hello, there’s a letter for me.”
source_text: “That’s all right,” was the reassuring answer. “You mustn’t be frightened at us. We’re just homely people—Hello, there’s a letter for me.”

text: “If there were set before you a great heap of dolls, and you were bidden to tear them to pieces and strip them for your own profit, you would choose the richest and the gayest. Come now! Would you not?”
source_text: “If you were shown a great heap of dolls, and were set upon them to pluck them to pieces and despoil them for your own advantage, you would pick out the richest and gayest. Say! Would you not?”

text: “I am going to sit here just as long as I consider proper, ma’am,” returned Mr. Bumble; “and though I was _not_ snoring, I shall snore, gape, sneeze, laugh, or cry according as the humour takes me; that being my prerogative.”
source_text: “I am going to sit here, as long as I think proper, ma’am,” rejoined Mr. Bumble; “and although I was _not_ snoring, I shall snore, gape, sneeze, laugh, or cry, as the humour strikes me; such being my prerogative.”

text: “I confess it. Yet there are thoughts that cannot be thrust from the mind, and that one was among mine. Must I not have a voice in this matter, now that I am your wife and the sharer of your doom?”
source_text: “I own it. Yet there are thoughts which cannot be kept out of mind, and that one was mine. Must I not have a voice in the matter, now I am your wife and the sharer of your doom?”

text: “How swiftly a rash act may be done!” the girl answered. Her lips trembled, and tears pressed so thickly into her eyes that she could scarcely tell apples from fern as she went on searching with anxious diligence to conceal her weakness.
source_text: “How quickly a rash thing can be done!” replied the girl. Her lips were quivering, and tears so crowded themselves into her eyes that she could hardly distinguish apples from fern as she continued industriously searching to hide her weakness.

text: He considered it. It was strange to come upon Teddy in this frame of mind, tense, grave, and all business. He felt that in the past he had done Teddy an injustice; the young man was not so trifling as he had imagined him....
source_text: He reflected. It was odd to find Teddy in this mood, strung up and serious and businesslike. He felt that in the past he had done Teddy injustice; this young man wasn't as trivial as he had thought him....

text: “I don’t throw you off—indeed, how could I? I never had you.” In the noon-bright certainty that she had never loved him, she forgot for a moment the careless turn she had taken that February day.
source_text: “I don’t throw you off—indeed, how can I? I never had you.” In her noon-clear sense that she had never loved him she forgot for a moment her thoughtless angle on that day in February.

text: It was a painful parting, though it was not for long. Yet her father comforted her, and at last, gently freeing himself from her clasping arms, said, “Take her, Charles! She is yours!”
source_text: It was a hard parting, though it was not for long. But her father cheered her, and said at last, gently disengaging himself from her enfolding arms, “Take her, Charles! She is yours!”

text: “As I am sure the men were at the window,” replied Oliver, pointing, as he spoke, to the hedge that separated the cottage garden from the meadow. “The tall man sprang over there; and the Jew, after running a few paces to the right, slipped through that opening.”
source_text: “As I am that the men were at the window,” replied Oliver, pointing down, as he spoke, to the hedge which divided the cottage-garden from the meadow. “The tall man leaped over, just there; and the Jew, running a few paces to the right, crept through that gap.”

text: “I did not mean that,” said Rose, weeping; “I only wish you had gone from here, that you might once more have turned yourself to lofty and noble pursuits; to pursuits truly worthy of you.”
source_text: “I did not mean that,” said Rose, weeping; “I only wish you had left here, that you might have turned to high and noble pursuits again; to pursuits well worthy of you.”

text: The woman looked at Yeobright and saw that he was colourless, covered with a cold sweat. “Isn’t there meaning in it?” she said softly. “What do you think of her now?”
source_text: The woman looked at Yeobright, and beheld him colourless, in a cold sweat. “Isn’t there meaning in it?” she said stealthily. “What do you think of her now?”

text: “You know that your parents had no great estate, and that what they had was secured to your mother and to you. There has been no fresh discovery of money, or of any other property; but--”
source_text: “You know that your parents had no great possession, and that what they had was secured to your mother and to you. There has been no new discovery, of money, or of any other property; but--”

text: “That is true. Well! It is a desperate hope at the best, and not much the less desperate for having been put off till dark. I should like to know how you get on; though, mind you, I expect nothing! When are you likely to have seen those terrible powers, Doctor Manette?”
source_text: “That’s true. Well! It is a forlorn hope at the best, and not much the forlorner for being delayed till dark. I should like to know how you speed; though, mind! I expect nothing! When are you likely to have seen these dread powers, Doctor Manette?”

text: “I slipped in. No one knows I’m here. I wanted to see you. I came to tell you I have been very foolish. I came because I could no longer keep away, because my heart drove me to come, because—because I wanted to come.”
source_text: “I slipped in. Nobody knows I am here. I wanted to see you. I came to tell you I have been very foolish. I came because I could no longer stay away, because my heart compelled me to come, because—because I wanted to come.”

text: “Let this day be held in memory. Let us ponder the great unfolding of Nature’s powers that took place within the long span of our old neighbor’s life; but let us also consider the shining pageant that is now opening before him in a better world.”
source_text: “Let this day be remembered. Let us think of the vast display of Nature's forces which was made within the long period of our old neighbor's life; but let us also reflect upon the bright pageant that is now unrolling itself before him in a better world.”

text: “Well, I married you despite it, and I do not regret it. How cold you seem this afternoon! and yet I used to think there was never a warmer heart than yours.”
source_text: “Well, I married you in spite of it, and don’t regret doing so. How cold you seem this afternoon! and yet I used to think there never was a warmer heart than yours.”

text: “Nobody. When I found that you were not at home, I thought some cruel thing had befallen you. I believe I heard his voice late last night; and so, being aware that something was amiss—”
source_text: “Nobody. I thought, when I found you were not at home, that something cruel had happened. I fancy I heard his voice late last night; and so, knowing something was wrong—”

text: Even if the bank should open again, Mr. Britling knew that his present balance was small; at the very most it came to twenty or thirty pounds. He had been looking for cheques from his English and American publishers, together with the customary _Times_ cheque. What if these remittances were intercepted!
source_text: Even if the bank did reopen Mr. Britling was aware that his current balance was low; at the utmost it amounted to twenty or thirty pounds. He had been expecting cheques from his English and American publishers, and the usual _Times_ cheque. Suppose these payments were intercepted!

text: “I know you’re hungry, Mart. It’s plain enough all over you. Come in to meals any time you want. I’ll send one of the children to let you know when Mr. Higginbotham ain’t going to be there. And Mart—”
source_text: “I know you’re hungry, Mart. It’s sticking out all over you. Come in to meals any time. I’ll send one of the children to tell you when Mr. Higginbotham ain’t to be there. An’ Mart—”

text: “I little supposed,” said Miss Pross, “that I should ever have need to make sense of your foolish language; yet I would give all I possess, save the clothes upon me, to know whether you suspect the truth, or any part of it.”
source_text: “I little thought,” said Miss Pross, “that I should ever want to understand your nonsensical language; but I would give all I have, except the clothes I wear, to know whether you suspect the truth, or any part of it.”

text: “I went in. ‘Pray pardon this intrusion,’ said I; ‘I am a traveller in need of a little rest, and you would greatly oblige me if you would permit me to remain a few minutes by the fire.’”
source_text: “I entered. ‘Pardon this intrusion,’ said I; ‘I am a traveller in want of a little rest; you would greatly oblige me if you would allow me to remain a few minutes before the fire.’

text: “Then that was a beautiful truth,” said Maria; “that father and Shakspeare would very likely be made quite different, up in Heaven; I always used to say father was not appreciated here.”
source_text: “Then that was a beautiful truth,” said Maria: “that father and Shakspeare would like enough be changed right round, in Heaven; I always said father wasn't appreciated here.”

text: “But I fear,” he said at last, “that the interest of my subject has led me to trespass on your patience; and with a word or two more I shall conclude.
source_text: “But I fear,” he said at last, “that the interest of my subject has made me transgress upon your patience; and with a word or two more I will close.

text: “I’ll give her ten minutes, and then I’m off,” he muttered; then, aloud to the Prince, he added: “I’ll join you in confounding all clocks, my friend, and women as well.”
source_text: “I’ll give her the ten minutes and then I’m off,” he muttered, and then aloud to the Prince: “I’ll join you in confounding all clocks, my friend, and throw in women, too.”

text: “I have twenty-one pounds more with me,” he said. “Two notes and a sovereign. But before I go I must have a paper signed—”
source_text: “I have twenty-one pounds more with me,” he said. “Two notes and a sovereign. But before I leave you I must have a paper signed—”

text: “He wouldn’t so much as give a bark in a witness-box, for fear of pledging himself; no, not if you lashed him into one, and kept him there without a morsel for a fortnight,” said the Dodger.
source_text: “He wouldn’t so much as bark in a witness-box, for fear of committing himself; no, not if you tied him up in one, and left him there without wittles for a fortnight,” said the Dodger.

text: It was a strange tale, yet it did not disturb my faith; and I answered with earnestness, “You are all mistaken; I know the murderer. Justine—poor, good Justine—is innocent.”
source_text: This was a strange tale, but it did not shake my faith; and I replied earnestly, “You are all mistaken; I know the murderer. Justine, poor, good Justine, is innocent.”

text: “But why,” said the young man, “why take the risk of what so nearly came to pass? If Rose had—I cannot speak that word now—if this illness had ended otherwise, how could you ever have forgiven yourself? How could I ever have known happiness again!”
source_text: “But why,” said the young man, “why run the chance of that occurring which so nearly happened? If Rose had—I cannot utter that word now—if this illness had terminated differently, how could you ever have forgiven yourself! How could I ever have know happiness again!”

text: “She was brought here last night,” replied the old woman, “by the overseer’s orders. She was found lying in the street. She had walked some distance, for her shoes were worn to shreds; but from what place she came, or to what place she was going, nobody knows.”
source_text: “She was brought here last night,” replied the old woman, “by the overseer’s order. She was found lying in the street. She had walked some distance, for her shoes were worn to pieces; but where she came from, or where she was going to, nobody knows.”

text: “I speak for myself,” replied Mr. Lorry, “and for Mr. Darnay, and for Miss Lucie—and, Miss Lucie, do you not think I may speak for us all?” He put the question to her with emphasis, and with a look toward her father.
source_text: “I speak for myself,” answered Mr. Lorry, “and for Mr. Darnay, and for Miss Lucie, and--Miss Lucie, do you not think I may speak for us all?” He asked her the question pointedly, and with a glance at her father.

text: “Don’t trouble about me, my dear,” said the old lady; “I’m only having a proper cry. There now, it’s all done with, and I feel quite comfortable.”
source_text: “Never mind me, my dear,” said the old lady; “I’m only having a regular good cry. There; it’s all over now; and I’m quite comfortable.”

text: “No. I had been at Budmouth until two days ago, and that was the place I had meant to take her; but when I came to fetch her, we resolved on Anglebury, forgetting that a fresh license would be needed. There was no time afterward to go on to Budmouth.”
source_text: “No. I had been at Budmouth—till two days ago—and that was where I had intended to take her; but when I came to fetch her we decided upon Anglebury, forgetting that a new license would be necessary. There was not time to get to Budmouth afterwards.”

text: “Yet I think that if you were to hear my plan, you might take some interest in it. There is no point in hating people—if you are to hate anything, you should hate what made them.”
source_text: “Still I think that if you were to hear my scheme you might take an interest in it. There is no use in hating people—if you hate anything, you should hate what produced them.”

text: “But your Junkers, your Imperial court, your foolish, vicious princes—what were such visions to them?... With a spiteful pleasure they cast all that Germany had achieved into the flames of war....”
source_text: _But your Junkers, your Imperial court, your foolish vicious Princes; what were such dreams to them?... With an envious satisfaction they hurled all the accomplishment of Germany into the fires of war...._

text: “My husband, it is indeed so. I fear he cannot now be brought back; there is hardly a hope that anything in his nature or his fortunes can be mended at this point. And yet I am certain he is capable of good things, of tender things, even of magnanimous things.”
source_text: “My husband, it is so. I fear he is not to be reclaimed; there is scarcely a hope that anything in his character or fortunes is reparable now. But, I am sure that he is capable of good things, gentle things, even magnanimous things.”

text: “And wot,” said Sikes, scowling fiercely at his agreeable friend, “wot makes you put yourself to so much trouble over one chalk-faced kid, when you know there are fifty boys snoozing about Common Garden every night, from among which you might pick and choose?”
source_text: “And wot,” said Sikes, scowling fiercely on his agreeable friend, “wot makes you take so much pains about one chalk-faced kid, when you know there are fifty boys snoozing about Common Garden every night, as you might pick and choose from?”

text: “Still, if you do not wish to be seen, draw your veil down; though in this light there is little danger of anyone recognizing you. There are many strangers here.”
source_text: “Still, if you don’t like to be seen, pull down your veil; though there is not much risk of being known by this light. Lots of strangers are here.”

text: “I shall make mention of that,” said Mr. Holt. “I trust you have something more, for I am afraid of running short of material; you see, I am a stranger here.”
source_text: “I will allude to that,” said Mr. Holt. “I hope you have something else, for I am afraid of running short of material: you see I am a stranger here.”

text: “Do I think so? If that girl had a proper chance to dress, Mr. Eden, and if she were taught how to comport herself, you would be quite dazzled by her, and all men would be as well.”
source_text: “Do I think so? If that girl had proper opportunity to dress, Mr. Eden, and if she were taught how to carry herself, you would be fairly dazzled by her, and so would all men.”

text: “And the other fortnight—what of that?” demanded Sikes. “What of the other fortnight you’ve kept me here, lying like a sick rat in its hole?”
source_text: “And what about the other fortnight?” demanded Sikes. “What about the other fortnight that you’ve left me lying here, like a sick rat in his hole?”

text: “Come here, come here!” was then spoken rapidly in anxious female tones, and Clym and the doctor could be heard hurrying forward from the rear of the shed to the place where Mrs. Yeobright lay.
source_text: “Come here, come here!” was then rapidly said in anxious female tones, and Clym and the doctor could be heard rushing forward from the back part of the shed to where Mrs. Yeobright lay.

text: “Give me your arm, my love. Come away from here with an appearance of cheerfulness and courage, for his sake. That was well done;” they had left the place; “it shall not be in vain. Charles is summoned for to-morrow.”
source_text: “Give me your arm, my love. Pass from here with an air of cheerfulness and courage, for his sake. That was well done;” they had left the spot; “it shall not be in vain. Charles is summoned for to-morrow.”

text: “It’s a damned hard business, I know,” the other went on, “but I sort of hate to see you fall off the wagon, Mart. Well, here goes!”
source_text: “It’s fair hell, I know,” the other went on, “but I kind of hate to see you come off the wagon, Mart. Well, here’s how!”

text: “Never will I desert my country—while she is at war,” said Cissie, tempering her first hot resolve, and then added, as though she were sorry for the concession, “anyhow.”
source_text: "Never will I desert my country--while she is at war," said Cissie, reducing her first fierce intention, and adding as though she regretted her concession, "Anyhow."

text: “Dreams, dreams! If there had been any system still left to devise, the universities would have discovered it long before now.”
source_text: “Dreams, dreams! If there had been any system left to be invented they would have found it out at the universities long before this time.”

text: “Yes,” continued Joseph Poorgrass—his shyness, which was so grievous a defect, now filling him with a quiet complacency since it had come to be treated as an engaging study. “It was blush, blush, blush with me every minute of the time, whenever she was speaking to me.”
source_text: “Yes,” continued Joseph Poorgrass—his shyness, which was so painful as a defect, filling him with a mild complacency now that it was regarded as an interesting study. “’Twere blush, blush, blush with me every minute of the time, when she was speaking to me.”

text: “Now, I should suppose, Cap’n, that Miss Eustacia had about as much in her head from books as anybody round here?”
source_text: “Now, I should think, Cap’n, that Miss Eustacia had about as much in her head that comes from books as anybody about here?”

text: “Huh? Seeking a bed?” Joe thrust a hand into his hip pocket and drew it forth full of small coin. “That’s better than honest labor,” he cried. “You only seemed a likely sort; that is the reason I laid into you.”
source_text: “Huh? Lookin’ for a bed?” Joe shot a hand into his hip pocket and brought it out filled with small change. “That beats hard graft,” he exulted. “You just looked good; that’s why I battered you.”

text: “I am ever ready to bestow praise where praise is due, and the fellow has earned it—that ’a do,” he observed, regarding the worthy thief as though he were the masterpiece of some artist of world-wide fame.
source_text: “I like to give praise where praise is due, and the man deserves it—that ’a do so,” he remarked, looking at the worthy thief, as if he were the masterpiece of some world-renowned artist.

text: “If I did not take my seat, the man would perceive that we were but pretending to be equals—and, what is more, playing our part in the deception very ill indeed.”
source_text: “If I did not sit, the man would perceive that we were only pretending to be equals--and playing the deception pretty poorly, too.”

text: “I have come,” said the man, and the man was Wildeve. “You grant me no peace. Why will you not leave me in tranquillity? All the evening have I been beholding your bonfire.” The words were not uttered without feeling, yet they kept their even cadence, as though sustained by a wary balance between extremes close at hand.
source_text: “I have come,” said the man, who was Wildeve. “You give me no peace. Why do you not leave me alone? I have seen your bonfire all the evening.” The words were not without emotion, and retained their level tone as if by a careful equipoise between imminent extremes.

text: You may be the bravest people on earth," said Mr. Direck, "but if you have no arms and the other fellows have them—you are just as though you were sheep.
source_text: "You may be the bravest people on earth," said Mr. Direck, "but if you haven't got arms and the other chaps have--you're just as if you were sheep."

text: “I must write a letter to the old father and mother,” Mr. Britling thought. “I cannot merely send the poor little fiddle—without a word. In all this piteous tempest of witless hatred—surely there may be one greeting—not hateful.”
source_text: "I must write a letter to the old father and mother," Mr. Britling thought. "I can't just send the poor little fiddle--without a word. In all this pitiful storm of witless hate--surely there may be one greeting--not hateful.

text: “There ain’t,” said Sikes, fixing his eyes upon her, and muttering the words under his breath; “there ain’t a stauncher-hearted gal alive, or I’d have cut her throat three months ago. The fever’s coming on her; that’s it.”
source_text: “There ain’t,” said Sikes, fixing his eyes upon her, and muttering the words to himself; “there ain’t a stauncher-hearted gal going, or I’d have cut her throat three months ago. She’s got the fever coming on; that’s it.”

text: “You never take me with you when you go there a-walking. Why then do you wander so much in it yourself, if it is so much against your liking?”
source_text: “You never take me with you when you walk there. Why do you wander so much in it yourself if you so dislike it?”

text: “Partly, I suppose, by paying me to aid you. But it is scarcely worth your while to apostrophise me, or the air, upon the matter; what you mean to do, that you do. You were ever in the foremost rank, and I was always left behind.”
source_text: “Partly through paying me to help you, I suppose. But it’s not worth your while to apostrophise me, or the air, about it; what you want to do, you do. You were always in the front rank, and I was always behind.”

text: “Oh,—and there is yet another particular. In those days, a master may engage a man for but a single day, or for one week, or for one month at a time, should he so desire.”
source_text: “Oh,--and there's another detail. In that day, a master may hire a man for only just one day, or one week, or one month at a time, if he wants to.”

text: “That is but another manner of saying that I am set upon the footing I have already indicated. I thank you, Darnay. May I make use of that freedom with your name?”
source_text: “That is another way of saying that I am placed on the footing I have indicated. I thank you, Darnay. I may use that freedom with your name?”

text: “Oh, thank ’ee,” said Gabriel, going after her to the hearth. “I’ve brought a lamb for Miss Everdene. I thought she might like to rear one; girls do.”
source_text: “Oh, thank ’ee,” said Gabriel, following her to the fireplace. “I’ve brought a lamb for Miss Everdene. I thought she might like one to rear; girls do.”

text: “You may rely upon me, young man,” replied Mr. Bumble, inclining himself by degrees toward the ladder, with extreme courtesy. “On everybody’s behalf, young man; and on my own, you understand, Mr. Monks.”
source_text: “You may depend upon me, young man,” answered Mr. Bumble, bowing himself gradually towards the ladder, with excessive politeness. “On everybody’s account, young man; on my own, you know, Mr. Monks.”

text: “I hope,” stammered Mr. Limbkins, “I hope the magistrates will not entertain the opinion that the authorities have been guilty of any improper conduct upon the unsupported testimony of a child.”
source_text: “I hope,” stammered Mr. Limbkins: “I hope the magistrates will not form the opinion that the authorities have been guilty of any improper conduct, on the unsupported testimony of a child.”

text: “We may,” added Mark Clark, with emphasis, and at once drank himself, so as to run no further hazard of forfeiting his chance by the event in question, while Jan, meantime, was melding his further thoughts of to-morrow into a song:—
source_text: “We may,” added Mark Clark, emphatically, at once drinking himself, to run no further risk of losing his chance by the event alluded to, Jan meanwhile merging his additional thoughts of to-morrow in a song:—

text: She had not been there long when a man came up the road and, after some hesitation, said to her, “How is he this evening, ma’am, if you please?”
source_text: She had not long been there when a man came up the road and with some hesitation said to her, “How is he tonight, ma’am, if you please?”

text: “Here,” said the turnkey, laying his hand upon his breast, to keep him down. “Here is somebody that wants to see you, I suppose, and ask you a few questions. Fagin, Fagin! Are you a man?”
source_text: “Here,” said the turnkey, laying his hand upon his breast to keep him down. “Here’s somebody wants to see you, to ask you some questions, I suppose. Fagin, Fagin! Are you a man?”

text: “And must you go? Ah! there was a time, Frank, when it would have needed many promises to other people to tear you from my side. You were wont to call me darling then. But now it matters little to you how my days are spent.”
source_text: “And must you go? Ah! there was a time, Frank, when it would have taken a good many promises to other people to drag you away from me. You used to call me darling, then. But it doesn’t matter to you how my days are passed now.”

text: “No, no; not in the least, I assure you,” the other protested. “Your request does not fall precisely within the province of the reference department, but I shall be only too happy to assist you.”
source_text: “No, no; not at all, I assure you,” the other protested. “Your request is not exactly in the scope of the reference department, but I shall be only too pleased to assist you.”

text: “They say, those vainglorious English, that it is the Refuge of many. Do you know of any compatriot who has found a Refuge there? A Doctor?”
source_text: “They say, those boastful English, that it is the Refuge of many. You know a compatriot who has found a Refuge there? A Doctor?”

text: “If you will go back again I shall—be something,” she said tenderly, laying her head close to his breast. “If you will consent, I will give you my promise, and not leave you waiting a single minute longer.”
source_text: “If you’ll go back again I’ll—be something,” she said tenderly, putting her head near his breast. “If you’ll agree I’ll give my promise, without making you wait a minute longer.”

text: “Those rooms are all in disorder; there has been hasty packing, and odds and ends lie upon the floor. There is no one in that room behind you! Let me look.”
source_text: “Those rooms are all in disorder, there has been hurried packing, there are odds and ends upon the ground. There is no one in that room behind you! Let me look.”

text: “I said as much! A brave number indeed! My fellow-citizen here would have it forty-two; ten additional heads are well worth the taking. The Guillotine works gallantly. I love it. Ho, forward! Whoop!”
source_text: “I said so! A brave number! My fellow-citizen here would have it forty-two; ten more heads are worth having. The Guillotine goes handsomely. I love it. Hi forward. Whoop!”

text: “Nephew, I believe—but at all events a degenerate successor—of the polished Marquis who was murdered,” said one. “I am happy to say I never knew him.”
source_text: “Nephew, I believe--but in any case degenerate successor--of the polished Marquis who was murdered,” said one. “Happy to say, I never knew him.”

text: “You have guessed aright; I have of late been so deeply engaged in one pursuit that I have not allowed myself sufficient repose, as you perceive; but I trust, I most sincerely trust, that all these employments are now brought to a close, and that I am at last free.”
source_text: “You have guessed right; I have lately been so deeply engaged in one occupation that I have not allowed myself sufficient rest, as you see; but I hope, I sincerely hope, that all these employments are now at an end and that I am at length free.”

text: “Let him speak! The king! The king! His humble subjects do hunger and thirst for words of wisdom from the mouth of their master, his Serene and Sacred Raggedness!”
source_text: “Let him speak! The king! The king! his humble subjects hunger and thirst for words of wisdom out of the mouth of their master his Serene and Sacred Raggedness!”

text: Click! the king and I were handcuffed together! Our companions, those servants, had done it; my lord Grip stood by and watched. The king broke forth into a rage, and said:
source_text: Click! the king and I were handcuffed together! Our companions, those servants, had done it; my lord Grip stood looking on. The king burst out in a fury, and said:

text: The dread tribunal of the five Judges, the Public Prosecutor, and the resolute Jury sat each day. Their lists went forth every evening, and were read aloud by the gaolers of the several prisons to the prisoners within. The familiar gaoler’s jest was: “Come out and hear the Evening Paper, you there inside!”
source_text: The dread tribunal of five Judges, Public Prosecutor, and determined Jury, sat every day. Their lists went forth every evening, and were read out by the gaolers of the various prisons to their prisoners. The standard gaoler-joke was, “Come out and listen to the Evening Paper, you inside there!”

text: “I was not in attendance at the ceremony; yet, in my judgment, you were,” said Carton. At this he laughed again, and they both laughed.
source_text: “I was not present at the ceremony; but my opinion is you were,” said Carton. At this, he laughed again, and they both laughed.

text:
As, winding hence, the throngs now part and disperse—but we, old man,  
Not for naught have I brought thee hither—we must remain,  
Thou to speak in thy turn, and I to listen and tell.
source_text:
As wending the crowds now part and disperse--but we old man,
Not for nothing have I brought you hither--we must remain,
You to speak in your turn, and I to listen and tell.

text:
ORSINO:
There is no need of that. Attend: thou know’st
Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s day; the man your father
Cast down from his office? And Marzio,
That desperate wretch, whom he but last year
Denied the meed of blood, well earned and due?
source_text:
ORSINO:
There is no need of that. Listen: you know
Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s time; him whom your father
Degraded from his post? And Marzio,
That desperate wretch, whom he deprived last year
Of a reward of blood, well earned and due?

text:
Soft floated the music down the gentle air,
While fluent Greek, in vowelled undertone,
Was kept among the guests, discoursing low
At first, for scarcely yet had wine begun;
But when the fortunate vintage touch’d their brains,
The louder grew their speech, and louder rose the strains
Of potent instruments:--the gorgeous dyes,
The breadth, the splendour, and the draperies’ guise,
The roof of awful richness, nectarous cheer,
Fair slaves, and Lamia’s self, stood all forth near,
Now, when the wine hath done its rosy deed,
And every soul from human fetters freed,
No longer seem so strange; for merry wine, sweet wine,
Will make Elysian shades not over-fair, too divine.
Soon was God Bacchus at meridian height;
Flush’d were their cheeks, and bright eyes doubly bright:
Garlands of every green, and every scent
From vales despoil’d, or forest-branches rent,
In baskets of bright osier’d gold were brought
High as the handles heap’d, to suit the thought
Of every guest; that each, as he did please,
Might fancy-fit his brows, silk-pillow’d, at his ease.
source_text:
Soft went the music the soft air along,
While fluent Greek a vowel'd undersong
Kept up among the guests, discoursing low
At first, for scarcely was the wine at flow;
But when the happy vintage touch'd their brains,
Louder they talk, and louder come the strains
Of powerful instruments:--the gorgeous dyes,
The space, the splendour of the draperies,
The roof of awful richness, nectarous cheer,
Beautiful slaves, and Lamia's self, appear,
Now, when the wine has done its rosy deed,
And every soul from human trammels freed,
No more so strange; for merry wine, sweet wine,
Will make Elysian shades not too fair, too divine.
Soon was God Bacchus at meridian height;
Flush'd were their cheeks, and bright eyes double bright:
Garlands of every green, and every scent
From vales deflower'd, or forest-trees branch-rent,
In baskets of bright osier'd gold were brought
High as the handles heap'd, to suit the thought
Of every guest; that each, as he did please,
Might fancy-fit his brows, silk-pillow'd at his ease.

text:
I crave for him the grace of summer boughs,
If such an outcast be,
He never heard that fleshless chant
Ascend so solemn in the tree,
source_text:
I crave him grace, of summer boughs,
If such an outcast be,
He never heard that fleshless chant
Rise solemn in the tree,

text:
An anguish at the naming,
That, when to patience grown,
I’ve known leave then granted
To join itself to its own.
source_text:
An anguish at the mention,
Which, when to patience grown,
I 've known permission given
To rejoin its own.

text:
They were the pride of the line of rangers,  
Peerless in horse, in rifle, song, and supper, in wooing,  
Stately, fierce, liberal, comely, haughty, and fond,  
Bearded, bronzed of sun, arrayed in the hunter’s free attire,  
Not one among them past thirty years of age.
source_text:
They were the glory of the race of rangers,
Matchless with horse, rifle, song, supper, courtship,
Large, turbulent, generous, handsome, proud, and affectionate,
Bearded, sunburnt, drest in the free costume of hunters,
Not a single one over thirty years of age.

text:
I sang of the wheeling stars,  
I sang of the crafty Earth,  
And of Heaven—and the Titan wars,  
And Love, and Death, and Birth,—  
And then I changed my piping,—  
Singing how down the vale of Maenalus  
I chased a maiden and embraced a reed.  
Gods and men, we are all beguiled thus!  
It bursts within our bosom and then we bleed:  
All wept, as I deem both ye now would,  
If envy or age had not frozen your blood,  
At the grief of my sweet piping.
source_text:
I sang of the dancing stars,
I sang of the daedal Earth,
And of Heaven—and the giant wars,
