And Love, and Death, and Birth,—
And then I changed my pipings,—
Singing how down the vale of Maenalus
I pursued a maiden and clasped a reed.
Gods and men, we are all deluded thus!
It breaks in our bosom and then we bleed:
All wept, as I think both ye now would,
If envy or age had not frozen your blood,
At the sorrow of my sweet pipings.

text:
Grieve not thus, dear mother, (the just-grown daughter speaks through
her sobs,
The little sisters cluster close in silence, pale with dread,)
See, dearest mother, the letter saith Pete shall soon be better.
source_text:
Grieve not so, dear mother, (the just-grown daughter speaks through
her sobs,
The little sisters huddle around speechless and dismay’d,)
See, dearest mother, the letter says Pete will soon be better.

text:
And is this death?—The pyre hath vanished clean,  
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;  
The flames fall hush’d—by slow degrees is seen  
The music of a breath-detaining song,  
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,  
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;  
With ever-shifting notes it glides along,  
Till on my passive soul there seem’d to creep  
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.
source_text:
And is this death?—The pyre has disappeared,
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;
The flames grow silent—slowly there is heard
The music of a breath-suspending song,
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;
With ever-changing notes it floats along,
Till on my passive soul there seemed to creep
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.

text:
The dust had settled on upraised roads,
The birds more jocund sang;
The sunshine flung his hat aside,
The orchard’s spangles hung.
source_text:
The dust replaced in hoisted roads,
The birds jocoser sung;
The sunshine threw his hat away,
The orchards spangles hung.

text:
I sat me by my warpin-wheel,
And aye I drave it round;
But every shot, and every knock,
My heart it gave a wound.
To the weaver’s, &c.
source_text:
I sat beside my warpin-wheel,
And aye I ca’d it roun’;
But every shot and evey knock,
My heart it gae a stoun.
To the weaver’s, &c.

text:
By all I lov’d, forsaken and forgot,
No friendly visage ever lights my squalid cot;
Shunn’d, hated, wrong’d, unpitied, unredrest,
The scornful jest that mock’d me to the rest!
Ev’n the poor stay of my wretched life,
Torn from me by the violence of legal strife.
Oft grateful for my very daily bread
To those on whom my family’s large bounty fed;
A welcome inmate at their homely fare,
My griefs, my woes, my sighs, my tears they share:
(Their vulgar souls unlike the souls refin’d,
The fashioned marble of the polish’d mind).
source_text:
By all I lov’d, neglected and forgot,
No friendly face e’er lights my squalid cot;
Shunn’d, hated, wrong’d, unpitied, unredrest,
The mock’d quotation of the scorner’s jest!
Ev’n the poor support of my wretched life,
Snatched by the violence of legal strife.
Oft grateful for my very daily bread
To those my family’s once large bounty fed;
A welcome inmate at their homely fare,
My griefs, my woes, my sighs, my tears they share:
(Their vulgar souls unlike the souls refin’d,
The fashioned marble of the polished mind).

text:
“And I thought so too, Mrs. Yeobright,” said Christian earnestly,  
“but Father there was so eager he had no manners left at all,  
and went from home almost afore ’twas dark.  
I told him ’twas scarcely decent in a’ old man to come so oversoon;  
but words be wind.”
source_text: “And I thought so too, Mrs. Yeobright,” said Christian earnestly, “but Father there was so eager that he had no manners at all, and left home almost afore ’twas dark. I told him ’twas barely decent in a’ old man to come so oversoon; but words be wind.”

text:
In Mauchline there bide six bonnie young belles,
The pride o’ the place and the country around a’;
Their carriage and dress, a stranger would guess,
In Lon’on or Paris, they’d gotten it a’.
source_text:
In Mauchline there dwells six proper young belles,
The pride of the place and its neighbourhood a’;
Their carriage and dress, a stranger would guess,
In Lon’on or Paris, they’d gotten it a’.

text:
Yet there is one above the lave,
That hath some wit, and sense, and that;
A bonny lass, I like her best,
And who would dare call that a sin?
For a’ that, &c.
source_text:
But there is ane aboon the lave,
Has wit, and sense, an’ a’ that;
A bonie lass, I like her best,
And wha a crime dare ca’ that?
For a’ that, &c.

text:
PROMETHEUS:
It doth afflict me: speech is swift and vain;
For a brief while grief is blind, and so was mine.
I would no living creature suffer pain.
source_text:
PROMETHEUS:
It doth repent me: words are quick and vain;
Grief for awhile is blind, and so was mine.
I wish no living thing to suffer pain.

text:
Is there a bard of homely song,
Who, unregarded, moves the throng,
That weekly to this place belong,
O, pass him by?
Nay, with a brother-feeling strong,
Here, breathe a sigh.
source_text:
Is there a bard of rustic song,
Who, noteless, steals the crowds among,
That weekly this area throng,
O, pass not by!
But, with a frater-feeling strong,
Here, heave a sigh.

text:
But thee, Theocritus, wha equals?
They’re no herd’s ballads, Maro’s jingles;
Squire Pope but trims his skinkle-patches
O’ heathen tatters:
I pass by hunders, nameless wretches,
That ape their betters.
source_text:
But thee, Theocritus, wha matches?
They’re no herd’s ballats, Maro’s catches;
Squire Pope but busks his skinklin’ patches
O’ heathen tatters:
I pass by hunders, nameless wretches,
That ape their betters.

text:
At least, it brings some solace to know  
That there doth exist a gold,  
Though I may prove, but just in time,  
Its far-off gleam behold!
source_text:
At least, it solaces to know
That there exists a gold,
Although I prove it just in time
Its distance to behold!

text:
And oftentimes unto those streams of upper air  
That whirl the earth through all its daily round,  
She would ascend, and win the spirits there  
To let her join their chorus. Mortals found  
That on those days the sky was calm and fair,  
And mystic snatches of harmonious sound  
Wandered upon the earth where’er she passed,  
And happy thoughts of hope, too sweet to last.
source_text:
And sometimes to those streams of upper air
Which whirl the earth in its diurnal round,
She would ascend, and win the spirits there
To let her join their chorus. Mortals found
That on those days the sky was calm and fair,
And mystic snatches of harmonious sound
Wandered upon the earth where’er she passed,
And happy thoughts of hope, too sweet to last.

text:
When roses no more blossom, dear,  
And violets are spent,  
When bumble-bees in solemn flight  
Have passed beyond the sun,
source_text:
When roses cease to bloom, dear,
And violets are done,
When bumble-bees in solemn flight
Have passed beyond the sun,

text:
PAGE 173. ll. 116-17. There is . . . voice. Cf. i. 72-8. The
secret majesty of the wind among the trees, be it in stillness or in storm.
source_text:
PAGE 173. ll. 116-17. There is . . . voice. Cf. i. 72-8. The
mysterious grandeur of the wind in the trees, whether in calm or storm.

text:
So it is: yet let us sing,  
Honor to the ancient bow-string!  
Honor to the bugle-horn!  
Honor to the forests unshorn!  
Honor to the Lincoln green!  
Honor to the archer keen!  
Honor to little, tight John,  
And the steed he rode upon!  
Honor to bold Robin Hood,  
Slumbering in the underwood!  
Honor to Maid Marian,  
And to all the Sherwood clan!  
Though their days have sped away  
Let us twain a burden try.
source_text:
So it is: yet let us sing,
Honour to the old bow-string!
Honour to the bugle-horn!
Honour to the woods unshorn!
Honour to the Lincoln green!
Honour to the archer keen!
Honour to tight little John,
And the horse he rode upon!
Honour to bold Robin Hood,
Sleeping in the underwood!
Honour to maid Marian,
And to all the Sherwood-clan!
Though their days have hurried by
Let us two a burden try.

text:
Follow unto the deep wood’s weeds,
Follow unto the wild-briar dingle,
Where we seek to blend and mingle,
And the violet breathes her tale
To the odour-laden gale,
For those twain have enough to do
Of such task as I and you.
source_text:
Follow to the deep wood’s weeds,
Follow to the wild-briar dingle,
Where we seek to intermingle,
And the violet tells her tale
To the odour-scented gale,
For they two have enough to do
Of such work as I and you.

text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
I feel no such enchantment stirring here.
My frame is all of winter, and I fain
Would have the flowers along our road be frost and snow.
But lo, how melancholy now ascends,
Half-lifting faintly her delayed pale beam,
The blank and unwelcome circle of the red moon,
And lends so evil a light, that every pace
Doth trip against some crag. With your leave,
I’ll summon Ignis-fatuus to our help:
I spy one there ablaze so merrily.
Halloo, my friend! I pray you, may I ask
That you would grace us with your shining company?
Why should you burn away there all in vain?
Pray be so kind as guide us with your light.
source_text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
Nothing of such an influence do I feel.
My body is all wintry, and I wish
The flowers upon our path were frost and snow.
But see how melancholy rises now,
Dimly uplifting her belated beam,
The blank unwelcome round of the red moon,
And gives so bad a light, that every step
One stumbles ’gainst some crag. With your permission,
I’ll call on Ignis-fatuus to our aid:
I see one yonder burning jollily.
Halloo, my friend! may I request that you
Would favour us with your bright company?
Why should you blaze away there to no purpose?
Pray be so good as light us up this way.

text:
“I’ve had the refusal o’t, ’tis true;
yet nothing is settled yet, and I have reasons for giving up.
I shall see out my year there as manager for the trustees,
but no more.”
source_text: “I’ve had the refusal o’ it ’tis true; but nothing is settled yet, and I have reasons for giving up. I shall finish out my year there as manager for the trustees, but no more.”

text:
I’m glad I cannot credit it,
For it would still my breath,
And I should like to look a little more
Upon so curious an earth!
I’m glad that they did credit it
Whom I have never found
Since that great autumn afternoon
I left them in the ground.
source_text:
I 'm glad I don't believe it,
For it would stop my breath,
And I 'd like to look a little more
At such a curious earth!
I am glad they did believe it
Whom I have never found
Since the mighty autumn afternoon
I left them in the ground.

text:
Farewell to the mountains, with snow on their head,  
Farewell to the straths and green valleys below spread;  
Farewell to the forests and wild-drooping woods,  
Farewell to the torrents and loud-rushing floods.  
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.
source_text:
Farewell to the mountains, high-cover’d with snow,
Farewell to the straths and green vallies below;
Farewell to the forests and wild-hanging woods,
Farewell to the torrents and loud-pouring floods.
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.

text:
There is no halting, nor can there ever be halting,  
If I, thou, and the worlds, and all beneath or above their surfaces,  
were this very moment brought back to a pallid drift, it would  
not profit the long course,  
We should assuredly rise again where now we stand,  
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.
source_text:
There is no stoppage and never can be stoppage,
If I, you, and the worlds, and all beneath or upon their surfaces,
were this moment reduced back to a pallid float, it would
not avail the long run,
We should surely bring up again where we now stand,
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.

text:
Banner and Pennant:  
Yet loftier, louder, mightier, bard! yet farther, broader cleave!  
No more let our children count us but for riches and for peace alone,  
We may be terror and slaughter, and are so now,  
Not now are we any one of these broad and haughty States, (nor  
any five, nor ten,)  
Nor mart nor depot are we, nor money-bank in the city,  
But these and all, and the brown and widening land, and the mines  
beneath, are ours,  
And the shores of the sea are ours, and the rivers great and small,  
And the fields they water, and the crops and the fruits are ours,  
Bays and channels and ships sailing in and out are ours—while we above all,  
Above the expanse spread underneath, the three or four millions of square  
miles, the capitals,  
The forty millions of people,—O bard! in life and death supreme,  
We, even we, henceforth fling forth masterful, high up above,  
Not for the present only, for a thousand years chanting through thee,  
This song to the soul of one poor little child.
source_text:
Banner and Pennant:
Yet louder, higher, stronger, bard! yet farther, wider cleave!
No longer let our children deem us riches and peace alone,
We may be terror and carnage, and are so now,
Not now are we any one of these spacious and haughty States, (nor
any five, nor ten,)
Nor market nor depot we, nor money-bank in the city,
But these and all, and the brown and spreading land, and the mines
below, are ours,
And the shores of the sea are ours, and the rivers great and small,
And the fields they moisten, and the crops and the fruits are ours,
Bays and channels and ships sailing in and out are ours--while we over all,
Over the area spread below, the three or four millions of square
miles, the capitals,
The forty millions of people,--O bard! in life and death supreme,
We, even we, henceforth flaunt out masterful, high up above,
Not for the present alone, for a thousand years chanting through you,
This song to the soul of one poor little child.

text:
Robin vow’d to me  
A’ my winter fare;  
Fient haet he had but three  
Guse-feathers and a whittle!  
Robin shure, &c.
source_text:
Robin promis’d me
A’ my winter vittle;
Fient haet he had but three
Guse-feathers and a whittle!
Robin shure, &c.

text:
The cold earth lay asleep below,  
And over it the cold sky glowed;  
And all around, with a shivering sound,  
From ice-walled caves and snowy fields,  
Night’s breath, like death, was drifting forth  
Beneath the sinking moon.
source_text:
The cold earth slept below,
Above the cold sky shone;
And all around, with a chilling sound,
From caves of ice and fields of snow,
The breath of night like death did flow
Beneath the sinking moon.

text:
We may dream, in that prolonged sleep,
That we are not those that weep;
E’en as Pleasure dreams of thee,
Life-abandoning Misery,
Thou mayst dream of her with me.
source_text:
We may dream, in that long sleep,
That we are not those who weep;
E’en as Pleasure dreams of thee,
Life-deserting Misery,
Thou mayst dream of her with me.

text:
"'T is daisy in the briefest;
A little farther on,
Nearest the door to rouse the first,
Tiny leontodon.
source_text:
"'T is daisy in the shortest;
A little farther on,
Nearest the door to wake the first,
Little leontodon.

text:
As they come unto an end,
Of that which lies beneath the earlier songs—of my intent in them,
Of the seed I have striven to sow in them,
Of joy, sweet joy, through many a year, in them,
(For them, for them have I lived, in them my task is done,)
Of many a dear aspiration, of many a dream and design;
Through Space and Time blended in a chant, and the flowing eternal sameness,
To Nature, enfolding these, enfolding God—to the joyous,
electric all,
To the thought of Death, and taking up exultant Death in its turn
as one with life,
The advent of man to sing;
To draw together you, ye parted, diverse lives,
To make accord of mountains and rocks and streams,
And the winds of the north, and the forests of oak and pine,
With you O soul.
source_text:
As they draw to a close,
Of what underlies the precedent songs--of my aims in them,
Of the seed I have sought to plant in them,
Of joy, sweet joy, through many a year, in them,
(For them, for them have I lived, in them my work is done,)
Of many an aspiration fond, of many a dream and plan;
Through Space and Time fused in a chant, and the flowing eternal identity,
To Nature encompassing these, encompassing God--to the joyous,
electric all,
To the sense of Death, and accepting exulting in Death in its turn
the same as life,
The entrance of man to sing;
To compact you, ye parted, diverse lives,
To put rapport the mountains and rocks and streams,
And the winds of the north, and the forests of oak and pine,
With you O soul.

text:
Out of the murk of heaviest clouds,
Out of the feudal wrecks and heap’d-up skeletons of kings,
Out of that ancient whole of European debris, the shatter’d mummeries,
Ruin’d cathedrals, crumble of palaces, tombs of priests,
Lo, Freedom’s features fresh and undimm’d look forth—the same immortal
face looks forth;
(A glimpse as of thy Mother’s face, Columbia,
A flash, significant as of a sword,
Beaming toward thee.)
source_text:
Out of the murk of heaviest clouds,
Out of the feudal wrecks and heap’d-up skeletons of kings,
Out of that old entire European debris, the shatter’d mummeries,
Ruin’d cathedrals, crumble of palaces, tombs of priests,
Lo, Freedom’s features fresh undimm’d look forth--the same immortal
face looks forth;
(A glimpse as of thy Mother’s face Columbia,
A flash significant as of a sword,
Beaming towards thee.)

text:
The sun, as usual, went abroad,  
The flowers, as they were wont, did blow,  
As though no soul had crossed the solstice  
That maketh all things new.
source_text:
The sun, as common, went abroad,
The flowers, accustomed, blew,
As if no soul the solstice passed
That maketh all things new.

text:
So steadfast in its stolid trust,
The shaft that never knew,
It shames the constancy that fled
Ere its own emblem flew.
source_text:
So constant to its stolid trust,
The shaft that never knew,
It shames the constancy that fled
Before its emblem flew.

text:
The crimson on the hill
Doth steal away my will;
If any mock or jeer,
Take heed, for God is here,
That is all.
source_text:
The red upon the hill
Taketh away my will;
If anybody sneer,
Take care, for God is here,
That's all.

text:
She talk’d, she smil’d, my heart she beguil’d;
She charm’d my soul, I wist not how;
And aye the stound, the deadly wound,
Came frae her een sae bonie blue.
Yet “spare to speak, and spare to speed;”
She’ll aiblins hear unto my vow:
Should she refuse, I’ll lay my dead
To her twa een sae bonie blue.
source_text:
She talk’d, she smil’d, my heart she wyl’d;
She charm’d my soul I wist na how;
And aye the stound, the deadly wound,
Cam frae her een so bonie blue.
But “spare to speak, and spare to speed;”
She’ll aiblins listen to my vow:
Should she refuse, I’ll lay my dead
To her twa een sae bonie blue.

text:
Wild beats my heart to trace thy steps,
Whose fathers, in days of yore,
Through hostile ranks and ruin’d gaps
Old Scotia’s bloody lion bore:
Ev’n I, who sing in rustic lore,
Haply my sires have left their shed,
And fac’d grim Danger’s loudest roar,
Bold-following where thy fathers led!
source_text:
Wild beats my heart to trace your steps,
Whose ancestors, in days of yore,
Thro’ hostile ranks and ruin’d gaps
Old Scotia’s bloody lion bore:
Ev’n I who sing in rustic lore,
Haply my sires have left their shed,
And fac’d grim Danger’s loudest roar,
Bold-following where your fathers led!

text:
There’s naught but care on every hand,
In every hour that glides, O:
What means the life of mortal man,
An’ wert it not for the lasses, O.
Green grow, &c.
source_text:
There’s nought but care on ev’ry han’,
In ev’ry hour that passes, O:
What signifies the life o’ man,
An’ ’twere na for the lasses, O.
Green grow, &c.

text:
Indifferently, amid the public, private haunts, in solitude,
Behind the mountain and the wood,
Companion of the city’s busiest streets, through the assemblage,
It and its radiance ever glide.
source_text:
Indifferently, ’mid public, private haunts, in solitude,
Behind the mountain and the wood,
Companion of the city’s busiest streets, through the assemblage,
It and its radiations constantly glide.

text:
To races fostered in the dark; --
How should your own commence?
Can flame be wrought in cochineal,
Or noontide in mazarine?
source_text:
To races nurtured in the dark; --
How would your own begin?
Can blaze be done in cochineal,
Or noon in mazarin?

text:
CENCI:
For which Aldobrandino now is debtor
My fief beyond the Pincian.—Cardinal,
One thing, I pray you, bear in mind hereafter,
And then our speech shall move with less constraint.
A man you knew had spoken of my wife and daughter—
He was wont to visit often at my house;
So on the morrow HIS wife and daughter came
And asked if I had seen him; and I smiled:
I think they never saw him any more.
source_text:
CENCI:
For which Aldobrandino owes you now
My fief beyond the Pincian.—Cardinal,
One thing, I pray you, recollect henceforth,
And so we shall converse with less restraint.
A man you knew spoke of my wife and daughter—
He was accustomed to frequent my house;
So the next day HIS wife and daughter came
And asked if I had seen him; and I smiled:
I think they never saw him any more.

text:
I am he who roams the States with a barbéd tongue, and questions each
one I encounter,
Who art thou that didst desire only to be told what thou knewest ere?
Who art thou that didst desire only a book to keep thee company in thy folly?
source_text:
I am he who walks the States with a barb’d tongue, questioning every
one I meet,
Who are you that wanted only to be told what you knew before?
Who are you that wanted only a book to join you in your nonsense?

text:
And all must cherish the human form,
In heathen, Turk, or Jew.
Where Mercy, Love, and Pity dwell,
There God doth dwell as true.
source_text:
And all must love the human form,
In heathen, Turk, or Jew.
Where Mercy, Love, and Pity dwell,
There God is dwelling too.

text:
“Ay; and there was a comely place at ‘seeds of love,’ and ’twas well heaved out.  
Though ‘love’ is a vile high corner when a man’s voice is growing crazed.  
Next verse, Master Poorgrass.”
source_text: “Ay; and there was a pretty place at ‘seeds of love.’ and ’twas well heaved out. Though ‘love’ is a nasty high corner when a man’s voice is getting crazed. Next verse, Master Poorgrass.”

text:
He bound the girdle round my life, --
I heard the buckle click,
And turned away, imperial,
My lifetime folding in
Deliberate, as a duke might fold
A kingdom’s title-deed, --
Henceforth a consecrate kind,
A citizen of cloud.
source_text:
He put the belt around my life, --
I heard the buckle snap,
And turned away, imperial,
My lifetime folding up
Deliberate, as a duke would do
A kingdom's title-deed, --
Henceforth a dedicated sort,
A member of the cloud.

text:
Had Nature one rejected face,
Could she despise a son,
Had Nature an Iscariot,
That mushroom, — he is one.
source_text:
Had nature any outcast face,
Could she a son contemn,
Had nature an Iscariot,
That mushroom, -- it is him.

text:
’Twas wrought from sudden pangs, in all their stress,
All at once they rise, and cry aloud,
“Father! our griefs, though grievous, were the less
source_text:
Twas done from sudden pangs, in their excess,
All of a sudden raise themselves, and say,
“Father! our woes, so great, were yet the less

text:
Sweet infant, in thy countenance  
A holy form I seem to trace;  
Sweet infant, once like thee  
Thy Maker lay, and wept for me:
source_text:
Sweet babe, in thy face
Holy image I can trace;
Sweet babe, once like thee
Thy Maker lay, and wept for me:

text:
But the sweetness of mercy had brew’d bitter destruction, and the
frighten’d monarchs return again,
Each cometh in state with his train, hangman, priest, tax-gatherer,
Soldier, lawyer, lord, jailer, and sycophant.
source_text:
But the sweetness of mercy brew’d bitter destruction, and the
frighten’d monarchs come back,
Each comes in state with his train, hangman, priest, tax-gatherer,
Soldier, lawyer, lord, jailer, and sycophant.

text:
The two old, simple problems ever intertwined,  
Close home, elusive, present, baffled, grappled.  
By each succeeding age unmastered, pass’d along,  
To ours at last—and we pass on the selfsame.
source_text:
The two old, simple problems ever intertwined,
Close home, elusive, present, baffled, grappled.
By each successive age insoluble, pass’d on,
To ours to-day--and we pass on the same.

text:
PAGE 118. l. 13. _'Mid . . . eyed._
Nature, in her appeal to every sense.
In this line rests the very soul of all
That makes the flower’s beauty satisfying,
And full of comfort.
source_text:
PAGE 118. l. 13. _'Mid . . . eyed._ Nature in its appeal to every sense.
In this line we have the essence of all that makes the beauty of flowers
satisfying and comforting.

text:
I swear I begin to discern but little or nothing in audible words,  
All gathers toward the showing-forth of the unspoken meanings of the earth,  
Toward him who sings the songs of the body and of the truths of the earth,  
Toward him who makes the dictionaries of words that print cannot touch.
source_text:
I swear I begin to see little or nothing in audible words,
All merges toward the presentation of the unspoken meanings of the earth,
Toward him who sings the songs of the body and of the truths of the earth,
Toward him who makes the dictionaries of words that print cannot touch.

text:
With tread as soft as wind it glided  
O’er the heads of men—so fleet  
That they felt the presence there,  
And looked,—but all was empty air.
source_text:
With step as soft as wind it passed
O’er the heads of men—so fast
That they knew the presence there,
And looked,—but all was empty air.

text:
But I, my life surveying, now in closing,  
With naught to show, from out its idle years to devise,  
No houses nor lands, nor signs of gems or gold for my friends,  
Yet certain remembrances of the war for you, and after you,  
And little keepsakes of camps and soldiers, with my love,  
I bind them all and leave them in this bundle of songs.
source_text:
But I, my life surveying, closing,
With nothing to show to devise from its idle years,
Nor houses nor lands, nor tokens of gems or gold for my friends,
Yet certain remembrances of the war for you, and after you,
And little souvenirs of camps and soldiers, with my love,
I bind together and bequeath in this bundle of songs.

text:
‘Like clouds enwoven in the silent sky,
By winds from far-off regions meeting there,
In the high name of truth and liberty,
Around the City millions gathered were,
By hopes that sprang from many a secret lair,—
Words which the lore of truth in hues of flame
Arrayed, thine own wild songs that in the air
Like homeless odors floated, and the name
Of thee, and many a tongue which thou hadst dipped in flame.
source_text:
‘Like clouds inwoven in the silent sky,
By winds from distant regions meeting there,
In the high name of truth and liberty,
Around the City millions gathered were,
By hopes which sprang from many a hidden lair,—
Words which the lore of truth in hues of flame
Arrayed, thine own wild songs which in the air
Like homeless odours floated, and the name
Of thee, and many a tongue which thou hadst dipped in flame.

text: “Truly, I had never deemed you bore that countenance. Well, and what said the last one unto ye? Naught that, after all, may not perhaps be got beyond?”
source_text: “Sure I should never have thought you had the face. Well, and what did the last one say to ye? Nothing that can’t be got over, perhaps, after all?”
“Is it peradventure that manner of thing which of late the unbelievers have brought from over the great seas, which, being boiled in oil, and an onion and salt added thereto, doth--”

text:
Within him were impelled by some hidden power,  
That bade them flame, and live, and sweep afar,  
Like lights and sounds, from haunted tower to tower
source_text:
Were driven within him by some secret power,
Which bade them blaze, and live, and roll afar,
Like lights and sounds, from haunted tower to tower

text:
I behold Christ breaking bread at his last supper amid
the young and the aged,
I behold the place where the mighty divine youth, Hercules, labored
truly and long, and then was dead,
I behold the spot of the pure rich life and the unlucky doom of the
fair nocturnal son, the full-limbed Bacchus,
I behold Kneph, in blossom, arrayed in blue, with a crown of plumes upon
his brow,
I behold Hermes, unguessed, dying, loved by all, saying to the people
Do not sorrow for me,
This is not my rightful land, I have lived exiled from my rightful land, and now I go back there,
I return to the heavenly sphere where each in his season goes.
source_text:
I see Christ eating the bread of his last supper in the midst of
youths and old persons,
I see where the strong divine young man the Hercules toil’d
faithfully and long and then died,
I see the place of the innocent rich life and hapless fate of the
beautiful nocturnal son, the full-limb’d Bacchus,
I see Kneph, blooming, drest in blue, with the crown of feathers on
his head,
I see Hermes, unsuspected, dying, well-belov’d, saying to the people
Do not weep for me,
This is not my true country, I have lived banish’d from my true
country, I now go back there,
I return to the celestial sphere where every one goes in his turn.

text:
Some weary comfort, at the last,
When a’ these days are o’er, man,
My pains o’ hell on earth are past,
And I’m assured o’ bliss aboon, man,
O aye my wife, &c.
source_text:
Some sairie comfort at the last,
When a’ thir days are done, man,
My pains o’ hell on earth is past,
I’m sure o’ bliss aboon, man,
O aye my wife, &c.

text:
Curst Common-sense, that imp o’ hell,
Came in wi’ Maggie Lauder;
But Oliphant aft made her yell,
An’ Russell sair misca’d her:
This day Mackinlay grips the flail,
An’ he’s the lad that’ll blaud her!
He’ll clap a shangan on her tail,
An’ set the bairns to daud her
Wi’ dirt this day.
source_text:
Curst Common-sense, that imp o’ hell,
Cam in wi’ Maggie Lauder;
But Oliphant aft made her yell,
An’ Russell sair misca’d her:
This day Mackinlay taks the flail,
An’ he’s the boy will blaud her!
He’ll clap a shangan on her tail,
An’ set the bairns to daud her
Wi’ dirt this day.

text:
The hours slipped by swiftly, as hours do,
Seized close in greedy hands;
So faces from two decks return the gaze,
Held fast by rival lands.
source_text:
The hours slid fast, as hours will,
Clutched tight by greedy hands;
So faces on two decks look back,
Bound to opposing lands.

text:
A young man comes to me carrying a message from his brother,  
How is the young man to know the when and why of his brother?  
Tell him to bring me the signs. And I stand before the young man  
face to face, and take his right hand in my left hand and his  
left hand in my right hand,  
And I answer for his brother and for men, and I answer for him who  
answers for all, and I send these signs.
source_text:
A young man comes to me bearing a message from his brother,
How shall the young man know the whether and when of his brother?
Tell him to send me the signs. And I stand before the young man
face to face, and take his right hand in my left hand and his
left hand in my right hand,
And I answer for his brother and for men, and I answer for him that
answers for all, and send these signs.

text:
Lo, the hurtling bowling orb!
Lo, the brother orbs about it, all the clustered suns and planets,
All the shining days, all the mystic nights with dreams,
Pioneers! O pioneers!
source_text:
Lo, the darting bowling orb!
Lo, the brother orbs around, all the clustering suns and planets,
All the dazzling days, all the mystic nights with dreams,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

text:
The door, with sudden force, shut, and I,  
I, drifting lost, went passing by, --  
Lost twice over, yet through contrast bright,  
A sorrow made to shed its light.
source_text:
The door as sudden shut, and I,
I, lost, was passing by, --
Lost doubly, but by contrast most,
Enlightening misery.

text:
Still lies the bustle in the brook,
Shut are the fragrant valves;
Mesmeric fingers softly brush
The eyes of many elves.
source_text:
Still is the bustle in the brook,
Sealed are the spicy valves;
Mesmeric fingers softly touch
The eyes of many elves.

text:
All rose to do the work He laid on each,  
Who shaped us for His purpose, not our own;  
The million rose to learn, and one to teach  
What none had ever yet known, nor could be known.  
And many rose  
Whose grief was such that dread grew dear;—  
Melchior and Lionel were not among those;  
They from the press of men had turned aside,  
And made their dwelling on the green hill’s side.  
It was that hill, whose intervening brow  
Shields Lucca from the Pisan’s jealous eye,  
While the encircling plain, flowing below,  
Like a broad lake of verdure, rich and high,  
With streams and fields and marshes bare,  
Parts it from the far Apennines—which lie  
Set like islands in the immeasurable sky.
source_text:
All rose to do the task He set to each,
Who shaped us to His ends and not our own;
The million rose to learn, and one to teach
What none yet ever knew or can be known.
And many rose
Whose woe was such that fear became desire;—
Melchior and Lionel were not among those;
They from the throng of men had stepped aside,
And made their home under the green hill-side.
It was that hill, whose intervening brow
Screens Lucca from the Pisan’s envious eye,
Which the circumfluous plain waving below,
Like a wide lake of green fertility,
With streams and fields and marshes bare,
Divides from the far Apennines—which lie
Islanded in the immeasurable air.

text:
“And, father, how could I love you
Or any one of my brothers more?
I love you like the tiny bird
That gathers crumbs beside the door.”
source_text:
‘And, father, how can I love you
Or any of my brothers more?
I love you like the little bird
That picks up crumbs around the door.’

text:
O to fall while pressing on!
Are some among us bound to flag and die? has the hour now arrived?
Then on the march we, most fitly, die; swift and certain, the gap is filled.
Pioneers! O pioneers!
source_text:
O to die advancing on!
Are there some of us to droop and die? has the hour come?
Then upon the march we fittest die, soon and sure the gap is fill’d.
Pioneers! O pioneers!

text:
“Many and sharp the countless ills
Entwined within our frame!
More keen still do we forge ourselves,
With regret, remorse, and shame!
And man, whose heaven-uplifted face
The smiles of love adorn,—
Man’s inhumanity to man
Makes countless thousands mourn!
source_text:
“Many and sharp the num’rous ills
Inwoven with our frame!
More pointed still we make ourselves,
Regret, remorse, and shame!
And man, whose heav’n-erected face
The smiles of love adorn,—
Man’s inhumanity to man
Makes countless thousands mourn!

text:
Such was the false dawn of my living,
Such were the joys I held in store:
But ere high noon, with tempests riving,
They laid my blooming bliss in gore.
Though fickle Fortune has betrayed me—
She pledged me much, and failed me still,
Of many a hope and pleasure frayed me—
I bear a heart that shall sustain me still.
source_text:
Such was my life’s deceitful morning,
Such the pleasures I enjoyed:
But lang or noon, loud tempests storming
A’ my flowery bliss destroy’d.
Tho’ fickle fortune has deceiv’d me—
She promis’d fair, and perform’d but ill,
Of mony a joy and hope bereav’d me—
I bear a heart shall support me still.

text:
l. 288. Like . . . among. Take this line word by word, and see how
many different ideas combine to make the eerily unmatched effect.
source_text:
l. 288. Like . . . among. Take this line word by word, and see how
many different ideas go to create the incomparably ghostly effect.

text:
ll. 76-8. Save . . . wave. Notice how the wind’s gust arrives and passes in  
the lift and dip of these lines, which open and close on the same sound.
source_text:
ll. 76-8. Save . . . wave. See how the gust of wind comes and goes in
the rise and fall of these lines, which begin and end on the same sound.

text:
Of all the winds that sweep the sky,
I dearly prize the west,
For there the bonny lassie dwells,
The lassie I love best:
source_text:
Of a’ the airts the wind can blaw,
I dearly like the west,
For there the bonie lassie lives,
The lassie I lo’e best:

text:
Fierce rages the midnight storm  
Over the savage mountain,  
Black clouds the night transform,  
Quickly rushes the fountain—
source_text:
Fierce roars the midnight storm
O’er the wild mountain,
Dark clouds the night deform,
Swift rolls the fountain—

text:
Soil of flint, if firmly worked,  
Will answer to the hand;  
Seed of palm beneath Libyan sun  
Bore fruit in desert sand.
source_text:
Soil of flint if steadfast tilled
Will reward the hand;
Seed of palm by Lybian sun
Fructified in sand.

text:
It’s scarce within a mortal’s pow’r  
At times to keep from turning sour,  
To mark how all things are dispensed;  
How best of men are often pinched,  
While fools on tens of thousands sprawl,  
And know not how to spend them all;  
But, Davie, lad, do not distress,  
Though little gear is ours to bless;  
We’re fit to win our daily bread,  
So long as we are stout and fed:  
“Ask no more, nor fear no more,”  
Old age ne’er heeds a fig;  
The worst of it, the end of it  
Is only just to beg.
source_text:
It’s hardly in a body’s pow’r
To keep, at times, frae being sour,
To see how things are shar’d;
How best o’ chiels are whiles in want,
While coofs on countless thousands rant,
And ken na how to wair’t;
But, Davie, lad, ne’er fash your head,
Tho’ we hae little gear;
We’re fit to win our daily bread,
As lang’s we’re hale and fier:
“Mair spier na, nor fear na,”
Auld age ne’er mind a feg;
The last o’t, the warst o’t
Is only but to beg.

text:
And yet it bore the taste of all;
The forms I have beheld
Laid in neatness, for burial,
Put mine back in mind,
source_text:
And yet it tasted like them all;
The figures I have seen
Set orderly, for burial,
Reminded me of mine,

text:
Lo, the sovereign sun, so serene and lofty,
The violet, purple dawn with barely felt airs,
The gentle, tender, boundless light,
The marvel widening, bathing all, the fulfilled noon,
The approaching eve delicious, the welcome night and stars,
Over my cities shining everywhere, wrapping man and land.
source_text:
Lo, the most excellent sun so calm and haughty,
The violet and purple morn with just-felt breezes,
The gentle soft-born measureless light,
The miracle spreading bathing all, the fulfill’d noon,
The coming eve delicious, the welcome night and the stars,
Over my cities shining all, enveloping man and land.

text: Pretraining Facilitates the Generation of Factual Summaries. Overall, 34.7% of the BERTS2S abstracts were faithful (26.9%) and/or factual (+7.8%). This is 7.4% absolute higher than that of the second-best model (PTGEN). The proportion of unfaithful but factual summaries produced by BERTS2S, 7.8%, was likewise the highest. Indeed, for extrinsic hallucinations, although PTGEN hallucinates slightly less than BERTS2S (63.3% vs. 64.1%), 6.6% of BERTS2S hallucinations were factual, compared with only 2.2% for PTGEN.5 Thus, if factual hallucinations are regarded as valid, this implies that even in extrinsic cases, BERTS2S hallucinates the least.
source_text: Pretraining Helps Generating Factual Summaries. In total, 34.7% of the BERTS2S abstracts were faithful (26.9%) and/or factual (+7.8%). This is 7.4% absolute better than the next-best model (PTGEN). The number of unfaithful yet factual summaries for BERTS2S, 7.8%, was also the highest. In fact, for extrinsic hallucinations, even though PTGEN hallucinates less than BERTS2S (63.3% vs. 64.1%), 6.6% of BERTS2S hallucinations were factual, compared to 2.2% of PTGEN. 5 Thus, if we consider factual hallucinations to be valid, this means that even for extrinsic cases, BERTS2S hallucinates the least.

text: Algorithm 1: Reinforced Fine-tune Learning Inputs: expert network ξ with pre-trained parameters φ, fine-tuning interval k, reward threshold th, number of pre-training steps d, target network update rate τ, and training interval η. Initialize random Q-network weights θ, random target network weights θ̄, replay buffer B = ∅, and fine-tuning data set D = ∅. For t ∈ 1, 2, ...d, obtain the conversational state s_t, sample an action from the expert policy a_E ∼ π^ξ_φ(s_t), execute a_E and observe (s_{t+1}, r_t), and add (s_t, a_E, r_t, s_{t+1}) to B. If r_t > th, add (s_t, a_E) to D. If t mod k = 0, update φ by φ ← arg min_φ − (s,a_E)∈D a_E log ξ_φ(s). If t mod η = 0, call train(). For t ∈ 1, 2, ... , obtain the conversational state s_t, sample an action from the behavior policy a_t ∼ π^Q_θ(s_t), execute a_t and observe (s_{t+1}, r_t), and add (s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1}) to B. If t mod η = 0, call train(). Procedure train(): sample transitions from B, compute the loss L(Q), carry out a gradient step to update θ, and if t mod τ = 0, set θ̄ ← θ.
source_text: Algorithm 1: Reinforced Fine-tune Learning Inputs: expert network ξ with pre-trained parameters φ, fine-tune interval k, a reward threshold th, number of pre-training steps d, target network update rate τ , training interval η Initialize: random Q-network weights θ, random target network weights θ , replay buffer B = ∅, fine-tune data set D = ∅ for t ∈ 1, 2, ...d do Get conversational state st Sample action from expert policy aE ∼ π ξ φ (st) Take action aE and observe (st+1, rt) B ← B ∪ (st, aE, rt, st+1) if rt > th then D ← D ∪ (st, aE) if t mod k = 0 then φ ← arg min φ − (s,a E )∈D aE log ξ φ (s) end if t mod η = 0 then train() end for t ∈ 1, 2, ... do Get conversational state st Sample action from behavior policy at ∼ π Q θ (st) Take action at and observe (st+1, rt) B ← B ∪ (st, at, rt, st+1) if t mod η = 0 then train() end Procedure train() Sample transitions from B Calculate loss L(Q) Perform a gradient step to update θ if t mod τ = 0 then θ ← θ

text: We introduced a new probabilistic generative framework for producing diverse and consistent question-answer (QA) pairs from a given text. In particular, our model learns the joint distribution of questions and answers conditioned on context using a hierarchically conditional variational autoencoder, while promoting consistency among generated QA pairs by maximizing their mutual information through a novel InfoMax regularizer. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first successful probabilistic QAG model. We assessed the QAG capability of our approach by measuring the accuracy of a BERT-base QA model trained on the generated questions across multiple datasets, where it substantially surpassed the state-of-the-art QAG baseline (+6.59–10.69 EM), even when using fewer QA pairs. We further verified our model in a semi-supervised QA setting, in which it improved the performance of the BERT-base QA model on SQuAD by 2.12 EM, clearly outperforming the state-of-the-art model. As future work, we intend to extend our QAG model to a meta-learning framework to support generalization across diverse datasets. Table 11: The effect of the F1-based replacement strategy in the semi-supervised setting of SQuAD+H×100%. All results are the performance on the validation set of Zhang and Bansal (2019), with a beam size of 10 for decoding. We also evaluate the Maxout-QG model on our SQuAD validation set using BLEU4 (Papineni et al., 2002), obtaining 15.68 points.
source_text: We proposed a novel probabilistic generative framework for generating diverse and consistent questionanswer (QA) pairs from given texts. Specifically, our model learns the joint distribution of question and answer given context with a hierarchically conditional variational autoencoder, while enforcing consistency between generated QA pairs by maximizing their mutual information with a novel In- foMax regularizer. To our knowledge, ours is the first successful probabilistic QAG model. We evaluated the QAG performance of our model by the accuracy of the BERT-base QA model trained using the generated questions on multiple datasets, on which it largely outperformed the state-of-theart QAG baseline (+6.59-10.69 in EM), even with a smaller number of QA pairs. We further validated our model for semi-supervised QA, where it improved the performance of the BERT-base QA model on the SQuAD by 2.12 in EM, significantly outperforming the state-of-the-art model. As future work, we plan to extend our QAG model to a meta-learning framework, for generalization over diverse datasets. Table 11 : The effect of F1-based replacement strategy in semi-supervised setting of SQuAD+H×100%. All results are the performance on validation set of Zhang and Bansal (2019) . beam size of 10 for decoding. We also evaluate the Maxout-QG model on our SQuAD validation set with BLEU4 (Papineni et al., 2002) , and get 15.68 points.

text: Because some of the methods under evaluation raise scalability concerns, we employ the truncated variant of the dataset, restricting each cluster to at most 100 articles (WCEP-100). The performance of the methods considered begins to level off beyond 100 articles (see Figure 2). We impose a maximum summary length of 40 tokens, consistent with the editorial guidelines in WCEP. This cap also matches the optimal length of an extractive oracle that maximizes ROUGE F1-scores 8. We recommend evaluating models with a dynamic, potentially longer output length using F1-scores and, optionally, reporting Recall results with truncated summaries. Extractive methods should return only lists of complete, untruncated sentences up to that limit. We evaluate lowercased summaries and otherwise do not alter the groundtruth or system summaries. Systems are compared using F1-score and Recall for ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L (Lin, 2004). In what follows, we abbreviate ROUGE-1 F1-score and Recall as R1-F and R1-R, respectively, and similarly for the other measures.
source_text: Due to scalability issues of some of the tested methods, we use the truncated version of the dataset with a maximum of 100 articles per cluster (WCEP-100). The performance of the methods that we consider starts to plateau after 100 articles (see Figure 2) . We set a maximum summary length of 40 tokens, which is in accordance with the editor guidelines in WCEP. This limit also corresponds to the optimal length of an extractive oracle optimizing ROUGE F1-scores 8 . We recommend to evaluate models with a dynamic (potentially longer) output length using F1-scores and optionally to provide Recall results with truncated summaries. Extractive methods should only return lists of full untruncated sentences up to that limit. We evaluate lowercased versions of summaries and do not modify groundtruth or system summaries otherwise. We compare and evaluate systems using F1-score and Recall of ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L (Lin, 2004) . In the following, we abbreviate ROUGE-1 F1-score and Recall with R1-F and R1-R, etc.

text: To validate our approach, we trained the baseline models both with and without the auxiliary loss objectives described in §3.3 and §3.4. In this setup, we used the CoNLL 2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) and GermEval 2014 (Benikova et al., 2014) datasets. The baselines included GloVe vectors combined with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + GloVe, GloVe + Char), BERT, and ELMo embeddings for English. For German, we used Wikipedia FastText vectors together with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + Wiki, Wiki + Char). We adopted a label-preserving training configuration (α = 1.0, ηtrain = 10%). Table 1 reports the results of this experiment. We observed that our auxiliary training objectives improved accuracy on noisy input data for all baseline models in both languages. At the same time, they maintained accuracy on the original input. The data augmentation objective appeared to perform slightly better than the stability objective. However, the selected hyperparameter values were somewhat arbitrary, since our aim was to demonstrate the usefulness and flexibility of both objectives.
source_text: To validate our approach, we trained the baseline models with and without our auxiliary loss objectives ( §3.3, §3.4) 9 . We used the CoNLL 2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) and the GermEval 2014 (Benikova et al., 2014) data sets in this setup 10 . The baselines utilized GloVe vectors coupled with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + GloVe, GloVe + Char), BERT, and ELMo embeddings for English. For German, we employed Wikipedia FastText vectors paired with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + Wiki, Wiki + Char) 11 . We used a label-preserving training setup (α = 1.0, η train = 10%). Table 1 presents the results of this experiment 12 . We found that our auxiliary training objectives boosted accuracy on noisy input data for all baseline models and both languages. At the same time, they preserved accuracy for the original input. The data augmentation objective seemed to perform slightly better than the stability objective. However, the chosen hyper-parameter values were rather ar-bitrary, as our goal was to prove the utility and the flexibility of both objectives.

text: Application II: Unsupervised Cross-Domain Model Adaptation. We assume that annotations are available in several source domains, but not in an unseen target domain. We further assume that the input distributions P(x) across the different source domains X(k) differ substantially, so that such annotations can scarcely be transferred to train a model for the target domain. In other words, the prediction distribution of each domain-specific model Pk(y|x) is close to the underlying truth distribution P(y|x) only when x ∈ X(k). For target-corpus sentences x ∈ T, this source model Pk(y|x) again diverges from the ground-truth distribution for the target domain PT(y|x) and can therefore be regarded as an imperfect annotator. Our goal in this setting is likewise to jointly model PT(y, x), while recognizing that there are substantial domain shifts between T and any other X(k).
source_text: Application II: Unsupervised Cross-Domain Model Adaptation. We assume there are available annotations in several source domains, but not in an unseen target domain. We assume that the input distributions P (x) in different source domains X (k) vary a lot, and such annotations can hardly be adapted for training a target domain model. That is, the prediction distribution of each domain model (P k (y|x)) is close to the underlying truth distribution (P (y|x)) only when x ∈ X (k) . For target corpus sentences x ∈ T , such a source model P k (y|x) again differs from underlying ground truth for the target domain P T (y|x) and can be seen as an imperfect annotators. Our objective in this setting is also to jointly model P T (y, x) while noticing that there are significant domain shifts between T and any other X (k) .

text: Information extraction for scientific publications. In recent years, several studies have investigated information extraction and knowledge base construction in the scientific domain (Augenstein et al., 2017; Luan et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2019; Buscaldi et al., 2019). We likewise pursue knowledge base construction, but our focus is on publications reporting materials science experiments, a domain that has received little attention in NLP to date. Information extraction for materials science. The study most closely related to ours is that of Mysore et al. (2019), who annotate a corpus of 230 paragraphs describing synthesis procedures with operations and their arguments, e.g., "The resulting [solid products M aterial ] were ...
source_text: Information extraction for scientific publications. Recently, several studies addressed information extraction and knowledge base construction in the scientific domain (Augenstein et al., 2017; Luan et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2019; Buscaldi et al., 2019) . We also aim at knowledge base construction but target publications about materials science experiments, a domain understudied in NLP to date. Information extraction for materials science. The work closest to ours is the one of Mysore et al. (2019) who annotate a corpus of 230 paragraphs describing synthesis procedures with operations and their arguments, e.g., "The resulting [solid products M aterial ] were ...

text: As noted above, the action space \(a_{B_n}\) is infinite. In practice, the REINFORCE algorithm (Williams, 1992) is used to approximate Equation 5 by sampling \(a_{B_n}\) from the policy \(p_\theta(a_{B_n}\mid s_{B_n})\). In addition, a baseline (Weaver and Tao, 2001)—here, the mean reward of a mini-batch—is subtracted from \(R(a_{B_n})\) to reduce variance. The agent generates tokens sequentially via multinomial sampling over the output distribution of \(B\), until the special token \([EOS]\) is sampled or the maximum permitted decoding step is reached (e.g., 32). Compared with beam search sampling, multinomial sampling yields greater diversity.
source_text: As aforementioned, the space of action a B n is infinite. In practice, REINFORCE algorithm (Williams, 1992) is leveraged to approximate Equation 5 by sampling a B n from policy p θ (a B n |s B n ). Furthermore, subtracting a baseline (Weaver and Tao, 2001) , here the mean reward of a mini-batch, is applied on R(a B n ) to reduce variance. The agent samples tokens one by one through multinomial sampling over the output distribution of B, until the special token [EOS] is sampled or exceeding the maximum allowed decoding step (e.g. 32). Compared to beam search sampling, multinomial sampling provides more diversities.

text: First, we observe that BPE-dropout yields the best performance across all dataset sizes (Figure 3). Second, models trained with subword regularization are less sensitive to the choice of vocabulary size: the performance gap between models with 4k and 32k vocabularies is much smaller than for models trained with standard BPE. This makes BPE-dropout appealing because it permits (i) avoiding vocabulary-size tuning for each dataset and (ii) selecting the vocabulary size according to the desired model characteristics: smaller vocabularies are advantageous in terms of parameter count, whereas larger vocabularies are advantageous in terms of inference time. 7 Finally, we note that the effect of using 6 Formally, for English/French/etc. with BPE-dropout, p = 0.1 sentences become on average about 1.25 times longer compared to segmented with BPE; for Chinese and Japanese, we need to set the value of p to 0.6 to achieve the same increase.
source_text: First, we see that BPE-dropout performs best for all dataset sizes (Figure 3) . Next, models trained with subword regularization are less sensitive to the choice of vocabulary size: differences in performance of models with 4k and 32k vocabulary are much less than for models trained with the standard BPE. This makes BPE-dropout attractive since it allows (i) not to tune vocabulary size for each dataset, (ii) choose vocabulary size depending on the desired model properties: models with smaller vocabularies are beneficial in terms of number of parameters, models with larger vocabularies are beneficial in terms of inference time. 7 Finally, we see that the effect from using 6 Formally, for English/French/etc. with BPE-dropout, p = 0.1 sentences become on average about 1.25 times longer compared to segmented with BPE; for Chinese and Japanese, we need to set the value of p to 0.6 to achieve the same increase.

text: At present, neural generation methods have enabled many compelling applications, such as poem generation, neural machine translation (NMT) (Bahdanau et al., 2015), and chatbots (Zhao et al., 2017). Conditional, also termed controllable, text generation is a significant subtask of text generation, whose goal is to produce realistic text with a specified attribute (for example, positive or negative sentiment). A widely used approach is to encode the condition as a vector representation and incorporate it into the text generation procedure (Hu et al., 2017; Mirza and Osindero, 2014). These neural models have yielded promising performance. However, when a new condition is introduced, such as a new topic in categorical generation, they must be fully retrained or fine-tuned. This is both time-consuming and computationally inefficient (Houlsby et al., 2019). In practical applications, neither fine-tuning nor retraining is ideal, because distributing updated large-scale weights, such as through the Internet, and redeploying them on the client side, for instance by delivering the updated weights to users, are often difficult.
source_text: Currently, neural generation techniques have powered many inspiring applications, e.g., poem generation , neural machine translation (NMT) (Bahdanau et al., 2015) and chatbot (Zhao et al., 2017) . Conditional (also known as controllable) text generation is an important task of text generation, aiming to generate realistic text that carries a specific attribute (e.g., positive or negative sentiment). A common solution is to encode the condition into a vector representation and then integrate it with the text generation process Hu et al., 2017; Mirza and Osindero, 2014) . These existing neural models have achieved encouraging results. However, when a new condition is added (e.g., a new topic for categorical generation), they require a full retraining or finetuning. This process is both time-consuming and computationally inefficient (Houlsby et al., 2019) . Both fine-tuning and retraining are not desirable in real-world applications since the delivery (e.g., transmitting updated weights through the Internet) and client-side re-deployment (e.g., distribute updated weights to users) of large-scale weights are often difficult.

source_text: The full list for the languages and the families they belong to, as classified in WALS (Dryer and Haspelmath, 2013 ) and, where WALS lacks an entry, Glottolog (Hammarström et al., 2019) , are given in Table 8 . . ">" implies that the translation is required from source to target language, vice versa for "<" I love her/him. Ofi'ã hilha lhiyohlili. I love lhiyohlili. (4) Ihooat sahollo.

text: Many studies have noted the same problem of repetitive or non-committal outputs produced by neural conversational systems, which are at least partly attributable to the shortage of sufficiently high-quality yes-ands addressed in this work; the strategies proposed to alleviate these issues differ. Most recent work aims to increase response diversity by adjusting training and decoding objectives (Li et al., 2016a (Li et al., ,b, 2017a (Li et al., , 2016c Xu et al., 2017; Shao et al., 2017) . Other approaches add latent variables to promote diversity (Serban et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017) . Still others investigate re-weighting training examples in ways that favor diversity (Liu et al., 2018; Lison and Bibauw, 2017; Du and Black, 2019) . Our method is complementary to all of the model-based approaches discussed above, because it is concerned only with constructing a particularly useful corpus, which can then be used for fine-tuning on top of these methods.
source_text: Many works have identified the same issues of repetitive or non-committal responses generated by neural conversational systems that are at least partially related to the lack of sufficiently high quality yes-ands we deal with in this work; approaches that mitigate these problems vary. The majority of recent works focus on diversifying the responses by modifying the training and decoding objectives (Li et al., 2016a (Li et al., ,b, 2017a (Li et al., , 2016c Xu et al., 2017; Shao et al., 2017) . Other methods introduce latent variables to encourage diversity (Serban et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017) . Some explore methods of re-weighing training instances that encourage diversity (Liu et al., 2018; Lison and Bibauw, 2017; Du and Black, 2019) . Our approach is complementary to all the model-based approaches described here, as it simply deals with the production of a particularly useful corpus, that can be used to fine-tune on top of these methods.

text: Test set. As a held-out test set for our predictors, we use the English Web Treebank (EWT), a hand-parsed corpus available in Universal Dependencies (UD) v2.4 (Silveira et al., 2014; Nivre, 2015). Applying our selection criteria, we extract 155 AAN triples for which scores are available for all of our predictors. Since this test set is very small, we additionally evaluate on a held-out subset of the parsed Common Crawl data. For the Common Crawl test set, after retaining only those AAN triples that have scores for all of our predictors, we obtain 41,822 AAN triples.
source_text: Test set As a held-out test set for our predictors, we use the English Web Treebank (EWT), a handparsed corpus, as contained in Universal Dependencies (UD) v2.4 (Silveira et al., 2014; Nivre, 2015) . Following our criteria, we extract 155 AAN triples having scores for all our predictors. Because this test set is very small, we also evaluate against a held-out portion of the parsed Common Crawl data. In the Common Crawl test set, after including only AAN triples that have scores for all of our predictors, we have 41,822 AAN triples.

text: We further extend the model by introducing auxiliary objectives and evaluate four distinct tasks: language modelling (LM), native language prediction (L1), POS-tagging, and UD prediction, in which we predict the UD type of a dependent given its head (see Section 3). These auxiliary objectives are motivated by prior work suggesting that learning to make such predictions is beneficial for tasks including essay scoring and grammatical error detection (Cheng et al., 2015; Rei and Yannakoudakis, 2017; Cummins and Rei, 2018; Johan Berggren et al., 2019; Bell et al., 2019).
source_text: We further extend the model to incorporate auxiliary objectives, and experiment with four different tasks: language modelling (LM), native language prediction (L1), POS-tagging, and UD prediction where we predict the UD type of a dependent with its head (see Section 3). These auxiliary objectives are based on previous work indicating that learning to make such predictions aids in tasks such as essay scoring and grammatical error detection (Cheng et al., 2015; Rei and Yannakoudakis, 2017; Cummins and Rei, 2018; Johan Berggren et al., 2019; Bell et al., 2019) .

text: We adopt the approach of Knight (1999), treating linearization as a traveling salesman problem (TSP). In this formulation, we must compute the cost from each node \(i\) to every other node \(j\), which can be viewed as the log likelihood of the bigram \((i, j)\). To estimate these costs, we employ the biaffine attention model of Dozat and Manning (2016), and we decode the TSP using the off-the-shelf solver OR-Tools¹.
source_text: We follow the idea in Knight (1999) to treat linearization as a TSP. Under the TSP formulation, we need to calculate the cost from every node i to every other node j, which can be interpreted as the log likelihood of the bigram (i, j). We use the biaffine attention model (Dozat and Manning, 2016) to obtain the costs, and use an off-the-shelf TSP solver, OR-Tools 1 , to decode the TSP.

text: Existing taxonomies, such as WordNet (Miller et al., 1990), are far from complete. For many domains, domain-specific taxonomies are either wholly unavailable or incomplete. In this paper, we focus on building taxonomies for such unseen domains 1. Because taxonomies are represented as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) (Suchanek et al., 2008), taxonomy construction can be cast as a DAG generation problem.
source_text: Existing taxonomies (e.g., WordNet (Miller et al., 1990) ) are far from being complete. Tax-onomies specific to many domains are either entirely absent or missing. In this paper, we focus on construction of taxonomies for such unseen domains 1 . Since taxonomies are expressed as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) (Suchanek et al., 2008) , taxonomy construction can be formulated as a DAG generation problem.

text: Annotation-based Evaluation. Some approaches directly instruct human annotators to score summaries according to specific guidelines, such as Responsiveness, which assesses the overall quality of summaries in terms of relevance, fluency, and readability, and Pyramid (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004), which evaluates summary relevance. More recently, systems have been introduced to facilitate the construction of Pyramid scores, for example (Hirao et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2019b; , but they still depend on human-annotated Summary Content Units (SCUs) in order to generate reliable scores. In addition to SCUs, recent research has investigated eliciting preferences over summaries (Zopf, 2018; Gao et al., 2018 Gao et al., 2019a) and annotations of important bi-grams (P.V.S and Meyer, 2017) as a basis for deriving summary ratings.
source_text: Annotation-based Evaluation. Some methods directly ask human annotators to rate summaries following some guidelines, e.g. Responsiveness, which measures the overall quality (relevance, fluency and readability) of summaries, and Pyramid (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004) , which measures summaries' relevance. Recently, systems have been developed to ease the construction of Pyramid scores, e.g. (Hirao et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2019b; , but they still require human-annotated Summary Content Units (SCUs) to produce reliable scores. Besides SCUs, recent work has explored eliciting preferences over summaries (Zopf, 2018; Gao et al., 2018 Gao et al., , 2019a and annotations of important bi-grams (P.V.S and Meyer, 2017) to derive summary ratings.

text: We compare the provided human response from the test set with seven variants of our TRANSRESNET RET model, mirroring their setup, in which our model is conditioned on seven styles that performed well in the evaluations reported in Section 5.2. Annotators evaluated response quality on a 1 to 3 scale, with 3 being the highest score, and reported the mean over approximately 2k questions. We then normalize this by the score of human-authored responses to obtain a percentage. The results are presented in Fig. 5 (left). Our model reduces the gap between human and model performance, achieving a higher proportion of the human score (62.9% vs. 54.2%). More detailed results and example predictions from our model are provided in Appendices E and F, including examples of both highly rated and poorly rated outputs.
source_text: We compare the provided human response (from the test set) with 7 variants of our TRANSRESNET RET model (mimicking their setup), whereby we have our model condition on 7 styles for which it performed well on evaluations in section 5.2. Annotators rated the quality of responses on a scale from 1 to 3, where 3 is the highest, reporting the mean over ∼2k questions. We then scale that by the score of human authored responses, to give a percentage. The results are shown in Fig. 5 (left) . Our model narrows the gap between human and model performance, yielding a higher percentage of the human score (62.9% vs. 54.2%). More detailed results and example predictions of our model can be found in Appendices E and F, including examples of highly rated and poorly rated outputs from our model.

text: Motivation: Pre-trained language models have achieved state-of-the-art results across a range of natural language processing tasks, including text classification, named entity recognition, and question answering. A major difficulty for practitioners is determining how to deploy these very large models in real-world settings. For example, models such as BERT Large (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), Megatron (Shoeybi et al., 2019), and T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) contain 340M, 1.5B, 8.3B, and 11B parameters, respectively. Although these models are trained offline, prediction still requires traversing the deep neural network stack with its vast number of parameters. This greatly increases both latency and memory demands.
source_text: Motivation: Pre-trained language models have shown state-of-the-art performance for various natural language processing applications like text classification, named entity recognition and questionanswering. A significant challenge facing practitioners is how to deploy these huge models in practice. For instance, models like BERT Large (Devlin et al., 2019) , GPT 2 (Radford et al., 2019) , Megatron (Shoeybi et al., 2019) and T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) have 340M , 1.5B, 8.3B and 11B parameters respectively. Although these models are trained offline, during prediction we need to traverse the deep neural network architecture stack involving a large number of parameters. This significantly increases latency and memory requirements.

text: The broader context of our work is the tracking of dialogue state in emergency medical service calls, where conversations are typically organized as sequences of questions and answers about a range of medical symptoms. The dominant approach to dialogue state tracking (DST) in speech is to first transcribe the speech using ASR (Henderson et al., 2014; Henderson, 2015; Mrkšić et al., 2017). In our setting, relying entirely on ASR is infeasible because the word error rate (WER) is substantially higher than in standard datasets. By way of illustration, although WER in our data is normally distributed with a mean of 37.6%, even the noisiest DST challenge datasets rarely exceed 30% WER (Jagfeld and Vu, 2017), and standard ASR benchmarks exhibit still lower WER (Park et al., 2019). Consequently, none of the standard ASR settings directly corresponds to a real-world ASR noise scenario.
source_text: The broader context of our work is to track the dialogue state in calls to emergency medical services, where conversations are typically formed as sequences of questions and answers that pertain to various medical symptoms. The predominant approach to dialogue state tracking (DST) in speech is to first transcribe the speech by using ASR (Henderson et al., 2014; Henderson, 2015; Mrkšić et al., 2017) . In our specific context, to entirely rely on ASR is prohibitive because of significantly higher WER in comparison to standard datasets. To exemplify, while WER is normally distributed with a mean of 37.6% in our data, the noisiest DST challenge datasets rarely involve with WER above 30% (Jagfeld and Vu, 2017) while standard ASR benchmarks offer even lower WER (Park et al., 2019) . None of the standard ASR scenarios thus directly apply to a real-life ASR noise scenario.

text: Regularization techniques are widely employed in training deep neural networks. Among the regularizers applied to network weights, the most widely used are Dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) and L2 regularization. Data augmentation methods in natural language processing include randomly removing tokens or swapping tokens at nearby positions (Iyyer et al., 2015; Artetxe et al., 2018; Lample et al., 2018), replacing tokens at random positions with a placeholder token (Xie et al., 2017), and substituting tokens at random positions with a token drawn from some distribution (e.g., according to token frequency or a language model) (Fadaee et al., 2017; Xie et al., 2017; Kobayashi, 2018). Although BPE-dropout may be regarded as a form of regularization, our aim is not to improve robustness by adding noise. Rather, by presenting the model with varying segmentations, we seek to teach it to better capture the composition of words and subwords, and to increase its flexibility in selecting a segmentation at inference time.
source_text: Regularization techniques are widely used for training deep neural networks. Among regularizations applied to a network weights the most popular are Dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) and L 2 regularization. Data augmentation techniques in natural language processing include dropping tokens at random positions or swapping tokens at close positions (Iyyer et al., 2015; Artetxe et al., 2018; Lample et al., 2018) , replacing tokens at random positions with a placeholder token (Xie et al., 2017) , replacing tokens at random positions with a token sampled from some distribution (e.g., based on token frequency or a language model) (Fadaee et al., 2017; Xie et al., 2017; Kobayashi, 2018) . While BPE-dropout can be thought of as a regularization, our motivation is not to make a model robust by injecting noise. By exposing a model to different segmentations, we want to teach it to better understand the composition of words as well as subwords, and make it more flexible in the choice of segmentation during inference.

text: NLI Datasets Natural language inference, or textual entailment, is a thoroughly studied text understanding task for which a number of datasets of different scales have been developed. The annual PASCAL RTE challenges (Dagan et al., 2005, inter alia) were accompanied by several thousand human-annotated entailment pairs. The SNLI dataset (Bowman et al., 2015) is the first large-scale entailment corpus to use image captions as premises, whereas MultiNLI (Williams et al., 2018) draws premises from multiple domains. The QNLI and WNLI datasets offer a different perspective by transforming the SQuAD question answering data (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) and Winograd Schema Challenge data (Levesque et al., 2012), respectively, into inference tasks. More recently, SciTail (Khot et al., 2018) and Adversarial NLI (Nie et al., 2019) have concentrated on constructing adversarial datasets; the former employs information retrieval to choose adversarial premises, while the latter relies on iterative annotation cycles designed to mislead models.
source_text: NLI Datasets Natural language inference/textual entailment is a well studied text understanding task, and has several datasets of various sizes. The annual PASCAL RTE challenges (Dagan et al., 2005 , inter alia) were associated with several thousands of human-annotated entailment pairs. The SNLI dataset (Bowman et al., 2015) is the first large scale entailment dataset that uses image captions as premises, while the MultiNLI (Williams et al., 2018) uses premises from multiple domains. The QNLI and WNLI datasets provide a new perspective by converting the SQuAD question answering data (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) and Winograd Schema Challenge data (Levesque et al., 2012) respectively into inference tasks. More recently, SciTail (Khot et al., 2018) and Adversarial NLI (Nie et al., 2019) have focused on building adversarial datasets; the former uses information retrieval to select adversarial premises, while the latter uses iterative annotation cycles to confuse models.

text: A well-established approach to addressing these challenges is to steer the dialog manager during RL training with expert demonstrations (Lipton et al., 2018; Gordon-Hall et al., 2020), with a high-level example illustrated in Figure 1. However, this strategy depends on a rule-based oracle to supply an appropriate system response for a given dialog state, and it fails to leverage the information available in the expanding collection of dialog datasets (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Rastogi et al., 2019).
source_text: An established way to deal with these difficulties is to guide the dialog manager with expert demonstrations during RL training (Lipton et al., 2018; Gordon-Hall et al., 2020) , a high-level illustration of which is shown in Figure 1 . This approach, however, requires a rule-based oracle to provide a suitable system response given a dialog state, and does not exploit the knowledge contained in the growing number of dialog datasets (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Rastogi et al., 2019) .

text: We evaluate our model on the CNN/Daily Mail dataset (Hermann et al., 2015) and the Gigaword dataset (Rush et al., 2015). All experiments are run on four NVIDIA P40 GPUs. We use a 6-layer encoder and a 6-layer decoder with 12 attention heads, and set \(h_{\text{model}} = 768\). For data preprocessing, we apply Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) word segmentation (Sennrich et al., 2016). We warm-start the model parameters from the MASS-pretrained base model 1 and train for about 10 epochs until convergence. During decoding, we employ beam search with a beam size of 5.
source_text: We evaluate our model in CNN/Daily Mail dataset (Hermann et al., 2015) and Gigaword dataset (Rush et al., 2015) . Our experiments are conducted with 4 NVIDIA P40 GPU. We adopt 6 layer encoder and 6 layers decoder with 12 attention heads, and h model = 768. Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) (Sennrich et al., 2016) word segmentation is used for data pre-processing. We warm-start the model parameter with MASS pre-trained base model 1 and trains about 10 epoches for convergence. During decoding, we use beam search with a beam size of 5.

text: To enable users to submit raw text as input for SyntagRank to disambiguate, our system uses a multilingual NLP pipeline that first carries out tokenization, sentence splitting, lemmatization, and Part of Speech (PoS) tagging. According to the input language, SyntagRank then relies either on the Stanford CoreNLP suite 4 (Manning et al., 2014) or on the models supplied by The Italian NLP Tool (Palmero Aprosio and Moretti, 2016, TINT).
source_text: In order to allow the user to provide an unprocessed text as input for SyntagRank to disambiguate, our system employs a multilingual NLP pipeline which preliminarily performs the functions of tokenization, sentence splitting, lemmatization and Part of Speech (PoS) tagging. Depending on the input language, SyntagRank utilizes either the Stanford CoreNLP suite 4 (Manning et al., 2014) , or the models provided by The Italian NLP Tool (Palmero Aprosio and Moretti, 2016, TINT).

text: Proposal Distribution Prior work employs proposal distributions q(z|x) that are essentially discriminative counterparts of the generative model, that is, models that predict the latent state conditioned on the text, with one important difference: they are conditioned not only on the sequence of tokens observed up to the current point, but also on future tokens on which the model will later be evaluated (a property we refer to as peeking). This conditioning does not violate any of the assumptions in Eqs. (3) and 4, and it helps prevent the generation of invalid structures, such as parse trees with more leaves than there are words in the text, as well as structures that are inconsistent with future tokens. Dyer et al. (2016) and Kim et al. (2019) further increase the entropy of the proposal distribution by dividing the logits by a temperature parameter τ, using τ = 1.25 and τ = 2.0, respectively.
source_text: Proposal Distribution Previous work uses proposal distributions q(z|x) that are essentially discriminative versions of the generative model (e.g., they are models that predict the latent state conditioned on the text), with one key distinction: they are conditioned not only on the sequence of tokens that have been observed so far, but also on future tokens that the model will be evaluated on (a trait we will refer to as peeking). This conditioning behavior does not contradict any of the assumptions in Eqn's (3) and 4, and is useful in preventing generation of invalid structures (for instance, parse trees with more leaves then there are words in the text), or ones that are inconsistent with future tokens. Dyer et al. (2016) and Kim et al. (2019) also increase the entropy of the proposal distribution by dividing logits by a temperature parameter τ (respectively using τ = 1.25 and τ = 2.0).

text: Baseline. Our baseline is the self-attention model for text infilling (Zhu et al., 2019). It is a transformer language model with a novel positional encoding scheme. The standard method of representing the absolute position of each token is not directly usable for our task, since the absolute positions of contextual tokens after the missing sentence are not known beforehand. To address this problem, Zhu et al. (2019) partition the text into segments.
