When there is only one masked sentence, the first (third) segment contains the contextual tokens before (after) the mask, and the second segment corresponds to the masked span. Each token is then indexed by its segment ID and its position ID within that segment. The missing tokens are generated sequentially from these IDs and the current surrounding context. When training the baseline model on our dataset, we follow the same hyperparameters as in the original paper, except that the batch size is reduced to 250 (it is 400 in Zhu et al., 2019), which prevents out-of-memory errors. It should be noted that we work with substantially longer sequences, usually exceeding 100 tokens, whereas Zhu et al. (2019) consider sequences with a maximum length of only 16 tokens.
source_text: Baseline. Our baseline is the self-attention model for text infilling (Zhu et al., 2019) . It is a transformer language model with novel positional encoding. The traditional approach of encoding the absolute position of each token is not directly applicable to our task because we do not know in advance the absolute positions of contextual tokens after the missing sentence. To resolve this issue, (Zhu et al., 2019) divides the text into segments. In the case of only one masked sentence, the first (third) segment consists of contextual tokens before (after) the mask, and the second corresponds to the mask. Then, each token is indexed by its segment ID and its position ID within the segment. The missing tokens are sequentially generated from these IDs and the current surrounding context. Training the baseline model on our dataset, we use the same set of hyperparameters as in the original reference except that the batch size is set to 250 (it is 400 in (Zhu et al., 2019) ). This avoids out-ofmemory errors. Note that we are handling much longer sequences (usually > 100 tokens) than (Zhu et al., 2019) , in which the maximum number of tokens in a sequence is only 16.

text: To address the second question, we conduct two analyses: (1) we perform a manual exploratory examination of model misclassifications on the most difficult test instances and group them into several linguistically motivated error categories; and (2) we translate these categories into testable hypotheses, which we then automatically evaluate on the full test set through adversarial rewriting—namely, by removing the suspected source of error and examining the resulting change in model prediction (Wu et al., 2019). We find that two classes of ambiguous relations account for most of the remaining errors. The dataset also includes cues that models without entity masking can exploit, for example, to classify relations correctly even when access to the sentential context is limited.
source_text: To answer the second question, we carry out two analyses: (1) we conduct a manual explorative analysis of model misclassifications on the most challenging test instances and categorize them into several linguistically motivated error categories; (2) we formulate these categories into testable hypotheses, which we can automatically validate on the full test set by adversarial rewriting -removing the suspected cause of error and observing the change in model prediction (Wu et al., 2019) . We find that two groups of ambiguous relations are responsible for most of the remaining errors. The dataset also contains clues that are exploited by models without entity masking, e.g. to correctly classify relations even with limited access to the sentential context.

text: In this paper, we have introduced models that can be employed to generate turn-switch offset distributions for SDS system responses. Prior work has demonstrated (e.g. Bögels et al., 2019) that humans are sensitive to these timings and that they can influence how a listener perceives responses. We would contend that they constitute an important, though often neglected, component of naturalistic interaction. With the emergence of commercial SDS systems designed to sustain user engagement across extended multi-turn interactions (e.g. Zhou et al., 2018), generating realistic response behavior is a potentially valuable enhancement to the overall experience.
source_text: In this paper, we have presented models that can be used to generate the turn switch offset distributions of SDS system responses. It has been shown in prior studies (e.g. (Bögels et al., 2019) ) that humans are sensitive to these timings and that they can impact how responses are perceived by a listener. We would argue that they are an important element of producing naturalistic interactions that is often overlooked. With the advent of commercial SDS systems that attempt to engage users over extended multi-turn interactions (e.g. (Zhou et al., 2018) ) generating realistic response behaviors is a potentially desirable addition to the overall experience.

text: , and the entire training domain is defined as the union of all sentence sets: X = (K) k=1 X (k). The objective of the multi-source learning task is to exploit such imperfect annotations to train a model that predicts the tag sequence y for any sentence x in a target corpus T. Note that the target corpus T may either follow the same distribution as X (Application I) or differ substantially from it (Application II). In the next two subsections, we formalize two representative tasks within this problem, as illustrated in Fig. 1.
source_text: , and the whole training domain as the union of all sentence sets: X = (K) k=1 X (k) . The goal of the multi-source learning task is to use such imperfect annotations to train a model for predicting the tag sequence y for any sentence x in a target corpus T . Note that the target corpus T can either share the same distribution with X (Application I) or be significantly different (Application II). In the following two subsections, we formulate two typical tasks in this problem as shown in Fig. 1 .

text: Following He and Glass (2019), we construct a list of malicious targets to test whether negative training can teach the model not to generate sentences from that list. However, beyond preventing the model from producing targets in a particular list, it is also important to examine whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. Accordingly, we also create a test target list containing targets that are similar to, but different from, those in the training list in order to evaluate generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is furthermore of interest to investigate whether using a larger number of malicious targets for negative training can reduce the hit rate on the test list. To this end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model on the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017), using a model with the same architecture as described in Section 2. The paraphrase model is then used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the unaugmented training list is first used for negative training, and then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets, respectively (i.e., 1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Examples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2. The same training, augmented training, and test lists are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between the training lists, whether augmented or not, and the test list.
source_text: Following (He and Glass, 2019) , a list of malicious targets are created to test whether negative training can teach the model not to generate sentences in the list. However, in addition to prevent the model from generating targets in a specific list, it is also important to check whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. So, a test target list which contains similar but different targets from the training list are also created to test generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is also interesting to investigate whether using more malicious targets for negative training can lower the hit rate on the test list. Towards that end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model using the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017) , with a model of the same structure as described in Section 2. Then, the paraphrase model is used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in the training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the training list without augmentation is first used for negative training, then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets respectively (1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Samples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2 . The same training, augmented training and test list are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between training lists (augmented or not) and the test list.

text: Policy learning for chitchat generation To mitigate the degeneration problem of word-level policy models (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018b), prior studies separate policy learning from response generation, and then adopt utterance-level latent variables or keywords (Yao et al., 2018) as RL actions to steer response generation. In this work, we examine how prior dialog-transition information can be exploited to support dialog policy learning. Knowledge aware conversation generation There is increasing interest in leveraging knowledge bases to produce more informative responses (Dinan et al., 2019; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Moghe et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019; Bao et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). In this work, instead of an external knowledge base, we employ a dialog-modeling oriented graph constructed from dialog corpora to facilitate multi-turn policy learning, rather than to improve dialog informativeness.
source_text: Policy learning for chitchat generation To address the degeneration issue of word-level policy models (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018b) , previous works decouple policy learning from response generation, and then use utterance-level latent variables or keywords (Yao et al., 2018) as RL actions to guide response generation. In this work, we investigate how to use prior dialog-transition information to facilitate dialog policy learning. Knowledge aware conversation generation There are growing interests in leveraging knowledge bases for generation of more informative responses (Dinan et al., 2019; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Moghe et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019; Bao et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020) . In this work, we employ a dialog-modeling oriented graph built from dialog corpora, instead of a external knowledge base, in order to facilitate multi-turn policy learning, instead of dialog informativeness improvement.

text: A recent method proposed by Wu et al. (2019) introduces an auxiliary loss that verifies whether the object words appear in the anticipated system response of a task-oriented dialogue system. It would be worthwhile to incorporate this auxiliary loss into the proposed method. By contrast, the REINFORCE algorithm (Williams, 1992) used in this paper is more general than that of Wu et al. (2019), since it can also accommodate other metrics, such as BLEU.
source_text: A recent method in (Wu et al., 2019) defines an auxiliary loss that checks if the object words exist in the expected system response of a task-oriented dialogue system. It would be interesting to apply this auxiliary loss in the proposed method. On the other hand, the REINFORCE (Williams, 1992) algorithm applied in this paper is more general than (Wu et al., 2019) to incorporate other metrics, such as BLEU.

text: We employ the sentence-pair classifier of Parikh et al. (2016), here referred to as the DA model. It computes attention-based word alignments over the input pair (the premise and hypothesis, or in our case question q_i and response r_ij) and combines them through feedforward networks. In addition to standard vector embeddings, we also evaluate the DA model with contextualized ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018), using the implementation provided in AllenNLP (Gardner et al., 2017).
source_text: We use the sentence pair classifier from (Parikh et al., 2016) , referred as the DA model. It finds attention based wordalignment of the input pair (premise and hypothesis, in our case question q i and response r ij ) and aggregates it using feedforward networks. Apart from standard vector embeddings, we also experiment with contextualized ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) embedding with the DA model using the version implemented in AllenNLP (Gardner et al., 2017) .

text: Graph neural networks (GNNs) (Scarselli et al., 2009) seek to learn a latent-state representation for each node in a graph by aggregating local information from neighboring nodes and the edges that connect them. Prior research has investigated various mechanisms for combining this local information, as in GCNs (Kipf and Welling, 2017), gated graph neural networks (GGNNs) (Li et al., 2016), and graph attention networks (GATs) (Veličković et al., 2018). Several studies have used GNNs in place of Seq2Seq models for text generation (Beck et al., 2018; Marcheggiani and Perez-Beltrachini, 2018; Guo et al., 2019), and some report better performance than Seq2Seq models. However, Damonte and Cohen (2019) employ both kinds of encoders and demonstrate that a GCN can help an LSTM model capture reentrant structures and long-range dependencies, although on a different task from ours. Our approach likewise uses both encoder types, but rather than having one support the other, it integrates them at the same time to exploit their complementary contributions.
source_text: Graph neural networks (GNN) (Scarselli et al., 2009) aim to learn a latent state representation for each node in a graph by aggregating local information from its neighbors and the connected edges. Previous work has explored different ways of aggregating this local information, such as in GCNs (Kipf and Welling, 2017), gated graph neural networks (GGNNs) (Li et al., 2016) , and Graph attention networks (GANs) (Veličković et al., 2018) Several works have applied GNNs instead of Seq2Seq models for text generation (Beck et al., 2018; Marcheggiani and Perez-Beltrachini, 2018; Guo et al., 2019; , and some of them outperform Seq2Seq models. However, Damonte and Cohen (2019) use both types of encoders and show that GCN can help LSTM capture reentrant structures and long-range dependencies, albeit on a different problem than ours. Our method also uses the two types of encoders but instead of using one to assist the other, it combines them simultaneously to capture their complementary effects.

text: We now examine attachment preferences in English. The models were trained on English Wikipedia. We evaluated the attachment preferences of the RNN language models using both the original stimuli from Fernández (2003) and a larger stimulus set, in order to obtain a better characterization of model behavior across a broader range of examples. For reasons of space, we report here only the results for the EXTENDED DATA set, the larger stimulus collection, although comparable patterns are observed for the Fernández (2003) stimuli (see Supplemental Materials).
source_text: We turn now to model attachment preferences in English. We trained the models using English Wikipedia. We tested the attachment preferences of the RNN LMs using the original stimuli from Fernández (2003) , and using a larger set of stimuli to have a better sense of model behavior on a wider range of stimuli. For space considerations, we only report here results of the EXTENDED DATA (the larger set of stimuli), but similar results hold for the Fernández (2003) stimuli (see Supplemental Materials).

text: Automatic summarization has attracted renewed attention in recent years, owing to the success of contemporary neural network–based methods (Cheng and Lapata, 2016; Nallapati et al., 2016; Nallapati et al., 2017; Zheng and Lapata, 2019) and the availability of large-scale datasets with hundreds of thousands of document-summary pairs (Sandhaus, 2008; Hermann et al., 2015; Grusky et al., 2018; Narayan et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu and Lapata, 2019). To date, most research has focused on summarizing news articles, which are typically relatively short and highly formulaic, following an “inverted pyramid” organization that presents the most essential, novel, and interesting elements of a story first, followed by supporting material and secondary details. This rigid structure is advantageous because important passages can be located in predictable positions—for example, by carrying out a “smart selection” of sentences from the beginning of the document—and because the structure itself can be explicitly incorporated into model design, for instance by encoding the relative and absolute position of each sentence.
source_text: Automatic summarization has enjoyed renewed interest in recent years thanks to the popularity of modern neural network-based approaches (Cheng and Lapata, 2016; Nallapati et al., 2016 Nallapati et al., , 2017 Zheng and Lapata, 2019) and the availability of large-scale datasets containing hundreds of thousands of document-summary pairs (Sandhaus, 2008; Hermann et al., 2015; Grusky et al., 2018; Narayan et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu and Lapata, 2019) . Most efforts to date have concentrated on the summarization of news articles which tend to be relatively short and formulaic following an "inverted pyramid" structure which places the most essential, novel and interesting el- ements of a story in the beginning and supporting material and secondary details afterwards. The rigid structure of news articles is expedient since important passages can be identified in predictable locations (e.g., by performing a "smart selection" of sentences from the beginning of the document) and the structure itself can be explicitly taken into account in model design (e.g., by encoding the relative and absolute position of each sentence).

text: For the error analysis, we select several failed PPVAE examples in Table 9. We divide the errors into two principal categories. (1) Grammatical. Grammatical issues are frequent in NLG. As our analysis indicates, such errors can be alleviated by using a deeper encoder and decoder, together with even more unlabeled data for pre-training. (2) Conditional. Conditional errors are especially relevant to us because they fall within our main focus. We select three representative errors and present them in Table 9. In the first sentence, “shocked” is a nuanced word that may convey either positive or negative sentiment depending on context. Therefore, under a greedy decoding strategy, it may be decoded incorrectly into the opposite polarity. We believe that this type of error could be addressed by more sophisticated decoding strategies (e.g., Weighted Decoding (See et al., 2019)). In the second sentence, the length is constrained by the nature of an interrogative sentence. As a linguistic fact, interrogative sentences often contain fewer words than declarative sentences. In the third sentence, we observe an overlap problem between classes. Some topics (e.g., music album) may occur in both business and entertainment news. In a sense, these samples may also be regarded as correctly conditioned, which underscores the importance of fine-grained human evaluation for this task.
source_text: For the error analysis, we pick some failed examples of PPVAE in Table 9 . We categorize the errors into two main classes. (1) Grammatical. Grammatical problems are common in NLG. As we analyze, this kind of errors can be mitigated with a deeper encoder and decoder with even more unlabeled data for pre-training. (2) Conditional. Conditional errors are of great interest to us since they lie in our focus. We choose three typical errors and list them in Table 9 . In the first sentence, "shocked" is a subtle word which may indicate either positive or negative sentiment depending on the context. Thus, with a greedy decoding strategy, it may be incorrectly decoded into the other polarity. We believe this kind of errors could be fixed with more elaborate decoding strategies (e.g., Weighted Decoding (See et al., 2019) ). In the second sentence, the length is limited by the nature of an interrogative sentence. As a linguistic fact, an interrogative sentence often has fewer words than a declarative sentence. In the third sentence, we remark an overlapping problem between classes. Some topics (e.g., music album) may appear in both business and entertainment news. In some way, these samples can also be considered as correctly conditioned ones, which highlights the importance of a fine-grained human evaluation on this task.

text: Other studies have highlighted the importance of characters and their development for story comprehension (Bamman et al., 2013, 2014; Chaturvedi et al., 2017; Iyyer et al., 2016) and for summarisation (Gorinski and Lapata, 2018). Another key component of narrative organisation is plot, that is, the ordering of events through which characters interact. Neural approaches have explicitly represented events (Martin et al., 2018; Harrison et al., 2017; Rashkin et al., 2018) or the outcomes of actions (Roemmele and Gordon, 2018; Liu et al., 2018a,b). By contrast, some neural generation models (Fan et al., 2018) simply employ a hierarchical model on top of a language model; our architecture adopts this strategy.
source_text: Other work emphasises the role of characters and their development in story understanding (Bamman et al., 2014 (Bamman et al., , 2013 Chaturvedi et al., 2017; Iyyer et al., 2016) or summarisation (Gorinski and Lapata, 2018) . A further important element of narrative structure is plot, i.e., the sequence of events in which characters interact. Neural models have explicitly modelled events (Martin et al., 2018; Harrison et al., 2017; Rashkin et al., 2018) or the results of actions (Roemmele and Gordon, 2018; Liu et al., 2018a,b) . On the other hand, some neural generation models (Fan et al., 2018) just use a hierarchical model on top of a language model; our architecture follows this approach.

text: Parameter Tuning. We observe that the performance of cascaded models can be affected substantially by model configurations such as beam width and the choice of ASR target preprocessing. Although Weiss et al. (2017) and Sperber et al. (2019) employ character targets for ASR, we use BPE, which yields an average improvement of 2 BLEU. Moreover, we note that the decoding search space has a major effect on cascaded model performance. In cascaded systems, errors introduced by ASR may be unrecoverable, since the MT module has access only to the ASR output. Although Sperber et al. (2019) use a beam size of 1 for the ASR component of their cascade to compare with their two-stage end-to- 202037.9 37.8 --20.1 20.2 Table 2 : End-to-end vs cascaded speech translation model performance in BLEU↑ on Fisher Spanish-English data from the literature. ( †) denotes the best previous academic result on the full dataset, ( * ) the best from industry. Component models for cascades reported on test on full dataset: ASR reported in WER↓ and MT in BLEU↑.
source_text: Parameter Tuning. We find cascaded model performance can be impacted significantly by model settings such as beam size and choice of ASR target preprocessing. While Weiss et al. (2017); Sperber et al. (2019) use character targets for ASR, we use BPE, which gave us an average increase of 2 BLEU. Further, we note that search space in decoding has significant impact on cascaded model performance. In cascaded models, errors produced by ASR can be unrecoverable, as the MT component has access only to ASR output. While Sperber et al. (2019) use a beam of size 1 for the ASR component of their cascade to compare with their two-stage end-to- 202037.9 37.8 --20.1 20.2 Table 2 : End-to-end vs cascaded speech translation model performance in BLEU↑ on Fisher Spanish-English data from the literature. ( †) denotes the best previous academic result on the full dataset, ( * ) the best from industry. Component models for cascades reported on test on full dataset: ASR reported in WER↓ and MT in BLEU↑.

text: This paper investigates how the pretraining advantages for downstream tasks vary as the number of training samples grows. To address this question, we concentrate on multi-class text classification because: (i) it is one of the most important problems in NLP, with applications across multiple domains; and (ii) large amounts of training data are available for many text classification tasks, or can be collected at relatively low cost through crowd workers (Snow et al., 2008). We select three sentiment classification datasets: Yelp review (yel, 2019) and Amazon sports and electronics reviews (Ni et al., 2019), with dataset sizes ranging from 6 to 18 million examples. 2 We fine-tune a RoBERTa model with progressively larger portions of the downstream dataset and evaluate performance at each increment. For instance, on the Yelp dataset, which contains 6 million examples, we train models on subsets whose sizes follow the sequence (60k, 600K, 1.8M, 3M, .., 6M). For comparison, we also train a standard BiLSTM and another BiLSTM that uses pretrained RoBERTa token embeddings. We find that when both models are trained on 1% of the data, the performance gap between BiLSTM and RoBERTa is largest, but as the training set size increases, BiLSTM accuracy continues to improve whereas RoBERTa accuracy remains largely unchanged. As the dataset grows, the accuracy gap narrows to within 1%.
source_text: This paper examines the pretraining benefits of downstream tasks as the number of training samples increases. To answer this question, we focus on multi-class text classification since: (i) it is one of most important problems in NLP with applications spanning multiple domains. (ii) large sums of training data exists for many text classification tasks, or can be obtained relatively cheaply through crowd workers (Snow et al., 2008) . We choose three sentiment classification datasets: Yelp review (yel, 2019), Amazon sports and electronics review (Ni et al., 2019) , ranging in size from 6 to 18 million examples. 2 We finetune a RoBERTa model ) with increments of the downstream dataset, and evaluate the performance at each increment. For example, on the Yelp dataset whose size is 6 million, we train the models on subsets of the data with each subset size being in the sequence (60k, 600K, 1.8M, 3M .., 6M). For comparison, we also train a vanilla BiLSTM, and another BiLSTM which uses pretrained Roberta token embeddings. We observe that when both models are trained on 1% of the data, the gap between BiLSTM and RoBERTa models is at its peak, but as the training dataset size increases, the BiLSTM model accuracy keeps on increasing whereas RoBERTa's accuracy remain mostly flat. As the dataset size increases, the accuracy gap shrinks to within 1%.

text: In the setting of structured prediction for natural language processing, active learning has been investigated both for eliciting complete structured outputs (e.g. Thompson et al., 1999; Culotta and Mc-Callum, 2005; Hachey et al., 2005) and for eliciting only parts of outputs (e.g. Ringger et al., 2007; Bloodgood and Callison-Burch, 2010). For sequence labeling tasks, Haertel et al. (2008) showed that annotation effort depends on both the number of words labeled, which we model, and a fixed reading cost, which we do not model.
source_text: In the context of structured prediction for natural language processing, active learning has been considered both for requesting full structured outputs (e.g. Thompson et al., 1999; Culotta and Mc-Callum, 2005; Hachey et al., 2005) and for requesting only pieces of outputs (e.g. Ringger et al., 2007; Bloodgood and Callison-Burch, 2010) . For sequence labeling tasks, Haertel et al. (2008) found that labeling effort depends both on the number of words labeled (which we model), plus a fixed cost for reading (which we do not).

text: (a) Intersection/Union (b) Bidir. Optimization (c) Gold Alignments Figure 8: An example of symmetrization using bidirectional attention optimization. In Subfigure 8a, we display all alignments extracted in the forward and backward directions with unidirectional attention optimization, with alignments appearing in only one direction shown in gray. Bidirectional attention optimization is able to recover the correct alignment between “übereinstimmend” and “proven,” even though this link does not appear in either the forward or the backward direction.
source_text: (a) Intersection/Union (b) Bidir. Optimization (c) Gold Alignments Figure 8 : Example of symmetrization with bidirectional attention optimization. We show all alignments extracted from the forward and backward direction with unidirectional attention optimization in Subfigure 8a (alignments that are only present in one direction are grey). Bidirectional attention optimization is able to extract the correct alignment between "übereinstimmend" and "proven" which did neither appear as an alignment link in the forward nor in the backward direction.

text:
‘Have thou and I been borne beyond pursuer,
And here we are.’—Then, turning to the steed,
She press’d the white moon on his brow with pure
And roseate lips, and many a scented weed
From the green waste she pluck’d, that he might feed;—
But I to a stone seat led that fair Maid,
And, kissing her clear eyes, said, ‘Thou hast need
Of rest,’ and heap’d the courser’s couch array’d
In a green mossy nook, with mountain flowers display’d.
source_text:
‘Have thou and I been borne beyond pursuer,
And we are here.’—Then, turning to the steed,
She pressed the white moon on his front with pure
And rose-like lips, and many a fragrant weed
From the green ruin plucked, that he might feed;—
But I to a stone seat that Maiden led,
And, kissing her fair eyes, said, ‘Thou hast need
Of rest,’ and I heaped up the courser’s bed
In a green mossy nook, with mountain flowers dispread.

text:
Divine am I within and without, and I hallow all I lay my hand on, or am  
touch’d by,  
The fragrance of these arm-pits, an aroma finer than prayer,  
This head more than churches, bibles, and all the creeds.
source_text:
Divine am I inside and out, and I make holy whatever I touch or am
touch’d from,
The scent of these arm-pits aroma finer than prayer,
This head more than churches, bibles, and all the creeds.

text:
And soon I could not have denied her—thus  
For ever, day and night, we two were ne’er  
Parted, save when brief sleep sundered us:  
And when the pauses of the lulling air  
At noon beside the sea had made a lair  
For her soothed senses, in my arms she slept,  
And I kept watch above her slumbers there,  
While, as the shifting visions o’er her swept,  
Amid her innocent rest by turns she smiled and wept.
source_text:
And soon I could not have refused her—thus
For ever, day and night, we two were ne’er
Parted, but when brief sleep divided us:
And when the pauses of the lulling air
Of noon beside the sea had made a lair
For her soothed senses, in my arms she slept,
And I kept watch over her slumbers there,
While, as the shifting visions over her swept,
Amid her innocent rest by turns she smiled and wept.

text:
Thou, mother of my mortal part,  
With cruelty didst fashion my heart,  
And with thy false self-deceiving tears  
Didst darken nostrils, eyes, and ears,
source_text:
Thou, mother of my mortal part,
With cruelty didst mould my heart,
And with false self-deceiving tears
Didst blind my nostrils, eyes, and ears,

text:
See, as they creep along the river’s side,
How she doth whisper to that aged dame,
And, after glancing round the champaign wide,
Displays to her a knife.--"What feverish hectic flame
Burns in thee, child?--What good can thee betide,
That thou should'st smile once more?"--The evening came,
And they had found Lorenzo’s earthy bed;
The flint was there, the berries at his head.
source_text:
See, as they creep along the river side,
How she doth whisper to that aged Dame,
And, after looking round the champaign wide,
Shows her a knife.--"What feverous hectic flame
Burns in thee, child?--What good can thee betide,
That thou should'st smile again?"--The evening came,
And they had found Lorenzo's earthy bed;
The flint was there, the berries at his head.

text:
‘Nay, seek not to say that thy love shall not go,
But spare me those ages of horror and woe,
For I swear unto thee here that I’ll perish ere day,
If thou goest unattended by Agnes away’—
source_text:
‘Nay seek not to say that thy love shall not go,
But spare me those ages of horror and woe,
For I swear to thee here that I’ll perish ere day,
If you go unattended by Agnes away’—

text:
Forbidden fruit a savor hath  
That mocks the lawful grove;  
How luscious lies the little pea  
Within the pod by Duty wove!
source_text:
Forbidden fruit a flavor has
That lawful orchards mocks;
How luscious lies the pea within
The pod that Duty locks!

text:
The sun but kissed the dawning;  
The dawning, glad and bright,  
Supposed that he had come to bide,  
And life should all be spring.
source_text:
The sun just touched the morning;
The morning, happy thing,
Supposed that he had come to dwell,
And life would be all spring.

text:
Nor whisper it at table,
Nor careless on the way
Intimate that within the riddle
One shall walk this day!
source_text:
Nor lisp it at the table,
Nor heedless by the way
Hint that within the riddle
One will walk to-day!

text:
The Powers aboon will watch thee,
Misfortune shallna steer thee;
Thou art like themselves sae lovely,
That ill they’ll ne’er come near thee.
source_text:
The Powers aboon will tent thee,
Misfortune sha’na steer thee;
Thou’rt like themselves sae lovely,
That ill they’ll ne’er let near thee.

text:
CYPRIAN:
How strange that, in the very sight
Of Antioch’s high towers, you should lose
Your way. Of all the avenues and green paths
Within this wild wood, there is not one but leads,
As to its heart, unto the walls of Antioch;
Take which you will, you cannot miss your road.
source_text:
CYPRIAN:
’Tis singular that even within the sight
Of the high towers of Antioch you could lose
Your way. Of all the avenues and green paths
Of this wild wood there is not one but leads,
As to its centre, to the walls of Antioch;
Take which you will, you cannot miss your road.

text:
Ma femme! for the brood beyond us and of us,
For those who belong here and those to come,
I, exultant, to be ready for them, will now shake forth carols stronger
and loftier than have yet been heard upon earth.
source_text:
Ma femme! for the brood beyond us and of us,
For those who belong here and those to come,
I exultant to be ready for them will now shake out carols stronger
and haughtier than have ever yet been heard upon earth.

text:
’Twas in the seventeen hunder year
O’ grace, and ninety-five,
That year I was the waefullest man
Of any man alive.
source_text:
’Twas in the seventeen hunder year
O’ grace, and ninety-five,
That year I was the wae’est man
Of ony man alive.

text:
I behold the mariners of the world,  
Some are in tempests, some in the night with the watch set on the lookout,  
Some drifting helplessly, some stricken with contagious diseases.
source_text:
I behold the mariners of the world,
Some are in storms, some in the night with the watch on the lookout,
Some drifting helplessly, some with contagious diseases.

text:
Low-hanging moon!  
What is that dusky blot in thy brown-gold?  
Ah, it is the shape, the shape of my mate.  
O moon, keep her not from me any longer.
source_text:
Low-hanging moon!
What is that dusky spot in your brown yellow?
O it is the shape, the shape of my mate.’
O moon do not keep her from me any longer.

text:
Soft dreams, cast a veil
O’er my dear child’s tender brow!
Soft dreams of gentle streams
Beneath glad, quiet, moonlit beams!
source_text:
Sweet dreams, form a shade
O’er my lovely infant’s head!
Sweet dreams of pleasant streams
By happy, silent, moony beams!

text:
What fortitude the soul contains,
That it can thus endure
The cadence of an approaching tread,
The opening of a door!
source_text:
What fortitude the soul contains,
That it can so endure
The accent of a coming foot,
The opening of a door!

text:
A cat in grievous distress,  
Naught more, nor less;  
Good folk, I must truly tell ye,  
As I am a sinner,  
It bides for some dinner  
To stuff out its own wee belly.
source_text:
A cat in distress,
Nothing more, nor less;
Good folks, I must faithfully tell ye,
As I am a sinner,
It waits for some dinner
To stuff out its own little belly.

text:
Others attain fulfilment, but the Republic is still in the making and ever
holds the far horizon,
Others deck the past, but you O days of the present, I deck you,
O days of the future I trust in you--I withdraw apart for your sake,
O America because thou buildest for mankind I build for thee,
O well-beloved stone-cutters, I guide them who devise with resolve
and science,
Lead the present with a friendly hand toward the future.
(Bravas to all impulses sending sane children to the next age!
But damn that which spends itself with no thought of the stain,
pains, dismay, feebleness, it is bequeathing.)
source_text:
Others take finish, but the Republic is ever constructive and ever
keeps vista,
Others adorn the past, but you O days of the present, I adorn you,
O days of the future I believe in you--I isolate myself for your sake,
O America because you build for mankind I build for you,
O well-beloved stone-cutters, I lead them who plan with decision
and science,
Lead the present with friendly hand toward the future.
(Bravas to all impulses sending sane children to the next age!
But damn that which spends itself with no thought of the stain,
pains, dismay, feebleness, it is bequeathing.)

text:
O Ayr! my own, my native soil,
Within thy presbyterial pile
A candid, liberal band doth toil
Of public teachers,
As men, as Christians, famed the while,
And manly preachers.
source_text:
O Ayr! my dear, my native ground,
Within thy presbyterial bound
A candid liberal band is found
Of public teachers,
As men, as Christians too, renown’d,
An’ manly preachers.

text:
Praise be to drink! it brings us mair
Than either school or college;
It kindles wit, it wakens lear,
It fills us fou wi’ knowledge:
Be’t whisky-gill or penny wheep,
Or ony sterner potion,
It never fails, when drink we deep,
To quicken up our notion,
By night or day.
source_text:
Leeze me on drink! it gies us mair
Than either school or college;
It kindles wit, it waukens lear,
It pangs us fou o’ knowledge:
Be’t whisky-gill or penny wheep,
Or ony stronger potion,
It never fails, or drinkin deep,
To kittle up our notion,
By night or day.

text:
O the magnet! the flesh again and again!
Go, dear friend, if need be give up all else, and begin to-day to
school yourself to pluck, reality, self-respect, exactness,
loftiness,
Rest not till you rivet and proclaim yourself out of your own Personality.
source_text:
O the magnet! the flesh over and over!
Go, dear friend, if need be give up all else, and commence to-day to
inure yourself to pluck, reality, self-esteem, definiteness,
elevatedness,
Rest not till you rivet and publish yourself of your own Personality.

text:
How blessed the hermit’s portion,
Who, all forgetting, all forgot,
Within his lowly cell,
The cavern wild with interwoven roots,
Sits o’er his freshly gathered fruits,
Beside his crystal well!

Or, perhaps, to his evening musing,
By some untraveled stream,
The ways of men come drifting in,
A faint and gathered dream;
While praising, and raising
His thoughts to heaven on high,
As, wandering, meandering,
He looks upon the solemn sky.
source_text:
How blest the solitary’s lot,
Who, all-forgetting, all forgot,
Within his humble cell,
The cavern, wild with tangling roots,
Sits o’er his newly gather’d fruits,
Beside his crystal well!
Or haply, to his ev’ning thought,
By unfrequented stream,
The ways of men are distant brought,
A faint, collected dream;
While praising, and raising
His thoughts to heav’n on high,
As wand’ring, meand’ring,
He views the solemn sky.

text:
A sight of joy and marvel to behold  
That river’s forms and shadows ever changing,  
Where the wide dawn brimmed over with deep gold  
Its whirling pools, where every tint was ranging;  
And where sweet falls in song and spray were ranging  
Among rocks garlanded with flowers, the foam and spray  
Shone like bright stars upon the sunlit river,  
Or when the moonlight shed a holier day,  
One broad, bright lake lay round green islands, glittering gray.
source_text:
A scene of joy and wonder to behold
That river’s shapes and shadows changing ever,
Where the broad sunrise filled with deepening gold
Its whirlpools, where all hues did spread and quiver;
And where melodious falls did burst and shiver
Among rocks clad with flowers, the foam and spray
Sparkled like stars upon the sunny river,
Or when the moonlight poured a holier day,
One vast and glittering lake around green islands lay.

text:
Wild the tempest howls around,
Wild the flashing lightnings dart,
Crashing thunder rocks the ground,
Fire and tumult fill the heart.—
source_text:
Fierce the tempest raves around,
Fierce the volleyed lightnings fly,
Crashing thunder shakes the ground,
Fire and tumult fill the sky.—

text:
Each one greets me as he passes,
And I my childish feathers
Raise, in grieving acknowledgment
Of their unconsidered drums.
source_text:
Each one salutes me as he goes,
And I my childish plumes
Lift, in bereaved acknowledgment
Of their unthinking drums.

text:
A boundlessness, like a neighbor, came, --
A wisdom with no face, no name,
A peace, as if the hemispheres were home, --
And then the night became.
source_text:
A vastness, as a neighbor, came, --
A wisdom without face or name,
A peace, as hemispheres at home, --
And so the night became.

text:
The shrines of Fear and Falsehood hear no more  
That voice which once aroused the throngs to war  
Pealing through all their arches: now they answer  
Only the funeral song of the sad wind:  
It were a thing of dread to witness there  
The deeds of faith and bondage, vast in scale,  
So rich in show, and yet so swiftly waning!  
Like some dead body lying by their walls.  
A thousand mourners veil the pageant’s close  
Today, and breathing marble shines above  
To keep its name alive, while lips are bent  
In talk of what it was: tomorrow, worms  
In hush and in the blackness claim their spoil.  
These wrecks shall soon leave not a trace behind:  
Their particles, strewn broad across the earth,  
Are shaped to fairer uses, and become  
Instruments of every blessed motion:  
So are mankind made whole, and earth itself,  
Like a child folded in its mother’s love,  
Is braced in all good qualities, and grows  
More lovely and more noble year by year.
source_text:
The fanes of Fear and Falsehood hear no more
The voice that once waked multitudes to war
Thundering thro’ all their aisles: but now respond
To the death dirge of the melancholy wind:
It were a sight of awfulness to see
The works of faith and slavery, so vast,
So sumptuous, yet withal so perishing!
Even as the corpse that rests beneath their wall.
A thousand mourners deck the pomp of death
To-day, the breathing marble glows above
To decorate its memory, and tongues
Are busy of its life: to-morrow, worms
In silence and in darkness seize their prey.
These ruins soon leave not a wreck behind:
Their elements, wide-scattered o’er the globe,
To happier shapes are moulded, and become
Ministrant to all blissful impulses:
Thus human things are perfected, and earth,
Even as a child beneath its mother’s love,
Is strengthened in all excellence, and grows
Fairer and nobler with each passing year.

text:
If I should die,
And you should live,
And time should murmur on,
And dawn should shine,
And noon should blaze,
As it has always done;
If birds should build as early,
And bees as busy fare, —
One might withdraw at leisure
From traffic here below!
’T is sweet to know that stocks will stand
When we with daisies lie,
That commerce will go forward,
And trade still hasten by.
It makes the leaving gentler
And keeps the spirit calm,
That gentlemen so cheerful
Still carry on the charm!
source_text:
If I should die,
And you should live,
And time should gurgle on,
And morn should beam,
And noon should burn,
As it has usual done;
If birds should build as early,
And bees as bustling go, --
One might depart at option
From enterprise below!
'T is sweet to know that stocks will stand
When we with daisies lie,
That commerce will continue,
And trades as briskly fly.
It makes the parting tranquil
And keeps the soul serene,
That gentlemen so sprightly
Conduct the pleasing scene!

text:
PAGE 5. l. 28. new passion. He has come often before to earth  
on errands much the same. Cf. ever-smitten, l. 7,  
and also ll. 80-93.
source_text:
PAGE 5. l. 28. passion new. He has often before been to earth on
similar errands. Cf. ever-smitten, l. 7, also ll. 80-93.

text:
The noon lay still and clear:—around that pillar  
The arching heaven and encircling sea  
Unrolled in hush most deep and grave and solemn  
The shadow of a brief madness over me,  
So that I knew not then my own misery:  
The islands and the mountains in the day  
Lay far off, cloudlike in their repose; and I could see  
The town among the woods below that lay,  
And the dark rocks that bounded the bright and glassy bay.
source_text:
The noon was calm and bright:—around that column
The overhanging sky and circling sea
Spread forth in silentness profound and solemn
The darkness of brief frenzy cast on me,
So that I knew not my own misery:
The islands and the mountains in the day
Like clouds reposed afar; and I could see
The town among the woods below that lay,
And the dark rocks which bound the bright and glassy bay.

text:
Children of the days to come,
Reading this resentful page,
Know that in a bygone time
Love, sweet love, was held a crime.
source_text:
Children of the future age,
Reading this indignant page,
Know that in a former time
Love, sweet love, was thought a crime.

text:
I leave myself to the soil, to rise again from the grass I cherish,  
If you would have me once more, seek me beneath your boot soles.
source_text:
I bequeath myself to the dirt to grow from the grass I love,
If you want me again look for me under your boot-soles.

text:
Till seraphs wave their snowy hats,  
And saints hurry to the panes,  
To watch the tiny tippler  
Bent up against the sun!
source_text:
Till seraphs swing their snowy hats,
And saints to windows run,
To see the little tippler
Leaning against the sun!

text:
Yet were those Florentines as self-withdrawn
In hungry pride and profit-loving fear,
As two close Hebrews in that land inspired,
Fenced pale and vineyarded from beggar-spies;
The hawks of ship-mast forests—the unwearied
And panniered mules for ducats and old lies--
Quick cat's-paws on the generous stray-away,--
Great wits in Spanish, Tuscan, and Malay.
source_text:
Yet were these Florentines as self-retired
In hungry pride and gainful cowardice,
As two close Hebrews in that land inspired,
Paled in and vineyarded from beggar-spies;
The hawks of ship-mast forests--the untired
And pannier'd mules for ducats and old lies--
Quick cat's-paws on the generous stray-away,--
Great wits in Spanish, Tuscan, and Malay.

text: Cross-lingual Representations. Cross-lingual text representations provide a means of capturing meaning across languages and enable cross-lingual transfer for downstream tasks (Klementiev et al., 2012; Rücklé et al., 2018; Josifoski et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). More recently, massively multilingual encoders such as multilingual M-BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM-on-RoBERTa (Conneau et al., 2020), and the sentence-based LASER have emerged as state-of-the-art methods for massively multilingual semantic encoding of text. Whereas LASER is jointly trained on parallel data from 93 languages, M-BERT is trained on a concatenation of monolingual data in more than 100 languages, without any explicit cross-lingual mapping signal. The cross-lingual capabilities of M-BERT have recently been the subject of active discussion (Pires et al., 2019; K et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2020). In particular, Cao et al. 2020 show that M-BERT often produces divergent vector-space representations for mutual translations and propose a multilingual remapping based on parallel corpora to address this problem. In this work, we propose remapping solutions that are more resource-efficient and rely on easily obtainable, small-scale word translation dictionaries rather than large parallel corpora.
source_text: Cross-lingual Representations. Cross-lingual text representations offer a prospect of modeling meaning across languages and support crosslingual transfer for downstream tasks (Klementiev et al., 2012; Rücklé et al., 2018; Josifoski et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020) . Most recently, the (massively) multilingual encoders, such as multilingual M-BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) , XLM-on-RoBERTa (Conneau et al., 2020) , and (sentence-based) LASER, have profiled themselves as state-of-the-art solutions for (massively) multilingual semantic encoding of text. While LASER has been jointly trained on parallel data of 93 languages, M-BERT has been trained on the concatenation of monolingual data in more than 100 languages, without any cross-lingual mapping signal. There has been a recent vivid discussion on the cross-lingual abilities of M-BERT (Pires et al., 2019; K et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2020) . In particular, Cao et al. 2020show that M-BERT often yields disparate vector space representations for mutual translations and propose a multilingual remapping based on parallel corpora, to remedy for this issue. In this work, we introduce re-mapping solutions that are resource-leaner and require easyto-obtain limited-size word translation dictionaries rather than large parallel corpora.

text: Knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Ba and Caruana, 2014), which was previously applied in computer vision, offers one method for compressing large neural networks into smaller ones. In this framework, shallow models, referred to as students, are trained to imitate the outputs of large models, referred to as teachers, using a transfer set. Related approaches have more recently been introduced for language model distillation. Limitations of prior work: Recent studies (Zhu et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2019; Turc et al., 2019) use soft logits from teachers as optimization objectives for distilling students, with some notable exceptions in concurrent work. Sun et al. (2019), Sanh (2019), Aguilar et al. (2019), and Zhao et al. (2019) additionally exploit internal teacher representations as extra signals. However, these methods are limited by architectural constraints such as the embedding dimension in BERT and the transformer architecture. As a result, it is difficult to substantially compress models, since network width cannot be reduced, or to adopt alternative architectures. For example, we find that BiLSTMs used as students are more accurate than Transformers in low-latency settings. Some concurrent works (Turc et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019) employ pre-training or dual training to distill students with arbitrary architectures. However, pre-training is costly in both time and computational resources.
source_text: Knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Ba and Caruana, 2014) earlier used in computer vision provides one of the techniques to compress huge neural networks into smaller ones. In this, shallow models (called students) are trained to mimic the output of huge models (called teachers) based on a transfer set. Similar approaches have been recently adopted for language model distillation. Limitations of existing work: Recent works Zhu et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2019; Turc et al., 2019) leverage soft logits from teachers as optimization targets for distilling students, with some notable exceptions from concurrent work. Sun et al. (2019) ; Sanh (2019) ; Aguilar et al. (2019) ; Zhao et al. (2019) additionally use internal teacher representations as additional signals. However, these methods are constrained by architectural considerations like embedding dimension in BERT and transformer architecture. This makes it difficult to massively compress models (without being able to reduce network width) or adopt alternate architecture. For instance, we observe BiLSTMS as students to be more accurate than Transformers for low latency configurations. Some concurrent works (Turc et al., 2019) ; (Zhao et al., 2019) adopt pre-training or dual training to distil students of arbitrary architecture. However, pre-training is expensive in terms of time and computational resources.

text: Asking Clarifying Questions: Another pertinent line of research has aimed to extend semantic parsers beyond one-shot interaction by developing agents that can pose clarifying questions to resolve ambiguities in the original query. For instance, Yao et al. (2019a) demonstrated that reinforcement-learning-based agents capable of asking clarifying questions can enhance semantic parsing performance in the “If-Then recipes” domain. The generation of clarifying questions has also been investigated across several domains, including addressing ambiguity arising from speech recognition errors (Stoyanchev et al., 2014), recovering omitted information in question answering (Rao and Daumé III, 2018), and clarifying information needs in open-domain information-seeking (Aliannejadi et al., 2019). Our work differs from this body of research in that we concentrate on enabling and exploiting human feedback that corrects an initial parse of a fully specified question, rather than detecting and clarifying ambiguities.
source_text: Asking Clarifying Questions: Another relevant research direction focused on extending semantic parsers beyond one-shot interactions by creating agents that can ask clarifying questions that resolve ambiguities with the original question. For example, Yao et al. (2019a) showed that using reinforcement learning based agents that can ask clarifying questions can improve the performance of semantic parsers in the "If-Then recipes" domain. Generating clarifying questions have been studied in multiple domains to resolve ambiguity caused by speech recognition failure (Stoyanchev et al., 2014) , recover missing information in question answering (Rao and Daumé III, 2018) or clarify information needs in open-domain informationseeking (Aliannejadi et al., 2019) . Our work is different from this research in that we focus on enabling and leveraging human feedback that corrects an initial parse of a fully specified question rather than spotting and clarifying ambiguities.

text: To date, prior work has not assessed the efficacy of the practices described in Section 3. In the next section, we conduct experiments to determine whether reporting estimates derived from small sample sizes is justified, and to gain a better understanding of the effects of peeking and of scaling the proposal distribution’s temperature. For RNNG and KGLM, we use the pretrained model weights. For EntityNLM, we train the model from scratch according to the procedure of Ji et al. (2017); the results may not be directly comparable because of differences in data preprocessing and hyperparameters. We evaluate the models on the datasets used in their original papers: RNNG on the Penn Treebank corpus (Marcus et al., 1993), EntityNLM on English data from the CoNLL 2012 shared task (Pradhan et al., 2014), and KGLM on the Linked WikiText-2 corpus (Logan et al., 2019).
source_text: Thus far, research has neglected to measure the effectiveness of the practices detailed in Section 3. In the following section, we perform experiments to determine whether reporting estimates obtained from small sample sizes is warranted, as well as better understand the consequences of peeking and scaling the temperature of the proposal distribution. For RNNG and KGLM we use the pre-trained model weights. For EntityNLM we train the model from scratch following the procedure described by Ji et al. (2017) ; results may not be directly comparable due to differences in data preprocessing and hyperparameters. We evaluate models on the datasets used in their original papers: RNNG is evaluated on the Penn Treebank corpus (Marcus et al., 1993) , EntityNLM is evaluated on English data from the CoNLL 2012 shared task (Pradhan et al., 2014) , and KGLM is evaluated on the Linked WikiText-2 corpus (Logan et al., 2019) .

text: producing remarkably coherent text (Zellers et al., 2019; See et al., 2019), (2) efficient text generation, and (3) conceptual simplicity, but they cannot infill effectively because they can exploit context only in one direction, usually the past. By contrast, approaches such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and SpanBERT (Joshi et al., 2019) can infill by using both preceding and following text. However, their bidirectional attention confines their infilling ability to fixed-length spans. This is problematic because, for many applications, the length of a missing span may not be known a priori. Zhu et al. (2019) introduce a method that can infill variable-length spans, but it relies on a specialized architecture and therefore cannot readily take advantage of large-scale pre-trained models.
source_text: ing remarkably coherent text (Zellers et al., 2019; See et al., 2019) , (2) efficient at generating text, and (3) conceptually simple, but cannot infill effectively as they can only leverage context in a single direction (usually the past). On the other hand, strategies such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and SpanBERT (Joshi et al., 2019) are able to infill using both preceding and subsequent text. However, their use of bidirectional attention limits their infilling capabilities to fixed-length spans. This is problematic as-for many applications-we may not know the length of a missing span a priori. Zhu et al. (2019) propose a method capable of infilling variable-length spans, but it uses a specialized architecture and hence cannot easily leverage large-scale pre-trained models.

text: Autonomous agents, such as domestic robots, must engage with the physical world across multiple modalities. For instance, in vision-and-language navigation (VLN) (Anderson et al., 2018), the agent traverses a photorealistic simulated environment (Chang et al., 2017) by adhering to a sequence of natural language instructions. To determine its current location and thereby choose its actions, the agent integrates visual perception, its trajectory, and the instructions (Fried et al., 2018; Anderson et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019a,b).
source_text: Autonomous agents such as household robots need to interact with the physical world in multiple modalities. As an example, in vision-and-language navigation (VLN) (Anderson et al., 2018) , the agent moves around in a photo-realistic simulated environment (Chang et al., 2017) by following a sequence of natural language instructions. To infer its whereabouts so as to decide its moves, the agent infuses its visual perception, its trajectory and the instructions (Fried et al., 2018; Anderson et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019a,b) .

text: To address these shortcomings, we propose a new method for VLN. The agent carries out a long navigation instruction by breaking it down into shorter instructions, or “micro-instructions” (i.e., BABY-STEPs), each of which defines an intermediate goal or task to be completed in sequence. To this end, the agent comprises three components: (a) a memory buffer that condenses the agent’s experiences so that they can be used to supply context for executing the next BABY-STEP; (b) in an initial “bitesize” learning phase, the agent learns from human experts. Rather than attempting to imitate the ground-truth paths in their entirety, the agent is provided with pairs consisting of a BABY-STEP and the corresponding human expert path, enabling it to learn action policies from shorter instructions; (c) in the second learning stage, the agent further refines these policies through curriculum-based reinforcement learning, in which it is presented with progressively longer navigation tasks to solve. In particular, this curriculum scheme reflects our desideratum that an agent optimized on shorter tasks should generalize effectively to slightly longer tasks and, eventually, to much longer ones.
source_text: To address these deficiencies, we propose a new approach for VLN. The agent follows a long navigation instruction by decomposing the instruction into shorter ones ("micro-instructions", i.e., BABY-STEPs) , each of which corresponds to an intermediate goal/task to be executed sequentially. To this end, the agent has three components: (a) a memory buffer that summarizes the agent's experiences so that the agent can use them to provide the context for executing the next BABY-STEP. (b) the agent first learns from human experts in "bitesize". Instead of trying to imitate to achieve the ground-truth paths as a whole, the agent is given the pairs of a BABY-STEP and the corresponding human expert path so that it can learn policies of actions from shorter instructions. (c) In the second stage of learning, the agent refines the policies by curriculum-based reinforcement learning, where the agent is given increasingly longer navigation tasks to achieve. In particular, this curriculum design reflects our desiderata that the agent optimized on shorter tasks should generalize well to slightly longer tasks and then much longer ones.

text: We begin by examining the standard phonetic classification results, reported in the column with a time shift of 0. It can be observed that APC features, irrespective of the objective used (L f or L m ), yield lower FER than log Mel features, indicating that the phonetic information encoded in the surface representations has been converted into a more readily accessible form, defined here in terms of greater linear separability. In addition, APC features trained with L m consistently outperform those trained with L f for all n. For n ≥ 5, where adding L r produces a clear gain in future prediction as shown in Figure 2b, the corresponding improvement in phonetic classification is also greater than for n ≤ 3. This result suggests that APC models that are more effective at forecasting the future also learn representations with richer phonetic content. It is also noteworthy that, when using L f , the best performance is obtained at n = 5 (31.9), whereas with L m , the lowest FER (27.8) is achieved at n = 7.
source_text: We first study the standard phonetic classification results, shown in the column where time shift is 0. We see that APC features, regardless of the objective (L f or L m ), achieve lower FER than log Mel features, showing that the phonetic information contained in the surface features has been transformed into a more accessible form (defined as how linearly separable they are). Additionally, we see that APC features learned by L m outperform those learned by L f across all n. For n ≥ 5 where there is a noticeable improvement in future prediction after adding L r as shown in Figure 2b , their improvement in phonetic classification is also larger than when n ≤ 3. Such an outcome suggests that APC models that are better at predicting the future do learn representations that contain richer phonetic content. It is also interesting that when using L f , the best result occurs at n = 5 (31.9); while with L m , it is when n = 7 that achieves the lowest FER (27.8).

text: Although earlier implementations of TEXT-RANK (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004) determine node similarity from symbolic representations such as tf*idf, we instead adopt a neural approach. In particular, we derive sentence representations using a pretrained encoder. In our experiments, we use the Universal Sentence Encoder (USE; Cer et al. 2018), although other embeddings are also possible. 1 We encode a scene as the mean of its sentence representations and compute scene similarity e i j with cosine similarity. 2 Following the original TEXTRANK algorithm (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004), scenes are ranked according to their centrality, and the M most central scenes are chosen to be included in the summary.
source_text: Although earlier implementations of TEXT-RANK (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004) compute node similarity based on symbolic representations such as tf*idf, we adopt a neural approach. Specifically, we obtain sentence representations based on a pretrained encoder. In our experiments, we rely on the Universal Sentence Encoder (USE; Cer et al. 2018), however, other embeddings are possible. 1 We represent a scene by the mean of its sentence representations and measure scene similarity e i j using cosine. 2 As in the original TEXTRANK algorithm (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004) , scenes are ranked based on their centrality and the M most central ones are selected to appear in the summary.

text: For continuous representations with the GloVe model, we employ the pretrained 100-dimensional embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014). An utterance is represented by averaging the embeddings of its constituent words, with a 100-dimensional zero vector used for words absent from the pretrained vocabulary. On the basis of the pretrained vocabulary, contractions (e.g., “can’t”) are decomposed. For continuous representations with the BERT model, utterances are segmented into sequences of subword tokens according to the authors’ specifications (Devlin et al., 2019), and the pretrained BERT (Base, Uncased) model yields a 768-dimensional, position-dependent representation.
source_text: For continuous representation using the GloVe model, we use the pretrained 100-dimensional embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014) . The representation of an utterance is computed as the average of embeddings for words in the utterance, with 0 100 used to represent words not in the pretrained vocabulary. Based on the pretrained vocabulary, contractions (e.g. "can't") are decomposed. For continuous representation with the BERT model, utterances are split into sequences of sub-word tokens following the authors' specifications (Devlin et al., 2019) , and the pretrained BERT (Base, Uncased) model computes a 768dimensional position-dependent representation.

text: Models. Because our proposed methodology is independent of the specific word emotion model selected, we will reuse models from prior work. In particular, we will depend on the multi-task learning feed-forward network (MTLFFN) developed by Buechel and Hahn (2018b). This network represents the current state of the art for monolingual emotion lexicon construction, that is, expanding an existing lexicon for a particular language, on many of the datasets listed in Table 2.
source_text: Models. Since our proposed methodology is agnostic towards the chosen word emotion model, we will re-use models from the literature. In particular, we will rely on the multi-task learning feed-forward network (MTLFFN) worked out by Buechel and Hahn (2018b) . This network constitutes the current state of the art for monolingual emotion lexicon creation (expanding an existing lexicon for a given language) for many of the datasets in Table 2 .

text: This presents a specific challenge, since most models employed for data preprocessing and representation learning have been trained on written rather than spoken language (Caines et al., 2017). Moreover, most existing approaches to speech grading do have access to audio features and, indeed, extract a wide range of prosodic and duration-based features (Zechner et al., 2009; Higgins et al., 2011; Loukina et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018a). Prosodic and phonological features derived from the audio and ASR model are unquestionably valuable for human evaluation of speech proficiency and for delivering feedback.
source_text: This poses a particular challenge as most models used in data pre-processing and representation learning have been trained on written not spoken texts (Caines et al., 2017) . Furthermore, most existing approaches to speech grading do have access to audio features, and indeed extract a large number of prosodic or duration-based features (Zechner et al., 2009; Higgins et al., 2011; Loukina et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018a) . Prosodic and phonological features extracted from the audio and ASR model are undoubtedly useful for human assessment of speech proficiency and for providing feedback.

text: Several contemporary machine-learning-based Natural Language Processing (NLP) systems can return a confidence score along with their predicted outputs. Such a score may serve as a measure of predictor confidence. A well-calibrated confidence score is a probability measure that is strongly aligned with the likelihood that a model’s output is correct. Consequently, NLP systems with calibrated confidence can anticipate when their predictions are likely to be wrong and thus should not be trusted. This capability is essential for the responsible use of NLP systems in safety-critical settings such as healthcare and finance. Predictor calibration is a well-studied issue in machine learning (Guo et al., 2017; Platt, 2000); however, the widely used approaches in this area are often formulated for binary or multi-class settings (Naeini et al., 2015; Nguyen and O'Connor, 2015). The structured-output formulations of NLP tasks such as information extraction (IE) (Sang and De Meulder, 2003) and extractive question answering (Rajpurkar et al., 2018) have output spaces that are often too large for standard multi-class calibration methods.
source_text: Several modern machine-learning based Natural Language Processing (NLP) systems can provide a confidence score with their output predictions. This score can be used as a measure of predictor confidence. A well-calibrated confidence score is a probability measure that is closely correlated with the likelihood of model output's correctness. As a result, NLP systems with calibrated confidence can predict when their predictions are likely to be incorrect and therefore, should not be trusted. This property is necessary for the responsible deployment of NLP systems in safety-critical domains such as healthcare and finance. Calibration of predictors is a well-studied problem in machine learning (Guo et al., 2017; Platt, 2000) ; however, widely used methods in this domain are often defined as binary or multi-class problems (Naeini et al., 2015; Nguyen and O'Connor, 2015) . The structured output schemes of NLP tasks such as information extraction (IE) (Sang and De Meulder, 2003) and extractive question answering (Rajpurkar et al., 2018) have an output space that is often too large for standard multi-class calibration schemes.

text: Precision@1 (P@1), 5 Max. F1, 6 and Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (PR-AUC). We train all classifier models on the SG training set and evaluate them on the SG test data. The resulting evaluation is reported in Table 2. The results indicate that the shortest response baseline (ShortResp) performs worse than the ML models, with an absolute P@1 gap ranging from 0.14 to 0.51 depending on the model. This confirms that annotation is not driven primarily by presentation bias, in which annotators simply choose the shortest response, i.e., the first one in the list, for each question. The language model baseline (LangModel) performs even more poorly, with an absolute difference of 0.41 to 0.78, showing that the task is unlikely to admit a trivial solution. The feature-based linear model achieves strong performance when trained with Softmax loss, outperforming many neural models in terms of PR-AUC and Max-F1. By examining the weight vector, we find that grammar features—especially the counts of prepositions, determiners, and “to”s in the response—receive the largest weights. This likely suggests that the main difficulty in this task is selecting the appropriate prepositions and determiners in the response. Other salient features are response length and the response’s 3-gram LM probabilities. The apparent limitation of feature-based models is that they fail to identify correct pronouns for unfamiliar named entities in the questions.
source_text: Precision@1 (P@1), 5 Max. F1, 6 and Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (PR-AUC). We train all classifier models on the SG training set and evaluate them on SG test data. The resulting evaluation is presented in Table 2 . The results show that the shortest response baseline (ShortResp) performs worse than the ML models (0.14 to 0.51 absolute P@1 difference depending on the model). This verifies that annotation is not dominated by presentation bias where annotators are just selecting the shortest (first in the list) response for each question. The language model baseline (LangModel) performs even worse (0.41 to 0.78 absolute difference), demonstrating that this task is unlikely to have a trivial solution. The feature-based linear model shows good performance when trained with Softmax loss beating many of the neural models in terms of PR-AUC and Max-F1. By inspecting the weight vector, we find that grammar features, specifically the number of prepositions, determiners, and "to"s in the response, are the features with the highest weights. This probably implies that the most important challenge in this task is finding the right prepositions and determiners in the response. Other important features are the response length and the response's 3-gram LM probabilities. The ostensible limitation of feature-based models is failing to recognize correct pronouns for unfamiliar named entities in the questions.

text: Our approach adopts the sentence-level QA system of Ahmad et al. (2019) for two reasons: (1) answers to many research questions cannot be captured within a brief phrase-level span, and a sentence-level answer can supply additional context needed to provide relevant solutions. (2) our preliminary study showed that a trainable retriever, rather than simple TF-IDF keyword matching, is important to achieve sufficient recall in the paper domain. Nevertheless, we retain a machine reader as an optional post-processing step to extract phrase-level spans from the retrieved sentences.
source_text: Our approach follows the sentence-level QA system from (Ahmad et al., 2019) for two reasons: (1) answers to many research questions cannot be cov-ered in a short phrase-level span, and a sentence answer can provide more context to deliver relevant solutions. (2) our preliminary study found that it is important to have a trainable retriever that goes beyond TF-IDF keyword matching to ensure enough recall in the paper domain. Nonetheless, we keep a machine reader as optional post-process to extract phrase-level span from the sentences.

text: Sarcasm is a type of irony that arises when there is a mismatch between the literal meaning and the intended meaning of an utterance. This mismatch is exploited to convey dissociation from a prior proposition, often taking the form of contempt or derogation (Wilson, 2006). Sarcasm is pervasive in social media text and can be highly disruptive for systems that exploit this data for sentiment and emotion analysis (Maynard and Greenwood, 2014). It is therefore essential to develop models for sarcasm detection. The performance of such models depends on the availability and quality of the labelled data used in training. However, collecting such data is difficult because sarcasm is subjective in nature. For example, Dress et al. (2008) observe a lack of consistency in the way sarcasm is employed by individuals from different socio-cultural backgrounds. Consequently, an utterance intended as sarcastic by its author may not be interpreted as such by audiences from different backgrounds (Rockwell and Theriot, 2001; Oprea and Magdy, 2020).
source_text: Sarcasm is a form of irony that occurs when there is some discrepancy between the literal and intended meanings of an utterance. This discrepancy is used to express dissociation towards a previous proposition, often in the form of contempt or derogation (Wilson, 2006) . Sarcasm is omnipresent in social media text and can be highly disruptive of systems that harness this data for sentiment and emotion analysis (Maynard and Greenwood, 2014) . It is therefore imperative to devise models for sarcasm detection. The effectiveness of such models depends on the availability and quality of labelled data used for training. Collecting such data is challenging due to the subjective nature of sarcasm. For instance, Dress et al. (2008) notice a lack of consistence in how sarcasm is used by people of different socio-cultural backgrounds. As a result, an utterance intended sarcastic by its author might not be perceived as such by audiences of different backgrounds (Rockwell and Theriot, 2001; Oprea and Magdy, 2020) .

text: Overview We introduce a neural model for generating these response timings in SDSs (see Fig. 1). The response timing network (RTNet) uses both acoustic and linguistic features derived from user and system turns. Its two principal modules are an encoder, which represents the system response h z , and an inference network, which accepts as input a concatenation of user features (x n ) and h z . RTNet is designed for use in an incremental SDS framework (Schlangen and Skantze, 2011), in which information about forthcoming system responses may be available before the user has completed speaking. RTNet also operates separately from the higher-level turn-taking decisions that are conventionally handled by the dialogue manager (DM). In standard systems, the DM determines when the system should take a turn and also provides the natural language generation (NLG) component with a semantic representation of the system response (e.g. intents, dialogue acts, or an equivalent neural representation). Any system response representations downstream of the DM’s output representation (e.g. lexical or acoustic features) may be used to produce the response encoding. Accordingly, we assume that the DM has already decided for the system to take a turn, and our goal is to predict, on a frame-by-frame basis, the appropriate moment to initiate the system turn.
source_text: Overview We propose a neural model for generating these response timings in SDSs (shown in Fig. 1 ). The response timing network (RTNet) operates using both acoustic and linguistic features extracted from user and system turns. The two main components are an encoder, which encodes the system response h z , and an inference network, which takes a concatenation of user features (x n ) and h z . RTNet operates within an incremental SDS framework (Schlangen and Skantze, 2011) where information about upcoming system responses may be available before the user has finished speaking. RTNet also functions independently of higher-level turn-taking decisions that are traditionally made by the dialogue manager (DM) component. Typically, the DM decides when the system should take a turn and also supplies the natural language generation (NLG) component with a semantic representation of the system response (e.g. intents, dialogue acts, or an equivalent neural representation). Any of the system response representations that are downstream from the DM's output representation (e.g. lexical or acoustic features) can potentially be used to generate the response encoding.
