Therefore, we assume that the decision for the system to take a turn has already been made by the DM and our objective is to predict (on a frame-by-frame basis) the appropriate time to trigger the system turn.

text: All NMT models were trained with the Fairseq toolkit, using the standard Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017a) and the training configuration outlined in Ott et al. (2018). For model training, we relied on publicly available parallel corpora: the Rapid collection of EU press releases (Rozis and Skadiņš, 2017) and Europarl (Koehn, 2005), which together comprise approximately 4 million parallel sentences.
source_text: All the NMT models were trained using the Fairseq toolkit based on the standard Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017a) and the training settings described in Ott et al. (2018) . We used publicly available parallel datasets for training the models: the Rapid corpus of EU press releases ( Rozis and Skadiņš, 2017) and Europarl (Koehn, 2005) , which amount to around 4M parallel sentences in total.

text: Because ConvAI2 was a competition, many participants developed strong models for it. The strongest results were achieved by large pre-trained transformers (Dinan et al., 2020). In particular, Wolf et al. (2019b) used pretraining following the approach of Radford et al. (2018) on the BooksCorpus dataset, which yielded the best perplexity and F1 scores. More recently, performance has improved further with more effective and larger-scale pretraining (Lewis et al., 2019), which we compare against here; that study also reports strong results on ELI5.
source_text: As ConvAI2 was a competition, a number of competitors built strong models on it. The best results were obtained by large pre-trained transformers (Dinan et al., 2020) . In particular, Wolf et al. (2019b) pre-trained via the method of Radford et al. (2018) using the BooksCorpus dataset, resulting in the best perplexities and F1 scores. Since then, results have gotten even better with the advent of better and larger pretraining (Lewis et al., 2019 ), which we compare to here; the same work also reports strong results on ELI5.

text: TRADE (Transferable Dialogue State Generator) employs a soft copy mechanism to either copy slot values from utterance pairs or generate them with an RNN decoder (Sutskever et al., 2014). This architecture is able to generate slot values that were not observed during training. It consists of three principal components: an RNN utterance encoder that forms a context vector from the preceding dialogue turns; a slot-gate predictor that identifies which (domain, slot) pairs must be monitored; and a state generator that yields the final word distribution at each decoder time step.
source_text: TRADE TRAnsferable Dialogue statE generator (TRADE) uses a soft copy mechanism to either copy slot-values from utterance pairs or generate them using an Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) (Sutskever et al., 2014) decoder. This model can produce slot-values not encountered during training. The model is comprised of three main parts: an RNN utterance encoder which generates a context vector based on the previous turns of the dialogue; a slot-gate predictor indicating which (domain, slot) pairs need to be tracked, and a state generator that produces the final word distribution at each decoder time-step.

text: Researchers have achieved substantial progress in predicting the reading time of individual words, thereby demonstrating the influence of different words on the human brain (Frank et al., 2013b; Shain, 2019; Goodkind and Bicknell, 2018) across many different texts (Futrell et al., 2018; Kennedy et al., 2003). Although this line of work is oriented more toward the cognitive effects of words, the findings indicate that scientists can accurately predict the reading time of single words in context. As machine learning methods have grown in popularity, many researchers have obtained the greatest success with these approaches, and the most recent studies report notable gains from combining neural networks as language models with linear mixed models (LMMs) (Goodkind and Bicknell, 2018; de Vries et al., 2018; van Schijndel and Linzen, 2018). Nevertheless, all prior work has been limited to the effect of a particular word in context, which naturally raises the question of how well these results generalize.
source_text: Researchers have made significant progress in predicting the reading time of single words, illustrating the effect of different words on the human brain (Frank et al., 2013b; Shain, 2019; Goodkind and Bicknell, 2018) for many different texts (Futrell et al., 2018; Kennedy et al., 2003) . Although this effort is focused more on the cognitive effects of words, these results show that scientists can accurately predict the reading time of individual words in context. With the rise in popularity of machine learning techniques, many scientists have found the most success through these methods, with the most recent research showing significant improvements from combining neural networks as language models with linear mixed models (LMMs) (Goodkind and Bicknell, 2018; de Vries et al., 2018; van Schijndel and Linzen, 2018) . However, all previous research has been confined to the effect of a specific word in context, which naturally leads to the question of how this research generalizes.

text: Question Generation (QG) seeks to enable machines to produce human-like questions from a variety of inputs, including natural language texts (Du et al., 2017), images (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016), and knowledge bases (Serban et al., 2016). In recent years, QG has attracted growing interest because of its broad range of applications. In dialogue systems, posing questions can improve the interactivity and continuity of human-machine interactions. QG also supports Question Answering (QA) models via data augmentation and joint learning. Moreover, it has significant relevance in educational (Heilman and Smith, 2010) and clinical (Weizenbaum et al., 1966) systems.
source_text: Question Generation (QG) aims to teach machines to ask human-like questions from a range of inputs such as natural language texts (Du et al., 2017) , images (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016) and knowledge bases (Serban et al., 2016) . In recent years, QG has received increasing attention due to its wide applications. Asking questions in dialog systems can enhance the interactiveness and persistence of humanmachine interactions . QG benefits Question Answering (QA) models through data augmentation and joint learning . It also plays an important role in education (Heilman and Smith, 2010) and clinical (Weizenbaum et al., 1966) systems.

source_text: Additionally, we compare with the Previous stateof-the-art reported by Kulal et al. (2019) . The results can be seen in Figure 5 and Table 1 , where we use the constraint type as a shorthand for the search algorithm under this constraint. Without constraints, the baseline algorithm performs especially poorly because it needs syntactic context to select relevant code pieces for 26% of the lines with empty pseudocode. Table 1 : Comparison of the fraction of program passed when B = 10 0,1,2,3 under different constraints; constraint satisfied by hierarchical beam search with the default hyper-parameters mentioned in Section 5. "Previous" refers to the previous state of the art model. Figure 5(d) , the lead of SymTable on Syntactic grows linearly: the more these two algorithms search, the more budget is needed by Syntactic to reach the same level as SymTable. Syntactic needs nearly 600 more budget to have comparable performance with SymTable that uses 400 budget. We notice that all of our constrained search methods outperform the previous state-of-the-art. Averaged across all test examples, Backoff can solve 55.1% of the problems within 100 budget, which is ≈ 10% higher than the previous work. On unseen workers (problems), the top 11 (top 52) candidates of Backoff solve the same fraction of problems as the top 3000 candidates of the best performing algorithm in Kulal et al. (2019) .

text: Each candidate’s recordings are transcribed using a teacher–student ASR system equipped with a lattice-free maximum mutual information acoustic model (Kanda et al., 2017). The teacher–student training scheme adopts Kullback–Leibler divergence between the word-sequence posteriors produced by the student model and those of a teacher ensemble as the loss function (Wong and Gales, 2016). This yields a computationally efficient ASR system, since the student can decode in a single pass to a level of performance comparable to that of an ensemble decoder that requires multiple passes (Hinton et al., 2014). Further details of the ASR system are provided in Wang et al. (2018b).
source_text: Each candidate's recordings are transcribed by a teacher-student ASR system with a latticefree maximum-mutual-information acoustic model (Kanda et al., 2017) . The teacher-student training procedure uses Kullback-Leibler divergence between the word sequence posteriors from the student model and a teacher ensemble as the loss function (Wong and Gales, 2016) . The result is a computationally efficient ASR system, as the student is able to decode in a single run to a similar level of performance as an ensemble decoder requiring multiple runs (Hinton et al., 2014) . There is more information about the ASR system in Wang et al. (2018b) .

text: Structured knowledge graphs encode rich semantics through entities and relations (Hayashi et al., 2019). Many earlier studies have concentrated on task-oriented dialogue systems built on domain-specific knowledge bases (Zhu et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2016). To produce responses from a large-scale knowledge base, Zhou et al. (2018a) and Liu et al. (2018) employ graph attention and knowledge diffusion to choose knowledge semantics for utterance understanding and response generation. Moon et al. (2019) addresses the entity selection task and leverages positive entities that occur in the gold response. In contrast to prior work, Concept-Flow explicitly models the conversation flow with a commonsense knowledge graph and proposes a novel attention mechanism over all concepts to steer the conversation flow in latent concept space.
source_text: The structured knowledge graphs include rich semantics represented via entities and relations (Hayashi et al., 2019) . Lots of previous studies focus on task-targeted dialog systems based on domain-specific knowledge bases Zhu et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2016) . To generate responses with a large-scale knowledge base, Zhou et al. (2018a) and Liu et al. (2018) utilize graph attention and knowledge diffusion to select knowledge semantics for utterance understanding and response generation. Moon et al. (2019) focuses on the task of entity selection, and takes advantage of positive entities that appear in the golden response. Different from previous research, Concept-Flow models the conversation flow explicitly with the commonsense knowledge graph and presents a novel attention mechanism on all concepts to guide the conversation flow in the latent concept space.

text: Because the semi-autoregressive generation setting makes it more difficult to handle coreference relations among questions, particularly across different groups, we generate questions in a coarse-to-fine fashion. Specifically, the decoder is required to produce only “coarse questions” in which every pronoun has been replaced with the placeholder “[p].” To obtain the final outputs, we then employ an additional pre-trained coreference resolution model to restore pronouns in the appropriate placeholders. For a fair comparison, we adopt the same coreference resolution model (Clark and Manning, 2016) used in prior work, namely CoreNQG (Du and Cardie, 2018) and CorefNet (Gao et al., 2019).
source_text: Since the semi-autoregressive generation scenario makes it more challenging to deal with coreferences between questions (especially questions in different groups), we perform question generation in a coarse-to-fine manner. The decoder only needs to generate "coarse questions" where all pronouns are replaced by a placeholder "[p]". To get final results, we use an additional pre-trained coreference resolution model to fill pronouns into different placeholders. To make a fair comparison, we use the coreference resolution model (Clark and Manning, 2016) adopted by prior works CoreNQG (Du and Cardie, 2018) and CorefNet (Gao et al., 2019) .

text: Controlled generation has recently focused on steering attributes such as sentiment (Shen et al., 2017), gender or political slant (Prabhumoye et al., 2018), topic (Wang et al., 2017), and so on. However, these techniques are unable to provide fine-grained control over a property such as syntax. Prior work on diverse paraphrase generation can be broadly grouped into three categories: diverse decoding, latent variable modeling, and syntax-based methods. The first category relies on heuristics such as Hamming distance or distinct n-grams to maintain diverse candidates during beam search decoding (Vijayakumar et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2019). The second category comprises methods that employ uninterpretable latent variables to disentangle syntax and semantics (Chen et al., 2019a), perturb latent representations to promote diversity (Gupta et al., 2018; Park et al., 2019), or condition on latent codes that encode different rewriting patterns (Xu et al., 2018). Qian et al. (2019) instead uses separate generators to produce diverse paraphrases. Although these methods yield some diversity, they do not offer interpretable control over generation. Finally, approaches based on explicit syntactic structures (Chen et al., 2019b) may force a sentence to conform to an inappropriate syntax; Kendall’s Tau indicates less rearrangement, and vice versa. Phrase-level approaches (Li et al., 2019) are intrinsically less flexible than our approach.
source_text: Controlled Generation Recent work on controlled generation aims at controlling attributes such as sentiment (Shen et al., 2017) , gender or political slant (Prabhumoye et al., 2018), topic (Wang et al., 2017) , etc. However, these methods cannot achieve fine-grained control over a property like syntax. Prior work on diverse paraphrase generation can be divided into three groups: diverse decoding, latent variable modeling, and syntax-based. The first group uses heuristics such as Hamming distance or distinct n-grams to preserve diverse options during beam search decoding (Vijayakumar et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2019) . The second group includes approaches that use uninterpretable latent variables to separate syntax and semantics (Chen et al., 2019a) , perturb latent representations to enforce diversity (Gupta et al., 2018; Park et al., 2019) or condition on latent codes used to represent different re-writing patterns (Xu et al., 2018; . Qian et al. (2019) uses distinct generators to output diverse paraphrases. These methods achieve some diversity, but do not control generation in an interpretable manner. Finally, methods that use explicit syntactic structures Chen et al., 2019b ) may try to force a Kendall's Tau indicates lower rearrangement and vice-versa. sentence to conform to unsuitable syntax. Phraselevel approaches (Li et al., 2019) are inherently less flexible than our approach.

text: The man loves the woman. The cat stinks. I love her/him. Translate the following into English: Ihooat sahollo. Ofi'at hilha. Kowi'ã lhiyohlili. Table 1: The “Chickasaw” puzzle (Payne, 2005) enables learning from just a few examples. This kind of System2-style modeling is still at an early stage in deep learning, but it is widely recognized as a much-needed next step for the field (McClelland et al., 2019; Marcus, 2020; LeCun, 2020; Bengio, 2020). To encourage research in this promising direction, we introduce a distinctive challenge in “learning from small data”: PuzzLing Machines, inspired by the Linguistic Olympiads, one of the 13 officially recognized International Science Olympiads for high-school students.
source_text: The man loves the woman. The cat stinks. I love her/him. Translate the following into English: Ihooat sahollo. Ofi'at hilha. Kowi'ã lhiyohlili. Table 1 : The "Chickasaw" puzzle (Payne, 2005) to enable learning from just a few examples. This System2-style modeling is still in its early stages in DL, but is recognized as a much needed next step in the field (McClelland et al., 2019; Marcus, 2020; LeCun, 2020; Bengio, 2020) . To foster research in this promising direction, we propose a unique challenge on "learning from small data": PuzzLing Machines, based on the Linguistic Olympiads-one of the 13 recognized International Science Olympiads targeted at high-school students.

text: • RNNG (Dyer et al., 2016), which captures syntactic structure; • EntityNLM (Ji et al., 2017), which models coreference chains; and • KGLM (Logan et al., 2019), which represents links to an external knowledge graph. Representative latent states for EntityNLM and KGLM are shown in Figure 1, illustrating latent coreference chains and connections to the knowledge graph. Additional notable latent language models include NKLM (Ahn et al., 2016) and LRLM (Hayashi et al., 2020); we do not examine them because they rely on alternatives to importance sampling, such as the forward-backward algorithm.
source_text: • RNNG (Dyer et al., 2016) which models syntactic structure, • EntityNLM (Ji et al., 2017) which models coreference chains, and • KGLM (Logan et al., 2019) which models links to an external knowledge graph. Example latent states for EntityNLM and KGLM are depicted in Figure 1 , showing latent coreference chains and links to the knowledge graph. Other notable latent language models include the NKLM (Ahn et al., 2016) and LRLM (Hayashi et al., 2020) ; we do not study them since they use alternatives to importance sampling (e.g., the forward-backward algorithm).

text: For the downstream tasks, all reported results are obtained by simply fine-tuning MobileBERT, in the same manner as the original BERT. To fine-tune the pre-trained models, we tune the optimization hyperparameters over a search space that includes different batch sizes (16/32/48), learning rates ((1-10) * 10e-5), and numbers of epochs (2-10). This search space differs from that used for the original BERT because we find that MobileBERT typically requires a larger learning rate and more training epochs during fine-tuning. We choose the test model based on its performance on the development (dev) set.
source_text: For the downstream tasks, all reported results are obtained by simply fine-tuning MobileBERT just like what the original BERT does. To finetune the pre-trained models, we search the optimization hyperparameters in a search space including different batch sizes (16/32/48), learning rates ((1-10) * e-5), and the number of epochs (2-10). The search space is different from the original BERT because we find that MobileBERT usually needs a larger learning rate and more training epochs in fine-tuning. We select the model for testing according to their performance on the development (dev) set.

text: For the MNLI task, SMART performs better in the out-of-domain setting than in the in-domain setting, e.g., (86.0% vs. 85 We also compare SMART against a variety of models that employ adversarial training, such as FreeLB. As shown in the bottom rows of Table 1, SMART surpasses PGD and FreeAT on all eight GLUE tasks. When compared with the current state-of-the-art adversarial training model, FreeLB, SMART achieves better results on six of the eight GLUE tasks (MNLI, RTE, QNLI, MRPC, SST-2, and STS-B), demonstrating the effectiveness of our model. Table 2 reports the current state-of-the-art models on the GLUE leaderboard. SMART delivers competitive performance relative to T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), the leading model on the GLUE leaderboard. T5 has 11 billion parameters, whereas SMART contains only 356 million. Even between this extremely large model (T5) and other ensemble models (e.g., ALBERT and ALICE), SMART, as a single model, still establishes new state-of-the-art results on SST-2, MRPC, and STS-B. When combined with the Multi-task Learning framework (MT-DNN), MT-DNN-SMART achieves new state-of-the-art results on GLUE, raising the GLUE benchmark to 89.9%. Further discussion is provided in Section 5.3.
source_text: MNLI task, the performance of SMART on the out-domain setting is better than the in-domain setting, e.g., (86.0% vs. 85 We also compare SMART with a range of models which used adversarial training such as FreeLB. From the bottom rows in Table 1 , SMART outperforms PGD and FreeAT across the all 8 GLUE tasks. Comparing with the current state-of-the-art adversarial training model, FreeLB, SMART outperforms it on 6 GLUE tasks out of a total of 8 tasks (MNLI, RTE, QNLI, MRPC, SST-2 and STS-B) showing the effectiveness of our model. Table 2 summarizes the current state-of-the-art models on the GLUE leaderboard. SMART obtains a competitive result comparing with T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) , which is the leading model at the GLUE leaderboard. T5 has 11 billion parameters, while SMART only has 356 millions. Among this super large model (T5) and other ensemble models (e.g., ALBERT, ALICE), SMART, which is a single model, still sets new state-of-the-art results on SST-2, MRPC and STS-B. By combining with the Multi-task Learning framework (MT-DNN), MT-DNN-SMART obtains new state-of-the-art on GLUE, pushing the GLUE benchmark to 89.9%. More discussion will be provided in Section 5.3.

text: Machine Translation Our work is motivated by the pre-ordering literature in machine translation. Such systems either rely on hand-engineered rules tailored to particular languages (Collins et al., 2005; Wang et al., 2007) or automatically learn syntax-based rewriting patterns (Xia and McCord, 2004; Dyer and Resnik, 2010; Genzel, 2010; Khalilov and Simaan, 2011; Lerner and Petrov, 2013). There are also methods that do not depend on syntactic parsers, but instead infer hierarchical representations to exploit for pre-ordering (Tromble and Eisner, 2009; DeNero and Uszkoreit, 2011). In translation settings, there is often a canonical reordering that ought to be applied to better match the target language; for example, head-final languages such as Japanese show highly regular, syntax-driven reorderings relative to English. By contrast, in diverse paraphrase generation, no single canonical reordering exists, which makes our problem substantially different.
source_text: Machine Translation Our work is inspired by pre-ordering literature in machine translation. These systems either use hand-crafted rules designed for specific languages (Collins et al., 2005; Wang et al., 2007) or automatically learn rewriting patterns based on syntax (Xia and McCord, 2004; Dyer and Resnik, 2010; Genzel, 2010; Khalilov and Simaan, 2011; Lerner and Petrov, 2013) . There also exist approaches that do not rely on syntactic parsers, but induce hierarchical representations to leverage for pre-ordering (Tromble and Eisner, 2009; DeNero and Uszkoreit, 2011) . In the context of translation, there is often a canonical reordering that should be applied to align better with the target language; for instance, head-final languages like Japanese exhibit highly regular syntax-governed reorderings compared to English. However, in diverse paraphrase generation, there doesn't exist a single canonical reordering, making our problem quite different.

text: As noted previously, research on summarization of narratives has attracted only limited attention, perhaps because of the scarcity of annotated data for both modeling and evaluation. Kazantseva and Szpakowicz (2010) produce summaries of short stories using importance-based criteria (for example, whether a segment contains information about the protagonist or the setting); their summaries are intended to help readers judge whether they would like to read the entire story, while avoiding disclosure of the plot. Mihalcea and Ceylan (2007) summarize books using an unsupervised graph-based method applied to segments, that is, topical units. Their procedure first produces a summary for each segment and then constructs an overall summary by selecting sentences from the individual segment summaries.
source_text: As mentioned earlier, work on summarization of narratives has had limited appeal, possibly due to the lack of annotated data for modeling and evaluation. Kazantseva and Szpakowicz (2010) summarize short stories based on importance criteria (e.g., whether a segment contains protagonist or location information); they create summaries to help readers decide whether they are interested in reading the whole story, without revealing its plot. Mihalcea and Ceylan (2007) summarize books with an unsupervised graph-based approach operating over segments (i.e., topical units). Their algorithm first generates a summary for each segment and then an overall summary by collecting sentences from the individual segment summaries.

text: Both flat text classification and HTC have been addressed with conventional machine learning classifiers (Liu et al., 2005; Kim et al., 2006) as well as deep neural networks (Peng et al., 2018; Conneau et al., 2017). Nonetheless, most recent approaches rely on models with a large number of parameters that entail prolonged training times. In the flat-classification setting, certain studies have attempted to improve efficiency by introducing methods that do not alter the model architecture itself, but instead enhance performance through external means (Joulin et al., 2017; Howard and Ruder, 2018). However, these strategies remain insufficiently developed for HTC, and the most recent and effective methods are still computationally demanding (Yang et al., 2019; Banerjee et al., 2019).
source_text: Both flat text classification and HTC have been tackled using traditional machine learning classifiers (Liu et al., 2005; Kim et al., 2006) or deep neural networks (Peng et al., 2018; Conneau et al., 2017) . Nevertheless, the majority of the latest approaches consider models with a large number of parameters that require extended training time. In the flat-classification scenario, some studies have addressed the problem of efficiency by proposing methods that do not focus on the model architecture, but in external ways of improving the results (Joulin et al., 2017; Howard and Ruder, 2018) . However, the listed strategies are still underdeveloped for HTC, and the most recent and effective methods are still computationally expensive (Yang et al., 2019; Banerjee et al., 2019) .

text: In this work, we propose a definition of the frequent response problem as a subproblem of the generic response problem (Li et al., 2016). Considerable prior research has sought to mitigate the generic response problem in end-to-end dialogue response generation. Li et al. (2016) adopt the maximal mutual information (MMI) objective and suggest using an auxiliary LM to discourage generic responses during decoding. Closely related to this line of work, more elaborate training frameworks based on GANs (Li et al., 2017) have also been shown to be effective, with methods such as variational information maximization and reward for every generation step (REGS) introduced to improve GAN training. However, our experiments indicate that a vanilla GAN approach yields unsatisfactory results. It remains an open question whether negative training 11 is complementary to these frameworks, and this deserves further investigation in future work.
source_text: In this work, we propose a definition for the frequent response problem, as a sub-problem of the generic response problem (Li et al., 2016) . Much research work has devoted to alleviate the generic response problem in end-to-end dialogue response generation, (Li et al., 2016) use the maximal mutual information (MMI) objective, and propose to utilize an auxiliary LM to penalize the generic response during decoding. Closely related to this work, sophisticated training frameworks based on GAN Li et al., 2017) have also been shown to be effective, where techniques such as variational information maximization or reward for every generation step (REGS) are proposed to improve GAN training. However, in our experiments it is shown that a vanilla GAN approach gives unsatisfactory results. Whether negative training 11 is complementary to these frameworks is worth investigating in future work.

text: Human Evaluation. We conducted human evaluation of our method on the TripAdvisor dataset. Using a crowd evaluation platform, we compared two systems and assessed their fluency, informativeness, and relevance to the surrounding context (coherence) on 500 randomly selected samples from the test set. In accordance with recommended best practices, each sample was rated by five judges. We applied basic spam detection by removing judges who were excessively fast or who performed too poorly on a gold set. To prevent bias, we randomized the placement of each system when asking judges to compare our systems, with and without keywords, against the ground truth. Table 2: Automatic evaluation. “w/ context” indicates that generation is conditioned on both keywords and context. “w/o context” indicates that generation is conditioned only on keywords, not on context. “Ent.” and “Len.” denote Entropy and the average generation length, respectively. Table 3: Human evaluation. “w/(w/o) keywords” and “w/(w/o) context” indicate whether generation is conditioned on keywords and context, respectively. All numbers are percentages.
source_text: Human Evaluation. We performed human evaluation of our method on the TripAdvisor dataset. We used a crowd evaluation platform to compare two systems and assess their fluency, informativeness, and relevance to the surrounding context (coherence) on 500 random samples from the test set. Following recommended best practices, each sample was evaluated by five judges. We performed simple spam detection by excluding judges that were too fast or performed too low on a gold set. To avoid bias, we randomized the position of each system while asking judges to compare our systems (with and without keywords) with the ground truth Table 2 : Automatic evaluation. "w/ context" indicates that the generation is based on both keywords and context. "w/o context" indicates that the generation is only based on keywords but not context. "Ent." and "Len." stand for Entropy and the average generation length, respectively. Table 3 : Human evaluation. "w/(w/o) keywords" and "w/(w/o) context" indicate whether the generation is based on keywords and context, respectively. All numbers are percentages.

text: Interestingly, within the area of efficient human annotation interfaces, it is already common practice to employ unified representations across a broad range of NLP tasks. Figure 1 presents one example of the BRAT (Stenetorp et al., 2012) annotation interface, which has been used to annotate data for tasks as diverse as part-of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, relation extraction, and many others. In particular, this interface uses a single unified format composed of spans (e.g., the span corresponding to an entity), labels attached to spans (e.g., the type of entity, such as “person” or “location”), and labeled relations between spans (e.g., “born-in”). Such labeled relations may be organized into a tree or graph structure, thereby representing the linguistic structure of sentences (e.g., a dependency tree). We describe this BRAT format and explain how it can be used to represent a wide range of natural language analysis tasks in Section 2.
source_text: Interestingly, in the domain of efficient human annotation interfaces, it is already standard to use unified representations for a wide variety of NLP tasks. Figure 1 shows one example of the BRAT (Stenetorp et al., 2012) annotation interface, which has been used for annotating data for tasks as broad as part-of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, relation extraction, and many others. Notably, this interface has a single unified format that consists of spans (e.g., the span of an entity), labels on the spans (e.g., the variety of entity such as "person" or "location"), and labeled relations between the spans (e.g., "born-in"). These labeled relations can form a tree or a graph structure, expressing the linguistic structure of sentences (e.g., dependency tree). We detail this BRAT format and how it can be used to represent a wide number of natural language analysis tasks in Section 2.

text: In this section, we evaluate the effect of GAN-BERT on sentence classification tasks under varying training conditions, namely the number of examples and the number of categories. We present results of our method to support the development of deep learning models when only a few labeled examples are available, across the following tasks: Topic Classification on the 20 News Group (20N) dataset (Lang, 1995), Question Classification (QC) on the UIUC dataset (Li and Roth, 2006), and Sentiment Analysis on the SST-5 dataset (Socher et al., 2013). Figure 2 shows the learning curves for the six tasks. We trained all models for 3 epochs except for 20N, which was trained for 15 epochs. The sequence length used was 64 for QC coarse, QC fine, and SST-5; 128 for both MNLI settings; and 256 for 20N. The learning rate was set to 2e-5 for all tasks except 20N, for which it was 5e-6.
source_text: In this section, we assess the impact of GAN-BERT over sentence classification tasks characterized by different training conditions, i.e., number of examples and number of categories. We report measures of our approach to support the development of deep learning models when exposed to few labeled examples over the following tasks: Topic Classification over the 20 News Group (20N) dataset (Lang, 1995) , Question Classification (QC) on the UIUC dataset (Li and Roth, 2006) , Sentiment Analysis over the SST-5 dataset (Socher et al., 2013) . We Figure 2 : Learning curves for the six tasks. We run all the models for 3 epochs except for 20N (15 epochs). The sequence length we used is: 64 for QC coarse, QC fine, and SST-5; 128 for both MNLI settings; 256 for 20N. Learning rate was set for all to 2e-5, except for 20N (5e-6).

text: We shift our attention to classification tasks. Unlike sequence tagging, we employ the final hidden state of the BiLSTM as the sentence-level representation for projection, regression, and softmax. Table 9 reports the distillation results of XtremeDistil with different teachers on four benchmark text classification datasets. We find that the student nearly matches teacher performance across all datasets. Performance also increases with a stronger teacher, although the gains are slight because the student capacity saturates. Table 10 presents distillation results using only 500 labeled examples per class. The distilled student outperforms the non-distilled model by 19.4 percent and matches the teacher on all tasks, underscoring the value of distillation in low-resource settings. Comparison with other distillation techniques: SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013) from GLUE (Wang et al., 2018) has served as a test bed for other distillation techniques for single-instance classification tasks, as in our work. Table 11 compares the accuracy of such methods reported on the SST-2 development set using the same teacher. We extract 11.7M sentences from all IMDB movie reviews in Table 1 to construct the unlabeled transfer set for distillation. We achieve the best distillation performance with BERT Large (uncased, whole word masking model) rather than BERT Base, showing improved student performance with a stronger teacher and surpassing other methods.
source_text: We switch gear and focus on classification tasks. In contrast to sequence tagging, we use the last hidden state of the BiLSTM as the final sentence representation for projection, regression and softmax. Table 9 shows the distillation performance of XtremeDistil with different teachers on four benchmark text classification datasets. We observe the student to almost match the teacher performance for all of the datasets. The performance also improves with a better teacher, although the improvement is marginal as the student capacity saturates. Table 10 shows the distillation performance with only 500 labeled samples per class. The distilled student improves over the non-distilled version by 19.4 percent and matches the teacher performance for all of the tasks demonstrating the impact of distillation for low-resource settings. Comparison with other distillation techniques: SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013) from GLUE (Wang et al., 2018) has been used as a test bed for other distillation techniques for single instance classification tasks (as in this work). Table 11 shows the accuracy comparison of such methods reported in SST-2 development set with the same teacher. We extract 11.7M M sentences from all IMDB movie reviews in Table 1 to form the unlabeled transfer set for distillation. We obtain the best performance on distilling with BERT Large (uncased, whole word masking model) than BERT Basedemonstrating a better student performance with a better teacher and outperforming other methods.

text: Our model architecture is similar to the VAEs with recurrent encoders and decoders proposed by Bowman et al. (2016), Ha and Eck (2018), and Roberts et al. (2018). Our use of a VAE to cluster dialogue acts follows the approach of Zhao et al. (2017). Our vector-based representation of dialogue acts is inspired by the “attribute vectors” employed by Roberts et al. (2018) to learn representations of musical structure. The model is also related to continuous turn-taking systems (Skantze, 2017), in that it is trained to predict future speech behavior on a frame-by-frame basis. The encoder adopts a multiscale RNN architecture similar to that proposed by Roddy et al. (2018) to integrate information across modalities. Models that explicitly generate responsive overlap have also been proposed by DeVault et al. (2011) and Dethlefs et al. (2012). In addition, other models have been proposed to generate appropriate response timing for fillers (Nakanishi et al., 2018; Lala et al., 2019) and backchannels (Morency et al., 2010; Meena et al., 2014; Lala et al., 2017).
source_text: Our model's architecture is similar to VAEs with recurrent encoders and decoders proposed in Bowman et al. (2016) ; Ha and Eck (2018) ; Roberts et al. (2018) . Our use of a VAE to cluster dialogue acts is similar to the approach used in Zhao et al. (2017) . Our vector-based representation of dialogue acts takes inspiration from the 'attribute vectors' used in Roberts et al. (2018) for learning musical struc-ture representations. Our model is also related to continuous turn-taking systems (Skantze, 2017) in that our model is trained to predict future speech behavior on a frame-by-frame basis. The encoder uses a multiscale RNN architecture similar to the one proposed in Roddy et al. (2018) to fuse information across modalities. Models that intentionally generate responsive overlap have been proposed in DeVault et al. 2011; Dethlefs et al. (2012) . While other models have also been proposed that generate appropriate response timings for fillers (Nakanishi et al., 2018; Lala et al., 2019) and backchannels (Morency et al., 2010; Meena et al., 2014; Lala et al., 2017) .

text: CoQA Baseline: Conversational Question Answering (CoQA) (Reddy et al., 2019) is a large-scale ConvQA dataset designed to develop models that can answer questions posed in a conversational context. Because we generate conversational responses for QA systems, it is appropriate to compare against such ConvQA models. As a baseline, we select one of the top-performing BERT-based CoQA models from the SMRCToolkit.10 We designate this model as the CoQA baseline. QuAC Baseline: Question Answering in Context is another ConvQA dataset. As a second baseline, we adopt the modified BiDAF model described in (Choi et al., 2018). Rather than using SEQ2SEQ generation, it extracts spans from the passage to serve as responses. We use the implementation of this model provided in AllenNLP (Gardner et al., 2017) and refer to it as the QuAC baseline. STs+BERT Baseline: We also evaluate our generation models against the method used to construct the SS and SS+ training datasets, namely, responses produced by STs and ranked by the BERT response classifier.
source_text: CoQA Baseline: Conversational Question Answering (CoQA) (Reddy et al., 2019 ) is a large-scale ConvQA dataset aimed at creating models which can answer the questions posed in a conversational setting. Since we are generating conversational responses for QA systems, it is sensible to compare against such ConvQA systems. We pick one of the best performing BERT-based CoQA model from the SMRCToolkit as a baseline. 10 We refer to this model as the CoQA baseline. QuAC Baseline: Question Answering in Context is another ConvQA dataset. We use the modified version of BiDAF model presented in (Choi et al., 2018) as a second baseline. Instead of a SEQ2SEQ generation, it selects spans from passage which acts as responses. We use the version of this model implemented in AllenNLP (Gardner et al., 2017) and refer to this model as the QuAC baseline. STs+BERT Baseline: We also compare our generation models with the technique that created the SS and SS+ training datasets (i.e. the responses generated by STs ranked with the BERT response classifier).

text: Following the Document Understanding Conference (DUC; Dang, 2005), most subsequent research has concentrated on evaluating both the content and linguistic quality of summaries (Nenkova, 2005). The most widely used automatic metric is ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2003), which computes unigram and bigram overlap (ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2) as a proxy for informativeness, as well as the longest common subsequence (ROUGE-L), for fluency. However, ROUGE may be misleading when it is used as the sole criterion for assessing summary informativeness (Schluter, 2017). For this reason, ROUGE scores are often supplemented by subjective human judgments of summaries. More objective measures have also been introduced to improve agreement among human annotators. The Pyramid method (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004) requires expert annotation of summaries for salient information. Narayan et al. (2018a,b) adopted a question-answering-based approach in which a summary serves as context for answering questions formulated from its reference summary. Hardy et al. (2019) proposed a reference-free approach in which a summary is evaluated against the source document, with the latter marked up for its relevant content.
source_text: Following the Document Understanding Conference (DUC; Dang, 2005) , a majority of work has focused on evaluating the content and the linguistic quality of summaries (Nenkova, 2005) . Most popular among them is the automatic metric ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2003) that measures the unigram and bigram overlap (ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2) as a proxy for assessing informativeness and the longest common subsequence (ROUGE-L), for fluency. ROUGE, however, can be misleading when used as the only means to assess the informativeness of summaries (Schluter, 2017) . Hence, the ROUGE score is often complemented with subjective human assessment of summaries. More objective measures have been proposed to improve agreement among human annotators. Pyramid method (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004) requires summaries to be annotated by experts for salient information. Narayan et al. (2018a,b ) used a questionanswering based approach where a summary is used as context to answer questions which were written based on its reference summary. Hardy et al. (2019) proposed a reference-less approach where a summary is assessed against the source document, highlighted with its pertinent content.

text: We learn to map a user’s utterance \(x = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}\) to a template-based tree representation (Figure 2), namely the bracketed form shown in Figure 3. Let the symbols in this bracketed representation be denoted by \(y = \{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m\}\). The conversion from \(x\) to \(y\) is carried out by four components that are trained jointly in an end-to-end manner: (1) an encoder, (2) a slot decoder, (3) a tree decoder (Figure 4), and (4) a pointer network. We briefly describe each of these components below.
source_text: We learn to map a user's utterance x = {x 1 , x 2 , ...x n } to a template-based tree representation ( Figure 2 ), specifically the bracketed representation in Figure 3 . We denote the symbols in the bracketed representation by y = {y 1 , y 2 , ..y m }. The translation from x to y is performed using four components that are jointly trained end-to-end, (1) an encoder, (2) a slot decoder, (3) a tree decoder ( Figure 4 ) and (4) a pointer network. Each of these components is briefly explained below.

text: Automated metric scores on the original benchmarks used to assess performance on a single skill (ConvAI2, WoW, ED), as reported in the literature, are presented in Table 5 (first row). Our poly-encoder models (rows 2–4), trained on individual tasks, match or surpass the metrics published for the corresponding benchmarks, with the exception of ED, where the result is close. Each single-skill model performs best on its own original benchmark and less well on the other benchmarks than the blended models do. By contrast, the blended models exhibit more even performance overall, in the sense that none of the single-skill models performs as well on average across the three categories, except that the ED model does slightly better than the random-skill model. The model fine-tuned on BST achieves balanced performance, but it does not reach the level of the single-skill models on their original benchmarks. The Multi-Task Two-Stage model improves by many points over simple random selection of single-skill models (Random-Skill), and this Random-Skill model itself performs roughly as well as the BST-fine-tuned model on the ED and WoW benchmarks. Among the blended models, the Multi-Task Single-Skills model performs best and comes very close to matching the single-skill models on all benchmarks, even exceeding them on WoW.
source_text: Automated metrics results on the original benchmarks used to gauge competency at a single skill (ConvAI2, WoW, ED) reported in the literature are shown in Table 5 (first row). Our poly-encoder models (rows 2-4) trained on single tasks match or exceed the metrics published with the corresponding benchmarks, except for ED, which is close. The single-skill models each perform the best on their respective original benchmark and not as well on other benchmarks, compared to the blended models. However, the performance of all blended models is more balanced, in the sense that none of the single-skill models does as well averaged over the three categories (except for the ED model doing a tiny bit better than the random-skill model). The model finetuned on BST shows balanced performance but fails to match the performance of the single-skill models on their original benchmarks. The performance of the Multi-Task Two-Stage model gains many points over that of simple random assignment of single-skill models (Random-Skill), and this Random-Skill model itself performs about as well as the BST-fine-tuned model on the ED and WoW benchmarks. The Multi-Task Single-Skills model performs best among the blended models, and nearly matches the performance of all singleskill models on all benchmarks (even surpassing it for the WoW benchmark).

text: Because participants in the DementiaBank dataset underwent multiple assessments, most subjects have multiple transcripts. To prevent the models from becoming biased toward particular individuals, we adopted the participant-level leave-one-out crossvalidation (LOOCV) evaluation protocol of Fritsch et al. (2019), in which all picture description transcripts for a single participant are held out for testing in turn, and the language models are trained on the remaining transcripts. The LMs are then used to compute perplexities on the held-out transcripts, yielding two perplexity values per transcript: one from the LM trained on the dementia transcripts (P dem ) and one from the LM trained on the control transcripts (P con ). The held-out transcripts were scored with these perplexity values, as well as with their difference (P con P dem ) .
source_text: As subjects in the DementiaBank dataset participated in multiple assessments, there are multiple transcripts for most of the subjects. In order to avoid biasing the models to individual subjects, we followed the participant-level leave-one-out crossvalidation (LOOCV) evaluation protocol of Fritsch et al. (2019) whereby all of the picture description transcripts for one participant are held out in turn for testing and the LMs are trained on the remaining transcripts. Perplexities of the LMs are then obtained on the heldout transcripts, resulting in two perplexity values per transcript, one from the LM trained on the dementia (P dem ) and control (P con ) transcripts. Held-out transcripts were scored using these perplexity values, as well as by the difference (P con P dem ) between them.

text: An important test of a document-level embedding is whether it can predict the class of the document. Here, we examine two classification tasks in the scientific domain: MeSH Classification. In this task, the objective is to classify scientific papers according to their Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) (Lipscomb, 2000). 7 We build a dataset of 23K academic medical papers, with each paper assigned to one of 11 top-level disease classes, such as cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, and digestive diseases, derived from the MeSH vocabulary. The largest category is Neoplasms (cancer), with 5.4K instances (23.3% of the full dataset), whereas the smallest category is Hepatitis (1.7% of the full dataset). We follow Feldman et al. (2019) in mapping the MeSH vocabulary to the disease classes.
source_text: An important test of a document-level embedding is whether it is predictive of the class of the document. Here, we consider two classification tasks in the scientific domain: MeSH Classification In this task, the goals is to classify scientific papers according to their Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) (Lipscomb, 2000) . 7 We construct a dataset consisting of 23K academic medical papers, where each paper is assigned one of 11 top-level disease classes such as cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, digestive diseases derived from the MeSH vocabulary. The most populated category is Neoplasms (cancer) with 5.4K instances (23.3% of the total dataset) while the category with least number of samples is Hepatitis (1.7% of the total dataset). We follow the approach of Feldman et al. (2019) in mapping the MeSH vocabulary to the disease classes.

text: Sequence-to-sequence models, such as those of Sutskever et al. (2014), have been extensively applied to natural language generation (NLG) and to the construction of conversational systems (Shang et al., 2015; Vinyals and Le, 2015; Li et al., 2016b). More recently, pretrained language models, including ELMO (Devlin et al., 2019), UniLM (Dong et al., 2019), and GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018), have further improved NLG performance through large-scale pretraining. However, the degeneration of irrelevant, off-topic, and non-useful responses remains one of the principal challenges in conversational generation (Rosset et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018).
source_text: Sequence-to-sequence models, e.g., Sutskever et al. (2014) , have been widely used for natural language generation (NLG), and to build conversation systems (Shang et al., 2015; Vinyals and Le, 2015; Li et al., 2016b; . Recently, pretrained language models, such as ELMO (Devlin et al., 2019) , UniLM (Dong et al., 2019) and GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) , further boost the NLG performance with large scale pretraining. Nevertheless, the degenerating of irrelevant, off-topic, and non-useful responses is still one of the main challenges in conversational generation (Rosset et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018; .

text: In this paper, we extend BERT training with unlabeled data within a generative adversarial framework. Specifically, we enrich the BERT fine-tuning procedure with an SS-GAN perspective, in the so-called GAN-BERT 1 model. In this setting, a generator creates “fake” instances that mimic the data distribution, whereas BERT serves as the discriminator. In this way, we leverage both BERT’s ability to learn high-quality representations of input texts and its capacity to incorporate unlabeled material so as to improve the network’s generalization on the target tasks. To the best of our knowledge, the use of SS-GANs in NLP has been explored only by Croce et al. (2019) through the so-called Kernel-based GAN. In that study, the authors augment a Kernel-based Deep Architecture (KDA, Croce et al., 2017) with an SS-GAN perspective. Sentences are mapped into low-dimensional embeddings that approximate the implicit space induced by a Semantic Tree Kernel function. However, that work only marginally examined how the GAN perspective might extend deep architectures for NLP tasks. In particular, a KGAN operates in a pre-computed embedding space by approximating a kernel function (Annesi et al., 2014). Although the SS-GAN enhances the quality of the Multi-layered Perceptron employed in the KDA, it does not modify the input representation space, which is statically obtained from the kernel-space approximation. In the present work, by contrast, all network parameters are taken into account during training, in accordance with SS-GAN approaches.
source_text: In this paper, we extend the BERT training with unlabeled data in a generative adversarial setting. In particular, we enrich the BERT fine-tuning process with an SS-GAN perspective, in the so-called GAN-BERT 1 model. That is, a generator produces "fake" examples resembling the data distribution, while BERT is used as a discriminator. In this way, we exploit both the capability of BERT to produce high-quality representations of input texts and to adopt unlabeled material to help the network in generalizing its representations for the final tasks. At the best of our knowledge, using SS-GANs in NLP has been investigated only by (Croce et al., 2019) with the so-called Kernel-based GAN. In that work, authors extend a Kernel-based Deep Architecture (KDA, (Croce et al., 2017) ) with an SS-GAN perspective. Sentences are projected into low-dimensional embeddings, which approximate the implicit space generated by using a Semantic Tree Kernel function. However, it only marginally investigated how the GAN perspective could extend deep architecture for NLP tasks. In particular, a KGAN operates in a pre-computed embedding space by approximating a kernel function (Annesi et al., 2014) . While the SS-GAN improves the quality of the Multi-layered Perceptron used in the KDA, it does not affect the input representation space, which is statically derived by the kernel space approximation. In the present work, all the parameters of the network are instead considered during the training process, in line with the SS-GAN approaches.

text: Modern ASR systems are based on end-to-end, alignment-free neural architectures, namely CTC (Graves et al., 2006) and sequence-to-sequence models (Graves et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017). The incorporation of attention mechanisms substantially improves performance in (Chorowski et al., 2015) and (Chan et al., 2016). More recently, the strong performance of transformer architectures on NLP tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019) has led speech researchers to examine their effectiveness for end-to-end ASR (Karita et al., 2019b). Zhou et al. apply an end-to-end transformer architecture to Mandarin Chinese ASR (Zhou et al., 2018). Speech-Transformer extends scaled dot-product attention to two dimensions and attains competitive results for character-level recognition (Karita et al., 2019a). Pham et al. introduce stochastic layer deactivation during training in order to build a very deep model (Pham et al., 2019). A key limitation of the transformer architecture is its quadratic memory complexity with respect to input sequence length. Most architectures therefore use consecutive feature stacking (Pham et al., 2019) or CNN preprocessing (Karita et al., 2019b) to downsample input feature vectors. Mohamed et al. (2019) employ a VGG-based input network to downsample the input sequence and obtain learnable positional embeddings.
source_text: Modern ASR systems rely on end-to-end, alignment free neural architectures, i.e. CTC (Graves et al., 2006) or sequence to sequence models (Graves et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017) . The use of attention mechanisms significantly improve results in (Chorowski et al., 2015) and (Chan et al., 2016) . Recently, the success of transformer architectures for NLP tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019) has motivated speech researchers to investigate their efficacy in end-to-end ASR (Karita et al., 2019b) . Zhou et. al., apply an end-to-end transformer architecture for Mandarin Chinese ASR (Zhou et al., 2018) . Speech-Transformer extends the scaled dot-product attention mechanism to 2D and achieves competitive results for character level recognition Karita et al., 2019a) . Pham et. al. introduce the idea of stochastically deactivating layers dur-ing training to achieve a very deep model (Pham et al., 2019) . A major challenge of the transformer architecture is the quadratic memory complexity as a function of the input sequence length. Most architectures employ consecutive feature stacking (Pham et al., 2019) or CNN preprocessing Karita et al., 2019b) to downsample input feature vectors. Mohamed et al. (2019) use a VGG-based input network to downsample the input sequence and achieve learnable positional embeddings.

text: GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), a pre-trained model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on many language generation tasks, is also evaluated in our experiments. We fine-tune the 124M GPT-2 in two ways: by concatenating all conversations and training it as a language model (GPT-2 lang), and by extending the GPT-2 model with an encoder-decoder architecture and supervising it with response data (GPT-2 conv).
source_text: GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019) , the pre-trained model that achieves the state-of-the-art in lots of language generation tasks, is also compared in our experiments. We fine-tune the 124M GPT-2 in two ways: concatenate all conversations together and train it like a language model (GPT-2 lang); extend the GPT-2 model with encode-decoder architecture and supervise with response data (GPT-2 conv).

text: We compare our models against several baselines, which fall into two categories: wrapper methods and joint models. To illustrate the theoretical upper bound on performance, we also train the base model with ground-truth annotations in the target domain (Gold). A wrapper method comprises a label aggregator and a deep learning model. These two components can be integrated in two ways: (1) first aggregating labels on the crowd-sourced training set and then passing the resulting labels to a Sequence Labeling Model (SLM) (Liu et al., 2017); (2) first feeding multi-source data to a Multi-Task Learning (MTL) (Wang et al., 2018) model and then aggregating the multiple predicted labels. We examine several label aggregation strategies. CONCAT treats all crowd annotations as gold labels. MVT performs majority voting at the token level, that is, the majority label among {y k i,j } is chosen as the gold label for each token x i,j . MVS is carried out at the sequence level, thereby addressing violations of Begin/In/Out (BIO) rules. DS (Dawid and Skene, 1979), HMM (Nguyen et al., 2017), and BEA (Rahimi et al., 2019) derive consensus labels using probabilistic models.
source_text: We compare our models with multiple baselines, which can be categorized in two groups: wrapper methods and joint models. To demonstrate the theoretical upper bound of performance, we also train the base model using ground-truth annotations in the target domain (Gold). A wrapper method consists of a label aggregator and a deep learning model. These two components could be combined in two ways: (1) aggregating labels on crowd-sourced training set then feeding the generated labels to a Sequence Labeling Model (SLM) (Liu et al., 2017) ; (2) feeding multi-source data to a Multi-Task Learning (MTL) (Wang et al., 2018) model then aggregating multiple predicted labels. We investigate multiple label aggregation strategies. CONCAT considers all crowd annotations as gold labels. MVT does majority voting on the token level, i.e., the majority of labels {y k i,j } is selected as the gold label for each token x i,j . MVS is conducted on the sequence level, addressing the problem of violating Begin/In/Out (BIO) rules. DS (Dawid and Skene, 1979) , HMM (Nguyen et al., 2017) and BEA (Rahimi et al., 2019) induce consensus labels with probability models.

text:
Or gold, for I am Prince of Mines;
Or diamonds, when I spy
A diadem to suit a dome
Still crowning me thereby.
source_text:
Or gold, who am the Prince of Mines;
Or diamonds, when I see
A diadem to fit a dome
Continual crowning me.

text:
I wondered which would miss me least,
And when Thanksgiving came,
If father’d put out more plates
To make the reckoning even.
source_text:
I wondered which would miss me least,
And when Thanksgiving came,
If father'd multiply the plates
To make an even sum.

text:
Depart, before the host hath drawn  
The bolt across the door,  
To seek the guest made perfect, --  
Her visitor no more.
source_text:
Depart, before the host has slid
The bolt upon the door,
To seek for the accomplished guest, --
Her visitor no more.

text:
Of robins in the trundle bed  
How many I descry  
Whose nightgowns could not veil the wings,  
Though I could hear them try!
source_text:
Of robins in the trundle bed
How many I espy
Whose nightgowns could not hide the wings,
Although I heard them try!

text:
Come back once more, fair Lesley,
Come back to Caledonie!
That we may boast we hae a lass
There’s nane again sae bonie.
source_text:
Return again, fair Lesley,
Return to Caledonie!
That we may brag we hae a lass
There’s nane again sae bonie.

text:
And, ere with rapid lips and knitted brow
I could demand the cause—a feeble shriek—
It was a feeble shriek, faint, far, and low,
Withheld me—my mien grew calm and meek,
And, grasping a small knife, I went to seek
That voice amid the crowd—’twas Cythna’s cry!
Beneath most tranquil resolve did anguish wreak
Its whirlwind rage:—so I passed on quietly
Till I beheld, where bound, that dearest child did lie.
source_text:
And, ere with rapid lips and gathered brow
I could demand the cause—a feeble shriek—
It was a feeble shriek, faint, far and low,
Arrested me—my mien grew calm and meek,
And grasping a small knife, I went to seek
That voice among the crowd—’twas Cythna’s cry!
Beneath most calm resolve did agony wreak
Its whirlwind rage:—so I passed quietly
Till I beheld, where bound, that dearest child did lie.

text:
If remembrance were forgetting,
Then I recall not;
And if forgetting, remembrance,
How near I had forgot!
And if to miss were merry,
And if to grieve were gay,
How very blithe the fingers
That gathered these to-day!
source_text:
If recollecting were forgetting,
Then I remember not;
And if forgetting, recollecting,
How near I had forgot!
And if to miss were merry,
And if to mourn were gay,
How very blithe the fingers
That gathered these to-day!

text:
Or would they yet continue aching
Through centuries above,
Made wise unto a vaster grief
By comparison with love.
source_text:
Or would they go on aching still
Through centuries above,
Enlightened to a larger pain
By contrast with the love.

text:
The zephyrs bore forlorn lutes,
And drenched them in the mirth;
The East flung out a solitary flag,
And signed the revel forth.
source_text:
The breezes brought dejected lutes,
And bathed them in the glee;
The East put out a single flag,
And signed the fete away.

text:
I hear and behold God in every object, yet understand God not in the least,  
Nor do I know who there may be more wondrous than myself.
source_text:
I hear and behold God in every object, yet understand God not in the least,
Nor do I understand who there can be more wonderful than myself.

text:
Once more I greet thee, thou dark, dismal December!
Once more I greet thee with sorrow and care;
Sad was the parting thou mak’st me remember—
Parting with Nancy, oh, never to meet more!
source_text:
Ance mair I hail thee, thou gloomy December!
Ance mair I hail thee wi’ sorrow and care;
Sad was the parting thou makes me remember—
Parting wi’ Nancy, oh, ne’er to meet mair!

text:
Before my eye was darkened quite,
I loved to look upon
As well as any creature-eyed,
That knows no other course.
source_text:
Before I got my eye put out,
I liked as well to see
As other creatures that have eyes,
And know no other way.

text:
‘The wild-eyed women throng about her way:
From their rich prisons, from the dust
Of baser thralls, from the oppressor’s sway,
Or from the blandishments of lust made just
By satiety, they come:—in her they trust;
The tyrants send their armed slaves to quell
Her might;—they, like a thunder-gust
Caught in some woodland, yield beneath the spell
Of that young maiden’s speech, and to their lords rebel.
source_text:
‘The wild-eyed women throng around her path:
From their luxurious dungeons, from the dust
Of meaner thralls, from the oppressor’s wrath,
Or the caresses of his sated lust
They congregate:—in her they put their trust;
The tyrants send their armed slaves to quell
Her power;—they, even like a thunder-gust
Caught by some forest, bend beneath the spell
Of that young maiden’s speech, and to their chiefs rebel.

text:
Friendship, hidden bond that binds the soul!
Sweetener of life, and solder of society!
Much do I owe thee—Blair.
source_text:
Friendship, mysterious cement of the soul!
Sweet’ner of Life, and solder of Society!
I owe thee much—Blair.

text:
And unto all the Immortals, every one,
A share was dealt of all that doth subsist;
But chief Mnemosyne did Maia’s son
Array in light of his resounding hymns;—
And, as each God was born, or first begun,
In due and ordered ranks, and fitting lists,
He sang their birth and being—and did move
Apollo unto love beyond all tongue.
source_text:
And how to the Immortals every one
A portion was assigned of all that is;
But chief Mnemosyne did Maia’s son
Clothe in the light of his loud melodies;—
And, as each God was born or had begun,
He in their order due and fit degrees
Sung of his birth and being—and did move
Apollo to unutterable love.

text:
I sing of a Whistle, a Whistle of worth,
I sing of a Whistle, the pride of the North.
Was borne to the court of our good Scottish King,
And long shall with this Whistle all Scotland yet ring.
source_text:
I sing of a Whistle, a Whistle of worth,
I sing of a Whistle, the pride of the North.
Was brought to the court of our good Scottish King,
And long with this Whistle all Scotland shall ring.

text:
Oft as beside the winding Nith I wait,  
At sober eve, or hail the gladsome dawn,  
I’ll miss thee sporting o’er the dewy lawn,  
And curse the ruffian’s aim, and mourn thy hapless fate.
source_text:
Oft as by winding Nith I, musing, wait
The sober eve, or hail the cheerful dawn,
I’ll miss thee sporting o’er the dewy lawn,
And curse the ruffian’s aim, and mourn thy hapless fate.

text:
Have the former struggles borne their fruit?
What has borne fruit? thyself? thy nation? Nature?
Now understand me well—it is written in the very heart of things that
from whatsoever fulfillment of success, no matter what,
there shall arise somewhat that makes a mightier struggle needful.
source_text:
Have the past struggles succeeded?
What has succeeded? yourself? your nation? Nature?
Now understand me well--it is provided in the essence of things that
from any fruition of success, no matter what, shall come forth
something to make a greater struggle necessary.

text:
“No—I confess to a blot there in Bath—a true blot.  
God had not supplied them with victuals as well as drink,  
and ’twas a drawback I could not get over at all.”
source_text: “No—I own to a blot there in Bath—a true blot. God didn’t provide ’em with victuals as well as drink, and ’twas a drawback I couldn’t get over at all.”

text:
SEMICHORUS 2:
Solemn, and still, and clear, and bright,
Guiding the Day and outrunning the Night,
With the might of a realm of perfect light;
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 2:
Solemn, and slow, and serene, and bright,
Leading the Day and outspeeding the Night,
With the powers of a world of perfect light;

text:
(Still here I bear my ancient sweet burdens,
I bear them, men and women, I bear them with me whereso I wander,
I swear it is not possible for me to be rid of them,
I am fill’d with them, and I shall fill them again in return.)
source_text:
(Still here I carry my old delicious burdens,
I carry them, men and women, I carry them with me wherever I go,
I swear it is impossible for me to get rid of them,
I am fill’d with them, and I will fill them in return.)

text:
The meadows are mine, the mountains mine, --
All forests, boundless stars,
As much of noon as I might hold
Between my limited eyes.
source_text:
The meadows mine, the mountains mine, --
All forests, stintless stars,
As much of noon as I could take
Between my finite eyes.

text:
I looked, and lo! one rose before me, eloquent.  
His eyes were dark and deep, and the clear brow  
That overhung them seemed like the morning sky,  
The cloudless Heaven of Spring, when in their flow  
Through the bright air, the gentle winds that blow  
Stir the green world awake—his gestures still obeyed  
The speaking mind that lit his features so,  
And where his curved lips lay half ajar,  
Passion’s most divine tide had forced its ardent way.
source_text:
I looked, and lo! one stood forth eloquently.
His eyes were dark and deep, and the clear brow
Which shadowed them was like the morning sky,
The cloudless Heaven of Spring, when in their flow
Through the bright air, the soft winds as they blow
Wake the green world—his gestures did obey
The oracular mind that made his features glow,
And where his curved lips half-open lay,
Passion’s divinest stream had made impetuous way.

text:
A few quadrillions of eras, a few octillions of cubic leagues, do  
not endanger the reach nor render it impatient,  
They are but fragments, every thing is but a fragment.
source_text:
A few quadrillions of eras, a few octillions of cubic leagues, do
not hazard the span or make it impatient,
They are but parts, any thing is but a part.

text:
The day, and fashion, of the shame;
And then the pious rite
That “God have mercy” on the soul
The jury voted him.
source_text:
The date, and manner of the shame;
And then the pious form
That "God have mercy" on the soul
The jury voted him.

text:
For language lay in Peter’s hand  
Like clay while he was still a potter;  
And he shaped songs for all the land,  
Sweet both to touch and understand,  
As pipkins once to mountain Cotter.
source_text:
For language was in Peter’s hand
Like clay while he was yet a potter;
And he made songs for all the land,
Sweet both to feel and understand,
As pipkins late to mountain Cotter.

text:
As blithely I keeked abroad,  
To view a scene sae fair,  
Three hizzies, early on the road,  
Came scouring up the air.  
Twa wore their mantles, black wi’ grief,  
But ane had grey inlining;  
The third, that lagged a wee behind,  
Was in the mode a-shining  
Fu’ braw that day.
source_text:
As lightsomely I glowr’d abroad,
To see a scene sae gay,
Three hizzies, early at the road,
Cam skelpin up the way.
Twa had manteeles o’ dolefu’ black,
But ane wi’ lyart lining;
The third, that gaed a wee a-back,
Was in the fashion shining
Fu’ gay that day.

text:
A printer’s boy, while folding pages,
Fell drowsily upon one side;
Like those renowned Seven, through their ages,
To wakeful frenzy’s sternest vigils—rages,
As if opiates had been supplied.
source_text:
A printer’s boy, folding those pages,
Fell slumbrously upon one side;
Like those famed Seven who slept three ages.
To wakeful frenzy’s vigil—rages,
As opiates, were the same applied.

text:
Wearied and grief-struck,
Ragged with their long lament,
Arm in arm, seven days
They followed the desert roads.
source_text:
Tired and woe-begone,
Hoarse with making moan,
Arm in arm, seven days
They traced the desert ways.

text:
Her breast is like the snow by night,  
When pale the keen dawn upward gleams,  
While underneath the murmuring runnels glide;  
And she has twa bright, roguish een.
source_text:
Her bosom’s like the nightly snow,
When pale the morning rises keen,
While hid the murm’ring streamlets flow;
An’ she has twa sparkling roguish een.

text:
Densities, growth, facades,  
Layers of mountains, earth, stone, towering trees,  
Born from afar, dying far off, living long enough to leave  
Eidolons that last forever.
source_text:
Densities, growth, facades,
Strata of mountains, soils, rocks, giant trees,
Far-born, far-dying, living long, to leave,
Eidolons everlasting.

text:
My tale has this one moral:
I have a missing friend, --
Pleiad by name, and robin,
And guinea in the sand, --
And when this mournful ditty,
Attended by a tear,
Shall meet the eye of traitor
In a country far from here,
Grant that grave repentance
May seize upon his mind,
And let him find no comfort
Beneath the sun, unkind.
source_text:
My story has a moral:
I have a missing friend, --
Pleiad its name, and robin,
And guinea in the sand, --
And when this mournful ditty,
Accompanied with tear,
Shall meet the eye of traitor
In country far from here,
Grant that repentance solemn
May seize upon his mind,
And he no consolation
Beneath the sun may find.

text:
Your pardon, sir, for this digression:  
I almost forgot my Dedication;  
But when divinity comes over me,  
My readers still are sure to lose me.
source_text:
Your pardon, sir, for this digression:
I maist forgat my Dedication;
But when divinity comes ’cross me,
My readers still are sure to lose me.

text:
It may be wilderness without,  
Far-off the feet of failing men,  
Yet holiday shuts out the night,  
And bells are ringing within.
source_text:
It may be wilderness without,
Far feet of failing men,
But holiday excludes the night,
And it is bells within.

text:
Of all the sounds sent out across,
There is no summons to me
Like that old cadence in the boughs,
That wordless melody
source_text:
Of all the sounds despatched abroad,
There's not a charge to me
Like that old measure in the boughs,
That phraseless melody

text:
I proclaim a life that will be abundant, ardent, spiritual, and brave,  
I proclaim an ending that will meet its passing lightly and in joy.
source_text:
I announce a life that shall be copious, vehement, spiritual, bold,
I announce an end that shall lightly and joyfully meet its translation.

text:
It may be easier  
To fail with shore in view,  
Than reach my blue peninsula  
And die of bliss anew.
source_text:
It might be easier
To fail with land in sight,
Than gain my blue peninsula
To perish of delight.

text:
Is this a sacred sight to see  
In a land so rich and fair,—  
Infants brought to misery,  
Fed by a cold and usuring hand?
source_text:
Is this a holy thing to see
In a rich and fruitful land,—
Babes reduced to misery,
Fed with cold and usurous hand?

text:
And they are gone: ah, ages long before
Those lovers vanished out into the gale.
That night the Baron dreamt of many a sore,
And all his warrior-guests, with shade and pale
Strange likenesses of witch and demon, and the snail
Of coffin-worm, were long in nightmare bound.
Angela the old died palsy-racked, a frail
And meagre face all marred; the Beadsman, found
Unsought for ever, after thousand aves, slept in ground.
source_text:
And they are gone: ay, ages long ago
These lovers fled away into the storm.
That night the Baron dreamt of many a woe,
And all his warrior-guests, with shade and form
Of witch, and demon, and large coffin-worm,
Were long be-nightmar'd. Angela the old
Died palsy-twitch'd, with meagre face deform;
The Beadsman, after thousand aves told,
For aye unsought for slept among his ashes cold.

text: We computed Fleiss’s Kappa (k) to measure inter-rater agreement when classifying a word in the summary as faithful, intrinsically hallucinated, or extrinsically hallucinated. The corresponding results are reported in Table 6. All models exhibited substantial agreement among their annotations (0.61 ≤ k ≤ 0.80; Landis and Koch, 1977). Table 6 further reports Fleiss’s Kappa (k) for assessing inter-rater agreement on factuality. For this metric, all models achieved almost perfect agreement among their annotations (0.81 ≤ k ≤ 1.0; Landis and Koch, 1977).
source_text: We estimated Fleiss's Kappa (k) to assess the agreement among our raters when categorizing a word in the summary as one of faithful, intrinsically hallucinated and extrinsically hallucinated. The results are shown in Table 6 . All models showed substantial agreement (0.61 ≤ k ≤ 0.80; Landis and Koch, 1977) among their annotations. Table 6 also shows Fleiss's Kappa (k) to assess the agreement among our raters for factuality. All models showed almost perfect agreement (0.81 ≤ k ≤ 1.0; Landis and Koch, 1977) among their annotations.

text: We employ a bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) to encode the context word embeddings into hidden representations, which are then passed to a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). We formulate \(p_{\psi}(z_y \mid z_x, c)\) as a categorical distribution \(\mathrm{Cat}(\pi)\), where the parameter \(\pi\) is computed from \(z_x\) and the contextual hidden representation using an additional MLP.
source_text: , we use a bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) to encode the word embeddings of the context into the hidden representations, and then feed them into a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). We model p ψ (z y |z x , c) following a categorical distribution Cat(π), by computing the parameter π from z x and the hidden representation of the context using another MLP.

text: Accuracy on MNLI was broadly comparable across augmentation methods and remained on par with the unaugmented baseline (0.84), indicating that syntactic augmentation with as many as 1215 examples does not degrade overall dataset performance. In contrast, accuracy on HANS differed markedly, and most models scored below chance on non-entailment examples, where chance performance is 0.50 on HANS, implying that they relied on the heuristics (Figure 1). The most effective augmentation method by a substantial margin was inversion with a transformed hypothesis. On the HANS word-overlap cases for which the correct label is non-entailment, such as the doctor saw the lawyer → the lawyer saw the doctor, accuracy was 0.28 without augmentation and 0.73 with the large version of this augmentation set. At the same time, this strategy reduced BERT’s accuracy on cases where the heuristic yields the correct prediction (The tourists by the actor called the authors → The tourists called the authors); indeed, the best model achieved similar accuracy on cases where lexical overlap led to correct and incorrect predictions, suggesting that this intervention prevented the model from adopting the heuristic.
