source_text: Accuracy on MNLI was very similar across augmentation strategies and matched that of the unaugmented baseline (0.84), suggesting that syntactic augmentation with up to 1215 examples does not harm overall performance on the dataset. By contrast, accuracy on HANS varied significantly, with most models performing worse than chance (which is 0.50 on HANS) on non-entailment examples, suggesting that they adopted the heuristics (Figure 1) . The most effective augmentation strategy, by a large margin, was inversion with a transformed hypothesis. Accuracy on the HANS word overlap cases for which the correct label is non-entailmente.g., the doctor saw the lawyer the lawyer saw the doctor-was 0.28 without augmentation, and 0.73 with the large version of this augmentation set. Simultaneously, this strategy decreased BERT's accuracy on the cases where the heuristic makes the correct prediction (The tourists by the actor called the authors → The tourists called the authors); in fact, the best model's accuracy was similar across cases where lexical overlap made correct and incorrect predictions, suggesting that this intervention prevented the model from adopting the heuristic.

text: Bridging Anaphora Resolution. Since the 1990s, empirical corpus-based studies on bridging have been conducted across a range of genres and languages (Fraurud, 1990; Poesio and Vieira, 1998; Poesio, 2004; Nissim et al., 2004; Gardent and Manuélian, 2005; Nedoluzhko et al., 2009; Eckart et al., 2012; Markert et al., 2012; Rösiger, 2018; Poesio et al., 2018). Among these resources, ISNotes (Markert et al., 2012), BASHI (Rösiger, 2018), and ARRAU (Poesio et al., 2018) are three recent public English corpora that include medium- to large-scale bridging annotations and have been employed to assess system performance on bridging anaphora recognition (Hou et al., 2013a; Hou, 2016), bridging anaphora resolution (Lassalle and Denis, 2011; Hou et al., 2013b; Hou, 2018a), as well as full bridging resolution (Hou et al., 2014). In this paper, we concentrate exclusively on the antecedent selection task.
source_text: Bridging Anaphora Resolution. Since the '90s, the empirical corpus studies related to bridging have been carried out on various genres and different languages (Fraurud, 1990; Poesio and Vieira, 1998; Poesio, 2004; Nissim et al., 2004; Gardent and Manuélian, 2005; Nedoluzhko et al., 2009; Eckart et al., 2012; Markert et al., 2012; Rösiger, 2018; Poesio et al., 2018) . Among those datasets, ISNotes (Markert et al., 2012) , BASHI (Rösiger, 2018) and ARRAU (Poesio et al., 2018) are recent three public English corpora which contain medium-to large-sized bridging annotations and have been used to evaluate systems' performance on bridging anaphora recognition (Hou et al., 2013a; Hou, 2016; , bridging anaphora resolution Lassalle and Denis, 2011; Hou et al., 2013b; Hou, 2018a) , as well as full bridging resolution (Hou et al., 2014 . In this paper, we focus exclusively on the task of antecedent selection.

text: Here we summarize the steps involved in constructing Forecaster; the remaining details are provided in the Appendix. We train the neural network model p θ on the training split for a given task and use the validation set to monitor the loss and perform early stopping. Once training is finished, we repurpose this validation data to build the training data for the forecaster. To obtain a low-variance estimate of the neural network logit outputs, we compute an MC-Dropout (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016) average over n = 10 samples. This averaged output is then passed to the decoding step of model p θ to produce top-k label-sequence predictions. We subsequently gather the relevant entities in φ(x), together with the I(y ∈ E) labels, to construct the forecaster training data. For the forecaster itself, we employ gradient boosted decision trees (Friedman, 2001) as a region-based model (Dong et al., 2018; Kuleshov and Liang, 2015).
source_text: Here we provide a summary of the steps involved in Forecaster construction. Remaining details are in the Appendix. We train the neural network model p θ on the training data split for a task and use the validation data for monitoring the loss and early stopping. After the training is complete, this validation data is re-purposed to create the forecaster training data. We use an MC-Dropout (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016) average of (n=10) samples to get a low variance estimate of logit outputs from the neural networks. This average is fed into the decoding step of the model p θ to obtain top-k label sequence predictions. We then collect the relevant entities in φ(x), along with the I(y ∈ E) labels to form the training data for the forecaster. We use gradient boosted decision trees (Friedman, 2001) as our region-based (Dong et al., 2018; Kuleshov and Liang, 2015) forecaster model.

text: To ensure a fair comparison, we reproduced the BERT baseline (BERT ReImp), because several results on the GLUE development set had not been matched. Our reimplemented BERT baseline is in fact even stronger than the results originally reported by Devlin et al. (2019). For example, the reimplemented model achieves 84.5% accuracy on the MNLI in-domain development set, compared with 84.4% for the original. On SST-2, BERT ReImp exceeds BERT by 0.2% accuracy (92.9% versus 92.7%). Taken together, these results confirm the fairness of our baselines.
source_text: For a fair comparison, we reproduced the BERT baseline (BERT ReImp ), since several results on the GLUE development set were missed. Our reimplemented BERT baseline is even stronger than the originally reported results in Devlin et al. (2019) . For instance, the reimplemented model obtains 84.5% (vs. 84.4%) on MNLI in-domain development in terms of accuracy. On SST-2, BERT ReImp outperforms BERT by 0.2% (92.9% vs. 92.7%) accuracy. All these results demonstrate the fairness of our baselines.

text: Autoregressive models that produce step-by-step alternatives for future continuations are computationally impractical for longer rollouts and are not cognitively plausible. They also diverge from Ely et al. (2015)’s account of suspense, which is framed in terms of Bayesian beliefs about a longer-term future state rather than stepwise prediction. Recent work (e.g., Ha and Schmidhuber (2018); Gregor et al. (2019)) has explored state-space approaches that represent beliefs as latent states via variational methods. In principle, such approaches would avoid the brute-force computation of a rollout, and, conceptually, forecasting longer-term states is consistent with theories of suspense.
source_text: Autoregressive models that generate step by step alternatives for future continuations are computationally impractical for longer rollouts and are not cognitively plausible. They also differ from the Ely et al. (2015) conception of suspense, which is in terms of Bayesian beliefs over a longer-term future state, not step by step. There is much recent work (e.g., Ha and Schmidhuber (2018) ; Gregor et al. (2019)), on state-space approaches that model beliefs as latent states using variational methods. In principle, these would avoid the brute-force calculation of a rollout and conceptually, anticipating longer-term states aligns with theories of suspense.

text: Researchers have also introduced search-based and model-based approaches to authorship obfuscation. For instance, Mahmood et al. (2019) proposed a genetic algorithm method to “search” for words whose modification, via a sentiment-preserving word embedding, would produce the greatest negative impact on authorship attribution. Bevendorff et al. (2019) presented a heuristic search algorithm for identifying words that, when altered using operators such as synonyms or hypernyms, increase the stylistic distance from the author’s text corpus. Shetty et al. (2018) employed Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to “transfer” the style of an input text to a target style. Emmery et al. (2018) used auto-encoders with a gradient reversal layer to “de-style” an input text, i.e., achieve style invariance.
source_text: Researchers have also proposed search and model based approaches for authorship obfuscation. For example, Mahmood et al. (2019) proposed a genetic algorithm approach to "search" for words that when changed, using a sentimentpreserving word embedding, would have the maximum adverse effect on authorship attribution. Bevendorff et al. (2019) proposed a heuristicbased search algorithm to find words that when changed using operators such as synonyms or hypernyms, increased the stylistic distance to the author's text corpus. Shetty et al. (2018) used Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to "transfer" the style of an input text to a target style. Emmery et al. (2018) used auto-encoders with a gradient reversal layer to "de-style" an input text (aka style invariance).

text: Overall, large pre-trained transformers have indeed achieved strong results on many of these tasks. Several large language modeling efforts have been conducted to demonstrate proficiency in multi-task capabilities (Radford et al., 2019; Keskar et al., 2019), and transformer-based methods have also been extended to language and vision tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2019a; Shuster et al., 2018). In the experiments section, we report the results of the relevant papers wherever possible, and we also train a BERT-based (Devlin et al., 2019) generative model as an additional baseline.
source_text: Overall, large pre-trained transformers indeed provide strong existing results on many of the tasks. Several large language modeling projects have been undertaken in order to show prowess in multi-tasking ability (Radford et al., 2019; Keskar et al., 2019) , and transformer-based approaches have been adapted to language and vision tasks as well (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2019a; Shuster et al., 2018) . As well as citing the relevant papers' results where possible in the experiments section, we also train a BERTbased (Devlin et al., 2019) generative model as an additional baseline.

text: Note that, owing to limitations in time and computational resources, all experiments reported below are conducted with the BERT BASE model. In this section, we examine the contribution of each component of SMART: smoothness-inducing adversarial regularization and Bregman proximal point optimization. For fast training, all models in this study employ BERT BASE as the encoder. In addition, we include the BERT BASE model itself as an extra baseline for a fair comparison. SMART refers to the proposed model. We then set λ s to 0, yielding -R s . The model with µ = 0 is denoted -D Breg . The results are presented in Table 3 . As expected, removing either component of SMART—smooth regularization or the proximal point method—should lead to a decline in performance. For instance, on MNLI, eliminating smooth regularization causes a 0.8% performance drop (85.6% vs. 84.8), whereas removing Breg proximal point optimization leads to a 0.2% drop (85.6% vs. 85.4%). This shows that the two components are complementary. Interestingly, all three proposed models outperform the BERT baseline model, which demonstrates the effectiveness of each module. Moreover, we observe that SMART yields greater generalization benefits on small datasets (i.e., RTE and MRPC) by mitigating overfitting.
source_text: Note that due to the limitation of time and computational resources, all the experiments reported below are based on the BERT BASE model. In this section, we study the importance of each component of SMART: smoothness-inducing adversarial regularization and Bregman proximal point optimization. All models in this study used the BERT BASE as the encoder for fast training. Furthermore, we also include the BERT BASE model as an additional baseline for a fair comparison. SMART denotes the proposed model. Then we set λ s to 0, which denotes as -R s . The model with µ = 0 is noted as -D Breg . The results are reported in Table 3 . It is expected that the removal of either component (smooth regularization or proximal point method) in SMART would result in a performance drop. For example, on MNLI, removing smooth regu-larization leads to a 0.8% (85.6% vs. 84.8) performance drop, while removing the Breg proximal point optimization, results in a performance drop of 0.2% (85.6% vs. 85.4%). It demonstrates that these two components complement each other. Interestingly, all three proposed models outperform the BERT baseline model demonstrating the effectiveness of each module. Moreover, we obersere that the generalization performance benefits more from SMART on small datasets (i.e., RTE and MRPC) by preventing overfitting.

text: Step-By-Step (Moryossef et al., 2019a): a transition-based statistical ranking method; Step-By-Step II (Moryossef et al., 2019b, UNSEEN). We exclude all single-triple instances from the planner evaluation, because planning for these cases is trivial. We assess performance using accuracy and BLEU-n (Papineni et al., 2002). For accuracy, a plan is considered correct only when it exactly corresponds to one of the human-generated plans. BLEU-n is less strict than accuracy and was also used by Yao et al. (2019) for plan evaluation. Here, we set n = 2. Table 1 reports the planning results. Our GCN method substantially outperforms all baselines (approximate randomization (Noreen, 1989; Chinchor, 1992), p < 0.05) by a wide margin on every test set and under both metrics, demonstrating the effectiveness of our planner. On the ALL and UNSEEN sets, the strongest baseline is Step-By-Step, but our method is more time-efficient. For instance, Step-By-Step requires 250 seconds to solve a single 7-triple instance, whereas our method processes all 4928 instances in under 10 seconds. On the SEEN set, the strongest models are GRU and Transformer. However, although their accuracies decrease by 0.46 on the UNSEEN test set, our method drops by only 0.02, suggesting stronger generalization ability.
source_text: Step-By-Step (Moryossef et al., 2019a) : a transition-based statistical ranking method; • Step-By-Step II (Moryossef et al., 2019b UNSEEN) . We remove all one-triple instances for planner's evaluation since the planning for these instances is trivial. Results are evaluated with accuracy and BLEU-n (Papineni et al., 2002) . For accuracy, we regard a plan as correct only if it exactly matches one of the human-generated plans. BLEU-n is more forgiving than accuracy. It is also adopted in Yao et al. (2019) for plan evaluation. Here we choose n = 2. Table 1 shows results of the planning experiments. Our GCN method significantly outperforms all the baselines (approximate randomization (Noreen, 1989; Chinchor, 1992) , p < 0.05) by a large margin on all the test sets and both measures, indicating the effectiveness of our planner. The most competitive baseline on ALL and UNSEEN sets is Step-By-Step, but our method is more time-efficient. For example, Step-By-Step needs 250 seconds to solve one 7-triple instance, but our method solves all 4928 instances in less than 10 seconds. For the SEEN set, the most competitive models are GRU and Transformer. However, while their accuracies drop by 0.46 on UNSEEN test set, our method drops only slightly by 0.02, indicating our method's better generalization power.

text: Natural Language Understanding (NLU) denotes the broad task of mapping natural language into formal representations. One research direction in the dialogue community seeks to detect slot-value pairs expressed in user utterances by casting the problem as a classification task (Henderson et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2014; Mrkšić et al., 2017; Vodolán et al., 2017). Another line of work concentrates on transforming single-turn user utterances into more structured meaning representations as a semantic parsing task (Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005; Jia and Liang, 2016; Dong and Lapata, 2018; Damonte et al., 2019).
source_text: Natural Language Understanding (NLU) refers to the general task of mapping natural language to formal representations. One line of research in the dialogue community aims at detecting slot-value pairs expressed in user utterances as a classification problem (Henderson et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2014; Mrkšić et al., 2017; Vodolán et al., 2017) . Another line of work focuses on converting single-turn user utterances to more structured meaning representa-tions as a semantic parsing task (Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005; Jia and Liang, 2016; Dong and Lapata, 2018; Damonte et al., 2019) .

text: The PARANMT-50M dataset includes sentence-level paraphrase pairs. However, to train our SOW model (described in Section 2.3), we require phrase-level paraphrases that contain syntactic abstractions. We obtain these from PARANMT-50M by applying the procedure illustrated in Figure 4. Following Zhang et al. (2020), we calculate a phrase alignment score for every pair of constituents in a sentence and its paraphrase.6 Based on these phrase alignment scores, we then derive a partial one-to-one mapping between phrases (the colored shapes in Figure 4); that is, not every phrase is aligned, but the aligned subset is matched one-to-one. Finally, we extract aligned chunks in a manner analogous to rule alignment in syntactic translation (Galley et al., 2004): when aligned phrases A and A subsume aligned phrase pairs (B, C) and (B′, C′), respectively, we can extract the aligned tuples (tA, B, C) and (tA, B′, C′). The phrases (B, C) and (B′, C′) are abstracted away to form training data for the phrase-level transducer, including supervision of whether o = MONOTONE or FLIP. Using this alignment strategy, we obtained more than 1 million aligned phrase pairs.
source_text: The PARANMT-50M dataset contains sentencelevel paraphrase pairs. However, in order to train our SOW model (outlined in section 2.3), we need to see phrase-level paraphrases with syntactic abstractions in them. We extract these from the PARANMT-50M dataset using the following procedure, shown in Figure 4 . We follow Zhang et al. (2020) and compute a phrase alignment score between all pairs of constituents in a sentence and its paraphrase. 6 From this set of phrase alignment scores, we compute a partial one-to-one mapping between phrases (colored shapes in Figure 4) ; that is, not all phrases get aligned, but the subset that do are aligned one-to-one. Finally, we extract aligned chunks similar to rule alignment in syntactic translation (Galley et al., 2004) : when aligned phrases A and A subsume aligned phrase pairs (B, C) and (B , C ) respectively, we can extract the aligned tuple (t A , B, C) and (t A , B , C ). The phrases (B, C) and (B , C ) are abstracted out to construct training data for the phrase-level transducer, including supervision of whether o = MONOTONE or FLIP. Using the above alignment strategy, we were able to obtain over 1 million aligned phrase pairs.

text: Applying the detection algorithm to our models results in word types being separated into distinct interior and non-interior sets (see Table 1). We ranked the top 500 words in each set according to the maximum probability they attained on the training corpora 1, and plot these values in Figure 2, which reveals a clear separation between the interior and non-interior sets. We also show the maximum trigram probabilities (Stolcke, 2002) smoothed with KN3 for the same top 500 words in each set, sorted separately. The gap between the NNLM and trigram curves for interior words indicates that models such as n-grams, which do not use a dot-product softmax, are not affected by the stolen probability effect and can assign higher probabilities. In addition, a random set of words equal in size to the interior set was created by uniform sampling and ranked among the top 500 words. Comparing the random and interior sets provides evidence that our detection algorithm effectively distinguishes the interior and non-interior sets, rather than merely performing random sampling. Our findings can be presented more compactly by examining the average probability mass assigned to the top 500 words in each set (see Table 2). The effect of the stolen probability effect for each model can be quantified as the difference between the interior set and each of the three reference sets (noninterior, random, and trigram) in the table. The interior average maximum probability is generally much smaller than that of the reference sets.
source_text: Applying the detection algorithm to our models yields word types being classified into distinct interior and non-interior sets (see Table 1 ). We ranked the top 500 words of each set by the maximum probability they achieved on the training corpora 1 , and plot these values in Figure 2 , showing a clear distinction between interior and non-interior sets. The maximum trigram probabilities (Stolcke, 2002) smoothed with KN3 for the same top 500 words in each set (separately sorted) are also shown. The difference between NNLM and trigram curves for interior words shows that models like n-grams, which do not utilize a dot-product softmax, are not subject to the stolen probability effect and can assign higher probabilities. A random set of words equal in size to the interior set was also constructed by uniform sampling, and ranked on the top 500 words. A comparison between the random and interior sets provides evidence that our detection algorithm is effective at separating the interior and non-interior sets, and is not simply performing random sampling. Our results can be more compactly presented by considering the average probability mass assigned to the top 500 words for each set (see Table 2 ). The impact of the stolen probability effect for each model can quantified as the difference between the interior set and each of the three reference sets (noninterior, random, and trigram) in the table. The interior average maximum probability is generally much smaller than those of the reference sets.

text: Compared with GeoQuery (Zelle and Mooney, 1996), Spider is substantially larger in scale, with 200 databases rather than a single database and thousands of SQL parses instead of hundreds. In contrast to WikiSQL (Zhong et al., 2017), questions in Spider require the induction of parses with more complex structures, including the use of multiple tables, joins, nesting, and related operations. Spider furthermore employs a cross-domain evaluation setting in which the databases used at test time are never observed during training.
source_text: ) and GeoQuery (Zelle and Mooney, 1996) , Spider is much larger in scale (200 databases vs. one database, and thousands vs. hundreds of SQL parses). Compared to WikiSQL (Zhong et al., 2017) , Spider questions require inducing parses of complex structures (requiring multiple tables, joining, nesting, etc.). Spider also adopts a crossdomain evaluation setup in which databases used at testing time are never seen at training time.

text: We now describe how knowledge of narrative structure is incorporated into our summarization models. For both models, this knowledge is conveyed through a network pre-trained on the TRIPOD 4 dataset introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019). This dataset comprises 99 movies annotated with turning points. TPs are pivotal narrative events that shape plot progression and are situated between successive acts, or thematic units. It is commonly assumed (Cutting, 2016) that a film consists of six acts (Figure 1), each separated by a turning point (shown as arrows in the figure). Moreover, each of the five TPs has a specific narrative role: we list each TP together with its definition as given in screenwriting theory (Hauge, 2017) and adopted by Papalampidi et al. (2019) in Table 1 (see Appendix A for a more detailed account of narrative structure theory). Papalampidi et al. (2019) detect movie scenes that align with these key events as a way to analyze the narrative structure of films. They first gather sentence-level TP annotations for plot synopses and then project them onto screenplays using distant supervision, producing silver-standard labels. We use this silver-standard dataset to pretrain a network for TP identification.
source_text: We now explain how to inject knowledge about narrative structure into our summarization models. For both models, such knowledge is transferred via a network pre-trained on the TRIPOD 4 dataset introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019) . This dataset contains 99 movies annotated with turning points. TPs are key events in a narrative that define the progression of the plot and occur between consecutive acts (thematic units). It is often assumed (Cutting, 2016) that there are six acts in a film (Figure 1 ), each delineated by a turning point (arrows in the figure). Each of the five TPs has also a well-defined function in the narrative: we present each TP alongside with its definition as stated in screenwriting theory (Hauge, 2017 ) and adopted by Papalampidi et al. (2019) in Table 1 (see Appendix A for a more detailed description of narrative structure theory). Papalampidi et al. (2019) identify scenes in movies that correspond to these key events as a means for analyzing the narrative 2019structure of movies. They collect sentence-level TP annotations for plot synopses and subsequently project them via distant supervision onto screenplays, thereby creating silver-standard labels. We utilize this silver-standard dataset in order to pretrain a network which performs TP identification.

text: We define a frame as the fundamental unit of SOCO. Each frame contains N, where N is the total number of frames, a_i as the candidate answer sentence, c_i as the surrounding context of a_i, and Q_i as a set of questions manually or synthetically associated with the answer a_i. Note that Q_i is optional, and only a small subset of frames are often manually labeled. Two neural network models are involved in SOCO QA. The first model, h_a = F_a(a, c), is an answer encoder that takes both the answer sentence and its surrounding context as input to produce a context-sensitive answer embedding h_a. The second model is a question encoder, h_q = F_q(q), which takes a query as input and maps it to a question embedding vector of the same dimensionality. Finally, we define the relevance between a query and an answer frame as s = cos(h_a, h_q).
source_text: We define a frame to be the basic building block of SOCO. Each frame contains N , where N is the total number of frames, a i is the potential answer sentence, c i is surrounding context of a i , and Q i is a set of questions that are manually/synthetically associated with the answer a i . Note that Q i is optional and often only a small set of frames are manually labelled. There are two neural network models involve in SOCO QA. The first model h a = F a (a, c) is an answer encoder that takes both the answer sentence and its surround context to create a contextsensitive answer embeddings h a . The second model is a question encoder h q = F q (q) that takes a query as input and maps it to a question embedding vector of the same size. Last, we define the relevance between a query and an answer frame to be s = cos(h a , h q ).

text: • Leveraging prior knowledge of the hierarchy, we employ conventional structure encoders to model label dependencies in both top-down and bottom-up directions, a setting that has not yet been explored for hierarchical text classification. • We introduce a novel end-to-end hierarchy-aware global model (HiAGM). In addition, we propose two variants for label-wise text features: a hierarchy-aware multi-label attention model (HiAGM-LA) and a hierarchy-aware text feature propagation model (HiAGM-TP). • Experiments show that both HiAGM variants yield consistent gains across multiple datasets when paired with different structure encoders. Our strongest model surpasses the state of the art by 3.25% in Macro-F1 and 0.66% in Micro-F1 on RCV1-V2.
source_text: • With the prior hierarchy knowledge, we adopt typical structure encoders for modeling label dependencies in both top-down and bottomup manners, which has not been investigated for hierarchical text classification. • We propose a novel end-to-end hierarchyaware global model (HiAGM). We further present two variants for label-wise text features, a hierarchy-aware multi-label attention model (HiAGM-LA) and a hierarchy-aware text feature propagation model (HiAGM-TP). • We empirically demonstrate that both variants of HiAGM achieve consistent improvements on various datasets when using different structure encoders. Our best model outperforms the state-of-the-art model by 3.25% of Macro-F1 and 0.66% of Micro-F1 on RCV1-V2.

text: • Span-oriented Tasks (Table 2a) – Named Entity Recognition (Sang and Meulder, 2003) NER is conventionally treated as a sequence labeling problem. Here, we represent named entities as spans covering one or more tokens. – Constituency Parsing (Collins, 1997) Constituency parsing seeks to generate a syntactic parse tree for each sentence. In this tree, every node corresponds to an individual span paired with a constituent label, and the spans are hierarchically nested. – Part-of-speech Tagging (Ratnaparkhi, 1996; Toutanova et al., 2003) POS tagging is also a sequence labeling task, in which each token is treated as an individual span annotated with a POS tag. – Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (Pontiki et al., 2014) ABSA is a task that involves identifying specific spans as aspect terms and predicting the sentiment associated with them.
source_text: • Span-oriented Tasks (Table 2a) -Named Entity Recognition (Sang and Meul der, 2003 ) NER is traditionally considered as a sequence labeling task. We model named entities as spans over one or more tokens. -Constituency Parsing (Collins, 1997) Constituency parsing aims to produce a syntactic parse tree for each sentence. Each node in the tree is an individual span associated with a constituent label, and spans are nested. -Part-of-speech Tagging (Ratnaparkhi, 1996; Toutanova et al., 2003) POS tagging is another sequence labeling task, where every single token is an individual span with a POS tag. -Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (Pontiki et al., 2014) ABSA is a task that consists of identifying certain spans as aspect terms and predicting their associated sentiments.

text: In contrast to Zhu et al. (2019), Ippolito et al. (2019), Joshi et al. (2020), Shih et al. (2019), Kang and Hovy (2019), and Bhagavatula et al. (2020), our approach is fundamentally different. We make full use of the existing large-scale pretrained models BERT and GPT-2 to learn smooth sentence embeddings in the latent semantic space, and then we process sentence-level information within this space.
source_text: Compared with (Zhu et al., 2019; Ippolito et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2020; Shih et al., 2019; Kang and Hovy, 2019; Bhagavatula et al., 2020; , our approach is clearly different. We fully exploit existing large-scale pretrained models BERT and GPT-2 to learn smooth sentence embeddings in the latent semantic space, and then process sentence-level information in this space.

text: All of the aforementioned datasets are described as “open-domain,” since the underlying corpus is Wikipedia. There are likewise datasets for specialized domains. The biomedical QA dataset (Tsatsaronis et al., 2015) included 29 development questions—arguably too few to train an automated system—and 282 test questions, organized into four categories: “yes/no,” factoid, list, and summary. InsuranceQA (Ins), a dataset for the insurance sector, is a corpus for intent detection rather than for MRC.
source_text: All of the above datasets are said to be "opendomain", as the corpus is Wikipedia. There are also datasets for specialized domains. The biomedical QA dataset (Tsatsaronis et al., 2015) contained 29 development questions (arguably too few for training an automated system) and 282 test questions, divided into four categories-'yes/no', factoid, list, and summary. InsuranceQA (Ins), a dataset for the insurance industry, is a corpus for intent detection, rather than for MRC.

text: WarmProtoZero (WPZ) (Fritzler et al., 2019) is a few-shot sequence labeling model that treats sequence labeling as the classification of each individual word. It pre-trains a prototypical network (Snell et al., 2017) on source domains and then uses it to perform word-level classification in target domains without further training. Fritzler et al. (2019) employ randomly initialized word embeddings. To remove the effect of differing embedding methods, we additionally implement WPZ with pre-trained GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) and BERT embeddings.
source_text: WarmProtoZero (WPZ) (Fritzler et al., 2019) is a few-shot sequence labeling model that regards sequence labeling as classification of every single word. It pre-trains a prototypical network (Snell et al., 2017) on source domains, and utilize it to do word-level classification on target domains without training. Fritzler et al. (2019) use randomly initialized word embeddings. To eliminate the influence of different embedding methods, we further implement WPZ with the pre-trained embedding of GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) and BERT.

text: One application of KGs, for instance, is drug discovery grounded in biomedical knowledge (Mohamed et al., 2019). Building a curated biomedical KG, which is necessary for such an approach, is difficult and limited by the amount of human labor and domain expertise available. Many tools that might help people construct KGs, such as entity linkers, already require a KG as input. In addition, existing KG-construction methods often depend on the extensive structure of Wikipedia, including links and infoboxes, which is absent in many domains. We therefore ask whether it is possible to make predictions about, for example, novel drug applications directly from raw text, without the intermediate step of KG construction. Open information extraction systems (OIE) (Etzioni et al., 2011) automatically extract ("subject text", "relation text", "object text") triples from unstructured sources such as text. We may regard OIE output as an open knowledge graph (OKG), where vertices correspond to mentions of entities and edges correspond to open relations (see Fig. 1). Our broader question is whether, and in what way, reasoning can be performed over an OKG without canonicalization and without any supervision on its latent factual knowledge. This study focuses on the challenges of benchmarking the inference capabilities of models in this setting.
source_text: An application for KGs, for example, is the problem of drug discovery based on bio-medical knowledge (Mohamed et al., 2019) . The construction of a curated bio-medical KG, which is required for 1 For brevity, "entities" denotes both entities (e.g. Prince) and concepts (e.g. musician) throughout the paper. such an approach, is challenging and constrained by the available amount of human effort and domain expertise. Many tools that could assist humans in KG construction (e.g., an entity linker) need a KG to begin with. Moreover, current methods for KG construction often rely on the rich structure of Wikipedia, such as links and infoboxes, which are not available for every domain. Therefore, we ask if it is possible to make predictions about, for example, new drug applications from raw text without the intermediate step of KG construction. Open information extraction systems (OIE) (Etzioni et al., 2011) automatically extract ("subject text", "relation text", "object text")-triples from unstructured data such as text. We can view OIE data as an open knowledge graph (OKG) , in which vertices correspond to mentions of entities and edges to open relations (see Fig. 1 ). Our overarching interest is whether and how we can reason over an OKG without any canonicalization and without any supervision on its latent factual knowledge. The focus of this study are the challenges of benchmarking the inference abilities of models in such a setup.

text: 8. SciBERT To isolate the benefit of SPECTER’s citation-based fine-tuning objective, we include a controlled comparison with SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019). Following Devlin et al. (2019), we use the last-layer hidden state associated with the [CLS] token as the overall document representation. 17 9. Sentence BERT Sentence BERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019) is a general-domain pretrained model designed to embed sentences. The authors fine-tuned BERT with a triplet loss, in which positive sentences were drawn from the same document section as the seed sentence, and distractor sentences were taken from other document sections. Because the model is intended to encode sentences rather than paragraphs, we embed the title and each sentence in the abstract separately, sum the resulting embeddings, and L2-normalize the outcome to obtain a final 768-dimensional paper embedding. 18 During hyperparameter optimization, we selected how to compute TF and IDF weight values by considering the following non-redundant combinations of scikit-learn’s TfidfVectorizer (Pedregosa et al., 2011) parameters: sublinear_tf, binary, use_idf, smooth_idf. In total, there were 9 parameter combinations. The IDF values were estimated on the training set. The remaining parameters were set as follows: min_df=3, max_df=0.75, strip_accents='ascii', stop_words='english', norm=None, lowercase=True. For fastText training, we used all default parameters except for setting dimension to 300 and minCount to 25, owing to the large corpus.
source_text: 8. SciBERT To isolate the advantage of SPECTER's citation-based fine-tuning objective, we add a controlled comparison with SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) . Following Devlin et al. (2019) we take the last layer hidden state corresponding to the [CLS] token as the aggregate document representation. 17 9. Sentence BERT Sentence BERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019 ) is a general-domain pretrained model aimed at embedding sentences. The authors fine-tuned BERT using a triplet loss, where positive sentences were from the same document section as the seed sentence, and distractor sentences came from other document sections. The model is designed to encode sentences as opposed to paragraphs, so we embed the title and each sentence in the abstract separately, sum the embeddings, and L2 normalize the result to produce a final 768-dimensional paper embedding. 18 During hyperparameter optimization we chose how to compute TF and IDF values weights by taking the following non-redundant combinations of scikit-learn's TfidfVectorizer (Pedregosa et al., 2011) parameters: sublinear_tf, binary, use_idf, smooth_idf. There were a total of 9 parameter combinations. The IDF values were estimated on the training set. The other parameters were set as follows: min_df=3, max_df=0.75, strip_accents='ascii', stop_words='english', norm=None, lowercase=True. For training of fasttext, we used all default parameters with the exception of setting dimension to 300 and minCount was set to 25 due to the large corpus.

text: A key property of the machine learning model is its robustness against adversarial attack. We (Nie et al., 2019) 57.4 48.3 43.5 49.3 ---44.2 XLNet LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) 67.6 50.7 48.3 55.1 ---52.0 RoBERTa LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) (Nie et al., 2019) evaluate our model on an adversarial natural language inference (ANLI) dataset (Nie et al., 2019). We assess the performance of SMART on each subset, namely R1, R2, and R3, of the ANLI development and test sets. The results are reported in Table 6. Table 6 presents the outcomes of training on the combined NLI data (ANLI (Nie et al., 2019) + MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) + SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015) + FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018)) as well as training on ANLI data alone. Under the combined-data setting, we observe that SMART RoBERTa-LARGE achieves the best performance among all strong baselines, raising the benchmark to 57.1%. Compared with the RoBERTa LARGE baseline, SMART RoBERTa-LARGE yields an absolute improvement of 3.4% on the development set and 7.4% on the test set, which demonstrates the robustness of SMART. We also observe that, in the ANLI-only setting, SMART RoBERTa-LARGE substantially outperforms the strong RoBERTa LARGE baseline by +5.2% (57.1% vs. 51.9%).
source_text: One important property of the machine learning model is its robustness to adversarial attack. We (Nie et al., 2019) 57.4 48.3 43.5 49.3 ---44.2 XLNet LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) 67.6 50.7 48.3 55.1 ---52.0 RoBERTa LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) (Nie et al., 2019) test our model on an adversarial natural language inference (ANLI) dataset (Nie et al., 2019) . We evaluate the performance of SMART on each subset (i.e., R1,R2,R3) of ANLI dev and test set. The results are presented in Table 6 . Table 6 shows the results of training on combined NLI data (ANLI (Nie et al., 2019 ) + MNLI (Williams et al., 2018 ) + SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015) + FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018) ) and training on only ANLI data. In the combined data setting, we obverse that SMART RoBERTa-LARGE obtains the best performance compared with all the strong baselines, pushing benchmarks to 57.1%. In case of the RoBERTa LARGE baseline, SMART RoBERTa-LARGE outperforms 3.4% absolute improvement on dev and 7.4% absolute improvement on test, indicating the robustness of SMART. We obverse that in the ANLI-only setting, SMART RoBERTa-LARGE outperforms the strong RoBERTa LARGE baseline with a large margin, +5.2% (57.1% vs. 51.9%)

text: An expanding body of work has demonstrated that English linguistic patterns align conveniently with nonlinguistic biases in neural language models, thereby yielding performance benefits for English models, even though such models are unable to acquire comparable patterns in languages that do not resemble English (e.g., Dyer et al., 2019). Combined with recent findings that these models exhibit a strong recency bias (Ravfogel et al., 2019), this implies that one attachment strategy, namely LOW, should be learned more readily. Consequently, the models may seem to behave in a humanlike manner on English while nevertheless failing to capture the crosslinguistically more prevalent HIGH attachment preference observed in Spanish. The present study addresses these issues by first showing, through a synthetic language experiment, that recurrent neural network (RNN) language models (LMs) can learn either attachment type (Section 4). We then show that, when trained on real corpus data in multiple languages (English and Spanish; Sections 5-7), these models nevertheless display a robust LOW preference.
source_text: A growing body of literature has shown that English linguistic structures conveniently overlap with non-linguistic biases in neural language models leading to performance advantages for models of English, without such models being able to learn comparable structures in non-English-like languages (e.g., Dyer et al., 2019) . This, coupled with recent work showing that such models have a strong recency bias (Ravfogel et al., 2019) , suggests that one of these attachment types (LOW), will be more easily learned. Therefore, the models might appear to perform in a humanlike fashion on English, while failing on the crosslinguistically more common attachment preference (HIGH) found in Spanish. The present study investigates these concerns by first establishing, via a synthetic language experiment, that recurrent neural network (RNN) language models (LMs) are capable of learning either type of attachment (Section 4). However, we then demonstrate that these models consistently exhibit a LOW preference when trained on actual corpus data in multiple languages (English and Spanish; Sections 5-7).

text: Perplexity is employed as an estimate of the degree of fit between a probabilistic language model and a segment of previously unseen text. The idea of using the perplexity of an n-gram model, which is a derivative of cross-entropy, as a proxy for syntactic complexity in spoken narratives was introduced by Roark et al. (2007) and later applied to transcribed responses from logical memory (story recall) tests produced by patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI: a common precursor to an AD diagnosis). In that study, sequences of part-of-speech (POS) tags were used to train bi-gram models on logical memory narratives, after which the cross-entropy of these models was evaluated on held-out cross-validation folds. The authors reported significantly higher mean cross-entropy values in the narratives of MCI patients than in those of controls. Later work extended the use of POS cross-entropy as one of the linguistic features in a predictive model for MCI detection (Roark et al., 2011).
source_text: Perplexity is used as an estimate of the fit between a probabilistic language model and a segment of pre-viously unseen text. The notion of applying n-gram model perplexity (a derivative of cross-entropy) as a surrogate measure of syntactic complexity in spoken narratives was proposed by Roark et al. (2007) and applied to transcribed logical memory (story recall) test responses by patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI: a frequent precursor to AD diagnosis). In this work, sequences of part-of-speech (POS) tags were used to train bi-gram models on logical memory narratives, and then cross-entropy of these models was computed on held-out crossvalidation folds. They found significantly higher mean cross-entropy values in narratives of MCI patients as compared to controls. Subsequent work expanded the use of POS cross-entropy as one of the language characteristics in a predictive model for detecting MCI (Roark et al., 2011) .

text: Encoder-decoder architectures, such as Seq2Seq models (Sutskever et al., 2014), constitute a widely used class of methods for mapping source sequences (here, words) to target sequences (here, slot labels) of variable length. Seq2Seq models have also been employed to produce agent responses without relying on intermediate dialogue components such as the DST or the Natural Language Generator (Gangadharaiah et al., 2018). Nevertheless, relatively little work has incorporated a deeper understanding of semantic representations in task-oriented dialogue. A notable exception is the recent study by Gupta et al. (2018), which adopted a hierarchical dialogue representation that can be readily parsed by off-the-shelf constituency-based parsers. Neural constituency parsers (Socher et al., 2011; Shen et al., 2018) operate directly on the input sentence; consequently, sentences that convey the same meaning may nevertheless be assigned different syntactic structures.
source_text: Encoder-Decoder architectures, e.g. Seq2Seq models (Sutskever et al., 2014) , are a popular class of approaches to the problem of mapping source sequences (here words) to target sequences (here slot labels) of variable length. Seq2Seq models have been used to generate agent responses without the need for intermediate dialog components such as the DST or the Natural Language Generator (Gangadharaiah et al., 2018). However, there has not been much work that uses deeper knowledge of semantic representations in task-oriented dialog. A notable exception is recent work by Gupta et.al (2018) , who used a hierarchical representation for dialog that can be easily parsed by off-the-shelf constituency-based parsers. Neural constituency parsers (Socher et al., 2011; Shen et al., 2018) work directly off the input sentence, and as a result, different sentences with the same meaning end up having different syntactic structures.

text: A wide range of natural language processing (NLP) tasks has been developed to examine different aspects of human language, including syntax (e.g., constituency and dependency parsing), semantics (e.g., semantic role labeling), information content (e.g., named entity recognition and relation extraction), and sentiment (e.g., sentiment analysis). At first sight, these tasks appear to differ markedly both in the form of their outputs and in the kinds of information they are intended to capture. To accommodate these disparate properties, researchers typically employ neural network architectures tailored specifically to each task. In this paper, we pose a simple question: are task-specific architectures truly necessary? Or, with an appropriate representational methodology, can we design a single model that can perform and achieve state-of-the-art results on a wide range of natural language analysis tasks?
source_text: A large number of natural language processing (NLP) tasks exist to analyze various aspects of human language, including syntax (e.g., constituency and dependency parsing), semantics (e.g., semantic role labeling), information content (e.g., named entity recognition and relation extraction), or sentiment (e.g., sentiment analysis). At first glance, these tasks are seemingly very different in both the structure of their output and the variety of information that they try to capture. To handle these different characteristics, researchers usually use specially designed neural network architectures. In this paper we ask the simple questions: are the task-specific architectures really necessary? Or with the appropriate representational methodology, can we devise a single model that can performand achieve state-of-the-art performance on -a large number of natural language analysis tasks?

text: Document-level relation extraction. Early work mainly addresses relation prediction between entities within a single sentence by modeling interactions in the input sequence (Zeng et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2020) or in the corresponding dependency tree (Xu et al., 2015a,b; Liu et al., 2015; Miwa and Bansal, 2016; . These methods do not model interactions across mentions and overlook relations that are expressed across sentence boundaries. More recent studies have started to investigate cross-sentence extraction (Peng et al., 2017; Gupta et al., 2018; Song et al., 2018c; Song et al., 2019). Rather than relying on discourse-structure understanding techniques (Liu et al., 2019a; Lei et al., 2017; Lei et al., 2018), these methods use the dependency graph to represent inter-sentence interactions, although their coverage is still restricted to a few sentences. More recently, the extraction scope has been extended to the full document (Verga et al., 2018; Jia et al., 2019; Sahu et al., 2019) in the biomedical domain by considering only a small number of relations among chemicals. In contrast to prior work, we study document-level relation extraction datasets (Yao et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016a; Wu et al., 2019) from different domains with a large number of relations and entities, which require understanding the entire document and carrying out multi-hop reasoning.
source_text: Document-level relation extraction. Early efforts focus on predicting relations between entities within a single sentence by modeling interactions in the input sequence (Zeng et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2020) or the corresponding dependency tree (Xu et al., 2015a,b; Liu et al., 2015; Miwa and Bansal, 2016; . These approaches do not consider interactions across mentions and ignore relations expressed across sentence boundaries. Recent work begins to explore cross-sentence extraction Peng et al., 2017; Gupta et al., 2018; Song et al., 2018c Song et al., , 2019 . Instead of using discourse structure understanding techniques (Liu et al., 2019a; Lei et al., 2017 Lei et al., , 2018 , these approaches leverage the dependency graph to capture inter-sentence interactions, and their scope is still limited to several sentences. More recently, the extraction scope has been expanded to the entire document (Verga et al., 2018; Jia et al., 2019; Sahu et al., 2019; in the biomedical domain by only considering a few relations among chemicals. Unlike previous work, we focus on document-level relation extraction datasets (Yao et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016a; Wu et al., 2019) from different domains with a large number of relations and entities, which require understanding a document and performing multi-hop reasoning.

text: BERT, SA, LM, ILM Score (%) 20 29 41 45 Table 2: Human evaluation results. We employ BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), the best model from Zhu et al. (2019) (SA), as well as our LM and ILM models, to replace random sentences in five-sentence stories from the STORIES test set. We then ask human annotators to determine which of the five sentences was produced by a machine. We report each model’s score as the percentage of infilled stories in which the human failed to identify the machine-generated sentence. Our ILM model obtains a higher score than all other models. Note that the maximum score is effectively 80%, since a perfect model would lead annotators to choose one of the five sentences at random.
source_text: BERT SA LM ILM Score (%) 20 29 41 45 Table 2 : Human evaluation results. We use BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) , the best model from Zhu et al. (2019) (SA), and our LM and ILM models to replace random sentences in five-sentence stories from the STORIES test set. Then, we task humans with identifying which sentence of the five was generated by a machine. We report the score of each model: the percentage of infilled stories where the human failed to identify the machine-generated sentence. Our ILM model achieves a higher score than all of the other models. Note that the max score is effectively 80%, as a perfect model would cause annotators to randomly choose one of the five sentences.

text: To evaluate LP performance, the LP model is trained on known facts and then assessed on its ability to predict unknown facts, that is, facts not observed during training. A simple but flawed way to adapt this procedure to OKGs is to sample a set of evaluation triples from the OKG and use the remaining portion of the OKG for training. To understand why this is problematic, consider the test triple ("NBC-TV", "has office in", "New York") and assume that the triple ("NBC", "has headquarter in", "NYC") is also included in the OKG. The latter triple effectively leaks the test fact. If such facts are not removed from the training data, a successful model merely paraphrases known facts rather than performing reasoning, that is, it does not predict genuinely new facts. In addition, we also want to measure whether there are other trivial explanations for predicting an evaluation fact. For instance, how much can be inferred from simple popularity statistics, such as only the mention, e.g. ("NBC-TV", ?), or only the relation, e.g. ("has office in", ?). Such non-relational information likewise does not require reasoning over the graph.
source_text: To evaluate LP performance, the LP model is trained on known facts and evaluated to predict unknown facts, i.e., facts not seen during training. A simple but problematic way to transfer this approach to OKGs is to sample a set of evaluation triples from the OKG and to use the remaining part of the OKG for training. To see why this approach is problematic, consider the test triple ("NBC-TV", "has office in", "New York") and suppose that the triple ("NBC", "has headquarter in", "NYC") is also part of the OKG. The latter triple essentially leaks the test fact. If we do not remove such facts from the training data, a successful models only paraphrases known facts but does not perform reasoning, i.e., does not predict genuinely new facts. Furthermore, we also want to quantify if there are other trivial explanations for the prediction of an evaluation fact. For example, how much can be predicted with simple popularity statistics, i.e., only the mention, e.g. ("NBC-TV", ?), or only the relation, e.g. ("has office in", ?). Such non-relational information also does not require reasoning over the graph.

text: Recently, Tao et al. (2017) introduced a hybrid referenced-unreferenced metric called RUBER, in which the metric is trained without human responses by bootstrapping negative samples directly from the dataset. However, referenced metrics, including RUBER because it is partly referenced, are not practical for evaluating dialogue models in an online setting—when the model is matched against a human agent (model-human) or another model agent (model-model)—because no reference response is available. In such settings, models are typically evaluated directly by humans, a process that is costly and requires careful training of annotators (Li et al., 2019).
source_text: Recently, Tao et al. (2017) proposed a hybrid referenced-unreferenced metric named RUBER, where the metric is trained without requiring human responses by bootstrapping negative samples directly from the dataset. However, referenced metrics (including RUBER, as it is part referenced) are not feasible for evaluation of dialogue models in an online setting-when the model is pitched against a human agent (model-human) or a model agent (model-model)-due to lack of a reference response. In this setting, models are usually eval-uated directly by humans, which is costly and requires careful annotator training (Li et al., 2019) .

text: Although most commercial dialogue systems depend on hand-crafted, narrow skills (e.g., see Zhou et al. (2020); Ram et al. (2018)), usually centered on distinct task-oriented functions such as setting alarms, adding calendar entries, and the like, our interest lies in models that exhibit a range of qualities in open-domain dialogue. Moreover, we concentrate on skills that can be learned end to end, since end-to-end learning offers the prospect of improved generalization to unseen domains.
source_text: While most commercial dialogue systems rely on hand-coded narrow skills (e.g., see Zhou et al. (2020) ; Ram et al. (2018) ), typically focusing on separate task-oriented features such as alarm setting, calendar entries, etc., we are interested in models that display various qualities in opendomain dialogue. Further, we focus on skills that can be learned end-to-end, as end-to-end learning affords the promise of better generalization to unseen domains.

text: The lower portion of Table 2 presents the performance of UNMT with XLM, which performs cross-lingual pretraining on concatenated non-parallel corpora (Lample and Conneau, 2019), as well as the performance of our AT/Bi-view AT models with anchored cross-lingual pretraining, namely ACP. The results indicate that our proposed AT methods remain superior even when equipped with cross-lingual pretraining. UNMT benefits substantially from XLM, attaining state-of-the-art unsupervised MT performance that surpasses Unsupervised SMT (Artetxe et al., 2019) and Unsupervised NMT (Lample et al., 2018b) on both close and distant language pairs. Nevertheless, ACP+AT/Bi-view AT consistently outperforms XLM+UNMT. In particular, on distant language pairs, ACP+Bi-view AT yields gains of 2.7–9.4 BLEU over the strong XLM+UNMT baseline. This suggests that AT/Bi-view AT with ACP induces closer language spaces through anchored pretraining and anchored training. We discuss this advantage in the analyses of Section 4.6.
source_text: The bottom part of Table 2 reports performances of UNMT with XLM, which conducts the crosslingual pretraining on concatenated non-parallel corpora (Lample and Conneau, 2019) , and performances of our AT/Bi-view AT with the anchored cross-lingual pretraining, i.e., ACP. The results show that our proposed AT approaches are still superior when equipped with the cross-lingual pretraining. UNMT obtains great improvement when combined with XLM, achieving state-of-the-art unsupervised MT performance better than Unsupervised SMT (Artetxe et al., 2019) and Unsupervised NMT (Lample et al., 2018b) across close and distant language pairs. ACP+AT/Bi-view AT performs consistently superior to XLM+UNMT. Especially on distant language pairs, ACP+Bi-view AT gains 2.7-9.4 BLEU improvements over the strong XLM+UNMT. This indicates that AT/Bi-view AT with ACP builds closer language spaces via anchored pretraining and anchored training. We present such advantage in the analyses of Section 4.6.

text: There have also been research efforts on parallel sentence extraction that do not depend on document alignment. Munteanu and Marcu (2002) extract parallel phrases from comparable corpora by using bilingual tries and seed dictionaries. Azpeitia et al. (2018) compute the Jaccard similarity of lexical translation overlap. Leong et al. (2018) employ an autoencoder together with a maximum entropy classifier. Bouamor and Sajjad (2018) examine cosine similarity over averaged multilingual word embeddings. Guo et al. (2018) propose a dual encoder architecture to learn multilingual sentence embeddings directly with additional negative examples. Wieting et al. (2019) derive sentence embeddings from sub-word embeddings and train a simpler model to discriminate between positive and negative examples. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) build on the work of Guo et al. (2018) and obtain state-of-the-art results by considering the margins of cosine similarities between pairs of nearest neighbours.
source_text: There are also research efforts on parallel sentence extraction without the reliance on document alignment. Munteanu and Marcu (2002) acquire parallel phrases from comparable corpora using bilingual tries and seed dictionaries. Azpeitia et al. (2018) computes Jaccard similarity of lexical translation overlap. Leong et al. (2018) use an autoencoder and a maximum entropy classifier. Bouamor and Sajjad (2018) consider cosine similarity between averaged multilingual word embeddings. Guo et al. (2018) design a dual encoder model to learn multilingual sentence em-beddings directly with added negative examples. Wieting et al. (2019) obtain sentence embeddings from sub-word embeddings and train a simpler model to distinguish positive and negative examples. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) refine Guo et al. (2018) 's work and achieve state-of-the-art by looking at the margins of cosine similarities between pairs of nearest neighbours.

text: With respect to contextualized models, the most prominent non-English contribution has been mBERT, which is released in two forms: (i) a single multilingual model covering 100 different languages and trained on Wikipedia data, and (ii) a single multilingual model for both Simplified and Traditional Chinese. Four fully trained monolingual ELMo models have been distributed for Japanese, Portuguese, German, and Basque5. In addition, 44 monolingual ELMo models6 were released by the HIT-SCIR team (Che et al., 2018) for the CoNLL 2018 Shared Task (Zeman et al., 2018), although their training sets were limited to 20 million words. A German BERT (Chan et al., 2019) and a French BERT model, CamemBERT (Martin et al., 2019), have also been released. Overall, however, no particular effort to build a set of high-quality monolingual contextualized representations has yet been demonstrated, or at least not at a scale comparable to that achieved for fixed word embeddings.
source_text: Regarding contextualized models, the most notable non-English contribution has been that of the mBERT , which is distributed as (i) a single multilingual model for 100 different languages trained on Wikipedia data, and as (ii) a single multilingual model for both Simplified and Traditional Chinese. Four monolingual fully trained ELMo models have been distributed for Japanese, Portuguese, German and Basque 5 ; 44 monolingual ELMo models 6 where also released by the HIT-SCIR team (Che et al., 2018) during the CoNLL 2018 Shared Task (Zeman et al., 2018) , but their training sets where capped at 20 million words. A German BERT (Chan et al., 2019) as well as a French BERT model (called CamemBERT) (Martin et al., 2019) have also been released. In general no particular effort in creating a set of highquality monolingual contextualized representations has been shown yet, or at least not on a scale that is comparable with what was done for fixed word embeddings.

text: We employ layer dropout with p = 0.2 and target embedding dropout with p = 0.1 (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). We also apply label smoothing with p = 0.1 (Szegedy et al., 2016) and constrain the target embedding norm to 1 (Nguyen and Chiang, 2018). During inference, we use a beam size of 15 and length normalization with exponent 1.5. The batch size is adjusted dynamically according to input sequence length, with an average batch size of 36. Optimization is performed with Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) using an initial learning rate of 0.0003, which is reduced by a factor of 0.5 when validation BLEU fails to improve for 10 epochs at first and thereafter for 5 epochs. We do not apply L2 weight decay or Gaussian noise, and we use a single model replica. In addition, we use input feeding (Luong et al., 2015) and omit utterances longer than 1500 frames during training to satisfy memory constraints.
source_text: We use layer dropout with p = 0.2 and target embedding dropout with p = 0.1 (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016) . We apply label smoothing with p = 0.1 (Szegedy et al., 2016) and fix the target embedding norm to 1 (Nguyen and Chiang, 2018). For inference, we use beam of size 15 and length normalization with exponent 1.5. We set the batch size dynamically depending on the input sequence length with average batch size was 36. We use Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with initial learning rate 0.0003, decayed by 0.5 when validation BLEU did not improve for 10 epochs initially and subsequently 5 epochs. We do not use L2 weight decay or Gaussian noise, and use a single model replica. We use input feeding (Luong et al., 2015) , and exclude utterances longer than 1500 frames in training for memory.

text: The procedure used to derive the dataset from OPIEC was as follows. At the outset, the dataset comprised more than 340M non-distinct triples, 5 enriched with metadata including the source sentence, linguistic annotations, confidence scores indicating the correctness of the extractions, and Wikipedia links in the triple’s subject or object. Triples of the following kinds were not useful for our purposes and were removed: (i) those with a confidence score < 0.3, 6 (ii) those containing personal or possessive pronouns, wh-determiners, adverbs, or determiners in one of their arguments, (iii) those whose relation came from an implicit appositive clause extraction, which we found to be very noisy, and (iv) those with a mention or relation longer than 10 tokens. This reduced the data to 80M non-distinct triples. We then lowercased the remaining 60M distinct triples and built an entity-mentions map from all triples with an annotated entity. We collected token frequencies and constructed a mention-token vocabulary containing the 200K most frequent tokens, as well as a relation-token vocabulary containing the 50K most frequent tokens. This was done to ensure that each token appeared at least ≈ 50 times. Finally, we retained only the triples whose tokens were included in these vocabularies, yielding the final 30M distinct triples.
source_text: The process of deriving the dataset from OPIEC was as follows. Initially, the dataset contained over 340M non-distinct triples, 5 which are enriched with metadata such as source sentence, linguistic annotations, confidence scores about the correctness of the extractions and the Wikipedia links in the triple's subject or object. Triples of the following types are not useful for our purpose and are removed: (i) having a confidence score < 0.3, 6 (ii) having personal or possessive pronouns, whdeterminer, adverbs or determiners in one of their arguments, (iii) having a relation from an implicit appositive clause extraction, which we found to be very noisy, and (iv) having a mention or a relation that is longer than 10 tokens. This left 80M nondistinct triples. Next, we lowercased the remaining 60M distinct triples and collect an entity-mentions map from all triples that have an annotated entity. We collected token counts and created a mention token vocabulary with the top 200K most frequent tokens, and a relation token vocabulary with the top 50K most frequent tokens. This was done to ensure that each token is seen at least ≈ 50 times. Finally, we kept only the triples whose tokens were contained in these vocabularies, i.e., the final 30M distinct triples.

text: Following the setting of (Hu et al., 2017), we primarily consider short text generation, with outputs no longer than 15 tokens, since this task is more amenable to both automatic and human evaluation. We conduct experiments on Yelp (Shen et al., 2017) and News Titles (Fu et al., 2018). Yelp is a corpus of restaurant reviews. We adopt the preprocessed version used in (Shen et al., 2017), which includes two polarity sentiment labels. For News Titles, we select titles from the Business, Entertainment, and Health categories for our experiments. Both Yelp and News Titles consist of relatively short texts. We remove texts longer than 15 words and then retain the top 8,900 and 10,000 words as the vocabularies for Yelp and News Titles, respectively. The dataset statistics are reported in Table 1. We discard the labels in the original training and validation splits. The original training split is used as the unlabeled corpus, the validation split is used to choose the best unsupervised models, and the test split is treated as the labeled conditional text.
source_text: Following the setting of (Hu et al., 2017) , we mainly focus on short text generation (no longer than 15 tokens), which is easier for both automatic and human evaluations. We use Yelp (Shen et al., 2017) and News Titles (Fu et al., 2018) for experiments. Yelp is a collection of restaurant reviews. We use the pre-processed version used in (Shen et al., 2017) , where two polarity sentiment labels are provided. For News Titles, we choose the titles belong to Business, Entertainment and Health categories for our experiments. Both Yelp and News Titles are datasets with relatively short text. We filter out text longer than 15 words, then choose the top 8,900 and 10,000 words as the vocabulary for Yelp and News Titles, respectively. The statistics of the two datasets are listed in Table 1 . We discard the labels in the original training and validation splits. We use the original training split as the unlabeled corpus; the validation split to select the best unsupervised models, and the test split as the labeled conditional text.

text: Because both interpretations are equally semantically plausible in the absence of contextual support, one might expect humans to select between HIGH and LOW at chance level. However, it has been widely shown that English speakers more often construe the relative clause as modifying the lower nominal rather than the higher nominal, that is, they exhibit a LOW bias (Carreiras and Clifton Jr., 1993; Frazier and Clifton, 1996; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003). In fact, LOW bias is typologically much less common than HIGH bias (Brysbaert and Mitchell, 1996). A prototypical example of a language with a HIGH attachment bias is Spanish (see Carreiras and Clifton Jr., 1993; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003).
source_text: As both interpretations are equally semantically plausible when no supporting context is given, we might expect that humans choose between HIGH and LOW at chance. However, it has been widely established that English speakers tend to interpret the relative clause as modifying the lower nominal more often than the higher nominal (i.e. they have a LOW bias; 1 Carreiras and Clifton Jr, 1993; Frazier and Clifton, 1996; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003) . LOW bias is actually typologically much rarer than HIGH bias (Brysbaert and Mitchell, 1996) . A proto-typical example of a language with HIGH attachment bias is Spanish (see Carreiras and Clifton Jr, 1993; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003) .

text: where x denotes a review in the corpus, 1(•) is an indicator function, and P, R, and F1 are the ROUGE-1 precision, recall, and F1, respectively. The reviews with the highest F1 are chosen as noisy variants of y, yielding the noisy set X(d) (see Figure 1). In total, we generate 2 * N noisy versions of y, i.e., X = X(c) ∪ X(d), and construct our synthetic training data D = {(X, y)} by producing |D| pseudoreview-summary pairs. Both noising strategies are required to obtain aspect diversity across the input reviews. Segment noising yields reviews that may refer to aspects absent from the summary, whereas document noising produces reviews whose content is similar to the summary. Using only one of the noise functions reduces performance (see the ablation studies in Section 5). Examples of these noisy versions are provided in the Appendix.
source_text: where x is a review in the corpus, 1(•) is an indicator function, and P, R, and F 1 are the ROUGE-1 precision, recall, and F 1 , respectively. The reviews with the highest F 1 are selected as noisy versions of y, resulting in the noisy set X (d) (see Figure 1) . We create a total of 2 * N noisy versions of y, i.e., X = X (c) ∪X (d) and obtain our synthetic train-ing data D = {(X, y)} by generating |D| pseudoreview-summary pairs. Both noising methods are necessary to achieve aspect diversity amongst input reviews. Segment noising creates reviews which may mention aspects not found in the summary, while document noising creates reviews with content similar to the summary. Relying on either noise function alone decreases performance (see the ablation studies in Section 5). We show examples of these noisy versions in the Appendix.

text: To identify which explanations matter most, we organize them into several semantic categories (see Appendix A.3) and then add them cumulatively to the NoExp baseline. In particular, for Spouse we divide the explanations into the groups MARRIED (10 explanations), CHILDREN (5), ENGAGED (3), NEGA-TIVES (13), and MISC (9). Figure 3 shows that ExpBERT matches the performance of the NoExp baseline with 20x less data on Spouse (Left) and with 3x less data on TACRED (Right). We find that adding additional explanation groups improves performance (Table 3), which suggests that wide coverage of different explanatory factors may be beneficial for performance. We also observe that the MARRIED group, which contains paraphrases of {o 1 } is married to {o 2 }, by itself improves over NoExp, suggesting that multiple paraphrases of the same explanation can improve performance.
source_text: To understand which explanations are important, we group explanations into a few semantic categories (details in Appendix A.3) and cumulatively add them to the NoExp baseline. In particular, we break down explanations for Spouse into the Figure 3 : ExpBERT matches the performance of the NoExp baseline with 20x less data on Spouse (Left), and with 3x less data on TACRED (Right). groups MARRIED (10 explanations), CHILDREN (5 explanations), ENGAGED (3 explanations), NEGA-TIVES (13 explanations) and MISC (9 explanations). We find that adding new explanation groups helps performance (Table 3) , which suggests that a broad coverage of various explanatory factors could be helpful for performance. We also observe that the MARRIED group (which contains paraphrases of {o 1 } is married to {o 2 }) alone boosts performance over NoExp, which suggests that a variety of paraphrases of the same explanation can improve performance.

text: When narrating stories, people rely on both their own experiences (episodic knowledge; Conway et al., 1996, 2003) and their broader understanding of the world (semantic knowledge; Bartlett, 1932; Oatley, 1999). For instance, in Figure 1 (top), a remembered account of a birth will likely describe concrete events from that day, drawing heavily on the author’s episodic memory (Tulving, 1972). On the ….her husband called me and then drove her to the hospital. I joined her at the hospital. When we got the hospital, things became complicated. Her husband did everything he could to stay with her and keep her strong. She eventually delivered perfectly.
source_text: When telling stories, people draw from their own experiences (episodic knowledge; Conway et al., 1996 Conway et al., , 2003 and from their general world knowledge (semantic knowledge; Bartlett, 1932; Oatley, 1999) . For example, in Figure 1 (top), a recalled story about a birth will likely recount concrete events from that day, relying heavily on the author's episodic memory (Tulving, 1972) . On the ….her husband called me and then drove her to the hospital. I joined her at the hospital. When we got the hospital things got complicated. Her husband tried his best to be with her and to keep her strong. She eventually delivered perfectly.

text: Step 3 score(r) = score(z)+score(r a )+score(r b ) R.push(r, score(r)) end for end for return R major steps: Figure 3 illustrates the complete workflow for a single iteration (with the sentence root node chosen here for exposition). First, we identify subphrase pairs in the input phrase that conform to parse-tree boundaries, with each pair comprising nonoverlapping phrases (Step 1). Because the objective is to learn general syntax-driven reorderings, we abstract away the two subphrases and substitute nonterminal symbols for them, preserving only the constituent tag information. For instance, Figure 3 presents three phrase pairs that can be abstracted to produce the reduced sentence forms. We then apply a seq2seq model to generate rearrangements for each abstracted phrase (Step 2). Finally, this higher-level rearrangement is combined with recursively constructed phrase rearrangements inside the abstracted phrases to produce sentence-level rearrangements (Step 3).
source_text: Step 3 score(r) = score(z)+score(r a )+score(r b ) R.push(r, score(r)) end for end for return R major steps: Figure 3 shows the overall workflow for one iteration (here, the root node of the sentence is selected for illustration). First, we select sub-phrase pairs of the input phrase that respect parse-tree boundaries, where each pair consists of non-overlapping phrases (Step 1). Since the aim is to learn generic syntax-governed rearrangements, we abstract out the two sub-phrases, and replace them with non-terminal symbols, retaining only the constituent tag information. For example, we show three phrase pairs in Figure 3 that can be abstracted away to yield the reduced forms of the sentences. We then use a seq2seq model to obtain rearrangements for each abstracted phrase (Step 2). Finally, this top-level rearrangement is combined with recursively-constructed phrase rearrangements within the abstracted phrases to obtain sentence-level rearrangements (Step 3).

text: Natural language generation (NLG) from structured data or knowledge (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018) is an important research problem for a variety of NLP applications. Examples include task-oriented dialogue and question answering (He et al., 2017; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Su et al., 2016; Saha et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2016), as well as interdisciplinary domains such as medicine (Hasan and Farri, 2019; Cawsey et al., 1997) and healthcare (Hasan and Farri, 2019; DiMarco et al., 2007). There is substantial promise in deploying automatic NLG systems across many real-world applications. In recent years, deep neural network-based NLG systems have been developed, including those in the E2E challenge (Novikova et al., 2017), WEATHER-GOV (Liang et al., 2009), and more complex settings such as WIKIBIO (Liu et al., 2018) and RO-TOWIRE (Wiseman et al., 2017).
