source_text: Demonstrations are not the only way to incor-porate external expertise into the dialog manager. One alternative is to use supervised learning to train a neural network policy on an in-domain dialog dataset, and then fine-tune it with policygradient RL on a user-simulator (Su et al., 2016; Williams et al., 2017; Liu and Lane, 2017) . fine-tune their RL policy on human rather than simulated users. Another, parallel, approach to RL-based DMs aims to increase the frequency of meaningful rewards. use inverse RL to learn a dense reward based on a dialog corpus, while Lu et al. (2019) decompose the task into subgoals that can be regularly assessed.

text: Other work on document representation learning includes extensions of word vectors to document-level representations (Le and Mikolov, 2014; Ganesh et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2018; Gysel et al., 2017), convolution-based methods (Zamani et al., 2018), and variational autoencoders (Holmer and Marfurt, 2018). Closely related to document embedding, sentence embedding is a comparatively mature research area. Notable approaches comprise seq2seq models (Claudes et al., 2015), BiLSTM Siamese networks (Williams et al., 2018), methods that exploit supervised signals from other corpora (Conneau et al., 2017), techniques based on discourse relations (Nie et al., 2019), and BERT-based approaches (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). In contrast to our proposed method, most of these approaches do not incorporate any concept of inter-document relatedness when learning document embeddings.
source_text: There have been other efforts in document representation learning such as extensions of word vectors to documents (Le and Mikolov, 2014; Ganesh et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2018; Gysel et al., 2017) , convolution-based methods Zamani et al., 2018) , and variational autoencoders (Holmer and Marfurt, 2018; . Relevant to document embedding, sentence embedding is a relatively well-studied area of research. Successful approaches include seq2seq models (Kiros et al., 2015) , BiLSTM Siamese networks (Williams et al., 2018) , leveraging supervised data from other corpora (Conneau et al., 2017) , and using discourse relations (Nie et al., 2019) , and BERT-based methods (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019) . Unlike our proposed method, the majority of these approaches do not consider any notion of inter-document relatedness when embedding documents.

text: McCoy et al. developed the HANS challenge set, which contains examples of a variety of such constructions, and used it to demonstrate that when BERT is fine-tuned on the MNLI corpus (Williams et al., 2018), the resulting model attains high accuracy on the test set derived from that corpus but shows little sensitivity to syntax; for instance, it incorrectly inferred that (1) entails (2). We consider two possible explanations for why BERT fine-tuned on MNLI performs poorly on HANS. Under the Representational Inadequacy Hypothesis, BERT fails on HANS because its pretrained representations lack certain essential syntactic information. Under the Missed Connection Hypothesis, BERT does extract the relevant syntactic information from the input (cf. Goldberg 2019; Tenney et al. 2019), but it does not learn to exploit this information for HANS because MNLI provides too few training examples indicating how syntax should contribute to NLI (McCoy et al., 2019b). Both hypotheses may be true: BERT may have failed to learn some aspects of syntax altogether, while other aspects may have been learned but not used for inference.
source_text: McCoy et al. constructed the HANS challenge set, which includes examples of a range of such constructions, and used it to show that, when BERT is fine-tuned on the MNLI corpus (Williams et al., 2018) , the fine-tuned model achieves high accuracy on the test set drawn from that corpus, yet displays little sensitivity to syntax; the model wrongly concluded, for example, that (1) entails (2). We consider two explanations as to why BERT fine-tuned on MNLI fails on HANS. Under the Representational Inadequacy Hypothesis, BERT fails on HANS because its pretrained representations are missing some necessary syntactic information. Under the Missed Connection Hypothesis, BERT extracts the relevant syntactic information from the input (cf. Goldberg 2019; Tenney et al. 2019) , but it fails to use this information with HANS because there are few MNLI training examples that indicate how syntax should support NLI (McCoy et al., 2019b) . It is possible for both hypotheses to be correct: there may be some aspects of syntax that BERT has not learned at all, and other aspects that have been learned, but are not applied to perform inference.

text:
Her lips were parted, and the gentle breath  
Was now discerned there;—her dark, entangled eyes  
Orb within orb, more deep than sleep or death,  
Drank in the glories of the burning skies,  
Which, blending with her heart’s profound ecstasies,  
Gushed from her looks and motions;—and a light  
Of fluid tenderness, like love, did rise  
From all her frame, an atmosphere that quite  
Arrayed her in its beams, trembling, soft, and bright.
source_text:
Her lips were parted, and the measured breath
Was now heard there;—her dark and intricate eyes
Orb within orb, deeper than sleep or death,
Absorbed the glories of the burning skies,
Which, mingling with her heart’s deep ecstasies,
Burst from her looks and gestures;—and a light
Of liquid tenderness, like love, did rise
From her whole frame, an atmosphere which quite
Arrayed her in its beams, tremulous and soft and bright.

text:
Once I pass’d through a thronging city, stamping my brain for future
use with its sights, its architecture, its customs, its traditions,
Yet now of all that city I remember only a woman I met by chance
there who detained me for love of me,
Day by day and night by night we were together— all else has long
since been forgotten by me,
I remember, I say, only that woman who fondly clung to me,
Again we wander, we love, we part again,
Again she clasps me by the hand, I must not go,
I see her close beside me with silent lips, sad and trembling.
source_text:
Once I pass’d through a populous city imprinting my brain for future
use with its shows, architecture, customs, traditions,
Yet now of all that city I remember only a woman I casually met
there who detain’d me for love of me,
Day by day and night by night we were together--all else has long
been forgotten by me,
I remember I say only that woman who passionately clung to me,
Again we wander, we love, we separate again,
Again she holds me by the hand, I must not go,
I see her close beside me with silent lips sad and tremulous.

text:
THIRD VOICE (FROM THE AIR):
Since Earth uprose, I had arrayed
Its wastes in hues not their own,
And oft my serene rest had been stayed
By many a rending groan.
source_text:
THIRD VOICE (FROM THE AIR):
I had clothed, since Earth uprose,
Its wastes in colours not their own,
And oft had my serene repose
Been cloven by many a rending groan.

text:
CAMILLO:
Guards, bring him nearer to the Lady Beatrice;
He cowers from her eye like the leaf of autumn
Before the sharp breath of the most tranquil north.
source_text:
CAMILLO:
Guards, lead him nearer the Lady Beatrice;
He shrinks from her regard like autumn’s leaf
From the keen breath of the serenest north.

text:
Then from that solitude the sceptred wretch  
I drew, and, moved to pity at his change,  
With sorrowing words allay’d his savage mood.  
Yet he, while pride and fear in conflict strange  
Contended deep, with morose craft of hate  
Ill-hid, did glare on me, as doth a snake  
Toothless, yet venomous in its bent estate.  
Not scorn, but pity, in my breast did wake,  
Though desolate the desolator stood, and unaware  
The curses which he mocked had seized him by the hair.
source_text:
The sceptred wretch then from that solitude
I drew, and, of his change compassionate,
With words of sadness soothed his rugged mood.
But he, while pride and fear held deep debate,
With sullen guile of ill-dissembled hate
Glared on me as a toothless snake might glare:
Pity, not scorn I felt, though desolate
The desolator now, and unaware
The curses which he mocked had caught him by the hair.

text:
Before the ice lies in the pools,  
Before the skaters go,  
Or any cheek by nightfall  
Is dimmed beneath the snow,
source_text:
Before the ice is in the pools,
Before the skaters go,
Or any cheek at nightfall
Is tarnished by the snow,

text:
The Kettle o’ the Kirk and State,  
Mayhap a patch may break in’t;  
But deil a foreign tinkler loun  
Shall e’er drive ca’ a nail in’t.  
Our fathers’ blude the Kettle bought,  
And wha wad dare despoil it;  
By Heav’ns! the impious cur  
Shall fuel be to boil it!  
By Heav’ns! the impious cur  
Shall fuel be to boil it!
source_text:
The Kettle o’ the Kirk and State,
Perhaps a clout may fail in’t;
But deil a foreign tinkler loun
Shall ever ca’a nail in’t.
Our father’s blude the Kettle bought,
And wha wad dare to spoil it;
By Heav’ns! the sacrilegious dog
Shall fuel be to boil it!
By Heav’ns! the sacrilegious dog
Shall fuel be to boil it!

text:
Then Rome arose, and from thy bosom deep and fairest,
Like a wolf-cub from a Cadmaean Maenad,
She drank the milk of greatness, though thy dearest
From that Elysian fare was yet unweaned;
And many a deed of awful uprightness
Was hallowed by thy tender love;
And in thy smile, and at thy side,
Saintly Camillus lived, and steadfast Atilius died.
But when tears soiled thy robe of vestal whiteness,
And gold profaned thy Capitolian throne,
Thou didst forsake, with spirit-winged lightness,
The senate of the tyrants: they sank prone
Slaves of one tyrant: Palatinus sighed
Faint echoes of Ionian song; that strain
Thou didst delay to hear, lamenting to disown
source_text:
Then Rome was, and from thy deep bosom fairest,
Like a wolf-cub from a Cadmaean Maenad,
She drew the milk of greatness, though thy dearest
From that Elysian food was yet unweaned;
And many a deed of terrible uprightness
By thy sweet love was sanctified;
And in thy smile, and by thy side,
Saintly Camillus lived, and firm Atilius died.
But when tears stained thy robe of vestal-whiteness,
And gold profaned thy Capitolian throne,
Thou didst desert, with spirit-winged lightness,
The senate of the tyrants: they sunk prone
Slaves of one tyrant: Palatinus sighed
Faint echoes of Ionian song; that tone
Thou didst delay to hear, lamenting to disown

text: “And how Farmer James would curse, and call thee a fool, would he not, Joseph, when he saw his name all turned inside-out-like?” continued Matthew Moon, with feeling.
source_text: “And how Farmer James would cuss, and call thee a fool, wouldn’t he, Joseph, when ’a seed his name looking so inside-out-like?” continued Matthew Moon with feeling.

text:
The red-coat lads, wi’ black cockauds,  
To meet them were na slack, man;  
They rush’d and push’d, and blude outgush’d,  
And mony a bouk fell back, man:  
The great Argyle led on his files,  
I wat they glanced full twenty miles;  
They hough’d the clans like nine-pin kyles,  
They hack’d and hash’d, while braid-swords clash’d,  
And thro’ they dash’d, and hew’d and smash’d,  
Till fey men died awa, man.  
La, la, la, la, &c.
source_text:
The red-coat lads, wi’ black cockauds,
To meet them were na slaw, man;
They rush’d and push’d, and blude outgush’d
And mony a bouk did fa’, man:
The great Argyle led on his files,
I wat they glanced twenty miles;
They hough’d the clans like nine-pin kyles,
They hack’d and hash’d, while braid-swords, clash’d,
And thro’ they dash’d, and hew’d and smash’d,
Till fey men died awa, man.
La, la, la, la, &c.

text:
Ev’n as he stands, cauld in his graff,  
The swap we yet shall do’t;  
Tak thou the carlin’s carcase aff,  
Thou’se win the saul o’boot.
source_text:
Ev’n as he is, cauld in his graff,
The swap we yet will do’t;
Tak thou the carlin’s carcase aff,
Thou’se get the saul o’boot.

text:
“Stop thief!” Dame Nature cried to Death,  
As Willy yielded up his latest breath;  
How shall I make a fool once more?  
My choicest pattern thou hast borne away.
source_text:
“Stop thief!” dame Nature call’d to Death,
As Willy drew his latest breath;
How shall I make a fool again?
My choicest model thou hast ta’en.

text:
As consequent upon the store of summer rains,  
Or wayward rills in autumn’s flowing season,  
Or many a herb-bound brook’s reticulations,  
Or subterranean sea-rills making for the sea,  
Songs of the years continued I do sing.
source_text:
As consequent from store of summer rains,
Or wayward rivulets in autumn flowing,
Or many a herb-lined brook’s reticulations,
Or subterranean sea-rills making for the sea,
Songs of continued years I sing.

text:
You left me, sweet, two bequests,—
A bequest of love
That e'en a Heavenly Father might accept,
Had He the offering of;
source_text:
You left me, sweet, two legacies, --
A legacy of love
A Heavenly Father would content,
Had He the offer of;

text:
The dainty breezes, with their brooms,
Do sweep the vale, the hill, the tree!
I prithee, my fair housewives,
Who may expected be?
source_text:
The tidy breezes with their brooms
Sweep vale, and hill, and tree!
Prithee, my pretty housewives!
Who may expected be?

text:
Swift at thy charm a shivering murmur like far thunder rolls,
Lo, where the armored men make haste—lo, through the dust-clouds the
gleam
of bayonets,
I see the soot-stained cannoneers, I note the crimson flash amid the
smoke, I hear the gun-roar cracking;
Nor war alone—thy dread music-song, wild player, summons every
sight of fear,
The deeds of merciless brigands, rapine, murder—I hear the cries for aid!
I see ships founder upon the sea, I behold on deck and under deck the
awful tableaux.
source_text:
Swift to thy spell a shuddering hum like distant thunder rolls,
Lo, where the arm’d men hasten--lo, mid the clouds of dust the glint
of bayonets,
I see the grime-faced cannoneers, I mark the rosy flash amid the
smoke, I hear the cracking of the guns;
Nor war alone--thy fearful music-song, wild player, brings every
sight of fear,
The deeds of ruthless brigands, rapine, murder--I hear the cries for help!
I see ships foundering at sea, I behold on deck and below deck the
terrible tableaus.

text:
Lord, we thank, and thee adore,  
For earthly gifts we little merit;  
At present we will ask no more—  
Let William Hislop yield the spirit.
source_text:
Lord, we thank, and thee adore,
For temporal gifts we little merit;
At present we will ask no more—
Let William Hislop give the spirit.

text:
The youngster and the ruddy-faced maid turn aside up the bushy hill,  
I peer at them from the summit.
source_text:
The youngster and the red-faced girl turn aside up the bushy hill,
I peeringly view them from the top.

text:
Geography, the world, is in it,  
The great Sea, the brood of isles, Polynesia, the coast beyond,  
The coast thou henceforth art facing--you Libertad! from thy Western  
golden shores,  
The lands there with their peoples, the millions en-masse  
are strangely here,  
The thronging market-places, the temples with idols ranged along the  
sides or at the end, bonze, brahmin, and llama,  
Mandarin, peasant, trader, artificer, and fisher,  
The singing-girl and the dancing-girl, the ecstatic beings, the  
shut-up emperors,  
Confucius himself, the mighty poets and heroes, the warriors, the castes,  
all,  
Marching up, pressing from every quarter, from the Altay mountains,  
From Thibet, from the four winding and far-streaming rivers of China,  
From the southern peninsulas and the half-continental islands, from  
Malaysia,  
These and whatever unto them belongs, palpable, display themselves to me, and  
are seiz’d by me,  
And I am seiz’d by them, and lovingly held by them,  
Till thus here I chant them all, Libertad! for themselves and for you.
source_text:
Geography, the world, is in it,
The Great Sea, the brood of islands, Polynesia, the coast beyond,
The coast you henceforth are facing--you Libertad! from your Western
golden shores,
The countries there with their populations, the millions en-masse
are curiously here,
The swarming market-places, the temples with idols ranged along the
sides or at the end, bonze, brahmin, and llama,
Mandarin, farmer, merchant, mechanic, and fisherman,
The singing-girl and the dancing-girl, the ecstatic persons, the
secluded emperors,
Confucius himself, the great poets and heroes, the warriors, the castes,
all,
Trooping up, crowding from all directions, from the Altay mountains,
From Thibet, from the four winding and far-flowing rivers of China,
From the southern peninsulas and the demi-continental islands, from
Malaysia,
These and whatever belongs to them palpable show forth to me, and
are seiz’d by me,
And I am seiz’d by them, and friendlily held by them,
Till as here them all I chant, Libertad! for themselves and for you.

text:
Pennant:

Come up here, bard, bard,
Come up here, soul, soul,
Come up here, dear little child,
To ride the clouds and winds with me, and play with the boundless light.
source_text:
Pennant:
Come up here, bard, bard,
Come up here, soul, soul,
Come up here, dear little child,
To fly in the clouds and winds with me, and play with the measureless light.

text:
In hollow voice, as faint a sound  
As in some charnel keeps its moan,  
What glides along the burial ground,  
The phantom claimed me as her own.
source_text:
In hollow voice, low as the sound
Which in some charnel makes its moan,
What floats along the burying ground,
The phantom claimed me as her own.

text:
A newer garden of making, no first-born loneliness,
Thick, glad, contemporary, crowded—myriads, towns and fields,
With iron woven through, compound, bound, many in one,
By all the earth’s giving—society of freedom, law, and thrift,
The crown and thriving paradise, thus far, of ages’ gatherings,
To make the past seem just.
source_text:
A newer garden of creation, no primal solitude,
Dense, joyous, modern, populous millions, cities and farms,
With iron interlaced, composite, tied, many in one,
By all the world contributed--freedom’s and law’s and thrift’s society,
The crown and teeming paradise, so far, of time’s accumulations,
To justify the past.

text:
I see the blood washed wholly from the axe,
Both blade and handle clean,
No more do they spurt the blood of European nobles, no more do they clasp
the necks of queens.
source_text:
I see the blood wash’d entirely away from the axe,
Both blade and helve are clean,
They spirt no more the blood of European nobles, they clasp no more
the necks of queens.

text:
O Liberty! O companion fit for me!  
Here, too, the blaze, the grape-shot, and the axe, held back in readiness, to bring  
them forth in time of need,  
Here, too, though long suppressed, can never be destroyed,  
Here, too, at last might rise, murderous and ecstatic,  
Here, too, demanding all the debt of vengeance paid.
source_text:
O Liberty! O mate for me!
Here too the blaze, the grape-shot and the axe, in reserve, to fetch
them out in case of need,
Here too, though long represt, can never be destroy’d,
Here too could rise at last murdering and ecstatic,
Here too demanding full arrears of vengeance.

text:
I yield nothing as duties,
What others yield as duties I yield as living impulses,
(Shall I make the heart’s action a duty?)
source_text:
I give nothing as duties,
What others give as duties I give as living impulses,
(Shall I give the heart’s action as a duty?)

text:
Life is only life, and death is death!
Joy is only joy, and breath is breath!
And if, in truth, I lose,
At least to know the worst is sweet.
Failure means only failure,
No darker thing can choose!
source_text:
Life is but life, and death but death!
Bliss is but bliss, and breath but breath!
And if, indeed, I fail,
At least to know the worst is sweet.
Defeat means nothing but defeat,
No drearier can prevail!

text:
Hail, string-empowerin’, clatterin’ Willie!  
Though fortune’s path be steep and stony hilly  
To every fiddling, rhyming billie,  
We pay no heed,  
But bear it like the unbroken filly,  
Proud in her speed.
source_text:
Hail, thairm-inspirin’, rattlin’ Willie!
Tho’ fortune’s road be rough an’ hilly
To every fiddling, rhyming billie,
We never heed,
But take it like the unback’d filly,
Proud o’ her speed.

text:
We fought the fight in detachments,  
Sallying out, we met the battle at different points, yet at each the luck was  
against us,  
Our foe pressed on, steadily gaining the advantage, and drove us back  
to the works on this hill,  
Till here we turned, threatening, and then he abandoned us.
source_text:
We fought the fight in detachments,
Sallying forth we fought at several points, but in each the luck was
against us,
Our foe advancing, steadily getting the best of it, push’d us back
to the works on this hill,
Till we turn’d menacing here, and then he left us.

text:
For whom I plundered the hollow,  
For whom I sold the vale,  
Many will surely question me,  
But I shall never tell!
source_text:
For whom I robbed the dingle,
For whom betrayed the dell,
Many will doubtless ask me,
But I shall never tell!

text:
Yet let me not grow overhasty,  
Long, indeed, have we lived, slept, filtered, and truly mingled  
into one;  
Then if we die, we die together, (yes, we shall stay one,)  
If we go anywhere, we’ll go together to meet whatever comes,  
Perhaps we’ll be better off, and merrier, and learn a thing or two,  
Perhaps it is you now really guiding me toward the true songs, (who  
can tell?)  
Perhaps it is you the mortal knob truly loosening, turning—so now, at last,  
Farewell—and hail, my Fancy.
source_text:
Yet let me not be too hasty,
Long indeed have we lived, slept, filter’d, become really blended
into one;
Then if we die we die together, (yes, we’ll remain one,)
If we go anywhere we’ll go together to meet what happens,
May-be we’ll be better off and blither, and learn something,
May-be it is yourself now really ushering me to the true songs, (who
knows?)
May-be it is you the mortal knob really undoing, turning--so now finally,
Good-bye--and hail! my Fancy.

text:
Songs of the prairies,  
Songs of the great flowing Mississippi, and onward to the Mexican sea,  
Songs of Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Iowa, Wisconsin, and Minnesota,  
Songs issuing from the heart from Kansas, and then in equal reach,  
Flashing in pulses of fire without pause to quicken all.
source_text:
Chants of the prairies,
Chants of the long-running Mississippi, and down to the Mexican sea,
Chants of Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Iowa, Wisconsin and Minnesota,
Chants going forth from the centre from Kansas, and thence equidistant,
Shooting in pulses of fire ceaseless to vivify all.

text:
O unstoppable, wandering race!
O cherished race in all! O my breast aches with gentle love for all!
O I grieve and yet rejoice, I am carried away with love for all,
Pioneers! O pioneers!
source_text:
O resistless restless race!
O beloved race in all! O my breast aches with tender love for all!
O I mourn and yet exult, I am rapt with love for all,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

text:
Forjesket sair, wi’ tired legs,  
Shoggin’ the corn through o’er the rigs,  
Or dealing out amang the naigs  
Their ten-hours’ bite,  
My awkward Muse wi’ pleading begs  
That I wad na write.
source_text:
Forjesket sair, with weary legs,
Rattlin the corn out-owre the rigs,
Or dealing thro’ amang the naigs
Their ten-hours’ bite,
My awkart Muse sair pleads and begs
I would na write.

text:
These are the days when birds return,
A scant few, a bird or two,
To cast a look behind them.
source_text:
These are the days when birds come back,
A very few, a bird or two,
To take a backward look.

text:
For me in vain the cowslips blow,
For me in vain the violets rise;
For me, in vale or woodland low,
The mavis and the lintwhite sing.
And maun I still, &c.
source_text:
In vain to me the cowslips blaw,
In vain to me the vi’lets spring;
In vain to me in glen or shaw,
The mavis and the lintwhite sing.
And maun I still, &c.

text:
It’s up yon heathered mountain,
An’ down yon stony glen,
We daurna gang a-milking,
For Charlie and his men,
An’ Charlie, &c.
source_text:
It’s up yon heathery mountain,
An’ down yon scroggie glen,
We daur na gang a milking,
For Charlie and his men,
An’ Charlie, &c.

text:
You are asking me questions too, and I hear you,  
I answer that I cannot answer; you must discover it on your own.
source_text:
You are also asking me questions and I hear you,
I answer that I cannot answer, you must find out for yourself.

text:
Perdition, darkling child of night!
Arise, and avenge the wounded right
Of Stewart’s royal line:
Drive on the unsilenced hounds of hell,
Till every affrighted echo tell
The crimson notes of the chase!
Straight on the prey direct their sight,
Straight on the vile usurping band,
The tools of faction, and the nation’s curse!
Hark, how the cry swells on the wind;
They leave the loitering gale behind,
Their savage rage, relentless, they outpour;
With murdering eyes already they devour;
See Brunswick worn, a wretched prey,
His life one poor despairing day,
Where every avenging hour still brings on one more curse!
Such havoc, roaring far and wide,
Their utter ruin bring,
The faithless apostates to their God,
Or rebels to their King.
source_text:
Perdition, baleful child of night!
Rise and revenge the injured right
Of Stewart’s royal race:
Lead on the unmuzzled hounds of hell,
Till all the frighted echoes tell
The blood-notes of the chase!
Full on the quarry point their view,
Full on the base usurping crew,
The tools of faction, and the nation’s curse!
Hark how the cry grows on the wind;
They leave the lagging gale behind,
Their savage fury, pitiless, they pour;
With murdering eyes already they devour;
See Brunswick spent, a wretched prey,
His life one poor despairing day,
Where each avenging hour still ushers in a worse!
Such havock, howling all abroad,
Their utter ruin bring,
The base apostates to their God,
Or rebels to their King.

text:
But one was silent; her cheeks and lips, most fair,  
Shifting their color like new-blown lilies,  
Beneath the bright acacia’s shadowy hair,  
Ruffled by the wind beneath the sunny noon,  
Betrayed the tremor of her soul; and very soon  
That Youth stood up, and, breathless, bent his gaze  
On her and me, as for some wordless boon:  
I smiled, and both their hands within my own I raised,  
And felt a gentle joy from what their spirits swayed.
source_text:
‘But one was mute; her cheeks and lips most fair,
Changing their hue like lilies newly blown,
Beneath a bright acacia’s shadowy hair,
Waved by the wind amid the sunny noon,
Showed that her soul was quivering; and full soon
That Youth arose, and breathlessly did look
On her and me, as for some speechless boon:
I smiled, and both their hands in mine I took,
And felt a soft delight from what their spirits shook.

text:
The wealth may prove a cheat,
And I be poorer than
This great purchaser could ever think,
The daily keep of Love
source_text:
The wealth might disappoint,
Myself a poorer prove
Than this great purchaser suspect,
The daily own of Love

text: Although inversion was the only transformation in this augmentation set, performance also improved substantially on constructions other than subject/object swap (Figure 2); for instance, the models handled examples involving a prepositional phrase more effectively, inferring, for example, that The judge behind the manager saw the doctors does not entail The doctors saw the manager (unaugmented: 0.41; large augmentation: 0.89). The improvement on cases designed to target the subsequence heuristic was much more modest, though still significant; this smaller gain suggests that contiguous subsequences are treated separately from lexical overlap more broadly. One exception was accuracy on “NP/S” inferences, such as the managers heard the secretary resigned The managers heard the secretary, which increased dramatically from 0.02 (unaugmented) to 0.50 (large augmentation). Additional gains for subsequence cases may therefore require augmentation with examples that include subsequences.
source_text: Although inversion was the only transformation in this augmentation set, performance also improved dramatically on constructions other than subject/object swap ( Figure 2) ; for example, the models handled examples involving a prepositional phrase better, concluding, for instance, that The judge behind the manager saw the doctors does not entail The doctors saw the manager (unaugmented: 0.41; large augmentation: 0.89). There was a much more moderate, but still significant, improvement on the cases targeting the subsequence heuristic; this smaller degree of improvement suggests that contiguous subsequences are treated separately from lexical overlap more generally. One exception was accuracy on "NP/S" inferences, such as the managers heard the secretary resigned The managers heard the secretary, which improved dramatically from 0.02 (unaugmented) to 0.50 (large augmentation). Further improvements for subsequence cases may therefore require augmentation with examples involving subsequences.

text: (Appendix C) while varying the infilling strategy and dataset. In addition to our proposed ILM strategy for infilling, we examine three baseline strategies: (1) language modeling (LM; “infilling” conditioned only on past context), (2) reverse language modeling (LM-Rev; “infilling” conditioned only on future context), and (3) language modeling over all available context (LM-All). LM-All simply concatenates x and x together, following Fedus et al. (2018). LM-All is arguably the most straightforward way to formulate infilling with LMs, but it yields long sequence lengths. Training examples for all strategies are shown in Figure 2. For each strategy, we also vary whether training is initialized from the pre-trained GPT-2 model or from scratch. Although there are mismatches between pre-training and our fine-tuning for most infilling strategies, all infilling experiments initialized from the pre-trained checkpoint outperformed their from-scratch counterparts. This suggests that ILM can effectively exploit large-scale language-modeling pre-training to improve infilling performance. Accordingly, we will henceforth discuss only the models initialized from the pre-trained checkpoint, although we report quantitative results for all models in Appendix D.
source_text: (Appendix C) while varying the infilling strategy and dataset. In addition to our proposed ILM strategy for infilling, we consider three baseline strategies: (1) language modeling (LM; "infilling" based only on past context), (2) reverse language modeling (LM-Rev; "infilling" based only on future context), and (3) language modeling based on all available context (LM-All). LM-All simply concatenates x andx together as in Fedus et al. (2018) . LM-All represents arguably the simplest way one could conceive of infilling with LMs, but results in long sequence lengths. Training examples for all strategies are depicted in Figure 2 . For each strategy, we also vary whether training is initialized from the pre-trained GPT-2 model or from scratch. Despite discrepancies between the pre-training and our fine-tuning for most infilling strategies, all of the infilling experiments initialized from the pre-trained checkpoint performed better than their from-scratch counterparts. This indicates that ILM can effectively leverage large-scale language modeling pre-training to improve infilling performance. Henceforth, we will only discuss the models initialized from the pre-trained checkpoint, though we report quantitative performance for all models in Appendix D.

text: For reasoning, our model treats the graph as a latent variable and infers it in an end-to-end manner. The structure induction module is developed on the basis of structured attention (Kim et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2018). Motivated by Liu and Lapata (2018), we adopt a variant of Kirchhoff's Matrix-Tree Theorem (Tutte, 1984; Koo et al., 2007) to infer the latent dependency structure.
source_text: for reasoning, our model treats the graph as a latent variable and induces it in an end-to-end fashion. The structure induction module is built based on the structured attention (Kim et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2018) . Inspired by Liu and Lapata (2018) , we use a variant of Kirchhoff's Matrix-Tree Theorem (Tutte, 1984; Koo et al., 2007) to induce the latent dependency structure.

text: Neural Network Language Models (NNLMs) have advanced rapidly over time, progressing from simple feedforward networks (Bengio et al., 2003) to models with recurrent connections (Mikolov et al., 2010) and LSTM units (Zaremba et al., 2014), and more recently to transformer architectures (Dai et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2019). These developments have led to steadily improving performance on benchmark datasets. Yet one component has remained comparatively unchanged: the output layer, typically implemented as a softmax over a dot product.
source_text: Neural Network Language Models (NNLMs) have evolved rapidly over the years from simple feed forward nets (Bengio et al., 2003) to include recurrent connections (Mikolov et al., 2010) and LSTM cells (Zaremba et al., 2014) , and most recently transformer architectures (Dai et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2019) . This has enabled ever-increasing performance on benchmark data sets. However, one thing has remained relatively constant: the softmax of a dot product as the output layer.

text: This work seeks to facilitate rapid understanding of a large scientific document by identifying a) the central concepts in a text and b) the most important relations that organize these central concepts. To this end, we introduce the task of Semi-Open Relation Extraction (SORE); Figure 1 depicts the SORE procedure. First, we identify the central concepts “safety” and “efficiency,” which participate in a TRADE-OFF relation. Next, by treating the argument concepts of the relation as anchor points, we can investigate additional concepts and relations, for example, “xylem” in Figure 1. Revealing such relations can help elucidate the meaning of unfamiliar concepts for a layperson (Mausam, 2016). We hypothesize that the SORE approach reduces the number of uninformative extractions without constraining RE to a fixed inventory of relations, which could more broadly benefit IE from scientific articles, such as materials discovery (Kononova et al., 2019) and drug-gene-mutation interactions (Jia et al., 2019).
source_text: This work aims to enable rapid comprehension of a large scientific document by identifying a) the central concepts in a text and b) the most significant relations that govern these central concepts. To this end, we introduce the task of Semi-Open Relation Extraction (SORE); Figure 1 illustrates the SORE process. First, we find the central concepts 'safety' and 'efficiency' involved in a TRADE-OFF relation. Then, by using the argument concepts of the relation as anchor points, we can explore further concepts and relations, e.g., 'xylem' in Figure 1. Uncovering these relations can elucidate the meaning of unfamiliar concepts to a layperson (Mausam, 2016) . The SORE approach is hypothesized to reduce the number of uninformative extractions without limiting RE to a finite set of relations, which could generally benefit IE from sci-entific articles, e.g., materials discovery (Kononova et al., 2019) and drug-gene-mutation interactions (Jia et al., 2019) .

text: We suspect that there are deep-seated systematic factors giving rise to this discrepancy between the expected distribution of human attachment preferences and the observed distribution of attachment in the Spanish training corpus. Findings from experimental psycholinguistics indicate that this problem may reflect a broader mismatch between language production and language comprehension. More specifically, Rohde (2015, 2018) has presented empirical evidence that the production and comprehension of these structures are shaped by distinct human biases. Production is governed by syntactic and information-structural considerations (e.g., topic), whereas comprehension is affected by those considerations together with pragmatic and discourse factors (e.g., coherence relations). Accordingly, the biases involved in language production constitute a proper subset of those involved in language comprehension. At present, RNN LMs are usually trained on production data, namely the generated text in Wikipedia.14 Consequently, they have access only to a subset of the biases required to acquire human-like attachment preferences. In its strongest form, this hypothesis implies that no quantity of production data (i.e. raw text) will ever be adequate for these models to generalize in a human-like way on comprehension tasks.
source_text: We suspect that there are deep systematic issues leading to this mismatch between the expected distribution of human attachment preferences and the actual distribution of attachment in the Spanish training corpus. Experimental findings from psycholinguistics suggest that this issue could follow from a more general mismatch between language production and language comprehension. In particular, Rohde (2015, 2018) have provided empirical evidence that the production and comprehension of these structures are guided by different biases in humans. Production is guided by syntactic and information structural considerations (e.g., topic), while comprehension is influenced by those considerations plus pragmatic and discourse factors (e.g., coherence relations). As such, the biases in language production are a proper subset of those of language comprehension. As it stands now, RNN LMs are typically trained on production data (that is, the produced text in Wikipedia). 14 Thus, they will have access to only a subset of the biases needed to learn human-like attachment preferences. In its strongest form, this hypothesis suggests that no amount of production data (i.e. raw text) will ever be sufficient for these models to generalizably pattern like humans during comprehension tasks.

text: Baselines We evaluate our systems against three prior methods: DRG (Li et al., 2018), Style Transfer Through Back-translation (BST) (Prabhumoye et al., 2018), and Style transfer from nonparallel text by cross alignment (Shen et al., 2017) (CAE). For DRG, we compare only with the best reported variant, delete-retrieve-generate. For all models, we adopt the experimental settings described in their original papers.
source_text: Baselines We compare our systems against three previous methods. DRG (Li et al., 2018) , Style Transfer Through Back-translation (BST) (Prabhumoye et al., 2018) , and Style transfer from nonparallel text by cross alignment (Shen et al., 2017) (CAE). For DRG, we only compare against the best reported method, delete-retrieve-generate. For all the models, we follow the experimental setups described in their respective papers.

text: Previous work on DST has adopted a range of approaches, from handcrafted features designed to extract utterance information (Wang and Lemon, 2013) to Convolutional Neural Networks for learning utterance representations. However, these models do not scale well because they do not share parameters across different slots. Zhong et al. (2018) and Nouri and Hosseini-Asl (2018) propose a global module that shares information to support knowledge transfer, but their methods depend on a predefined ontology. Xu and Hu (2018) employ a pointer network within a Seq2Seq architecture to address unseen slot values. They use a pre-trained BERT model (Devlin et al., 2019) to encode slots and utterances, and apply multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017) to identify relevant information in the dialogue context for slot-value prediction. Wu et al. (2019) introduce an encoder-decoder architecture with a copy mechanism, sharing all model parameters across all domains. Zhou and Small (2019) cast multidomain DST as a question answering task and apply reading comprehension techniques to produce answers through either span prediction or value prediction. Johnson et al. (2017b) propose single encoder-decoder models for zero-shot machine translation by jointly encoding the language and the input sentence, and Zhao and Eskenazi (2018) propose cross-domain zero-shot language generation using a cross-domain embedding space.
source_text: Previous works on DST use different approaches, ranging from using handcrafted features to elicit utterance information Wang and Lemon, 2013) . use Convolutional Neural Networks to learn utterance representations. However, their models do not scale as they do not share parameters across different slots. Zhong et al. (2018) and Nouri and Hosseini-Asl (2018) propose a new global module that shares information to facilitate knowledge transfer. However, they rely on a predefined ontology. Xu and Hu (2018) use a pointer network with a Seq2Seq architecture to handle unseen slot values. use a pre-trained BERT model (Devlin et al., 2019) to encode slots and utterances and uses multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017) to find relevant information in the dialogue context for predicting slot values. Wu et al. (2019) introduce an encoder-decoder architecture with a copy mechanism, sharing all model parameters between all domains. Zhou and Small (2019) formulate multidomain DST as a question answering task and use reading comprehension techniques to generate the answers by either span or value prediction. Johnson et al. (2017b) propose single encoderdecoder models for zero-shot machine translation by encoding language and input sentence jointly, and Zhao and Eskenazi (2018) propose cross-domain zero-shot language generation using a cross-domain embedding space.

text: All of our experiments are carried out in the fairseq (Ott et al., 2019) environment. We adopt the fairseq reimplementation of the 12-layer Transformer tailored for IWLST data. In particular, the embedding dimension is set to 512 for both the encoder and decoder, the number of attention heads is 4, and the feed-forward embedding dimension is 1024. This model is in fact larger than the base model of Vaswani et al. (2017) (76M versus 65M parameters). Note that the parameter count of the mixing model is k times larger, where k denotes the number of domains. For a fair comparison, all baselines are evaluated with both the above model and an enlarged variant whose embedding dimension is √k times larger (so that the weight matrices are k times larger). The enlarged model and the mixing model have the same number of parameters, and the reported baseline results are the best of the two. For optimization, we follow the training recipe supplied by fairseq. Specifically, we use Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.98, and weight decay set to 10−4. The learning rate is scheduled according to the inverse square root schedule (Vaswani et al., 2017), with 4000 warm-up steps, an initial warm-up learning rate of 10−7, and a maximum learning rate of 5 × 10−4. For effective training, Lgen is replaced with a label-smoothing cross-entropy loss using a smoothing parameter of 0.1 (Szegedy et al., 2016).
source_text: All of our experiments are conducted under fairseq (Ott et al., 2019) environment. We follow the fairseq re-implementation of 12-layer Transformer designed for IWLST data. Specifically, the embedding dimension is 512 for both the encoder and decoder, the number of heads is 4, and the embedding dimension in the feed-forward layer is 1024. Such a model is actually larger than the base model in Vaswani et al. (2017) (76M vs. 65M parameters) . Notice that, the number of parameters of the mixing model is k times larger (k is the number of domains). For a fair comparison, all baselines are tested using both the above model and an enlarged model, which has √ k times larger embedding dimension (so the weight matrices are k times larger). The enlarged model and the mixing model has the same number of parameters. The presented baseline results are the best of the two. In terms of the optimization, we follow the training recipe provided by fairseq. Specifically, we use Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) with β 1 = 0.9, β 2 = 0.98 with a weight decay parameter of 10 −4 . The learning rate follows the inverse square root schedule (Vaswani et al., 2017) with warm-up steps of 4000, initial warm-up learning rate of 10 −7 , and the highest learning rate of 5 × 10 −4 . For effective training, L gen is replaced by a label-smoothing cross-entropy loss with a smoothing parameter of 0.1 (Szegedy et al., 2016) .

text: Latent language models are generative models of text that jointly model the text and its underlying latent structure, such as the syntactic parse, coreference chains among entity mentions, or links from entities and relations mentioned in the text to an external knowledge graph. The advantages of modeling such structure include interpretability (Hayashi et al., 2020), improved performance on tasks that require structure (Dyer et al., 2016; Ji et al., 2017), and a better ability to generate coherent mentions of entities (Clark et al., 2018) and factually accurate text (Logan et al., 2019). However, showing that these models outperform traditional language models by evaluating their likelihood on benchmark datasets can be challenging, since exact computation requires marginalizing over all possible latent structures.
source_text: Latent language models are generative models of text that jointly represent the text and the latent structure underlying it, such as: the syntactic parse, coreference chains between entity mentions, or links of entities and relations mentioned in the text to an external knowledge graph. The benefits of modeling such structure include interpretability (Hayashi et al., 2020) , better performance on tasks requiring structure (Dyer et al., 2016; Ji et al., 2017) , and improved ability to generate consistent mentions of entities (Clark et al., 2018) and factually accurate text (Logan et al., 2019) . Unfortunately, demonstrating that these models provide better performance than traditional language models by evaluating their likelihood on benchmark data can be difficult, as exact computation requires marginalizing over all possible latent structures.

text: We evaluate individual scoring methods, multi-choice methods, and human performance in our experiment. Given a context c and four candidates (r 1 , r 2 , r 3 , r 4 ) , the individual scoring method computes a score for each option independently via g(c, r i ) and selects the candidate with the highest score among the four. In contrast, the multi-choice method identifies the best response through classification over all options, formulated as h (c, r 1 , r 2 , r 3 , r 4 ) . TF-IDF: The correct response typically shares more words with the context than do the incorrect ones. Following Lowe et al. (2015) , we compute TF-IDF vectors for the context and for each candidate response, respectively, and then choose the candidate whose cosine similarity with the context is highest as the model output. The “IDF” is computed using only the training set.
source_text: We evaluate individual scoring methods, multichoice methods and human performance in our experiment. Given a context c and four candidates (r 1 , r 2 , r 3 , r 4 ) , the individual scoring method computes a score for each choice independently with a score g(c, r i ), and selects the individual with the highest score among four candidates. On the contrary, the multi-choice method selects the best one by classification over all choices, formulated as h (c, r 1 , r 2 , r 3 , r 4 ) . TF-IDF: The correct response tends to share more words with the context than the incorrect ones. Following Lowe et al. (2015) , we calculate the TF-IDF vectors for the context and each of the candidate responses, respectively, and then select the highest cosine similarity between the context and the candidate response as the model output. The "IDF" is calculated only on the training set.

text: In narratology, uncertainty about outcomes is traditionally regarded as suspenseful (e.g., O'Neill, 2013; Zillmann, 1996; Abbott, 2008). Other scholars argue that suspense may arise without uncertainty (e.g., Smuts, 2008; Hoeken and van Vliet, 2000; Gerrig, 1989), and that readers can experience suspense even when rereading a story for a second time (Delatorre et al., 2018), which is unexpected if suspense is identified with uncertainty; this is known as the paradox of suspense (Prieto-Pablos, 1998; Yanal, 1996). Returning to Romeo and Juliet, the first view holds that suspense is motivated chiefly by uncertainty about what will occur: Who will be injured or killed in the fight? What will follow the marriage? Yet at the start of the play we are told, “from forth the fatal loins of these two foes, a pair of starcrossed lovers take their life,” and thus the suspense is more about being engaged in the plot than about not knowing the ending, aligning more closely with the second view, namely that suspense can exist without uncertainty. We do not address the paradox of suspense directly in this paper, but this debate informs our operationalisation of methods intended to accommodate both perspectives. The Hale model is closer to the traditional conception of suspense as a matter of uncertainty. By contrast, the Ely model better accords with the view that uncertainty matters less than outcomes that differ in their consequences.
source_text: In narratology, uncertainty over outcomes is traditionally seen as suspenseful (e.g., O'Neill, 2013; Zillmann, 1996; Abbott, 2008) . Other authors claim that suspense can exist without uncertainty (e.g., Smuts, 2008; Hoeken and van Vliet, 2000; Gerrig, 1989) and that readers feel suspense even when they read a story for the second time (Delatorre et al., 2018) , which is unexpected if suspense is uncertainty; this is referred to as the paradox of suspense (Prieto-Pablos, 1998; Yanal, 1996) . Considering Romeo and Juliet again, in the first view suspense is motivated by primarily by uncertainty over what will happen. Who will be hurt or killed in the fight? What will happen after marriage? However, at the beginning of the play we are told "from forth the fatal loins of these two foes, a pair of starcrossed lovers take their life", and so the suspense is more about being invested in the plot than not knowing the outcome, aligning more with the second view: suspense can exist without uncertainty. We do not address the paradox of suspense directly in this paper, but we are guided by the debate to operationalise methods that encompass both views. The Hale model is closer to the traditional model of suspense as being about uncertainty. In contrast, the Ely model is more in line with the second view that uncertainty matters less than consequentially different outcomes.

text: Hierarchical Text Generation. Hierarchical text generation with high-level semantic planning has been investigated in many prior works. Sordoni et al. (2015a) present a hierarchical recurrent encoder-decoder architecture for context-aware query suggestion. Another study proposes a framework for inferring semantic features for response generation through self-supervised learning. Prior work has also employed multi-level LSTM encoders (Hu et al., 2020) and hierarchical autoencoders (Li et al., 2015) to learn hierarchical representations for long texts. Shen et al. (2019) use a variational autoencoder to encode an entire paragraph into a single latent variable, from which the paragraph can then be generated hierarchically. In contrast, our task is to generate intermediate sentences in the surrounding context.
source_text: Hierarchical Text Generation. Hierarchical text generation with high-level semantic planning has been studied in many previous works. (Sordoni et al., 2015a) presents a hierarchical recurrent encoder-decoder architecture for context-aware query suggestion. proposes a framework to infer semantic features for response generation using self-supervised learning. Previous works have used multi-level LSTM encoders Hu et al., 2020) and hierarchical autoencoders (Li et al., 2015) to learn hierarchical representations for long text. (Shen et al., 2019) uses a variational autoencoder to encode an entire paragraph into a single latent variable, from which the paragraph can be generated hierarchically. In comparison, our task is to generate intermediate sentences in the surrounding context.

text: We employ ExactMatch (EM), BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), and SARI (Xu et al., 2016) scores 5 as evaluation metrics for fluency. We also use precision, recall, and F1 score as evaluation metrics for fidelity. For WEBEDIT, we extract entities from both the generated text and the reference text and then compute precision, recall, and F1 scores. For ROTOEDIT, we use the information extraction tool provided by Wiseman et al. (2017) to calculate the scores.
source_text: We utilize ExactMatch (EM), BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) and SARI (Xu et al., 2016 ) scores 5 as evaluation metrics for fluency. We also utilize precision, recall, and F1 score as evaluation metrics for fidelity. For WEBEDIT, we extract the entities from the generated text and the reference text and then calculate the precision, recall, and F1 scores. For ROTOEDIT, we use the information extraction tool provided by Wiseman et al. (2017) for calculation of the scores.

text: In this study, we examined whether RNN language models can prioritize multiple simultaneously valid interpretations in a manner similar to humans, as in John met the student of the teacher that was happy. Although both LOW attachment (i.e. the teacher was happy) and HIGH attachment (i.e. the student was happy) are semantically plausible in the absence of disambiguating context, human readers nevertheless show preferences for one attachment over the other (for example, English speakers tend to prefer LOW attachment, whereas Spanish speakers tend to prefer HIGH attachment). Motivated by recent work suggesting that RNN LMs acquire abstract syntactic representations, we evaluated the hypothesis that these models also develop human-like attachment preferences. Our results indicate that they do not.
source_text: In this work, we explored the ability of RNN LMs to prioritize multiple simultaneous valid interpretations in a human-like way (as in John met the student of the teacher that was happy). While both LOW attachment (i.e. the teacher was happy) and HIGH attachment (i.e. the student was happy) are equally semantically plausible without a disambiguating context, humans have interpretation preferences for one attachment over the other (e.g., English speakers prefer LOW attachment and Spanish speakers prefer HIGH attachment). Given the recent body of literature suggesting that RNN LMs have learned abstract syntactic representations, we tested the hypothesis that these models acquire human-like attachment preferences. We found that they do not.

text: In general, the French results corroborate those obtained for English. First, all model variants achieve higher scores than the random baseline model, including on fluency (p < 0.01), which again confirms that the rhyme constraint and topical constraint do not impair the grammaticality of the verse. Second, the rhyme constraint yields a significant gain in poeticness (p < 0.05 relative to rnn), whereas the topical constraint improves both coherence (p < 0.05) and meaningfulness (p < 0.01). In contrast to the English findings, only a small fraction of poems produced by the rhyme model are considered human-written. We again observe that poeticness is markedly higher (p < 0.01) for nmf spec than for nmf rand, which appears to suggest that the topic of a poem is an important factor in people’s judgments of poeticness. Finally, nmf spec again attains the best scores across all criteria, and nearly half of its poems are judged to have been written by a human.
source_text: Generally speaking, we see that the results for French confirm those for English. First of all, all model instantiations obtain better scores than the random baseline model, even with regard to fluency (p < 0.01), again confirming that the application of the rhyme constraint and topical constraint are not detrimental to the grammaticality of the verse. Secondly, the rhyme constraint significantly improves the score for poeticness (p < 0.05 compared to rnn), while the topical constraint improves both coherence (p < 0.05) and meaningfulness (p < 0.01). Contrary to the English results, only a small proportion of poems from the rhyme model are thought to be human. We do again see that the score for poeticness is considerably higher (p < 0.01) for nmf spec than for nmf rand , which seems to indicate that the topic of a poem is an important factor in people's judgements on poeticness. Finally, we again see that the best scores on all criteria are obtained with nmf spec , for which almost half of the poems are judged to be written by a human.

text: In this study, we concentrate on text generation from RDF data. 1 The input to this task consists of a set of RDF triples, each triple (s, p, o) comprising a subject, a predicate, and an object. For instance, ("U.K.", "cap- Figure 2: The architecture of the proposed DUALENC model. The input triples are transformed into a graph and then passed to two GCN encoders for plan and text generation (Planner and Graph Encoder, top center). The resulting plan is subsequently encoded by an LSTM network (Plan Encoder, bottom center). Finally, an LSTM decoder integrates the hidden states from both encoders to produce the text (Text Decoder, middle right).
source_text: In this work we focus on text generation from RDF data. 1 The input for this task is a set of RDF triples, where each triple (s, p, o) contains a subject, a predicate, and an object. For example, ("U.K.", "cap- Figure 2 : The architecture of the proposed DUALENC model. The input triples are converted as a graph and then fed to two GCN encoders for plan and text generation (Planner and Graph Encoder, top center). The plan is then encoded by an LSTM network (Plan Encoder, bottom center). Finally an LSTM decoder combines the hidden states from both the encoders to generate the text (Text Decoder, middle right).

text: We leverage neural language models and commonsense models to investigate how the cognitive processes of recollection and imagination are recruited in storytelling. Our analysis focuses on two central properties of stories: narrative flow, that is, how the story is read, and semantic versus episodic knowledge, namely the kinds of events contained in the story. As a measure of narrative flow, we propose the likelihood of sentences under generative language models conditioned on varying amounts of preceding context. We then operationalize semantic knowledge by measuring the frequency of commonsense events from the ATOMIC knowledge graph (Sap et al., 2019), and episodic knowledge by counting realis events (Sims et al., 2019), both illustrated in Figure 1.
source_text: We harness neural language and commonsense models to study how cognitive processes of recollection and imagination are engaged in storytelling. We rely on two key aspects of stories: narrative flow (how the story reads) and semantic vs. episodic knowledge (the types of events in the story). We propose as a measure of narrative flow the likelihood of sentences under generative language models conditioned on varying amounts of history. Then, we quantify semantic knowledge by measuring the frequency of commonsense events (from the ATOMIC knowledge graph; Sap et al., 2019) , and episodic knowledge by counting realis events (Sims et al., 2019) , both shown in Figure 1 .

text: where α j denotes a gate vector with the same dimensionality as the denoised encodings. In a similar manner, we obtain s input. A key advantage of our approach is that it can incorporate techniques commonly used in supervised models, such as attention (Bahdanau et al., 2014) and copying (Vinyals et al., 2015). Pseudo-reviews generated via segment noising contain a variety of chunk permutations, which can produce ungrammatical and incoherent text. Applying a copy mechanism to such texts may therefore degrade the fluency of the output. We thus permit copying only for document-noisy inputs (see module (d) in Figure 2).
source_text: where α j is a gate vector with the same dimensionality as the denoised encodings. Analogously, we obtain s input. An advantage of our method is its ability to incorporate techniques used in supervised models, such as attention (Bahdanau et al., 2014) and copy (Vinyals et al., 2015) . Pseudo-reviews created using segment noising include various chunk permutations, which could result to ungrammatical and incoherent text. Using a copy mechanism on these texts may hurt the fluency of the output. We therefore allow copy on document noisy inputs only (see module (d) in Figure 2 ).

text: We train the Transformer models using the supervised data both with and without non-entailment instances. Since removing non-entailment instances reduces the number of training examples, we also employ the self-training strategy (Murao et al., 2019) to match the number of training instances in the full supervision data. More specifically, we generated headlines for the source documents discarded in Section 4.1 and added pairs of source documents and generated headlines as pseudo-supervision data. The experiments compare models trained on the full supervision data (full), the data filtered by the entailment classifier (filtered), and the filtered data augmented with self-training (filtered+pseudo).
source_text: We train the Transformer models on the supervision data with and without non-entailment instances. Because removing non-entailment instances decreases the number of training instances, we also apply the self-training strategy (Murao et al., 2019) to obtain the same amount of training instances to the full supervision data. More specifically, we generated headlines for the source documents discarded in Section 4.1, and added pairs of source documents and generated headlines as pseudo supervision data. The experiments compare models trained on the full supervision data (full), the one filtered by the entailment classifier (filtered), and the one filtered but augmented by the self-training (filtered+pseudo).

text: Performance trade-off: Parameter compression does not necessarily lead to lower inference latency, and the reverse is also true. We investigated model behavior under parameter compression, inference latency, and F1 to illustrate this trade-off in Fig. 1 and Table 16 in the Appendix. Multilingual word embeddings: Randomly initialized word embeddings perform well. An even better, and likewise parameter-efficient, initialization is obtained via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) applied to fine-tuned mBERT word embeddings, yielding the best downstream-task performance (Table 7). Generalization: The proposed distillation techniques and strategies are model-, architecture-, and language-agnostic, and can readily be adapted to arbitrary tasks and languages, even though this study concentrates on NER and classification. Massive compression: Our methods achieve substantial compression (35x in parameters) and inference acceleration (51x in latency) while preserving 95% of teacher performance, thereby enabling the practical deployment of deep pre-trained models.
source_text: Performance trade-off: Parameter compression does not necessarily reduce inference latency, and vice versa. We explored model performance with parameter compression, inference latency and F 1 to show trade-off in Fig. 1 and Table 16 in Appendix. Multilingual word embeddings: Random initialization of word embeddings work well. A better initialization, which is also parameter-efficient, is given by Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) over fine-tuned mBERT word embeddings with the best performance for downstream task (Table 7) . Generalization: The outlined distillation techniques and strategies are model-, architecture-, and language-agnostic and can be easily extended to arbitrary tasks and languages, although we only focus on NER and classification in this work. Massive compression: Our techniques demonstrate massive compression (35x for parameters) and inference speedup (51x for latency) while retaining 95% of the teacher performance allowing deep pre-trained models to be deployed in practice.

text: We evaluate CLWE on two downstream tasks: document classification and dependency parsing. We keep the model’s embedding layer fixed to CLWE and adopt the zero-shot setting, in which the model is trained in English and then evaluated in the target language. Document Classification Our first downstream task is document-level classification. We employ MLDoc, a multilingual classification benchmark (Schwenk and Li, 2018), using the standard split of 1K training documents and 4K test documents. Following Glavas et al. (2019), we use a convolutional neural network (Kim, 2014). We apply 0.5 dropout to the final layer, train with Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) using default parameters for ten epochs, and report the mean accuracy over ten runs.
source_text: We compare CLWE on two downstream tasks: document classification and dependency parsing. We fix the embeddng layer of the model to CLWE and use the zero-shot setting, where a model is trained in English and evaluated in the target language. Document Classification Our first downstream task is document-level classification. We use MLDoc, a multilingual classification benchmark (Schwenk and Li, 2018) using the standard split with 1K training and 4K test documents. Following Glavas et al. (2019) , we use a convolutional neural network (Kim, 2014) . We apply 0.5 dropout to the final layer, run Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with default parameters for ten epochs, and report the average accuracy of ten runs.

text: We employ a pre-trained language model as the generator, which serves as the “innate language skill.” Because the number of training examples is small and the vocabulary is therefore limited, we keep the pre-trained word embeddings fixed while fine-tuning the remaining parameters of the pre-trained language model, enabling it to generalize to tokens not seen during training. Figure 1 presents our model architecture. Specifically, we use GPT-2 1, the pre-trained language model introduced in (Radford et al., 2019), which is a 12-layer transformer. The transformer’s final hidden state is then used to compute attention weights and the copy Table 1: BLEU-4 results on three domains. Base-original: the original method in (Liu et al., 2018); Base: uses pre-trained word embeddings; Base+switch: incorporates the switch policy; Base+switch+LM-scratch: has the same architecture as our method, but trains the model from scratch without pre-trained generator weights. Template: manually designed templates switch p copy. We first input the embedded attribute-value list as the context for generation. In this architecture, the generator is fine-tuned from pre-trained parameters, whereas the encoder and attention component are learned from scratch, so the initial geometry of the two parts differs. We therefore need to assign a larger weight to the copy loss p copy, providing the model with a stronger signal to “teach” it to copy facts from the input table.
source_text: We use a pre-trained language model as the generator, serving as the "innate language skill". Due to the vocabulary limitation of few training instances, we leave the pre-trained word embedding fixed while fine-tuning other parameters of the pretrained language model, so that it can generalize with tokens unseen during training. Figure 1 shows our model architecture. We use the pre-trained language model GPT-2 1 proposed in (Radford et al., 2019) , which is a 12-layer transformer. The final hidden state of the transformer is used to calculate attention weights and the copy Table 1 : BLEU-4 results on three domains. Base-original: the original method in (Liu et al., 2018) ; Base: applies pre-trained word embedding; Base+switch: adds the switch policy; Base+switch+LM-scratch: makes the same architecture as our method, but trains the model from scratch without pre-trained weights for the generator. Template: manually crafted templates switch p copy . We first feed the embedded attributevalue list serving as the context for generation. In this architecture, the generator is fine-tuned from pre-trained parameters while the encoder and attention part is learned from scratch, the initial geometry of the two sides are different. Therefore we need to apply larger weight to the copy loss p copy , to give the model a stronger signal to "teach" it to copy facts from the input table.

text: To reduce latency in speech recognition, we adopt a streaming setup in which the recorded audio is transmitted to IBM Watson servers for transcription and then interpreted through a command-oriented interface. In this way, our speech module not only supports dictation, as in Teixeira et al. (2019), but also enables in-place correction of errors. The transcription is displayed at the top of the right target view (see Figure 1c). As available commands, the user may "insert", "delete", "replace", and "reorder" words or subphrases. When the target position is ambiguous, it can be specified by means of anchors such as "after"/"before"/"between", or by indicating the occurrence of the token ("first"/"second"/"last"). A complete example is "insert A after second B", where A and B may be words or subphrases. Unlike the other modalities, character-level commands are not supported; accordingly, rather than deleting an ending, the user should replace the word. As before, spaces between words and punctuation marks are automatically corrected after modifications. For German, nouns are automatically capitalized using the list of nouns from Wiktionary 7.
source_text: To minimize lag during speech recognition, we use a streaming approach, sending the recorded audio to IBM Watson servers to receive a transcription, which is then interpreted in a command-based fashion. Thus, our speech module not only handles dictations as in Teixeira et al. (2019) but can correct mistakes in place. The transcription itself is visualized at the top of the right target view (see Figure 1c) . As commands, the user has the option to "insert", "delete", "replace", and "reorder" words or subphrases. To specify the position if it is ambiguous, one can define anchors as in "after"/"before"/"between", or define the occurrence of the token ("first"/"second"/"last"). A full example is "insert A after second B", where A and B can be words or subphrases. In contrast to the other modalities, character-level commands are not supported, so instead of deleting an ending, one should replace the word. Again, spaces between words and punctuation marks are automatically fixed upon changes. For the German language, nouns are automatically capitalized using the list of nouns from Wiktionary 7 .

text: PTGEN and TCONVS2S model predictions are reported by Narayan et al. (2018a), while Transformer model predictions from GPT-TUNED, TRANS2S, and BERTS2S are provided by Rothe et al. (2020). Both PT-GEN and TCONVS2S employ a Stanford-tokenized vocabulary of 50k. TRANS2S and BERTS2S use a vocabulary of approximately 30k WordPieces (Wu et al., 2016) to align with the pretrained BERT vocabulary, whereas GPT-TUNED uses a vocabulary of approximately 50k SentencePieces (Kudo and Richardson, 2018) to match the pretrained GPT-2 vocabulary. All models share the same uncased vocabulary on both the source and target sides. Summaries for both PTGEN and TCONVS2S were produced with beam search using a beam size of 10, while the Transformer models use a beam size of 4. For further details on these models, see Narayan et al. (2018a) and Rothe et al. (2020).
source_text: PTGEN and TCONVS2S model predictions are provided by Narayan et al. (2018a) and Transformer model predictions from GPT-TUNED, TRANS2S and BERTS2S, by Rothe et al. (2020) . Both PT-GEN and TCONVS2S use a Stanford tokenized vocabulary size of 50k. TRANS2S and BERTS2S use a vocabulary size of around ∼30k WordPieces (Wu et al., 2016) to match BERT pretrained vocabulary and, GPT-TUNED, a vocabulary size of around ∼50k SentencePieces (Kudo and Richardson, 2018) to match the GPT-2 pretrained vocabulary. All models use the same uncased vocabulary on both source and target sides. Both PTGEN and TCONVS2S summaries were generated using beam search with beam size 10, the Transformer models use beam size of 4. See Narayan et al. (2018a) and Rothe et al. (2020) for more details on these models.

text: As described in Section 4, we gathered aspect-based summary labels for all episodes in the CSI corpus. Figure 4 shows the average makeup of a summary across the various aspects encountered in a criminal investigation (e.g., crime scene, victim, cause of death, perpetrator, evidence). Most of these aspects account for 10–15% of a summary, which corresponds to roughly two scenes in an episode. Only the “Evidence” aspect covers a substantially larger share of the summary (36.1%), corresponding to five scenes. However, some scenes address multiple aspects and are therefore assigned more than one label, and some episodes do not contain any scenes relevant to a particular aspect (e.g., if the murder was a suicide, there is no perpetrator).
source_text: As described in Section 4, we collected aspectbased summary labels for all episodes in the CSI corpus. In Figure 4 we illustrate the average composition of a summary based on the different aspects seen in a crime investigation (e.g., crime scene, victim, cause of death, perpetrator, evidence). Most of these aspects are covered in 10-15% of a summary, which corresponds to approximately two scenes in the episode. Only the "Evidence" aspect occupies a larger proportion of the summary (36.1%) corresponding to five scenes. However, there exist scenes which cover multiple aspects (an as a result are annotated with more than one label) and episodes that do not include any scenes related to a specific aspect (e.g., if the murder was a suicide, there is no perpetrator).

text: More recently, in the area of dialogue personalization, Zemlyanskiy and Sha (2018) proposed a post-processing approach for re-ranking candidates produced by beam search, whereas Olabiyi et al. (2019) used adversarial methods to address the consistency issue involving interlocutors’ names. Madotto et al. (2019) applied meta-learning to enable rapid adaptation to new speakers, and Tigunova et al. (2019) extracted user attributes from everyday dialogues. In contrast to these studies, our work advances persona-based dialogue generation from a novel perspective.
source_text: More recently, under the topic of dialogue personalizing, Zemlyanskiy and Sha (2018) proposed a post-processing method to re-rank candidates generated by beam search, while Olabiyi et al. (2019) employed adversarial approaches to solve the consistency problem on interlocutors' names. Madotto et al. (2019) applied meta-learning to quickly adapt to new speakers, and Tigunova et al. (2019) extracted user attributes from daily dialogues. Compared with them, our work enhances persona based dialogue generation from a novel perspective.

text: In the recommendation task, we assess whether paper embeddings can improve performance in a production recommendation system. The objective of this task is to support users in navigating the scientific literature by ranking a set of “similar papers” for a given input paper. For this purpose, we use a user clickthrough dataset consisting of 22K clickthrough events collected from a public scholarly search engine. We split the examples temporally into training (20K examples), validation (1K), and test (1K) sets. As is common in clickthrough data over ranked lists, clicks are skewed toward the upper positions of the original ranking shown to the user. To mitigate this bias, we estimated propensity scores via a swap experiment (Agarwal et al., 2019). These propensity scores quantify, for each rank position, the relative extent to which that position is overrepresented in the data because of exposure bias. We then obtain de-biased evaluation metrics by dividing the score of each test example by the propensity score associated with the clicked position. We report propensity-adjusted variants of the standard ranking metrics Precision@1 (P @1) and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG).
source_text: In the recommendation task, we evaluate the ability of paper embeddings to boost performance in a production recommendation system. Our recommendation task aims to help users navigate the scientific literature by ranking a set of "similar papers" for a given paper. We use a dataset of user clickthrough data for this task which consists of 22K clickthrough events from a public scholarly search engine. We partitioned the examples temporally into train (20K examples), validation (1K), and test (1K) sets. As is typical in clickthrough data on ranked lists, the clicks are biased toward the top of original ranking presented to the user. To counteract this effect, we computed propensity scores using a swap experiment (Agarwal et al., 2019). The propensity scores give, for each position in the ranked list, the relative frequency that the position is over-represented in the data due to exposure bias. We can then compute de-biased evaluation metrics by dividing the score for each test example by the propensity score for the clicked position. We report propensity-adjusted versions of the standard ranking metrics Precision@1 (P @1) and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG).

text: 1 Introduction Kahneman (2011) distinguishes between two modes of human thought, which neatly mirror the present state (so-called System1) and the aspirational state (System1+System2) of the deep learning field. System1 supports operations that people perceive as rapid, intuitive, and automatic, such as object detection and document classification. Recent deep learning (DL) models have demonstrated strong promise on these kinds of tasks, owing to the availability of large training datasets. However, human-like abstract reasoning arises through slow, rational, and sequential processes, Chikasaw English 1. Ofi'at kowi'ã lhiyohli. The dog chases the cat. 2. Kowi'at ofi'ã lhiyohli. The cat chases the dog. 3. Ofi'at shoha.
source_text: 1 Introduction Kahneman (2011) discusses the two modes of human thinking which perfectly encapsulate the current (so called System1) and the desired state (Sys-tem1+System2) of the deep learning field. System1 handles tasks that humans consider fast, intuitive and automatic, such as object detection and document classification. Recent deep learning (DL) models have shown great promise at this type of tasks-thanks to large training datasets. Yet, it is through slow, rational and sequential mechanisms that human-like abstract reasoning happens, Chikasaw English 1. Ofi'at kowi'ã lhiyohli. The dog chases the cat. 2. Kowi'at ofi'ã lhiyohli. The cat chases the dog. 3. Ofi'at shoha.

text: “Sooner would I go dry at Lammas-tide than be a man without a moon,” Christian went on, in that same broken chant. “’Tis said I am but the rames of a man, and fit for naught in my line at all; and I reckon that must be the reason o’t.”
source_text: “I’d sooner go without drink at Lammas-tide than be a man of no moon,” continued Christian, in the same shattered recitative. “’Tis said I be only the rames of a man, and no good for my race at all; and I suppose that’s the cause o’t.”

text:
Who reared this little Alban house  
And drew the windows down so close
