We first pretrain the captioning model for up to 20 epochs, then train it with reinforcement learning for 20 additional epochs, and finally test the best model on the validation set.
source_text: We conduct experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , investigating benefits brought by the Guider network. In image captioning, instead of using a discriminator to define final rewards for generated sentence, we adopt evaluation metrics computed based on human references. The final rewards appear more important as they contain reference (ground-truth) information. Feature-matching rewards work as a regularizer of the final rewards. We call our model in this setting a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is provided in the Appendix. We test our proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , containing 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least 5 captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , 5,000 images are used for both validation and testing. We report BLEU-k (k from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015) , and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) scores. We consider two settings: (i) using a pre-trained 152layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-way pool5 layer from ResNet-152, pretrained on the ImageNet dataset; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features . We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units with mini-batches of size 64. Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) is used for optimization, with learning rate 2 × 10 −4 . We pretrain the captioning model for the maximum 20 epochs, then use the reinforcement learning to train it for 20 epochs and test on the best model on the validation set.

text: Learning-to-rank formulations for answer selection in QA systems are standard practice, and they most often depend on pointwise ranking models (Severyn and Moschitti, 2015; Garg et al., 2019). Our use of discriminative re-ranking (Collins and Koo, 2005) with softmax loss is more akin to pairwise ranking, in which the multiclass margin between correct and incorrect answers is maximized (Joachims, 2002; Burges et al., 2005; Köppel et al., 2019). This represents an important difference from TREC-style answer selection, since our ST-generated candidate responses exhibit lower semantic, syntactic, and lexical variance, which makes pointwise methods less effective.
source_text: Learning to Rank formulations for answer selection in QA systems is common practice, most frequently relying on pointwise ranking models (Severyn and Moschitti, 2015; Garg et al., 2019) . Our use of discriminative re-ranking (Collins and Koo, 2005) with softmax loss is closer to learning a pairwise ranking by maximizing the multiclass margin between correct and incorrect answers (Joachims, 2002; Burges et al., 2005; Köppel et al., 2019) . This is an important distinction from TREC-style answer selection as our ST-generated candidate responses have lower semantic, syntactic, and lexical variance, making pointwise methods less effective.

text: Natural language explanations can encode different forms of inductive bias and prior knowledge, although some kinds of prior knowledge are naturally better incorporated through alternative mechanisms. We conclude our experiments with a vignette illustrating how language explanations can complement other approaches to feature and representation engineering. We focus on Disease, for which we have access to an external ontology, the Comparative Toxicogenomic Database (CTD) from Wei et al. (2015), containing chemical–disease interactions. Following Hancock et al. (2018), we append 6 bits to the explanation representation v(x) to indicate whether the provided chemical–disease pair satisfies specific relations in CTD (for example, whether it appears in the ctd-therapy dictionary). Table 5 shows that, as anticipated, additional information sources can complement language explanations in ExpBERT.
source_text: Natural language explanations can capture different types of inductive biases and prior knowledge, but some types of prior knowledge are of course better introduced through other means. We wrap up our experiments with a vignette on how language explanations can complement other forms of feature and representation engineering. We consider Disease, where we have access to an external ontology (Comparative Toxicogenomic Database or CTD) from Wei et al. (2015) containing chemicaldisease interactions. Following Hancock et al. (2018), we add 6 bits to the explanation representation v(x) that test if the given chemical-disease pair follows certain relations in CTD (e.g., if they are in the ctd-therapy dictionary). Table 5 shows that as expected, other sources of information can complement language explanations in ExpBERT.

text: As in prior academic studies on this corpus (Bansal et al., 2018), we employ a sequence-to-sequence architecture inspired by Weiss et al. (2017), adapted to operate under lower-resource conditions; in particular, each model reaches convergence in approximately 5 days on a single GPU. We implement encoder-decoder models with attention in xnmt using 512 hidden units. Our pyramidal encoder consists of 3-layer BiLSTMs with linear network-in-network (NiN) projections and batch normalization between layers (Zhang et al., 2017). These NiN projections downsample by a factor of 2 between layers, yielding the same overall 4× temporal downsampling as the extra convolutional layers in Weiss et al. 2017; Bansal et al. 2019. This provides the advantage of increased depth with fewer additional parameters. We use single-layer MLP attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015) with 128 units and a single decoder layer, rather than the 3 or 4 layers used in previous work, because we did not observe consistent gains from greater depth.
source_text: As in previous academic work on this corpus (Bansal et al., 2018; , we use a sequence-to-sequence architecture inspired by Weiss et al. (2017) modified to train within lower resources; specifically, each model converges within ≈5 days on one GPU. We build encoder-decoder models with attention in xnmt with 512 hidden units. Our pyramidal encoder uses 3-layer BiLSTMs with linear network-in-network (NiN) projections and batch normalization between layers Zhang et al., 2017) . The NiN projections are used to downsample by a factor of 2 between layers, resulting in the same total 4× downsampling in time as the additional convolutional layers from Weiss et al. 2017; Bansal et al. 2019: They give us the benefit of added depth with fewer additional parameters. We use single layer MLP attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015) with 128 units and 1 decoder layer as opposed to 3 or 4 in previous workwe did not see consistent benefits from additional depth.

text: From a different perspective, research in audio recognition has primarily focused on detecting simple single-word commands or keyword spotting (de Andrade et al., 2018), identifying acoustic events such as environmental or urban sounds (Salamon et al., 2014; Piczak, 2015; Xu et al., 2016), or music patterns, as well as document-level classification of complete audio sequences (Liu et al., 2017). McMahan and Rao (2018) offer a more comprehensive overview. Although methods in this area are related to ours—for example, in their use of convolutional networks over audio (Sainath and Parada, 2015; Salamon and Bello, 2017)—our task involves questions as linguistic units of substantially greater complexity.
source_text: From another viewpoint, work in audio recognition mainly involves with detecting simple singleword commands or keyword spotting (de Andrade et al., 2018) , recognizing acoustic events such as environmental or urban sounds (Salamon et al., 2014; Piczak, 2015; Xu et al., 2016) or music patterns, or document-level classification of entire audio sequences (Liu et al., 2017) . McMahan and Rao (2018) provide a more extensive overview. While approaches in this line of work relate to ours, e.g. in the use of convolutional networks over audio (Sainath and Parada, 2015; Salamon and Bello, 2017) , our challenge features questions as linguistic units of significantly greater complexity.

text: For our experiments, we rely on several well-established dialogue corpora as baselines, namely Persona-Chat (Zhang et al., 2018), Cornell (Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil and Lee, 2011) (the unfiltered corpus from which we extract yes-ands, as described in Section 2.2), and DailyDialog (Li et al., 2017b). Each is an English-language open-domain casual conversation corpus containing 100k–300k turns. For each dataset, we either simply fine-tune on that dataset, or fine-tune and then further Figure 5: Interface used by human evaluators to rank responses by their quality as a yes-and, where a rank of 1 is most preferred. The correct ranking is shown in this example. The option ranked 1 is a yes-but: it does not deny a reality, but instead rejects a suggestion and provides refinement information that is most coherent with the prompt.
source_text: For our experiments, we use several established dialogue datasets as baselines, namely Persona-chat (Zhang et al., 2018) , Cornell (Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil and Lee, 2011) (the unfiltered corpus out of which we extract yes-ands, as described in Section 2.2), and DailyDialog (Li et al., 2017b) . Each of these is an English-language open-domain casual conversation corpus with 100k-300k turns. For each of these datasets, we either simply finetune on that dataset, or fine-tune and then further Figure 5 : Interface used by human evaluators to rank responses based on their quality as a yes-and, where a rank of 1 is most preferred. The correct ranking is shown for this example. The option ranked 1 is a yesbut: it does not reject a reality but rather rejects a suggestion and provides refining information that is most coherent to the prompt.

text: Training Data To train our model, we use a subset of the Semantic Scholar corpus comprising approximately 146K query papers (about 26.7M tokens) together with their corresponding outgoing citations, and we reserve an additional 32K papers for validation. For each query paper, we create up to 5 training triples, each consisting of a query, a positive, and a negative paper. Positive papers are sampled from the query’s direct citations, whereas negative papers are selected either at random or from citations of citations (as described in §2.4). Empirically, we found it beneficial to use 2 hard negatives (citations of citations) and 3 easy negatives (randomly selected papers) for each query paper. This yields roughly 684K training triples and 145K validation triples.
source_text: Training Data To train our model, we use a subset of the Semantic Scholar corpus consisting of about 146K query papers (around 26.7M tokens) with their corresponding outgoing citations, and we use an additional 32K papers for validation. For each query paper we construct up to 5 training triples comprised of a query, a positive, and a negative paper. The positive papers are sampled from the direct citations of the query, while negative papers are chosen either randomly or from citations of citations (as discussed in §2.4). We empirically found it helpful to use 2 hard negatives (citations of citations) and 3 easy negatives (randomly selected papers) for each query paper. This process results in about 684K training triples and 145K validation triples.

text: We employ AllenNLP’s wrapper around HuggingFace’s Transformers code 1 for our implementation 2. For general-domain datasets, we initialize models with BERT-base-cased weights, and for clinical datasets we use bio-BERT weights (Lee et al., 2019) as initialization. Our analysis uses cased models, since bio-BERT (Lee et al., 2019) is itself cased. A single learning rate of 2e-5 was used across all experiments. We relied on validation splits supplied by the datasets. When no validation set was provided, as in MADE 1.0, EMRQA, or SQuAD1.1, we reserved 10% of the training data for validation. We set early stopping patience to 5, with each epoch comprising 20,000 steps. Rather than negative log likelihood (NLL), we monitored the final evaluation metric for training and early stopping. This was done to lessen the mis-calibration of the underlying p θ model, given Guo et al. (2017)’s observation that neural networks overfit on NLL. The implementation details for each experiment are given in the following subsections.
source_text: We use AllenNLP's wrapper with HuggingFace's Transformers code 1 for our implementation 2 . We use BERT-base-cased weights as the initialization for general-domain datasets and bio-BERT weights (Lee et al., 2019) as the initialization for clinical datasets. We use cased models for our analysis, since bio-BERT (Lee et al., 2019) uses cased models. A common learning rate of 2e-5 was used for all experiments. We used validation data splits provided by the datasets. In cases where the validation dataset was not provided, such as MADE 1.0, EMRQA or SQuAD1.1, we use 10% of the training data as the validation data. We use a patience of 5 for early stopping the model, with each epoch consisting of 20,000 steps. We use the final evaluation metric instead of negative log likelihood (NLL) to monitor and early stop the training. This is to reduce the mis-calibration of the underlying p θ model, since Guo et al. (2017) observe that neural nets overfit on NLL. The implementation for each experiment is provided in the following subsections.

text: Context and significance. Learning to assign labels to text sequences is among the most extensively studied problems in natural language processing. More recently, neural networks have been used not only for sequential labeling tasks such as part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) and named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016), but also to reformulate inherently non-sequential tasks, such as syntactic parsing, within a labeling framework (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019).
source_text: Context and relevance. Learning to label sequences of text is one of the more thoroughly explored topics in natural language processing. In recent times, neural networks are applied not only to sequential labeling like part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) or named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016) , but also to cast into a labeling framework otherwise non-sequential tasks such as syntactic parsing (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019) .

text: In our corpora, each orthographic word corresponds to a unique correct segment sequence. For convenience, we therefore identify \(s_{1:n}\) with its associated orthographic form. By abuse of notation, we write \(p(\cdot \mid \cdot)\) for the distribution over segmental forms induced by the language model. The listener receives a segment sequence \(s_{1:n} = (s_1, \ldots, s_n)\)—for example, [SIg@ôEt] (“shigarette”)—drawn from a channel distribution conditioned on the speaker’s intended segment sequence: \(s_{1:n} \sim p(\cdot \mid s_{1:n})\). This captures the effect of noise on the signal received by the listener.
source_text: In our corpora, there is a unique correct segment sequence for a given orthographic word. For ease of exposition, we therefore identify 1∶ with its corresponding orthographic form . Abusing notation, we will write ( 1∶ | ) for the distribution over segmental forms induced by the language model. 2 4. The listener receives a segment sequence 1∶ = ( 1 , ..., ) -e.g. [SIg@ôEt] ('shigarette') -drawn from a channel distribution conditioned on the speaker's intended segment sequence: 1∶ ∼ (⋅| 1∶ ). This represents the effects of noise on the signal received by the listener.

text: Limiting Behavior Another important observation is that importance-sampled estimates of perplexity are consistent, that is, they converge as the number of samples tends to infinity. To establish this, we first note that p(x) is consistent, a standard consequence of the strong law of large numbers (Geweke, 1989). Consequently, log p(x) is also consistent by the continuous mapping theorem (Van der Vaart, 2000).
source_text: Limiting Behavior Another important observation is that importance sampled estimates of perplexity are consistent, e.g., will converge as the number of samples approaches infinity. To prove this, we first observe thatp(x) is consistent, which is a well-known consequence of the strong law of large numbers (Geweke, 1989) . Accordingly, logp(x) is also consistent due to the continuous mapping theorem (Van der Vaart, 2000) .

text: Despite these limitations, we hope that LEAQI offers a useful, and relatively simple, bridge for incorporating rule-based systems, heuristics, and unsupervised models as components of more complex supervised learning systems. This is especially appealing in settings where very strong rule-based systems are available, since such systems often surpass the best statistical systems, as in coreference resolution (Lee et al., 2011), information extraction (Riloff and Wiebe, 2003), and morphological segmentation and analysis (Smit et al., 2014). We present the performance obtained using different confidence parameters b. These plots suggest that our model is robust to different confidence parameters.
source_text: Despite these limitations, we hope that LEAQI provides a useful (and relatively simple) bridge that can enable using rule-based systems, heuristics, and unsupervised models as building blocks for more complex supervised learning systems. This is particularly attractive in settings where we have very strong rule-based systems, ones which often outperform the best statistical systems, like coreference resolution (Lee et al., 2011) , information extraction (Riloff and Wiebe, 2003) , and morphological segmentation and analysis (Smit et al., 2014 . We show the performance of using difference confidence parameters b. These plots indicate that our model is robust to difference confidence parameters.

text: The pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm has achieved substantial success in NLP applications in recent years and has been adopted across a range of NLP tasks (Radford et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2019). Howard and Ruder (2018) proposed pre-training a language model on a large general-domain corpus and then fine-tuning it for the target task with novel techniques such as discriminative fine-tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing. In this way, such pretrained models achieve excellent results even when only limited labeled data are available. Pre-training approaches are often formulated with different objectives, including language modeling (Peters et al., 2018; Howard and Ruder, 2018; Yang et al., 2019b) and masked language modeling (Devlin et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019). Their performance can also be improved by training larger models on more data (Yang et al., 2019b; Liu et al., 2019).
source_text: The pre-training and fine-tuning framework has achieved huge success on NLP applications in recent years, and has been applied to a variety of NLP tasks (Radford et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2019) . Howard and Ruder (2018) proposed to pre-train a language model on a large general-domain corpus and fine-tune it on the target task using some novel techniques like discriminative fine-tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing. In this manner, such pretrained models show excellent performance even with small amounts of labeled data. Pre-training methods are often designed with different objectives such as language modeling (Peters et al., 2018; Howard and Ruder, 2018; Yang et al., 2019b) and masked language modeling (Devlin et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019) . Their performances are also improved with training larger models on more data (Yang et al., 2019b; Liu et al., 2019) .
